1.51: Now some, scattering only a handful of grain, go from the garhapatya to the ahavaniya with the verse 'idam visnur vicakrame' (Visnu strode forth). But one should not do it that way. For if someone were to say there, 'This sacrificer has obtained only a small portion; soon he will obtain a still smaller portion and will lose his preeminence,' it would indeed be so. One should do it in this manner: Taking a water vessel or a water jar, one should go from the garhapatya to the ahavaniya with the very same verse 'idam visnur vicakrame.' For the rc is a divine purifier; water is a divine purifier. Whatever of the sacrifice remains unquieted, whatever is impure -- water is the pacification of all that.
1.52: They ask: If, while this performer of the long sattra is offering the agnihotra and the agnihotra milk being milked should spill, what is the procedure, what is the expiation? Whatever remains in the pot, with that he should offer. But if the pot should overturn or break, what is the procedure, what is the expiation? For the expiation of spilling, he should touch it saying 'askann adhita' (it has not spilled). Then whatever else he obtains, with that he should offer. For when it spills, it is deposited. Semen is milk; this earth is the womb. He thus establishes the semen in the womb. His semen, having been shed, generates offspring -- he who knows thus. From above Parjanya spills down; here below plants and trees are born. From a man semen spills forth; from that, cattle come. From that all this has been born. He should know: 'The gods have favored me; my progeny has become more abundant; I shall become better.' It would indeed be so. Now, wherever it is broken, one should bring near a water vessel or a water jar with the utterances bhur bhuvas svah. These utterances are the expiation for everything.
1.53: Then, having gathered together those potsherds, one should deposit them where the ashes of the ahavaniya have been taken out. This is the procedure for that case. Now they also ask: If it should spill while being carried eastward after being taken up, what is the procedure, what is the expiation? Whatever remains in the ladle, with that he should offer. But if the ladle should overturn or break, what is the procedure, what is the expiation? The counter-touching for that has been stated. Now some, returning from there, take it up again. But one should not do it that way. For if someone were to say there, 'What he intended to accomplish with the agnihotra has turned back; therefore his heavenly world will not come about,' it would indeed be so. One should do it in this manner: Wherever it spills, there one should sit down. Then they should bring him a pot and a ladle and a spoon, having rinsed them. It is then said, 'I shall take it up from here.' Then, having taken it up as prescribed, taking a fuel stick, he should go forth eastward. Just as one who rises up would defeat enemies, even so, having struck away the non-return and the evil, he reaches the oblation. Now some do obtain it while seated. 'Unoffered is that of him who offers with the remainder of the agnihotra, for it is exhausted,' they say. But one should not know it thus.
1.54: They ask: If, while this performer of the long sattra is offering the agnihotra and the agnihotra milk being milked should become impure, what is the procedure, what is the expiation? Now some think it should indeed be offered, saying, 'The gods do not feel disgust at anything.' But one should not know it thus. A man indeed might feel disgust; the gods too feel disgust at what is disgusting. One should do it in this manner: Having scraped out the hot ashes of the garhapatya, one should silently pour it into that. It is neither offered nor unoffered. One should pour water after it. By water one purifies it. Then whatever else one obtains, with that one should offer. Now they also ask: If the milk that has been milked should become impure, what is the procedure, what is the expiation? One should push back the very coals on which one was about to heat it and silently pour it on them. It is neither offered nor unoffered. One should pour water after it. By water one purifies it. Then whatever else one obtains, with that one should offer. Now they also ask: If what has been placed on the fire should become impure, what is the procedure, what is the expiation? One should push back the very coals on which it was placed and silently pour it on them. It is neither offered nor unoffered. In that one pours it on them, thereby it is offered; in that one lets them follow it, thereby it is unoffered. One should pour water after it. By water one purifies it.
1.55: If what has been carried eastward should become impure, what is the procedure, what is the expiation? Now some think it should indeed be offered, saying, 'This has departed; no offering applies to it.' And some sprinkle it away with water. But one should not do it that way. For if someone were to say there, 'This man has poured away the agnihotra in the wrong direction; this sacrificer will be poured away,' it would indeed be so. One should do it in this manner: Having placed a fuel stick on the ahavaniya itself, having scraped out the hot ashes of the ahavaniya, one should silently pour it into that. It is neither offered nor unoffered. One should pour water after it. By water one purifies it. Then whatever else one obtains, with that one should offer. Now they also ask: If it should rain, what is the procedure, what is the expiation? He should know: 'From above, brightness has come upon me; my progeny has become more abundant; I shall become better.' It would indeed be so. Now they also ask: If, when the first oblation has been offered, coals should follow, where should one offer the second? On whatever fragment of coal is nearest, placing it on that, one should offer. For fire is in each piece of wood. If it does not stay on that, one should offer gold upon it. Gold is the seed of Agni. He who is the father is also the son.
1.56: If, when the milk has been placed on the fire, the sacrificer should die, what is the procedure, what is the expiation? Having heaped fuel around it, one should let it boil over. This is the procedure for that case. And indeed this is the expiation for all haviryajñas. Now they also ask: If, after the milk has been carried eastward, the sacrificer should die, what is the procedure, what is the expiation? Where the four-ladled ghee has been designated, there one should pour it up. This is the procedure for that case. Now they also ask: If, when the first oblation has been offered, the sacrificer should die, what is the procedure, what is the expiation? Now some think it should indeed be offered, saying, 'The agnihotra of him whose first oblation has been offered becomes complete.' But one should not know it thus. For one does not offer the agnihotra for one who has died. Where the four-ladled ghee has been designated, there one should pour it up.
1.57: They ask: If, while this performer of the long sattra is offering the agnihotra, the agnihotra cow should sit down while being milked, what is the procedure, what is the expiation? Now some raise her up with a yajus formula. For she sits down having seen the non-return, the evil of the sacrificer -- she whose agnihotra cow sits down while being milked. They raise her up with 'ud asthad devy aditih' (The goddess Aditi has risen). This earth is the goddess Aditi. They raise this earth for him thereby. 'ayur yajnapatav adhat' (She has placed life in the lord of the sacrifice) -- thereby they place life in him. 'mitraya varunaya ca' (For Mitra and Varuna) -- Mitra and Varuna are the in-breath and the out-breath; thereby they place the in-breath and the out-breath in him. That cow, when that oblation has been offered, they give to a brahmana.
1.58: Now Vajasaneya said: 'From those who lack faith the cow withdraws. They destroy the oblation through distress.' One should do it in this manner: Having simply taken a stick, one should beat her with it and make her rise. Just as a mule going along becomes weary, or a yoked bull sits down, and with a stick-driven, goad-driven animal one reaches whatever path one desires -- even so with this stick-driven, goad-driven cow one reaches whatever heavenly world one desires. One should keep her for oneself.
1.59: They ask: If, while this performer of the long sattra is offering the agnihotra, the calf of the agnihotra cow should be lost, what is the procedure, what is the expiation? Aruni said: 'Heaven is the agnihotra cow; the sun is her calf; this earth is the agnihotra pot. The calf of the agnihotra cow of one who knows thus is never lost. For where could it be lost? Nor does the agnihotra milk of one who knows thus spill while being milked, for it is thus established on this earth. Nor does the agnihotra cow of one who knows thus sit down while being milked. When she rains well, they say of her, having indeed sat down, she has truly rained.' Now they also ask: If she should yield blood, what is the procedure, what is the expiation? For she yields blood having seen the non-return, the evil of the sacrificer. He departs. Having spoken beyond, one should say to cover the anvaharya-pacana fire. Having placed it on the fire and having made a stirring stick, one should cook it. That one should silently pour out. For Prajapati is unexpressed; the agnihotra belongs to Prajapati. Or with the utterances bhur bhuvas svah. These utterances are the expiation for everything.
1.60: If the fires should follow (i.e., spread uncontrollably), what is the procedure, what is the expiation? Now some churn a new fire from the firebrand itself, saying, 'From whatever quarter a man's fire is ultimately lost, from that quarter one should seek its expiation.' But one should not know it thus. For one would simply take the firebrand from there and carry it, or cut a piece from the firebrand. One should do it in this manner: Having split off a piece from the firebrand, one should churn it in the fire-sticks. He attains the desire of one who churns from a firebrand, and also of one who churns from the fire-sticks. Now they also ask: If the āhavanīya, after being taken up, should follow (spread), what is the procedure, what is the expiation? It is not yet lost as long as the gārhapatya does not follow. Even if it follows many times, one should take it up again and again. This is the procedure for that case. Now they also ask: If, when the āhavanīya has been taken up, the gārhapatya should follow, what is the procedure, what is the expiation? Now some carry it forward from there in an eastward direction. But one should not do it that way. For if someone were to say there, 'This man has torn away the vital breaths of the sacrificer in the forward direction; this sacrificer will die,' it would indeed be so. Now some bring it back from there in a westward direction, saying, 'The gārhapatya is the prāṇa, the āhavanīya is the apāna. These two, prāṇa and apāna, being in accord, eat food.' And also: 'The gārhapatya is the house. Houses are the foundation. He who would say there, "This man has been dislodged from his foundation; this sacrificer will die," it would indeed be so.' Now some, having churned in the gārhapatya itself, pile it up. But one should not do it that way. For if someone were to say, 'He has generated fire upon fire; soon a hostile rival will be born to him,' it would indeed be so. Now some, having let it go out, churn. But one should not do it that way. For if someone were to say, 'Even what remained he has destroyed; not even an heir of his will survive,' it would indeed be so. One should do it in this manner: One should mount it upon the fire-sticks with 'ayaṃ te yonir ṛtviyo yato jāto arocathāḥ taṃ jānann agna ārohāthā no vardhayā rayim athā vardhayā giraḥ' (This is your womb, O seasonal one, from which, being born, you shone forth. Knowing that, O Agni, mount up; then increase our wealth, then increase our hymns). He should dwell offering the oblation at the new settlement. At a new settlement, his oblation is offered for both nights; he commits no omission of anyone. In the morning, having removed the ashes, having smeared with a lump of cow dung, he should establish the fires as before.
1.61: If the sun should set (while the āhavanīya is not yet taken up), what is the procedure, what is the expiation? All the gods withdraw from him whose āhavanīya has not been taken up when the sun sets. He should tie a gold piece with a darbha grass blade and carry it behind. The form of him who shines yonder is thus fashioned. It is the form of the day. The form of the day is thus fashioned. Then, having lit a fuel stick, they should carry it eastward. Having piled it up, having taken four-ladled ghee, he should offer with 'viśvebhyo devebhyas svāhā' (To all the gods, svāhā!). Just as one would address an angry householder coming with a gift of an ox-load, or with some other pleasing thing, even so one thereby addresses all the gods. They all become unangered toward him.
1.62: If the sun should rise (while the āhavanīya is not yet taken up), what is the procedure, what is the expiation? All the gods withdraw from him whose āhavanīya has not been taken up when the sun rises. He should tie a silver piece with a darbha grass blade and carry it in front. The form of the moon is thus fashioned. It is the form of the night. The form of the night is thus fashioned. Then, having lit a fuel stick, they should carry it westward. Having piled it up, having taken four-ladled ghee, he should offer with 'viśvebhyo devebhyas svāhā' (To all the gods, svāhā!). Where there is a flame, there one should take it up as 'Here is the gārhapatya,' knowing thus. That one yonder is the kinsman. Now some say: 'They who offer looking toward the sun, they indeed offer seeing the heavenly world.'
1.63: If the fires should come together (merge), what is the procedure, what is the expiation? If another fire should come from beyond, burning toward him, he should know: 'From beyond, brightness has come upon me; my progeny has become more abundant; I shall become better.' It would indeed be so. But if it should wound his heart, he should offer an iṣṭi to Agni Vivici. These are the fifteen kindling verses, the two ājyabhāgas in vārtraghna mode, the two virāj verses as saṃyājyas. And these are the yājyā and puronuvākyā: 'vi te viṣvag vātajūtāso agne bhāmāsaś śuce śucayaś caranti tuvimrakṣāso divyā navagvā vanā vananti dhṛṣatā rujantaḥ' (Your flames, O Agni, driven by the wind, blazing, bright ones move in all directions; the divine Navagvas, bold destroyers, overthrow the forests, shattering them). And the yājyā: 'tvām agne mānuṣīr īl̥ate viśo hotrāvidaṃ viviciṃ ratnadhātamam guhā santaṃ subhaga viśvadarśataṃ tuviṣmaṇasaṃ suyajaṃ ghṛtaśriyam' (You, O Agni, the human clans praise, the knower of the offering, the discriminator, the best bestower of treasures, hidden, fortunate, visible to all, of great power, easy to worship, splendid with ghee). And he who wishes to turn away from evil should sacrifice with this. He quickly turns away from evil. But if this fire should come from here, burning toward the other, he should offer an iṣṭi to Agni Saṃvarga.
1.64: 'ma no asmin mahadhane para varg bharabhrd yatha sam vargam sam rayim jaya' (Do not let us be overcome in this great contest like a burden-bearer; win together the spoils, win together the wealth). And the yajya: 'parasya adhi samvato avaram abhy a tara yatraham asmi tam ava' (From beyond the boundary, cross over to the nearer side; protect that where I am). And he who wishes to devour his rival's possessions should sacrifice with this desire. He quickly devours his. Now they also ask: If one should take up fire upon fire, what is the procedure, what is the expiation? He should offer an isti to Agni Agnimat. These are the fifteen kindling verses, the two ajyabhagas in vartraghna mode, the two viraj verses as samyajyas. And these are the yajya and puronuvakya: 'agninagnis samidhyate kavir grhapatir yuva havyavad juhvasyah' (By Agni is Agni kindled -- the poet, the householder, the young one, the oblation-bearer with offering-mouth). And the yajya: 'tvam hy agne agnina vipro viprena san sata sakha sakhya samidhyase' (For you, O Agni, by Agni, inspired one by inspired one, being true by truth, friend by friendship, are kindled). And he who desires brahmavarcas should sacrifice with this. He becomes splendid, full of brahmavarcas. Now they also ask: If the ahavaniya and garhapatya should come together, what is the procedure, what is the expiation? He should offer an isti to Agni Vita. These are the fifteen kindling verses, the two ajyabhagas in vartraghna mode, the two viraj verses as samyajyas. And these are the yajya and puronuvakya: 'agna a yahi vitaye grnano havyadataye ni hota satsi barhisi' (O Agni, come for the feast, being praised, for the giving of oblations; sit down, O invoker, on the barhis). And the yajya: 'yo agnim devavitaye havishmam avivasti tasmai pavaka mrdaya' (He who serves Agni with oblations for the feast of the gods -- to him, O purifier, be gracious).
1.65: The gayatri is the light of procreation and of the deities; the viraj is the light of the metres; the viraj is the light of speech. It is established in Agni. It attains the viraj. Therefore it is called 'light.' Two stomas carry the morning pressing, just as the eye and the breath -- so is that. Two stomas carry the midday pressing, just as the ear and the arms -- so is that. Two stomas carry the third pressing, just as the middle and the foundation -- so is that. With the himkara, with light, the gods placed light in the trivrt for brahmavarcas. With the trivrt, with brahmavarcas, they placed light in the pancadasa for vigor and strength. With the pancadasa, with vigor and strength, they placed light in the saptadasa for offspring, for cattle, for procreation. With the saptadasa, with offspring, with cattle, with procreation, they placed light in the ekavimsa for firm foundation. This is a stoma placing light in stoma after stoma. Therefore it is called jyotistoma. And because stoma after stoma carries forward each pressing, therefore it is called jyotistoma.
1.66: This sacrifice is light, proportioned to the person. Whatever desire one performs it for, that desire is fulfilled for him, for with a light vehicle one goes wherever one wishes to go. When Prajāpati created creatures, he created them with the agniṣṭoma. With the tenth stotra of the agniṣṭoma he created them, and with the tenth month of the year. With the twelfth stotra of the agniṣṭoma he enclosed them, and with the twelfth month of the year. Therefore cattle carry embryos for ten months. They are born in the eleventh. None carries beyond the twelfth, for they are enclosed by that. Of those enclosed ones, the mule escaped beyond. He took its semen by following it. He deposited it in the donkey, in the mare, in cattle, in plants. Therefore the donkey has double semen; therefore the mare has double semen. Therefore cattle give birth to two at a time. Therefore plants grow without being anointed. Therefore the mule should not be given as the barhis offering, for it escaped beyond, it departed. Its semen was taken away; it is barren. It should be given at the ṣoḍaśin stotra. For the ṣoḍaśin is the surplus of the stotras, and the mule is the surplus of cattle. He thus places the surplus upon the surplus, for the completeness of the sacrifice. He who, knowing thus, chants the udgītha with the agniṣṭoma generates creatures that are born and encloses those that are born. The agniṣṭoma is the foremost sacrifice, the sacrifice of Prajāpati.
1.67: Prajāpati alone was this universe in the beginning. Mind was Prajāpati, the deity. He desired: 'May I become many; may I procreate; may I attain abundance.' From his head, from his mouth, he created the trivṛt stoma, the gāyatrī metre, the rathantara sāman, Agni as deity, the brāhmaṇa among men, and the goat among animals. Therefore the brāhmaṇa has the gāyatrī as his metre, Agni as his deity. Therefore he is the foremost of creatures, for he created him from the mouth. He desired: 'May I procreate further.' From his arms, from his chest, he created the pañcadaśa stoma, the triṣṭubh metre, the bṛhat sāman, Indra as deity, the kṣatriya among men, and the horse among animals. Therefore the kṣatriya has the triṣṭubh as his metre, Indra as his deity. Therefore he performs deeds of valor with his arms, for he created him from the arms, from the chest, from valor.
1.68: From his belly, from the middle, he created the saptadasa stoma, the jagati metre, the vamadeya saman, the Visve Devah as deity, the vaisya among men, and the cow among animals. Therefore the vaisya has the jagati as his metre, the Visve Devah as his deity. Therefore he is prolific, for he was created from the belly, from the organ of procreation. He desired: 'May I procreate further.' From his feet, from his foundation, he created the ekavimsa stoma, the anustubh metre, the yajnayajniya saman, no particular deity, the sudra among men, and the sheep among animals. Therefore the sudra has the anustubh as his metre, the master of the house as his god. Therefore he seeks to live by washing feet, for he was created from the feet, from the foundation. With this creation Prajapati created creatures. He who knows this thus attains abundance through offspring and cattle. Therefore they praise this sacrifice most of all -- the agnistoma.
1.69: Prajapati distributed vigor to his creatures. That became the udumbara wood. Prajapati is the udgatr; the udumbara belongs to Prajapati. He chooses the other priests but not the udgatr. When the udgatr grasps the udumbari post with his first ritual act, he thereby chooses himself for the priestly office through his own deity. He grasps it saying: 'ayos tva sadane sadayami avatas chayayam samudrasya hrdaye namas samudraya namas samudrasya caksase ma ma yonorvam hasih' (I seat you in the seat of life, in the shade of the well, in the heart of the ocean. Homage to the ocean, homage to the eye of the ocean. May the yonorvam not abandon me). When he says 'ayos tva sadane sadayami,' the sacrifice is ayus (life); its seat is thus made. 'avatas chayayam' -- the sacrifice is the well; the sacrificial hall is its shade. 'samudrasya hrdaye' -- the heart is in the middle of the self; therefore the udumbari is placed in the middle of the hall. 'namas samudraya namas samudrasya caksase' -- speech is the ocean, mind is the eye of the ocean. To these two deities he makes obeisance. 'ma ma yonorvam hasih' -- about to perform the priestly office: the saman is the yonorvam; to the saman he makes this obeisance. About to perform the priestly office, the saman does not carry him away. He does not come to harm through the saman. He is not diminished in the saman -- he who, knowing thus, makes obeisance to the saman.
1.70: The sacrificial hall is the belly of Prajapati. The udumbara is vigor. When the udumbari is placed in the middle of the hall, one thereby places food and vigor in the middle of the creatures. Therefore food eaten in the middle nourishes. Therefore where this becomes used up, there the creatures become hungry. But they do become well-nourished. They deliberate whether it should be touched or not touched. Everything here is indeed endowed with a self. He who desires food as if greedily -- him it hates most. If one were to touch it, one would lose one's food. If one were not to touch it, one would exclude oneself from food. Having touched it but chanting as if not having touched -- he neither loses his food nor excludes himself from food. The saman is the food of the gods; the udumbara is vigor. When the udgatr leans on the udumbari, he thereby places food and vigor in the saman of the gods.
1.71: Prajāpati is the udgātṛ; the udumbara is vigor. This Prajāpati, established upon vigor, distributes vigor and food to his creatures. Sitting facing north, he chants. Thereby he endows the northern direction with vigor. Sitting facing west, he performs the prastāva. Thereby he endows the western direction with vigor. Sitting facing south, he performs the pratihāra. Thereby he endows the southern direction with vigor. The other priests face east while performing the priestly office. Thereby they endow the eastern direction with vigor. Therefore this is the most vigorous, the most sustaining of the directions, favored by the most. Therefore when creatures are led to destruction, they go eastward, for this is the most vigorous, the most sustaining of the directions, favored by the most. 'If the udgātṛs have deities of the north, then why do they perform the priestly office turning to the opposite directions?' they ask. One should say: 'For the conquest of the directions, for the attainment of the directions. Therefore food is found in all directions.' He offers upon the udumbarī with: 'ud divaṃ stabhānāntarikṣaṃ pṛṇa pṛthivīm upareṇa dṛṃha dyutānas tvā māruto minotu mitrāvaruṇayor dhruveṇa dharmaṇā ghṛtena dyāvāpṛthivī ā prīṇīthām supippalā oṣadhīḥ kṛdhi svāhā' (Prop up heaven, fill the atmosphere, make firm the earth from above. May the shining Māruta establish you by the firm ordinance of Mitra and Varuṇa. May heaven and earth be pleased with ghee. Make the plants rich in fruit. Svāhā!).
1.72: Prajapati created creatures. He wished to emit Agni from his mouth. That Agni, shrinking from the mouth, burst upward, shattered the skull, and was emitted. The gods and rsis, having come together, said: 'This skull of ours is shattered; let it not remain so. Let us make it into something, placing it upon something.' They said to Brhaspati: 'Receive Soma in it.' Brhaspati said: 'But this is as if fierce, unpurified, impure, uncooked.' 'We shall make it purified, pure, cooked for you,' they said. 'Then bring it to me,' he said. 'So be it.' They gave it to him. He received it with: 'devasya tva savituh prasave asvinor bahubhyam pusno hastabhyam pratigrhnimi' (I receive you with the impulse of god Savitr, with the arms of the Asvins, with the hands of Pusan). With divine limbs he thus received it. Having received it, he purified it: 'vasavas tva punantu gayatrena chandasa suprajavaniim rayas posavanim rudras tva punantu traistubhena chandasa suprajavaniim rayas posavanim adityas tva punantu jagatena chandasa suprajavaniim rayas posavanim' (May the Vasus purify you with the gayatri metre, for good progeny, for increase of wealth. May the Rudras purify you with the traistubha metre, for good progeny, for increase of wealth. May the Adityas purify you with the jagata metre, for good progeny, for increase of wealth). With metres and deities he thus purified it. Having purified it, he placed it behind the axle, saying: 'barhaspatyam asi vanaspatyam prajapater murdhatyayupatram' (You are of Brhaspati, made of wood, the head of Prajapati, the vessel surpassing in life). When he says 'barhaspatyam asi,' it is because Brhaspati first received it. When he says 'vanaspatyam,' it is because they make it from a tree. When he says 'prajapater murdha,' it is because it was the head of Prajapati. When he says 'atyayupatram,' it is because this vessel surpasses other vessels. Because this divine vessel is yoked first and unyoked last, it thereby surpasses all other vessels. He who knows thus attains a superior vessel.
1.73: 'namah pitrbhyah purvasadbhyo namas sakamnisadbhyah yujye vacam satapadiim gaye sahasravartaniim gayatram traistubham jagad visva rupani sambhrtam deva okansi cakrire' (Homage to the fathers who sat before; homage to those who sit together. I yoke the hundred-footed speech; I sing the thousand-pathed. The gayatra, the traistubha, the jagata -- all forms assembled -- the gods have made their dwellings). When he says 'namah pitrbhyah purvasadbhyah,' the fathers sit there before; to them he makes obeisance. 'namas sakamnisadbhyah' -- to the very brahmanas with whom he sits together, about to perform the priestly office, to them he makes this obeisance. 'yujye vacam satapadiim' -- he thereby yokes speech with its hundred feet. 'gaye sahasravartaniim' -- he sings it thus yoked, become thousand-pathed. 'gayatram traistubham jagat' -- these are the three pressings; he thereby takes hold of them upon himself.
1.74: Kusurvinda Hauddālaki chanted the udgītha at a soma sacrifice. The sons of Asitamṛga of the Kaśyapa clan said to him: 'Who is this cruel one who chants among us? Let us challenge him.' They approached the āstāva to make the challenge. He said: 'Let there be homage to the brāhmaṇa. Even before today, before the morning recitation, I established the sacrifice in all forms through the gāyatra. Just as one who has gone among cows to find a wild ox might drive along a weary, feeble one, even so we are driving along this body of the sacrifice.' They rose up and departed, saying, 'Let there be homage to that brāhmaṇa! He indeed has accomplished this.'
1.75: They ask: 'Adhvaryu, which stotra was praised that the hotṛ recited with the morning litany?' He says: 'We have done what was our work,' and says, 'Ask the hotṛ.' 'Hotṛ, which stotra was praised that you recited with the morning litany?' He says: 'We have done what was our work,' and says, 'Ask the udgātṛ.' 'Udgātṛ, which stotra was praised that the hotṛ recited with the morning litany?' He should say: 'We have done what was our work,' and 'We have sung what was to be sung here.' If they should say to him, 'You have sung the darknesses, not the lights,' he should reply: 'I have sung them; I should strike you with darkness and evil.'
1.76: They sit down facing east from behind. Thereby they secure speech from the mouth. If they were to sit down facing east from in front, they would turn speech to the back. Speech would be apt to depart from them. Speech withdrew from the gods. She entered the trees. She is in the axle. When they push the axle down into the droṇakalaśa, they thereby secure the divine speech. The chanting priests push the axle down into the droṇakalaśa for the udgītha. He who knows thus excludes from this world the hating rival. One should not touch the axle. If one were to touch the axle, one would direct prosperity to the rival. One pushes it between the two wheels. Therefore what is between the two wheels of a cart is the most sustaining.
1.77: The Vasus first pushed this forward. They became weary. They called upon the Rudras. The Rudras pushed it forward. They became weary. They called upon the Ādityas. The Ādityas pushed it forward. One pushes it saying: 'vasavas tvā prohantu gāyatreṇa chandasā rudrās tvā prohantu traiṣṭubhena chandasā ādityās tvā prohantu jāgatena chandasā' (May the Vasus push you forward with the gāyatrī metre. May the Rudras push you forward with the traiṣṭubha metre. May the Ādityas push you forward with the jāgata metre). Thereby one pushes it forward in the manner of the gods' pushing. Or one may push it saying: 'devasya tvā savituḥ prasave 'śvinor bāhubhyāṃ pūṣṇo hastābhyāṃ prohāmi' (I push you forward with the impulse of god Savitṛ, with the arms of the Aśvins, with the hands of Pūṣan). Thereby one pushes it forward with divine limbs. Bṛhaspati first pushed this forward. He became diminished in form and splendor. He reflected: 'What is the expiation for this?' He found it in the droṇakalaśa itself. He touched it, saying: 'tanūpā asi tanvaṃ me pāhi varcodhā asi varco me dhehi āyurdhā asy ayur me dhehi vayodhā asi vayo me dhehi' (You are the protector of the body; protect my body. You are the bestower of splendor; bestow splendor on me. You are the bestower of life; bestow life on me. You are the bestower of vigor; bestow vigor on me). Whatever of his self was diminished there, that is the means of restoration. The udgātṛ likewise touches it: 'tanūpā asi tanvaṃ me pāhi...' Whatever of his self is diminished there, that is restored thereby. This world is twofold: the brahman and the kṣatra. Both are in the droṇakalaśa.
1.78: Having turned the pressing stones face to face, he touches them saying: 'syena ajira rtasya garbhah prayuto napatah parvatanaam kakubha a nas tam viram vahata yam bahava upajivanmo abhisastikrtam anabhisastenyam anyasyabhisastyah kartaram' (Swift eagles, embryos of rta, progeny of the mountain peaks--bring to us that hero upon whom many depend, the maker of accusations against others, himself beyond accusation, the maker of accusations against others). Indeed a hero is born to him--a maker of accusations, himself beyond accusation, a maker of accusations against others. He raises the dronakalasa, saying: 'idam aham mam brahmavarcase 'dhyuhami' (I raise myself up in brahmavarcas). If he has a ksatriya who opposes him, having turned the pressing stones away, he should silently raise the dronakalasa, remove that one to the south, and say: 'idam aham amum viso niruhami idam asya rastram nyubjami' (I remove that one from the people; I overturn his kingdom). He should overturn the dronakalasa downward. Having turned the pressing stones face to face, taking the name of his ksatriya, he should raise the dronakalasa saying: 'idam aham amum visy adhyuhami' (I raise that one upon the people). The other is restrained; the other prevails. If, having noticed him, the other should disregard him, and he should wish, 'Let him remain confined right here,' having turned the stones away, he should silently raise the dronakalasa, and these pressing stones are like heaps of sand. He should place it on top saying: 'idam aham amusmin visy adhyuhami' (I raise upon that people). He prevails over the one whose strength is consumed, for a long time. And if that one, having found out, should come running, he would say: 'In this brahmana indeed dwells whatever is in this word of his.' Having turned the stones face to face, he should raise the dronakalasa saying: 'idam aham amum visy adhyuhami' (I raise that one upon the people).
1.79: The dronakalasa is the kingdom; the pressing stones are the people. If the dronakalasa were loose, the kingdom would be loose. Following that, the people after the people, the sacrificer after the sacrificer, the offspring after that. He makes it firm: 'devi tva dhisane nipatam dhruve sadasi sida isa urje sida' (May the two divine dhisanas support you. Sit in the firm seat. Sit for nourishment and vigor). These two are the divine dhisanas; this is the firm seat. With these worlds he makes it firm. 'isa urje sida' -- rain is for nourishment; what thrives above the rain is for vigor. Thereby he secures that. Svarbhanu the Asura struck the sun with darkness. The gods and rsis tried to heal him. They said to Atri: 'O rsi, you remove this.' 'So be it.' Atri removed it. They said: 'For him who found light for us who were struck by darkness, let light be his portion.' That is the Atri gold that is brought. Formerly a gold piece of a hundred weight was brought. But now they give whatever amount they give. Gold is indeed the light in all the worlds.
1.80: When he first removed it, that was the black appearance. When he removed it a second time, that was the smoky appearance. When he removed it a third time, that was the pale-reddish appearance. He whom one wishes to become worse -- one should cast black into his filter; he becomes worse. He whom one wishes to be neither better nor worse -- one should cast smoky into his filter; he becomes neither better nor worse. He whom one wishes to become better and attain splendor -- one should make his filter pale-reddish; he becomes better and attains splendor. For there too the touch of the sun is present. Having carried it between, he wipes it: 'vasavas tva sammrjantu gayatrena chandasa rudras tva sammrjantu traistubhena chandasa adityas tva sammrjantu jagatena chandasa' (May the Vasus wipe you with the gayatri metre. May the Rudras wipe you with the traistubha metre. May the Adityas wipe you with the jagata metre). Then he spreads the filter with its fringes pointing north: 'pavitram te vitatam brahmanaspate' (Your filter is spread, O lord of prayer) -- for completeness. 'tapos pavitram vitatam divas pade' (The filter of heat is spread in the seat of heaven) -- for one desiring heaven. 'arurucad usasah prsnir agrayuh' (The speckled one of dawn has shone forth, going first) -- for one desiring offspring. One should spread it with all of them, for the attainment of all these desires. He brings the king Soma. He addresses it: 'sa pavasva sudhama devanam abhi praiyani dhama trir devebhyo pavathas trir adityebhyas trir angirobhyah yena turyena brahmana brhaspataye pavathas tena mahyam pavasva sa nah pavasva sam gave sam janaya sam arvate sam rajann osadhibhyah' (Flow, O you of good abode, toward the excellent abode of the gods. Thrice purify for the gods, thrice for the Adityas, thrice for the Angirases. With that fourth prayer with which you purify for Brhaspati, with that purify for me. Purify for us for the good of the cow, for the good of the people, for the good of the steed, for the good, O king, of the plants). It is good for the udgatr, good for the sacrificer, good for the offspring. The adhvaryu, taking the agrayaṇa cup, performs the himkara. That becomes the himkara. Then the udgatr sings the gayatra in a single verse: 'ucca te jatam andhasa' (Born on high with the pressed juice). With 'ud' (upward) he carries the offering to the gods from here; with 'divi sad bhumy a dade' (seated in heaven, he took hold on earth) he brings rain down from above. He thus makes these two worlds co-dwellers. They yield desire for him.
1.81: It is as if something cruel is done to the sacrifice when, in the havirdhana, having slain King Soma with the pressing stones, they proceed to the bahispavamana. What is the expiation for that? they say. They should touch water. Water is the pacification of all. With water they thereby pacify it. Speech indeed first became wearied for that journey, proceeding to the bahispavamana. Prajapati said to her: 'I shall make a share for you; then proceed.' One about to proceed to the bahispavamana should offer the oblation: 'justo vaco bhuyasam justo vacaspatyuh devi vag yat te vaco madhumat tasmin ma dhah svaha sarasvatyai' (May I be pleasing to Speech, pleasing to the lord of Speech. O divine Speech, whatever of your speech is sweet, in that place me. Svaha to Sarasvati!). If one should offer with 'sarasvatyai svaha,' one would encompass Speech, Sarasvati, with the svahakara. But since one offers with 'svaha sarasvatyai,' one places Speech beyond the svahakara. With her thus encompassed, one extends the sacrifice. For the sacrifice is stretched upward by speech. Or one may offer: 'vekura namasi presita divyaya karmane siva nas suyama bhava satyasir yajamanaya svaha' (You are named Vekura, sent forth for the divine work. Be auspicious to us, easy to guide. With true blessings for the sacrificer, svaha!). Speech is indeed Vekura. Speech is brahman.
1.82: Those who proceed to the bahispavamana go as if into the wilderness. They are liable to be struck by raksas or some other destructive force. Then he offers a second oblation: 'suryo ma devo divyebhyo raksobhyah patu vata antarikshebhyo gnih parthivebhyah svaha' (May the god Surya protect me from the celestial raksas, Vayu from those of the atmosphere, Agni from those of the earth, svaha!). Having entrusted himself to the very lords of these worlds, he proceeds without coming to harm. When Prajapati created the sacrifice, he created it in the havirdhana itself. That created sacrifice ran northward. Having overtaken it there, they praised it. When they carry it northward and praise it with the bahispavamana, they thereby overtake the sacrifice and praise it. He who knows the divine steed becomes a steed himself. The sacrifice is indeed the divine steed. He who mounts an untamed, ungroomed human steed -- that steed indeed injures or destroys him.
1.83: The adhvaryu proceeds carrying the prastara. He addresses it: 'etad aham daivyam vajinam sammaarjmi' (I now groom this divine steed). He thus mounts it groomed and tamed, and does not come to harm. He who chants the udgitha of the Soma indeed settles on the field of the gods. He who settles on the field of a human lord without announcement -- that lord indeed injures or drives him away. But what of one who settles on the field of the divine lord without announcement? He says: 'somodgayodgaya soma' (Soma, chant! Chant, O Soma!). Soma is the lord of the divine field. Having thus announced to Soma, the lord of the divine field, he chants and does not come to harm. 'For my tejas, for my brahmavarcas, for my food, for my abundance, for my prosperity, for my procreation, for the abundance of my offspring, for the prosperity of my offspring, for the procreation of my offspring, for the sovereignty of King Soma, for my leadership of the people.' Grava Maitreya used to say: 'Why should I sin against just one? Why against just one?' For he used to say thus: 'somodgayodgaya soma, this for so-and-so, this for so-and-so.' Those desires of his were indeed fulfilled. The fire-hearths are as divine snares spread out when the dhisnya fires are distributed. He who, knowing thus, passes among them unlooses the divine snares. This is indeed the door of the sacrifice: what is between the agnidhra hearth and the catvala. One should enter by that. One should exit by that. Dha (establisher) is indeed the name of the vedi. Entering it, one should say: 'dha asi sudham me dhehi ayusmantastvad varcasvanta udgesma' (You are the Establisher. Establish good fortune for me. May we rise from you endowed with life and splendor).
1.84: They proceed to the bahispavamana extending the vital breaths toward the heavenly world, carrying the cups from the havirdhana. They proceed continuously. The heavenly world is indeed continuous. For the continuity of the heavenly world, for its non-interruption. They proceed as if stooping forward. The heavenly world is as if against the slope from here. Just as one going uphill against the slope stoops forward -- even so is that. For the attainment of the heavenly world, for its non-missing. The adhvaryu proceeds first; he is the prana of the sacrifice. Therefore if they were to be separated from him, they would be separated from the prana and would be subject to premature death. The prastotr proceeds second; he is the mouth of the saman. Therefore if they were to be separated from him, they would come to harm in what is foremost. The udgatr proceeds third; he is all-devatied, Prajapati. Therefore if they were to be separated from him, they would be deprived of everything. The pratihartr proceeds fourth; he is the fourth part of the saman. Therefore if they were to be separated from him, they would be tormented by the fourth part of themselves. The sacrificer proceeds fifth. The sacrifice is fivefold; cattle are fivefold.
1.85: The brahman proceeds sixth. Six are the metres. With the metres they thereby strike away the raksas, the evil. All this is indeed brahman. Since the brahman is behind, with brahman itself they strike away the raksas, the evil, and proceed to the heavenly world. They proceed carrying the prastara. The sacrificer is the prastara; the barhis is the offspring. Since they carry the prastara above the barhis, they thereby raise the sacrificer above the offspring. Therefore the sacrificer is always above other offspring. If one were to sit down (during the procession), the sacrificer would become one whose heavenly world exists, but he would be cut off from this world. If one were to overshoot, he would be established in this world but cut off from the heavenly world. They say: 'The wife is indeed half the self of the sacrificer. Since she remains outside the vedi, he is not cut off from this world.' He should indeed sit down -- and yet the wife may not be present. Also, even if she is present, he should sit down for one and overshoot for another.
1.86: The sun was indeed here at first, where the catvala is on this side and the fire on that. He heated everything here. The gods feared his burning. They said: 'He will burn up all this. Let us remove him.' They took him up from here with three verses, from the atmosphere with three, and made him go to heaven with three. From there he then shone outward. They saw this last syllable 'a.' With that they turned him hitherward. From then on he shines hitherward. With this very syllable 'a' of the well they placed fire in this world. He who wishes 'May this sacrificer attain the heavenly world' should, having announced it at the catvala, chant the udgitha. They take him up from here with three, from the atmosphere with three, and make him go to heaven with three. One should indeed praise with all of them continuously, but should speak the last one first, then the next lower, then the next lower. They are all directed hitherward. But they say: 'This is entirely a return; for they indeed sacrifice desiring heaven.'
1.87: They sing the last verse in the rathantara melody. This world is indeed the rathantara. In this world one thereby finds firm foundation. Prajapati created creatures. Those creatures, when created, hungry, sat around him on all sides. For them he created food with the himkara. That food, once created, he stopped with the omkara. The udgatr is Prajapati. The creatures, hungry, sit around him on all sides. For them he creates food with the himkara. That food, once created, he stops with the omkara. If, having made the himkara, he were to make the omkara, food would go away from the gods. If he were to utter the saman first, he would reach the power of the ksatra. For the saman is the ksatra. If he were to utter the rc first, he would reach the power of the vis (people). For the rc is the vis. Since he utters the yajus first -- the yajus is brahman -- he thereby makes brahmavarcas. 'Bhur bhuvas svah. I shall make sweetness. I shall generate sweetness. There will be sweetness. Auspicious, auspicious. Nourishment and vigor.' When he says 'bhur bhuvas svah,' that is this brahman, this yajus. When he says 'I shall make sweetness,' creatures are indeed sweetness; he thereby makes them. When he says 'I shall generate sweetness,' he thereby generates them. When he says 'There will be sweetness,' he thereby leads them to prosperity. 'Auspicious, auspicious' -- whatever is a man's possession, that is auspicious: the house is auspicious, the offspring are auspicious, the cattle are auspicious. That he thereby secures.
1.88: One should chant the udgitha having arranged Prajapati. He who chants having arranged Prajapati -- for him the directions, the intermediate directions, the upper directions, the side directions, and the upward directions are arranged. The directions are indeed Prajapati. Having arranged Prajapati, one thus chants. It is arranged for him who knows thus. Kapivana Bhauvaayana used to say: 'What good is it that they go to the sacrifice if they proceed or run forward without even having tasted the Soma?' One about to proceed to the bahispavamana should say: 'Whichever brahmana one considers to be pure, bring him every day.' Having summoned him thus, one should bless him: 'yo devanam iha somapitho smin yajne barhisi vedyam tasyedam bhaksayamasi' (That drink of Soma which belongs to the gods here, at this sacrifice, on the barhis, on the vedi -- of that we partake). Whether he runs forward or not, the divine Soma has been tasted by him. Wild animals have this (the bahispavamana) as their abode. Because they praise the bahispavamana in the wilderness, wild animals therefore frequent the wilderness. They praise in verses directed away. Therefore wild animals move from wilderness to wilderness. They praise in verses of uniform color. Therefore wild animals are of uniform color. The last syllable is 'a.' Therefore each single other kind of animal is found in the village. And what is killed in the wilderness they bring to the village. Having praised, they run back. They thereby send the sacrificer to the heavenly world. They raise their arms. They thereby establish the sacrificer in the heavenly world. But they indeed do something untrue, those who send a mortal to immortality. They become diminished in form and splendor. Now, if another who knows thus should rise up, one should rise up grasping him as he rises. One does not become diminished in form and splendor, nor does one come to harm. One should discern what is about to happen. If the creatures should appear as if agitated, the livelihood will be agitated. One should know: 'Thus has this udgatr chanted.' If they should appear as if calm, the livelihood will be calm. One should know: 'Thus has this udgatr chanted.'
1.89: 'upa asmai gayata narah' (Sing to him, O men!) -- one desiring fame, prosperity, and procreation should use this as the pratipad. It is to this earth that one sings. Everything is indeed upon this earth. One procreates offspring and cattle upon this earth -- he who knows thus. Nara indeed means the divine people; the ksatra is Soma Indu. By this one secures both the vis and the ksatra. 'abhi devam iyaksate' (They approach the god reverently) -- by this one worships all the deities without obstacle. 'upo su jatam apturam' (Come to the one born, the fulfiller) -- one desiring offspring should use this as the pratipad. One procreates offspring and cattle upon oneself. One should use this same pratipad the following day. For through 'apturam' (the fulfiller) one becomes better and wealthier. 'gobhir bhangam pariskrtam' (Adorned with cows, the portion) -- the sacrifice is indeed the 'bhanga' with cows, the cattle are what adorns it. By this one secures both the sacrifice and cattle. 'indum deva ayasisuh' (The gods desired the drop) -- the sacrificer is Soma the King, Indu; the stomas are the gods.
1.90: 'pavasva vaco agriyah' (Flow forth, O foremost of speech!) -- one desiring preeminence should use this as the pratipad. Prajapati created creatures. Those creatures, when created, did not honor him. He desired: 'May I attain preeminence among these creatures.' He saw this pratipad. He praised with it. Thereby he attained preeminence among those creatures. He attains preeminence among his own -- he who knows thus. This is indeed the complete stotriya, whose beginning is 'pavasva.' His praise is done with a complete stotriya. He who knows the divine couple procreates through the couple. 'pavasva' and 'vacah' are the divine couple. He procreates through the couple -- he who knows thus. This is the pratipad of all three samans: 'pavasva' belongs to the vamadeya, 'vacah' to the rathantara, 'agriyah' to the brhat. His praise is accomplished with the pratipad of all the samans. 'soma citrabhir utibhih' (Soma, with brilliant aids) -- Soma is indeed the marvelous among the gods; by Soma, the marvelous of the gods, one becomes abundant, one procreates.
1.91: 'pavasvendo vrsa sutah' (Flow, O drop, pressed out as bull!) -- one desiring firm establishment among people should use this as the pratipad. 'krdhi no yasaso jane' (Make us famous among the people) -- for through this one becomes famous among the people. 'vrsa sutah' (the bull pressed out) -- like a bull, like a progenitor, like a chief, he becomes in that community in which he prospers. Like a bull, like a progenitor, like a chief, he shines in that community in which he lives -- he who knows thus. Everyone indeed desires to thrive among the people and gives gifts. 'visva apa dviso jahi' (Strike away all hatreds!) -- by this one strikes away all hatreds. 'taya pavasva dharaya' (With that stream flow forth!) -- one should use this as the pratipad for one about to engage in battle. 'yaya gava ihagaman janyasa upa no grhan' (By which the cows came here, of foreign people, to our houses) -- he who goes to battle indeed wishes to seize the foreign cows. He seizes the foreign cows. One should use this same pratipad when about to go on a raid. He who goes on a raid indeed wishes to seize the foreign cows. He seizes the foreign cows; the foreign cows bow down to him -- he who knows thus. 'agna ayumsi pavase' (O Agni, you purify lifespans) -- one should use this as the pratipad for one who is ill. For Agni is what his body comes to, Vayu is what his breath comes to. By Agni, the pavamana, one joins together his body and his breath. One should use this same pratipad for those among the consecrated who have died. For those among the consecrated who have died consider themselves as if unpurified, impure.
1.92: One desiring brahmavarcas should use this. Agni is indeed the brahman among the gods. By Agni, the brahman of the gods, one becomes resplendent. One should use this same pratipad when practicing sorcery. Agni is indeed the brahman among the gods. By Agni, the brahman of the gods, one subdues whomever one hates. One should use this same pratipad when desiring food. 'a suvorjam isam ca nah' (Bring us vigor and nourishment!) -- for through this one secures both nourishment and vigor. One should use this same pratipad when being attacked by sorcery. He who practices sorcery, he who attacks, he who desires evil -- he is indeed inauspicious. 'are badhasva ducchunam' (Drive away the inauspicious one far off!) -- by this one drives away far off the hating rival, the evil one, downward. 'davidyutatya ruca' (With flashing splendor) -- one desiring brahmavarcas should use this as the pratipad. The gayatri is indeed flashing. The gayatri is brahmavarcas; vigor and strength are the tristubh; procreation is the jagati. One becomes endowed with brahmavarcas, vigor, strength, offspring, and cattle -- he who knows thus. 'paristobhantya krpa somaas sukra gavasirah' (The bright somas, mixed with milk, resound on all sides) -- the sacrifice is indeed the bright somas; cattle are what is mixed with milk. By this one secures both the sacrifice and cattle. 'asya pratnam anu dyutam' (Following his ancient splendor) -- one should use this as the pratipad he whose father or grandfather was more eminent, but who considers himself inferior. He attains that very paternal and ancestral glory. 'sukram duduhre ahrayah' (The unblushing ones milked forth the bright one) -- the sacrifice is indeed the bright one; cattle are the unblushing ones. By this one secures both the sacrifice and cattle. 'payas sahasrasam rsim' (The milk, the thousandfold rsi).
1.93: 'ete asrgram indavah' (These drops have flowed forth) -- one should use this as the pratipad when many are sacrificing together. With 'ete' (these) he addresses them for preeminence; he makes them sharers in it through the metre endowed with form. The blessing of the sacrifice comes to them together. When Prajapati created creatures, he created them with this very pratipad. With 'ete' (these) he created the gods; with 'asrgram' (they flowed forth) the humans; with 'indavah' (the drops) the fathers; with 'tirah pavitram' (through the filter) the cups; with 'asavah' (the swift ones) the stomas; with 'visvani' (all) the ukthas; with 'abhi saubhaga' (toward good fortune) he anointed them, once born, with good fortune. Prajapati himself, having become, creates creatures -- he who, knowing thus, chants the udgitha with this pratipad. And thereby he anoints them with good fortune. 'asrksata pra vajinah' (The swift ones flowed forth) -- one should use this as the pratipad when three are sacrificing together. He makes them sharers through the metre endowed with form. The blessing of the sacrifice comes to them together. 'gavya somaso asvaya' (The somas desiring cows, desiring horses) -- by this one secures for them cows and horses. 'sukraso virayasavah' (The bright ones, swiftly seeking heroes) -- the sacrifice is indeed the bright one; the offspring is the hero. By this one secures for them both the sacrifice and offspring. 'yuvam hi sthah svahpati' (For you two are lords of well-being) -- one should use this as the pratipad when two are sacrificing together. He makes them sharers through the metre endowed with form.
1.94: 'indras ca soma gopati isana pipyatam dhiyah' (Indra and Soma, lords of cattle, inspiring, nourish the hymns!) -- Indra and Soma desired: 'May we attain the sovereignty and lordship over all creatures.' They saw this pratipad. They praised with it. Thereby they attained the sovereignty and lordship over all creatures. He attains sovereignty and lordship over his own -- he who praises with this pratipad, knowing thus. 'indrayendo marutvate' (For Indra accompanied by the Maruts, O drop!) -- one should use this as the pratipad for a rajanyabandhu (one connected to the ksatriya class). The rajanya belongs to Indra; the vis (people) belong to the Maruts. One thereby makes the vis follow the ksatra. Therefore the vis follows the ksatra. 'yo jinati na jiyate' (He who conquers and is not conquered) -- he conquers and is not conquered. 'hanti satrum abhitya' (He slays the enemy, having approached) -- having approached from wherever, he slays the enemy; the enemy does not become one who advances against him. 'sa pavasva sahasrajit' (Flow forth, O thousand-conquering one!) -- he who conquers everything is the one who conquers all. He conquers all, he conquers everything.
1.95: 'apaghnan pavate mrdhah' (He flows forth, striking away hostilities) -- one should use this as the pratipad for one who is being accused. For hostile, unwelcome forces attend him whom they accuse. 'He strikes away the evil hostilities. Soma strikes away the niggardly' -- for niggardly ones, as it were, attend him whom they accuse. 'gacchann indrasya niskrtam' (Going to Indra's redemption) -- for he who is being accused becomes devoid of Indra, devoid of deity. By this one makes him endowed with Indra, accompanied by deity. One should use the agni-varuṇa pratipad for one who is ill, who is long ill. For one who is ill, who is long ill, is seized by Agni and Varuna. By the agneya verses one releases him from Agni, by the varuṇa verses from Varuna. When the longer rc has the shorter verse, or the shorter has the longer -- one desiring to bring something should use that as the pratipad. For the shorter seeks to obtain from the longer, the longer from the shorter. The shorter attains from the longer -- he who knows thus. 'esa devo amartyah' (This god is immortal) -- one should use this as the pratipad he who desires: 'May I alone be the foremost among my own; may I attain splendor.' With 'esa esah' (this, this) he addresses it for supremacy and preeminence. He becomes the foremost among his own and attains splendor. But it is likely that no hero equal to him will be born among his descendants. 'This one and no other' -- so speech seems to address him. But it is not really so. For he says 'devo amartyah' (god, immortal). He is indeed godless and mortal whose hero, being a hero, does not beget a heroic son. But he is truly a god and immortal whose hero, being a hero, begets a heroic son.
1.96: The gods and asuras were in conflict. The gods fashioned the thunderbolt, the razor-edged one -- the person himself. They hurled him against the asuras. He, having driven back the asuras, turned back against the gods. Therefore the gods feared him. Having seized him, they broke him into three parts. Broken into three parts, he rose up again. They seized him and examined him. They saw that the deities and metres had entered into this person. They said: 'The deities and metres have entered into this person. By the meritorious life he lives in this world -- by ista and purta, by tapas and good deeds -- he will follow after us.' They said: 'Let us act in such a way that he does not follow after us.'
1.97: Sleep, weariness, anger, hunger, the desire for gambling, the desire for women -- these are the evils that attend a person in this world. 'O Agni, whatever of good one who has crossed beyond these wishes to accomplish in this world, do you frustrate that for him in this world' -- thus they placed Agni in this world; Vayu in the atmosphere; Aditya in heaven. Ugradeva the Rajanir used to say: 'I do not bear ill will toward any man, for I hear of three foremost of the gods who bear ill will toward him. Indeed the greatest ill will is borne toward him against whom these deities bear ill will.' But these deities do not bear ill will toward him who knows thus. And they indeed frustrate the one who would frustrate one who knows thus.
1.98: Of those, they wrested away the prana with the gayatri, sight with the tristubh, hearing with the jagati, speech with the anustubh. From this world they cut them off with the gayatri, from the atmosphere with the tristubh, from yonder world with the jagati, from cattle with the anustubh. They cut them off from everything. Thereby the gods prevailed and the asuras were defeated. He prevails by himself; his hating rival is defeated -- he who knows thus. When the gods overwhelmed the asuras, that was the 'dhura' nature of the dhuras (yoke-pieces). He overwhelms the hating rival -- he who knows thus. When Prajapati created creatures, he created them with the dhuras themselves. He poured seed with the retasya (seed-chant); he set the prana in motion with the gayatri, sight with the tristubh, hearing with the jagati, speech with the anustubh. The self he established with the pankti.
1.99: He sings the retasya. Thereby he deposits the seed. He sings continuously. For the seed is as if continuous. He sings the first part in a veiled manner. For the seed is as if veiled. He should not touch the rc. If he were to touch the rc, he would cut off the seed. He sings with three 'udasa' sections. With the first udasa he encompasses the prana, with the second the apana, with the third the vyana. He sings with three udasa sections. With the first udasa he encompasses the self, with the second the wife, with the third the offspring. He sings with three udasa sections. With the first udasa he ascends this world, with the second the atmosphere, with the third yonder world. He who, knowing thus, sings with three udasa sections conquers the world of the rival. He who wishes to cut off the rival's world should sing with only two udasa sections. His hating rival perishes quickly; he himself attains glory. But one is liable to be subject to premature death. For one departs from the vital breaths by omitting the third udasa. One should therefore act in such a way that one lives the full span of life. One should cut off the rival's world by other means. One should sing with three udasa sections. One should not make the himkara.
1.100: They say: 'By the himkara Prajapati created food for the creatures. If one were not to make the himkara, the creatures would be hungry.' One should indeed make the himkara ... with 'bhuh.' 'If one does not release the himkara, one thereby cuts off the seed; it does not pass beyond the himkara,' they say. But they also say: 'From here the seed is as if crushed, as if made more harsh. The himkara could cut it off even with a hair.' At the very moment of the himkara, one should meditate on the himkara with the mind. That does not cut off the seed, nor does it fail to pass beyond the himkara. He makes the final syllable 'bhuh.' When Prajapati first spoke, he spoke 'bhuh.' He thereby created this earth.
1.151: One should perform Jamadagni's seven-day rite either as an abhicāra (sorcery rite) or when one has a rival. Jamadagni was the purohita of the Mahenās. He surpassed them in wealth. They in turn sought to harm him. He reflected: 'How might I slay seven times seven of their domestic animals in a single day?' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereupon he slew seven times seven of their domestic animals in a single day. And so indeed they slay theirs in the same way. This is a rival-slaying rite. He who knows thus slays the hostile rival. And because Jamadagni saw it, it is called 'Jamadagni's seven-day rite.' On this the following verse is sung: 'They grew beyond measure, they nearly touched the sky; having harmed Bhṛgu, the Mahenās came to irrecoverable ruin.'
1.152: The devas and asuras competed in the sacrifice. The devas drove out the asuras from the bahiṣpavamāna. The asuras held fast to the āgneya ājya. The devas to the maitrāvaruṇa. The asuras to the aindra. The devas to the aindrāgna. The asuras to the midday pavamāna. The devas to the rathantara. The asuras to the vāmadevya. The devas to the naudhasa. The asuras to the kāleya. By the kāleya itself they pursued them. By the kāleya itself they destroyed them from the kāleya. Because they destroyed them (akālayanta), that is the kāleya-ness of the kāleya. He who knows thus destroys the hostile rival. He destroys the hostile rival in succession who knows thus. 'Taro vai yajñas stomo vidadvasuḥ' — by the sacrifice and by the stoma they destroyed them.
1.153: The asuras advanced with the ṛc, the devas with the sāman. The devas, seizing the asuras by the ṛc itself, crushed them with the sāman — thus one crushes for the slaying of the hostile rival. 'Indraṃ sabādha ūtāya' — singing bṛhat, the adhvaryus at the soma pressing. 'Dhvārā' is the refrain of the rathantara-sāman; 'dhoro' is that of the bṛhat-sāman. Both of these follow that pattern. Therefore one should sing both forms — both that which is rāthantara and that which is bārhata. The brahmasāmans of these two differ. But both follow this pattern. Therefore one should sing both forms. It becomes an ailā (chant). Cattle are ilā. By cattle they destroyed them. Therefore, when a rival who has cattle and one who is without cattle compete, the one who has cattle prevails over the other. This sāman pervaded these creatures. They approach this extended ilā at the end, for the non-dislodging of creatures from their proper place. Therefore creatures are not dislodged from their proper place. Devas, pitṛs, and humans were on one side; asuras, rakṣases, and piśācas on the other. They competed in these worlds. Then the Kali Gandharvas went about in between, not regarding either side. The devas, pitṛs, and humans overcame the asuras, rakṣases, and piśācas. They divided these worlds: the devas took the world of the devas, the pitṛs the world of the pitṛs, and humans the world of humans. The Kali Gandharvas approached them and said: 'Give us a share in these worlds.' 'No,' they said. 'You went about without regard, not heeding either side.'
1.154: 'No,' they said. 'Our worlds are well divided; we cannot confuse them.' They said: 'Let whatever we ourselves see here be ours.' To them they gave these kalindā women: 'Toil among these.' Because they gave the kalindās to the Kalis, that is the kalindā-ness of the kalindās. Kali Vaitadanya saw this sāman. He praised with it. By it he beheld this intermediate region, this hard-to-win world. He conquered it. This is a world-winning sāman. He who has praised with it wins a world. However, he does not conquer beyond the gandharva-world; he wins only up to the gandharva-world. And because Kali Vaitadanya saw it, it is called kāleya. The devas, having torn away the third pressing from the first two pressings, went to the heavenly world. These worlds were torn apart; the sacrifice was torn apart. The devas desired: 'Let us put together these worlds; let us put together the sacrifice.' They saw this sāman. They praised with it. By it they put together these worlds and put together the sacrifice. Because they put together these worlds, that is its heavenly quality. By that one conquers beyond the gandharva-world. 'Taro vai yajñas stomo vidadvasuḥ' — by the sacrifice and by the stoma they put together these worlds and put together the sacrifice. By the sacrifice and by the stoma his sacrifice becomes unified, and so do these worlds — for him who knows thus. Soma the king is indeed the kāleya. His sacrifice becomes possessed of the deity. Just as a cow whose calf is tied lows to be milked, so indeed Indra comes to that soma at which they praise with the kāleya. Therefore one should never depart from this sāman, thinking 'May my sacrifice be with Indra, with the deity.' By this he makes his sacrifice with Indra, with the deity. It becomes an ailā acchāvāka-sāman. Cattle are ilā.
1.155: In the beginning there were only two pressings. By two pressings the devas first conquered. Having conquered, when they were seated in their proper worlds, Indra approached them and said: 'There are three metres, three breaths — prāṇa, apāna, vyāna — three are these worlds; among the devas they say "thrice." Come, let us make three pressings.' They said: 'We shall not be able; we are already exhausted from the former ritual work.' Indra said: 'Then I shall fashion the third pressing from myself alone.' He fashioned the third pressing from himself. Thus the third pressing among the deities belongs to Indra. For him who, knowing thus, praises with the third pressing, his praise among the deities is through Indra. The third pressing is like a sucked-out remnant. The jāgata Suparṇa brought it and sucked it. Therefore it is as if pressed out. With 'svādiṣṭhayā' they make it sweet. With 'madiṣṭhayā' they place flavour and intoxication in it. They pour the āśira; with cattle they make it swell. 'Rakṣohā viśvacarṣaṇir abhi yonim ayohatam' — this is the transition back to the first two pressings.
1.156: Then the saṃhita (combined chant), having a two-syllable ending, is for firm foundation. The sacrificer is two-footed, for firm standing. The devas and asuras competed. They said: 'Let us pool together both our treasures. Whichever of us wins, let both treasures be combined for them.' They pooled both their treasures. The devas desired: 'May we win both these treasures combined.' They saw this sāman. They praised with it. By it they won both treasures combined. Because they won both treasures combined, that is the saṃhita-ness of the saṃhita. He who knows thus wins both, combined, over the hostile rival. Āruṇi said about this: 'That saṃhita was indeed a horse. In it their treasure was combined. That is what they wrested from the asuras — they wrested Prajāpati himself from them.' Thereupon the devas prevailed and the asuras were defeated.
1.157: Now, the third pressing was as if cut off from the midday pressing. The devas, having examined this, knew: 'This third pressing is as if cut off from the midday pressing.' They saw this sāman. By it they joined them together. Because they joined them together, that is the saṃhita-ness of the saṃhita. For him who knows thus, this third pressing becomes joined, uncut-off. By the uṣṇih and the kakubh, Indra raised his vajra against Vṛtra, standing upon two gāyatrīs. They could not raise it. Upon those two he placed four-footed animals — the cow, the horse, the goat, and the sheep. Then they raised it. Standing upon the uṣṇih and the kakubh, making his two arms into sabhā and pauṣkala, he struck. He slew him.
1.158: He stumbled forward with one foot and backward with the other. That with which he stumbled forward became the kakubh. Therefore the kakubh has more syllables in its first half. And that with which he stumbled backward became the uṣṇih. Therefore the uṣṇih has more syllables in its latter half. These two metres are like a milk-cow and a draft-ox. Therefore animals eat most with their front and hind parts. They carry with the front part, are milked from the hind part, and give birth from it.
1.159: Among these is the sabhā chant, for the 'assembly-quality' of the sacrifice. For whatever in the sacrifice is badly praised, badly recited, or defective — this serves as its assembly-quality. 'Sabham avibham asad' — thus they praise with it. Sābha is indeed its name. By the sabhā the devas took from the asuras their vigour, strength, vital power, heroic might, cattle, and food; by the sabhā they made themselves splendid. That is the sabhā-ness of the sabhā. He who knows thus, by the sabhā takes from the hostile rival his vigour, strength, vital power, heroic might, cattle, and food; by the sabhā he makes himself splendid. Prajāpati created creatures. Having been created, they departed from him. He desired: 'May the creatures not depart from me; may they turn back to me.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereupon the creatures turned back to him; thereupon they ceased departing from him. He said: 'By the sabhā I became splendid among the creatures.' That is the sabhā-ness of the sabhā. He who knows thus becomes splendid, unseparated from cattle. It becomes a kākubha svāra chant. The svara is prāṇa; the kakubh is the human metre. Prāṇa is pre-eminence; the human being is pre-eminence. By pre-eminence it joins pre-eminence to pre-eminence; by pre-eminence they find firm standing in pre-eminence. Moreover, they thereby empower the sacrificer with prāṇa. He for whom things prosper is one who knows thus. He could not distinguish them, since they were all of one form. He saw this pauṣkala sāman. By it he differentiated their forms. They became of various colours — white, red, black. Before that they were indeed all of one form, all reddish. He who knows thus obtains cattle of many forms and various colours; he becomes rich in cattle. Now the pauṣkala: Puṣkala Āṅgirasa, desiring cattle, performed tapas. He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereby he obtained cattle. This is a cattle-winning sāman. He who knows thus obtains cattle and becomes rich in cattle. Because Puṣkala Āṅgirasa saw it, it is called pauṣkala. It has a four-syllable ending. Four-footed are the animals. The uṣṇih is like animals.
1.160: If the metres were to follow in their regular order, they would make the anuṣṭubh last. The anuṣṭubh is speech. They would place her outside the sacrifice. By this they give her a share in the middle of the sacrifice. These verses are: 'Purojitī vo andhasaḥ' — these go forward, slaying evil, forward driving away evil. What has been won and conquered, they continue to secure. 'Sutāya mādayitnave, apa śvānaṃ śnathiṣṭana sakhāyo dīrghajihviyam.' There was a long-tongued demoness. She would lick up each and every soma. She was in the northern ocean. Whatever soma was pressed in the southern ocean, or in the eastern, or in the western — she would lick it up from right there. Indra, wanting to seize her, was unable to catch her.
1.161: Now Sumitra Kautsa was handsome. He (Indra) said to him: 'Sumitra, you are handsome. Women are easily won by a handsome man. Seduce this long-tongued one.' He approached her and said: 'Dīrghajihvī, desire me!' She said: 'You have one phallus, but I have testicles on every limb — that will not work.' He went back and reported: 'She says you have one phallus but she has testicles on every limb, so it will not work.' 'I shall make phalluses on every one of your limbs,' he (Indra) said. He fashioned those and went forth.
1.162: He showed them to her. They pleased her. She said: 'Come then.' 'What is your name?' she asked. 'My name is Sumitra.' 'Your name is auspicious,' she said. They lay together. When he had accomplished his purpose with her, he seized hold of her then and there. She cried: 'But did you not say you were Sumitra (good friend)?' He said: 'I am indeed Sumitra — a good friend to the friendly, a bad friend to the hostile.' He saw these saumitrā sāmans. He praised with them. By them he invoked Indra. Indra, raising this anuṣṭubh as his vajra, rushed at her: 'Purojitī vo andhasaḥ sutāya mādayitnave, apa śvānaṃ śnathiṣṭana sakhāyo dīrghajihviyam' — thus he struck her. These are rival-slaying, demon-slaying verses. He who has praised with these verses slays the hostile rival, drives away the demon and evil. 'Yo dhārayā pāvakayā pariprasyandate sutaḥ, indur aśvo na kṛtviyaḥ. Taṃ duroṣam abhī naras somaṃ viśvācyā dhiyā, yajñāya santv adrayaḥ.' This is the transition back to the first two pressings. Among these is the śyāvāśva chant. Śyāvāśva Ārcanānasa, left behind as the fire-maintainer when the sattrins had gone to the heavenly world — he desired: 'May I rise up, may I reach the heavenly world, may I rejoin the sattrins.' He saw this sāman.
1.163: 'Purojitī vo andhasaḥ ehiyā. Sūtāya mādayitnavā ehiyā. Āpa śvānaṃ śnāthīṣṭānā. Aihovā ehiyā' — thus the Maruts summoned him to the heavenly world. Thereupon he reunited with the sattrins. He, this Śyāvāśva, is indeed with the Maruts. This is a heaven-winning sāman. He who knows thus attains the heavenly world. Because Śyāvāśva Ārcanānasa saw it, it is called śyāvāśva. Indra, shrinking from the third pressing, departed from it, for it was like a sucked-out remnant. With this sāman the Viśve Devas called him back, saying 'ohoiyā.' Thereupon Indra returned to the third pressing. Thereupon he ceased departing from it. This is a sāman 'with Indra.' His sacrifice becomes possessed of Indra, possessed of the deity. Indra turns toward his sacrifice; Indra does not depart from his sacrifice — for him who knows thus. It becomes a svāra chant. Prāṇa is the svara. Those who cause something of the sacrificial ritual to be transgressed, whether they actually cause a transgression or not — they are indeed deprived of their vital breaths. For who knows whether they cause a transgression or not?
1.164: Now the andhīgava. It has a middle ending, for firm foundation. Those who approach the ārbhava pavamāna are like people swimming in the ocean without support. That it has a middle ending is for firm standing. Just as one who has swum across the ocean, finding an island, climbs out and rests — so, having reached the nidhana, they may rest at ease while still praising. It becomes an ailā chant. Cattle are ilā; cattle are complete food. He who sets out on a journey without a resting place does not complete it. But he who sets out with a resting place does complete it. This ocean without support is the atmosphere. Without a resting place there is no way through it, neither this way nor that. Making cattle, the complete food, their resting place, they proceed. The Śāktyas, desiring food, undertook the consecration. Andhīgu Śāktya saw this sāman. He praised with it. This ten-syllable virāj came to him in the middle. The virāj of ten syllables is food. Thereby they obtained the virāj-food. This is the sāman for obtaining the virāj-food. He who knows thus obtains the virāj-food and becomes the foremost food-eater among his own people.
1.165: Then these verses occur: 'Abhi priyāṇi pavate canohitaḥ.' Prajāpati created creatures. He created them without breath. With these very verses he placed breaths in them. 'Abhi priyāṇi pavate canohitaḥ' — creatures are what is dear; with 'abhi' he breathed upon them with prāṇa. 'Nāmāni yahvo adhi yeṣu vardhate' — names are indeed creatures, great ones. 'Ā sūryasya bṛhato bṛhann adhi rathaṃ viṣvañcam aruhad vicakṣaṇaḥ' — prāṇa is indeed what goes in all directions; it goes in all directions. These are life-giving verses. He who has praised with them lives a full lifespan. Among these is the kāva. Kavi Bhārgava desired among the devas the immortal gandharva-world. He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereby he attained among the devas the immortal gandharva-world. This is a world-winning sāman. He who has praised with it attains the immortal gandharva-world among the devas. Because Kavi Bhārgava saw it, it is called kāva. It becomes a svāra chant. By the svara, food is given to the gods at the end. The ṛc and the sāman are equal at the end of the pavamāna. They do not let the sāman exceed the ṛc, nor the ṛc the sāman. That they praise now here, now there in the pavamāna is for the sake of this equality, for proper arrangement. The gāyatra with syllable-by-syllable svara comes at the beginning; the syllable-by-syllable svara at the end of the pavamāna. Therefore creatures have breath from both sides — both those rising upward and those going downward. When a father and son launch a boat together, it does not come to harm.
