The sacrifice is this divine boat. These two, father and son, launch it. The auśana comes at the beginning, the kāva at the end — for the unharmed state of the sacrifice. Therefore when people board a boat quickly, a father and son board first. Five metres occur in the ārbhava pavamāna and seven sāmans — that makes twelve. Twelve months make a year. There are really five sāmans in the midday pavamāna — they are the seasons. The months are arranged in the ārbhava pavamāna, the seasons in the midday pavamāna.
1.166: This year is indeed Prajāpati. He stands for six months on one foot, then the other, holding one aloft. When he lifts up the hot foot, then it becomes hot above and cold below. Therefore in summer it is hot above and cold is found below. Therefore in summer the well-waters are declared to be cool. But when he lifts up the cold foot, then it becomes cold above and hot below. Therefore in winter it is cold above and warmth is found below. Therefore in winter the well-waters are declared to be warm. Thus this Prajāpati, the year, sustains creatures. Now they offer this saumya caru (porridge offering to Soma). Whatever the udgātṛ does that is deficient or excessive in the sacrifice while performing — that goes to the world of Yama. Even the dead say of it: 'We saw this in the assembly of Yama.' With this offering it is brought back, when they offer this saumya caru. He should look upon it reciting: 'Whatever mind of mine has gone to Yama, or whatever of mine has gone elsewhere — with King Soma we place that back in ourselves. I place sight in my mind, mind in my sight. O life-giving verses, do not cut me off; may I not be separated from the share of the sāman.' He should also look at his shadow, for the non-destruction of the self. He should also place ghee on both eyes, for the nourishment of sight. This would also serve as a sign: he who cannot see himself reflected would be about to die.
1.167: When the Aṅgirasas were seated in their sattra, cataracts formed in their eyes. They desired: 'May we not be blind; may we see.' They placed this saumya śyāma caru on their eyes. They applied it with this mantra: 'With that by which they won the race, the clear-sighted one; with which the eagle, the bird, the suparṇa — what they call the eye, infinite in Aditi — may Soma, the clear-sighted, place that in me.' Thereupon they became free from blindness; they could see. He who knows thus becomes free from blindness and sees. On this they say: 'Should it be eaten or not?' He who, though learned, would be lacking in fame and afflicted — he should eat it. The learned are certainly capable of fame here. But he who, though learned, is lacking in fame and afflicted — his fame has gone to the other world — it is brought back by eating this saumya śyāma caru. But Śāṭyāyani said: 'It should certainly not be eaten. Who would bring fame from there, where one would spend a longer night?'
1.168: 'Yajñāyajñā vo agnaye.' This is the fourth part of the sāman. By this they establish the sacrifice in the sāman. This is the body of the sāmans. By this they give the sāmans a body. He who would depart from this sāman would get a skin disease, or ill repute would speak against him. The gāyatrī, having become a bird, rises up. She flew to heaven. While she was aloft, the directions brought a bride-price in the anuṣṭubh. His creatures become bride-price bringers. The bride-price is brought to him from the directions — for him who knows thus. 'Vayo yajñā vo agnaye' — thus he begins the prastāva. 'O yirā yirā ca dakṣase popriṃ vayam amṛtaṃ jātovāhoyi vidosam' — thus he performs the pratihāra. 'Yajñā' is two syllables; 'girā' is two syllables; 'poprim' is two syllables — that makes six. The tūṣṇīṃśaṃsa has six syllables. The tūṣṇīṃśaṃsa is released at the āgnimāruta. For that is the end. Agni Vaiśvānara rose up against creatures. He heated everything.
1.169: 'Let us remove the heat of this one,' they said. They removed his heat. That which they removed first — regarding it they deliberated: 'Where shall this go?' 'It shall be in every sacrifice,' they said. That became the yajñāyajñīya. That is the yajñāyajñīya-ness of the yajñāyajñīya. What they removed second became the nārmedha. What they removed third became the dāśaspatya. What they removed fourth became the viśoviśīya. What they removed fifth became the vāravantīya. Agni Vaiśvānara is indeed the sacrifice. Accordingly these agniṣṭoma-sāmans possess his quality.
1.170: One desiring protection should employ the nārmedha. Nṛmedha and Suvrata were brothers. They established their fires. Nṛmedha sang the udgītha for Suvrata. It had been praised with the yajñāyajñīya. Then they attacked him. The attackers killed the son of the sacrificer and the two sons of the udgātṛ — Antakadhṛti Sauvratin, Nakira, and Śakapūta. He, grasping the arms of the attackers, spoke: 'Brahmins, this sacrifice is yours; with it you may sacrifice for whomever you wish. But by it I shall dispel that.' They wrapped him in an audumbara frame and set fire to him with hemp sticks. He desired: 'May I rise up from here, may I find a way and protection, may this fire not burn me.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereupon he found a way and protection; the fire did not burn him. It even burned away the binding with which he had been wrapped. This is a way-finding, protection-finding sāman. He found a way and protection. He who knows thus finds a way and protection.
1.171: One desiring pre-eminence should employ the dāśaspatya. The devas and asuras competed. The devas approached Indra: 'Under your leadership let us conquer.' Indra desired: 'May we conquer the asuras.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereupon the devas conquered the asuras. When he sat down after the victory, as the lord of ten, they brought him tribute-shares saying 'daśapataye daśapataya ā.' That is the dāśaspatya-ness of the dāśaspatya. He who knows thus receives tribute and becomes worthy of tribute. One desiring food should employ the viśoviśīya. Agni desired: 'May I be the food-eater, the foremost, the overlord of every settlement.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereby he became the food-eater, the foremost, the overlord of every settlement. Then every settlement brought him offerings. Because every settlement offered to him, that is the viśoviśīya-ness of the viśoviśīya. He is indeed the food-eater, the foremost, the overlord of every settlement. He who knows thus becomes the food-eater, the foremost, the overlord of every settlement. One desiring cattle should employ the vāravantīya. Prajāpati created cattle. Having been created, they departed from him. By the vāravantīya he restrained them. Because he restrained them, that is the vāravantīya-ness of the vāravantīya.
1.172: He who knows the udder of the sacrifice milks the sacrifice when it has been offered. The yajñāyajñīya is the udder of the sacrifice. Its teats are the gāyatra, the rathantara, the bṛhat, and the vāmadevya. One should begin the prastāva as if it were the gāyatra; one should insert the stobhas as if of the rathantara; one should ascend the rohas as if of the bṛhat; the hiṃkāra is the vāmadevya; the yajñāyajñīya itself stands on its own. The sacrificer milks the sacrifice thus. He becomes better from the very moment of praising. It is called 'yajñāyajñā vo agnaye.' This is indeed the sacrifice of sacrifices, the yajñāyajñīya. 'It carries sacrifice upon sacrifice' — that is the yajñāyajñīya-ness of the yajñāyajñīya. In former times all the stotras were praised with this. They say: 'Those who sacrifice ascend upward to the heavenly world. They are likely to fall off beyond.' Therefore they praise with the yajñāyajñīya at the end. The yajñāyajñīya is this earth. By it they find firm standing upon her. They praise having set it aside — since Agni Vaiśvānara is the yajñāyajñīya — for his pacification, to prevent burning. The wife rolls it forth with her thighs. She thereby pacifies Agni Vaiśvānara. She rolls it forth making her thigh bare, for with a bare thigh the wife performs the act of potency. They cause her to emit seed for the placing of semen.
1.173: She should gaze upon it reciting: 'Vāmī nāma saṃdṛśi viśvā vāmāni dhīmahi.' Offspring are vāma (desirable). Thereby one places offspring in oneself. 'Vṛṣṇas te vṛṣṇyāvato viśvāretāṃsi dhīmahi' — with this the other one gazes. They say: 'One should sing the udgītha with a covering' — since Agni Vaiśvānara is the yajñāyajñīya — for his pacification, to prevent burning. But one is likely to become devoted to the pitṛ-deities if one sings the udgītha with a covering. Others say: 'One should cover only up to the ears while singing the udgītha.' But others say: 'One hears with the ears, one sees with the eyes. Therefore one would cut oneself off from all this if one sang the udgītha with a covering.' Moreover, in the third pressing they pour the āśira. Creatures and cattle are the āśīḥ (blessings). Therefore one would cut oneself off from all this if one sang the udgītha with a covering.
1.174: The yajñāyajñīya is a bird. He who desires that the sacrificer should attain the heavenly world should begin the prastāva with 'vayo yajñā vo agnaye.' As a bird it carries him to the heavenly world. But that is somewhat unfavourable for longevity — for what is a bird is like something scattered, without a resting place. Therefore one should not begin the prastāva thus. 'O yirā yirā cā dākṣāsā': if one were to say 'girā girā ca,' Agni Vaiśvānara would swallow the sacrificer. But when one says 'o yirā yirā cā dākṣāsā' — irā is food — one thereby places food at the mouth of Agni Vaiśvānara. However, one is likely to become harsh if one says 'o yirā yirā cā dākṣāsā.'
1.175: But in that way one does not become harsh. The former brahmins used to ponder this: 'Who indeed has passed through the open jaws of the śiśumāra (crocodile)?' In the ekāyana this śiśumāra stands with jaws gaping in reverse — that is the yajñāyajñīya. Having placed food at its mouth, one passes safely through. 'Popriṃ vayam amṛtaṃ jāto vā': if one were to say 'pra pra vayam,' the sacrificer would die prematurely. But when one says 'poprim,' that popri (nourishment) comes to him. 'Hiṃ māyi dāyivam': if one were to say 'dāsam,' the sacrificer would become servile. But when one says 'dāyivam' — they say the praised sacrifice should be sent to heaven — thereby they send the praised sacrifice to heaven. 'Priyaṃ mitraṃ nu śaṃsiṣam': if one were to say 'priyaṃ mitraṃ na śaṃsiṣam,' one would not praise the dear friend.
1.176: 'Prāyāṃ māyitrān nū śāṃsīṣām ūrjo napātaṃ sa hy āyumā': if one were to say 'nāyumā,' the sacrificer would not be long-lived. But when one says 'āyumā,' the udgātṛ thereby places lifespan in both himself and the sacrificer. 'Hiṃ māyāsmāyūḥ āśemā havyadātāyā u vā': if one were to say 'dāśema,' biting insects would afflict him. But when one says 'āśemā,' one obtains indeed. 'Āśemā hāvyā dātāyāyi bhuvad vājeṣv avitā bhūvād dhiṃ māyi vārdhā' — for at this point the sacrifice has grown full. 'Uta trātā bahūnām': if one were to say 'tanūnām,' the sacrificer would become thin.
1.177: They say: the verses of the yajñāyajñīya are short by one syllable each. That is a breach in the sacrifice. If the udgātṛ, while singing, does not close it, he makes a breach in the yajñāyajñīya. Through the breach in the yajñāyajñīya the sacrifice leaks away; after the sacrifice the sacrificer; after the sacrificer his offspring. Into that gap one should place speech. Speech is brahman. 'Uta trātā vāg bahūnā u vā.' Some place 'gauḥ' (cow) — the cow being complete food — for the obtaining of complete food. But they say one need not worry about this, since it has a speech-ending (vāṅnidhana), and it is thereby joined. They say: the metres are released in order, according to seniority. Following the arrangement and release of the metres, creatures reach their proper end. 'Yajño vo agnaye' — he begins the prastāva with six syllables. 'Girā ca dakṣase' — he takes up the beginning with six syllables. That makes twelve. The jagatī has twelve syllables. They turn the jagatī first. They are released with the jagatī. 'Pra vayam amṛtaṃ jātavedasam' — eleven syllables. The triṣṭubh has eleven syllables. They are released with the triṣṭubh. 'Priyaṃ mitraṃ nu śaṃsiṣam' — they reach the nidhana with eight syllables. The gāyatrī has eight syllables. They are released with the gāyatrī. Thus the metres are released in order, according to seniority.
1.178: The devas and asuras competed in the sacrifice. The devas drove out the asuras from the sacrifices. The asuras, having torn away the essence and potency of the three pressings, entered blind darkness. Whatever is beyond the agniṣṭoma is blind darkness. Whatever is beyond the year is blind darkness. The sacrifice extends only as far as the agniṣṭoma. The year extends equally far. Twelve are the stotras of the agniṣṭoma; twelve are the months of the year. Twelve stotras, twelve śastras. Twelve bright fortnights, twelve dark fortnights. Whatever is beyond that is blind darkness. Into that they entered. The devas pursued the asuras. The asuras rose up against them with these very uktha hymns. Because they rose up with the uktha hymns, that is the uktha-ness of the ukthyas. They drove them out from there completely. They entered blind darkness — the night.
1.179: 'I!' said Varuṇa. 'I shall carry the light for you two,' said Agni. Those two pursued. Those two produced one third. By that they replenished the morning pressing. Therefore they praise with āgneya hymns. The aindrāvāruṇa is recited as a subsidiary. Therefore they praise with gāyatrī verses, for the morning pressing has the gāyatrī character. He again said: 'Who, along with me, will pursue this?' 'I!' said Bṛhaspati. Those two pursued. Those two again produced one third. By that they replenished the midday pressing. Therefore they praise with aindrī hymns. The aindrābārhaspatya is recited as a subsidiary. Therefore they praise with kakubh verses, for the midday pressing has the kākubha character. He again said: 'Who, along with me, will pursue this?' 'I!' said Viṣṇu. Those two pursued. Those two again produced one third. By that they replenished the third pressing. Therefore they praise with aindrī hymns. The aindrāvaiṣṇava is recited as a subsidiary. Therefore they praise with anuṣṭubh verses, for the third pressing has the ānuṣṭubha character. They cut them off from everything. They lead forth from the pravatīs into the ehivatīs, for everything here both goes forth and comes. Thereupon the devas prevailed and the asuras were defeated.
1.180: These are three, having two deities each — the aindrāvāruṇa, the aindrābārhaspatya, and the aindrāvaiṣṇava. With these six 'wish-cows' they produced: the cow, the horse, the goat, the sheep, rice, and barley. These six wish-cows attend upon him who knows thus. The sacrifice extends only as far as the agniṣṭoma. They ask: if the sacrifice extends only as far as the agniṣṭoma, and it has come to an end, why do they lead forth the uktha hymns? One should say: the verses of the yajñāyajñīya are short by one syllable each — that is a breach in the sacrifice. Thereby one places the sacrifice whole and complete in it. They lead forth in the āgneya hymns, from the āgneya hymns. That is like piling fires upon fire. If one were to scatter a single fire into many parts, there would be many. If one were to gather those many together, there would be just one.
1.181: Among these is the sākamaśva. The devas, longing for heaven, performed tapas. They saw this sāman. They praised with it. It, having become a horse, carried them to the heavenly world. They said: 'We went to the heavenly world together (sākam) with the horse (aśva).' That is the sākamaśva-ness of the sākamaśva. This is a heaven-winning sāman. He who knows thus attains the heavenly world. One who has a rival should employ the pramaṃhiṣṭhīya. Indra raised his vajra against Vṛtra. He could not hurl it. Only when he saw 'pra' did he slay him. He who knows thus slays the hostile rival. Because he saw 'pra,' it is called pramaṃhiṣṭhīya. One whose property remains unconquered should employ the satrāsāhīya. Indra conquered the asuras but did not conquer their property completely. He desired: 'May I completely overcome all the asuras.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. By it he completely overcame all the asuras. Because he completely overcame all the asuras, that is the satrāsāhīya-ness of the satrāsāhīya. He who knows thus completely overcomes the hostile rival. One desiring the office of purohita should employ the daivodāsa. The brāhmaṇa belongs to Agni, the kṣatriya to Indra. This sāman belongs to both Agni and Indra. Brahman is Agni, kṣatra is Indra. By brahman it upholds kṣatra, by kṣatra brahman.
1.182: One seized by a rakṣas should employ the hārivarṇa as the brahma-sāman. When the Aṅgirasas were going to the heavenly world, the rakṣases pursued them through these worlds. They desired: 'May we drive away the rakṣases.' Harivarṇa Āṅgirasa saw this sāman. He praised with it. By it he drove away the rakṣases from these worlds. 'Hariśriyam' — from this world; 'virājasi' — from the atmosphere; 'divedive' — from the world beyond. Thereupon, having driven away the rakṣases from all these worlds, they ascended to the heavenly world. He who knows thus, having driven away the rakṣases from all these worlds, ascends to the heavenly world.
1.183: One desiring protection should employ the traita. The Āptyas — Ekata, Dvita, and Trita — were wandering in the wilderness when thirst overcame them. On the arid land they found a well. Neither the first nor the second wished to descend into it. So Trita descended. When those two had drunk and were satisfied, they covered him with a chariot wheel and drove off with the cattle. He desired: 'May I rise up from here, may I find a way and protection.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. 'Sa sam indubhiḥ' — thus he reached the nidhana. Parjanya floated him upward with rain, and the flood lifted the chariot wheel with which he had been covered. This is a way-finding, protection-finding sāman. He found a way and protection thereby. He who knows thus finds a way. He followed their trail on foot. Seeing him coming, one became a bear, the other a monkey, and they fled into the forest. This is a rival-slaying sāman. Those two went to the state of being his enemies — the two whom he turned into a bear and a monkey. Moreover, Parjanya sends rain for him. This is also a cattle-winning sāman. He made those cattle solely his own. He who knows thus obtains cattle and becomes rich in cattle.
1.184: One desiring food should employ the traikakubha. Indra gave the Yatis to the wolves. While they were being devoured, three boys remained: Rāyovāja, Pṛthuraśmi, and Bṛhadgiri. They praised Indra. He said to them: 'What do you desire, boys, that you praise me?' 'Just carry us, O Maghavan,' they said. He seated them between his shoulders. They hung at his three kakubh-points. These worlds, though existing together, spread out in three directions. Food followed them in three directions. The three peaks of these three worlds held food. He reflected: 'If I could obtain the food at the three peaks of these three worlds, I could take away these three peaks.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. By it he obtained the food at the three peaks of these three worlds and took away these three peaks. Because he obtained the food at the three peaks of these three worlds, that is the traikakubha-ness of the traikakubha. This is the sāman for obtaining food.
1.185: This sāman has a threefold Indra-quality. The ṛc verses belong to Indra, the sāman belongs to Indra. The nidhana is 'indraḥ.' Three are the powers of a person: the self, offspring, and cattle. By this he embraces those in himself. He said to them: 'What does one of you desire? What does the second? What does the third?' Rāyovāja said: 'I desire cattle.' To him he gave ilā. Cattle are ilā. Pṛthuraśmi said: 'I desire kṣatra.' To him he gave kṣatra. He is Pṛthu Vainya. Bṛhadgiri said: 'I desire food.' To him he gave the athakāra. The athakāra is food. One desiring offspring should employ the saubhara — one should reach the nidhana with 'huḥ.' One desiring food — with 'ūrk.' One desiring rain — with 'hiḥ.' One desiring elevation — with 'ut.' One desiring heaven — with 'ū.' The Bhāradvājāyanas, seated in a sattra, were asked: 'With what did you praise when desiring offspring?' 'With the saubhara.' 'When desiring food?' 'With the saubhara.' 'When desiring rain?' 'With the saubhara.' 'When desiring elevation?' 'With the saubhara.' 'When desiring heaven?' 'With the saubhara.' Such indeed is this sāman. These desires are in it. One obtains these very desires.
1.186: One should employ the saubhara as the brahma-sāman if one desires: 'May a son be born in my family who is conspicuous, handsome, of kṣatriya form, of manly form.' The offspring of a man is his glory; the saubhara is the glory of the bṛhat. The bṛhat is kṣatra. That offspring is of kṣatriya nature. A son is born in his family who is conspicuous, handsome, of kṣatriya form, of manly form — for him who knows thus. Prajāpati created creatures. Those he created perished. All crawling things came into being, except serpents. He created a second time. Those too perished. They became fish. He created a third time. Those too perished. They became birds. He created a fourth time. He reflected: 'How might these creatures, once created, not perish?' He saw this sāman. By it, saying 'ūrk,' he touched them. Joined with nourishment, they throve. He said: 'I have well-borne these creatures.' That is the saubhara-ness of the saubhara.
1.187: With the nārmedha they praise for the acchāvāka at the atirātra. With the ilā they extend the night. They say: 'Those who make the acchāvāka-sāman in uṣṇih verses go to anuṣṭubh verses.' But the uṣṇih is on this side. One of these is a lower uṣṇih, one a middle uṣṇih, one a front uṣṇih; one is an unrestrained metre — unrestrained is what speech speaks. Speech is anuṣṭubh. Therefore they do not go to anuṣṭubh verses. The last of these is a manifest anuṣṭubh. Therefore they certainly do not go to anuṣṭubh verses. Just as honey-makers gather the essences of flowers, so these deities gathered the essences of the metres. His praise becomes endowed with the essence of the metres — for him who knows thus. This sāman has the form of day and night. The ṛc verses belong to Indra; the sāman to Agni. The day belongs to Indra; the night to Agni. He who would depart from this sāman at the atirātra would be torn from the form of day and night. If someone were to say: 'He has been torn from the form of day and night' — so indeed it would be.
1.188: The night is shorter than the day. It becomes an ailā chant, to prevent tearing apart. What exceeds the ṛc and the sāman becomes the udvaṃśīya. He who knows thus finds firm standing in both ṛc and sāman. One should employ this for a gentle person, not a cruel one — for a cruel person has a limited lifespan, while a gentle person has an extended lifespan — for the obtaining of what is extended. With this sāman the devas, raising it like a bamboo pole, strode over the asuras. Because they strode over the asuras raising it like a bamboo pole, that became the udvaṃśīya. That is the udvaṃśīya-ness of the udvaṃśīya. He who knows thus conquers what is unconquered and does not lose what is conquered. They sing this sāman which has the form of all the pṛṣṭha-sāmans: 'Gāyanti tvā gāyatriṇaḥ' is of the gāyatra; 'arcanty arkam arkāyiṇā' is of the rathantara; 'brahmāṇas tvā hoyi śatakratau' is of the bṛhat; 'udvaṃśam iva yāyimirāyi' is of the vairūpa; 'ud vaṃśāmi' is of the returned vairāja; 'vā yā vu vā up' is of the mahānāmnīs; 'mā irā' is of the revatīs; 'hā i' is of the vāmadevya.
1.189: Every ṣoḍaśin is complete. Where the udvaṃśīya is performed, one repeats the last word. Thereby the ṣoḍaśī form is created. The ṣoḍaśī is a vajra against the rival. Thereby one hurls the vajra at the hostile rival, to strike him down. Darkness was between the devas and asuras. The devas saw this half-ṛc. By it they pierced through the darkness. One should meditate mentally on him whom one hates. Having approached this half-ṛc, it indeed pierces through him. One intones with a half-syllable more. Therefore smoky streams flow down. Aṣāḍha Sāvayasa performed the atirātra. Atidhanvan Śaunaka was present. He made the udvaṃśīya the acchāvāka-sāman at the atirātra. He said: 'Do not do thus. The ilā is cut short; the svara, unsupported beyond, has the form of a subordinate chant.' He replied: 'We shall approach the complete ilā and, having joined it by the svara, extend through the night.' 'No,' said the other. 'Indra indeed pursued the asuras with that sāman. They, recognizing his strength in the svara, turned and fled. He attacked them. Having attacked, he struck them down with this half-ṛc.'
1.190: Only the nārmedha should be used. It becomes an ailā chant. Cattle are ilā. By cattle they drove them out in the night. Therefore only the nārmedha should be used. One desiring prosperity should employ the āṣṭādaṃṣṭra. Aṣṭādaṃṣṭra Vairūpa saw these two sāmans after the other ṛṣis. He feared: 'My two sāmans will become ones not praised with.' He said: 'May they prosper; may one who praises with my sāmans be satisfied with soma.' These two sāmans are accompanied by prosperity. Those who praise with them prosper. Aṣṭādaṃṣṭra Vairūpa, desiring cattle, performed tapas. He saw these two sāmans. He praised with them. He approached this ilā. Cattle are ilā. Thereby he obtained cattle. These are two cattle-winning sāmans. He who knows thus obtains cattle and becomes rich in cattle. Because Aṣṭādaṃṣṭra Vairūpa saw them, they are called āṣṭādaṃṣṭra. It becomes an ailā acchāvāka-sāman. Cattle are ilā.
1.191: When Prajāpati distributed bodies to the devas, the one that was harivatī (golden-hued) he kept for himself. He gave it to Indra. With it Indra attained pre-eminence. Therefore, when they praise with harivatī verses, that is for pre-eminence. The harivatī verses are esoterically anuṣṭubh verses. When fully praised, they have thirty-four syllables. From those two syllables which are the thirty-fourth of the sāman, the world is apportioned, they say. If they praised with thirty-five syllables, they would attain the higher form, that of the bṛhat. If with thirty-three, the lower form, that of the anuṣṭubh. If the stoma were seventeen-fold, they would attain the highest form. If fifteen-fold, the lowest. When they praise with the ṣoḍaśī in the sixteen-fold stoma with thirty-four syllables, they empower him with his own proper basis. Through that, fulfilled, he becomes prosperous among his own people. Sixteen stotras and sixteen śastras make thirty-two. The anuṣṭubh has thirty-two syllables. The ṣoḍaśī, of anuṣṭubh nature, is fulfilled in the anuṣṭubh.
1.192: The one-syllable was the lowest of the devas; the seven-syllable the highest. The nine-syllable was the lowest of the asuras; the fifteen-syllable the highest. The devas desired: 'With the lesser may we wrest away the greater of the asuras.' With the one-syllable they wrested away the fifteen-syllable; with the two the fourteen; with the three the thirteen; with the four the twelve; with the five the eleven; with the six the ten; with the seven the nine; with the eight the eight. When these came together as sixteen and sixteen, that is the ṣoḍaśin-ness of the ṣoḍaśin. Then the gāyatrī as the seventeenth came alongside. Therefore they say: 'Prajāpati is the seventeenth.' Indra wished to slay Vṛtra. He ran to Prajāpati: 'Weapons against Vṛtra!' Prajāpati gave him this ṣoḍaśin as vajra — its potency — and the śakvarī verses. He could not slay him.
1.193: He gave him the śakvarī verses. Thereupon Indra slew Vṛtra. He who knows thus slays the hostile rival. He reflected: 'If I give it to him by day, the rivals' realm will increase at night. If at night, by day.' He saw this: he gave it at the moment when the sun is setting. By that he cut off the asuras from both day and night. He who knows thus cuts off the hostile rival from both day and night. Just as one places a beam atop to support the structure, so the ṣoḍaśī is set atop the stotras, for the completion of the heavenly world.
1.194: They say: 'The trivṛt stoma alone should be used.' The trivṛt is a vajra; the vajra is the ṣoḍaśī. Others say the fifteen-fold; others the sixteen-fold. The fifteen-fold is the vajra. Indra, raising the vajra, is the sixteenth. 'With the vajra together with Indra may I slay Vṛtra, the evil one.' With the vajra together with Indra he slays the hostile evil rival — he who knows thus. Others say the seventeen-fold. Prajāpati is the seventeenth — the overseer, protector, encourager: 'Strike! Slay! Do not retreat!' With the vajra together with Indra and Prajāpati he slays the hostile evil rival. Others say the twenty-one-fold. From all seven hotrās Indra fashioned the vajra — three for each office. That twenty-one-fold ṣoḍaśī came together. With it Indra slew Vṛtra. He who knows thus slays the hostile rival. Yāmana Bhrātalāyana said: 'The ṣoḍaśī is for the attainment of virāj. The ṣoḍaśī belongs to the virāj; it is established in every virāj.'
1.195: The devas and asuras were arrayed against each other over day and night. The devas conquered the day; the asuras retreated to the night. The devas said: 'We have won only half of this realm. How shall we completely conquer the night? Let us attack.' They attacked. They strove at the latter half of the day. The asuras, setting forth at night, strove at its first half. This latter half of the day is the evening. This first half of the night is also the evening. Each side strove at its own boundary. The metres were their own domain.
1.196: The one-syllable was the lowest of the devas; the seven-syllable the highest. The nine-syllable was the lowest of the asuras; the fifteen-syllable the highest. The seven-syllable and nine-syllable stood close together; the others on each side were arrayed at a distance. Prajāpati, stepping between them with the anuṣṭubh, took his stand. Looking at each other, they stood watching, each seeking the other's weak point. The devas approached Prajāpati: 'Let us conquer the asuras.' He gave them this ṣoḍaśin as vajra: 'Having wrested their metres with your metres, bring them to me.' With the one-syllable they wrested the fifteen-syllable; with the two the fourteen — that became an anuṣṭubh and they brought it to Prajāpati. With the three the thirteen; with the four the twelve — another anuṣṭubh for Prajāpati. With the five the eleven; with the six the ten — another anuṣṭubh. With the seven the nine; with the eight the eight — another anuṣṭubh. Having wrested their metres with their own, they drove out the asuras. Those driven out, Prajāpati, becoming a reddish-tawny horse, thrust them away. They are found at the borderlands, each one alone. Thereupon the devas prevailed and the asuras were defeated.
1.197: The devas were fewer, the asuras more numerous. Yet with the fewer they wrested away the greater. Therefore a smaller village challenges a larger one. Therefore fewer men conquer more numerous enemies. He who has fewer conquers one who has more — he who knows thus. This is manifestly the paṅkti — five syllables, five feet. With the paṅkti they praise with the ṣoḍaśin. The paṅkti is the human metre. Since the human has the paṅkti nature, from the human one fashions accomplished speech. Therefore the human speaks all speeches. When they praise in anuṣṭubh, it is like running to great rivers with only jars. They pile the lesser metres upon the greater. The lesser are those whose deities have been conquered — therefore they pile them upon the greater. The most potent of these metres are those with three syllables and one foot. By those they obtain potency. One intones the nidhana with extra emphasis. The devas strode from day over night. He who knows thus conquers the rival's realm.
1.198: They buy the soma with the adhikarṇī (surcharge-cow). The ṣoḍaśī is mounted upon the stotras like an ear-ornament. Saying 'We buy the ṣoḍaśin together,' they buy the ṣoḍaśin when the adhikarṇī is the soma-purchase cow. Thereby the ṣoḍaśī is bought and obtained. They buy the soma with the adhikarṇī. From pressing to pressing they make the soma overflow. They praise with janadvatī verses. All forms are taught. When they buy with the adhikarṇī, the sacrificer generates it. When they make the soma overflow, the adhvaryu generates it. When they praise with janadvatī verses, the udgātṛ generates it. When all forms are taught, the hotṛ generates it. These are the means of generation of the ṣoḍaśin.
1.199: The brilliance (haras) exceeded Indra. He said to the devas: 'How might the brilliance not exceed me?' The Viśve Devas said: 'We shall praise you with the harivatī mantra; thereby the brilliance will not exceed you.' They praised him with the harivatī mantra. Thereupon the brilliance no longer exceeded Indra. When they praise with harivatī verses, they empower Indra with brilliance. Indra is the deity nearest to the sacrificer. When they praise with harivatī verses, they empower the sacrificer with brilliance and splendour.
1.200: The ṣoḍaśī is like the trace-horse among the stotras. When a trace-horse pulls unyoked, it either breaks loose or sweeps aside. By the last stotra one attains the same stoma. Just as one yokes a trace-horse to the trace-yoke, so one yokes the ṣoḍaśin after the previous stotras, to prevent sweeping aside. The ṣoḍaśī is like the axle-pin among stotras — by the last stotra one attains the same stoma, to prevent wearing out. The ṣoḍaśī is like a kinship bond among stotras — by the last stotra one attains the same stoma, to prevent the bond from breaking. Cattle are the uktha hymns; the vajra is the ṣoḍaśī. Because they praise with the ṣoḍaśin at the end of the uktha hymns, they seize the cattle with the vajra, to prevent them from being lost.
1.251: The udgātṛ indeed pours the semen that has become the yajamāna when he sings the bahiṣpavamāna. With the retasyā he stirs up the mind; with the gāyatrī, the prāṇa; with the triṣṭubh, the eye; with the jagatī, the ear; with the anuṣṭubh, speech. With the paṅkti he restores the self. Now the final tṛca is indeed the birth. With it he generates forth. Because it is sung with noise and rumble, therefore offspring are born with noise and rumble. On this they say: it should be sung without noise. For when a woman giving birth cries out in pain, then she makes noise. But softly, as it were, without noise, one gives birth with great joy. Moreover, that which is gāyatra is truth. One would spoil its correspondence if one were to practice it with rathantara-coloring. Therefore it should be sung without noise. Now the āgneya ājya has one deity. That is a going forth. The maitrāvaruṇa has two deities. That is a firm standing. The aindra has one deity. That is a going forth. The aindrāgna has two deities. That is both a going forth and a firm standing. He who knows thus stands firm. By the trivṛt and pañcadaśa, a person is established in this world. That which he carries is the trivṛt; that by which he stands firm is the pañcadaśa. These two are both trivṛt and both pañcadaśa. The trivṛt stoma is splendor and brahmic glory; the pañcadaśa is vigor and heroic strength.
1.252: Now the gāyatrī of the midday pavamāna: the downward prāṇa is this very one. In it are two sāmans. Therefore with this prāṇa one does two things: one makes ashes and one makes wind. The prastāvas are separate, the nidhanas are separate, with a hiṃkāra in between. Therefore the one does not overpower the other. For by this they are kept apart. Now the bṛhatī: the forward prāṇa is this very one. In it are two sāmans. Therefore with this prāṇa one does two things: one discharges semen and one urinates. The prastāvas are separate, the nidhanas are separate, with a hiṃkāra in between. Therefore the one does not overpower the other. For by this they are kept apart. Now the triṣṭubh: that is the navel. In it is one sāman. Therefore with this one prāṇa one does only one thing: that which one exhales from the vital breaths. Now the pṛṣṭha-sāmans: the pṛṣṭha-sāmans are indriya and vīrya. Therefore whatever beings share the spine -- of cattle, horses, or humans -- the spine is the strongest part. For the pṛṣṭha-sāmans are indriya and vīrya. They are in the bṛhatī meters. Therefore these ribs, as it were bṛhatīs, and the cattle, as it were bṛhatīs, come together at the back. Now the gāyatrī of the ārbhava pavamāna: prāṇa is this very one. In it are two sāmans. Therefore with prāṇa one does two things: one breathes in and breathes out. The prastāvas are separate, the nidhanas are separate, with a hiṃkāra in between. Therefore the one does not overpower the other. For by this they are kept apart. Now the uṣṇih and kakubh: those are the two eyes. The meter is the same. There are two sāmans. Therefore the two eyes see the same. They are in three-footed verses. Therefore the eye is threefold: the white, the dark, and the pupil. They are in the parovarīyas verses. Therefore a person sees what is far away. The prastāvas are separate, the nidhanas are separate, with a hiṃkāra in between. Therefore the two eyes differ in power. Now the anuṣṭubh: that is speech. In it are two sāmans. Therefore with speech one does two things: one eats food with it and one speaks with it. The prastāvas are separate, the nidhanas are separate, with a hiṃkāra in between. Therefore one does not speak everything as truth with speech, nor everything as untruth. For by this they are kept apart. Now the jagatī: that is the ear. In it is one sāman. Therefore with the ear one does only one thing: that which one hears. It is in a four-footed verse. Therefore, being in one place, one hears in all directions. Even when turned away, one hears what is spoken from behind. Now the yajñāyajñīya: that is the head. The head is mounted upon the other limbs. The yajñāyajñīya is mounted upon the other stotras. He who knows thus is mounted above his own people. The head is above the other limbs. The yajñāyajñīya is above the other stotras. He who knows thus is above his own people. This sacrifice is directly the one whose evil has been struck away. There is no blame nor evil-doing for him. If one should interrupt him at the bahiṣpavamāna, one should say: 'You have spoiled the semen of the sacrifice, O sacrifice-spoiler; your offspring shall be without semen.' If one should interrupt him at the one-deity ājya, one should say: 'You have spoiled the going forth of the sacrifice, O sacrifice-spoiler; you shall die when you go to the world of the departed.' If one should interrupt him at the two-deity ājya, one should say: 'You have spoiled the firm standing of the sacrifice, O sacrifice-spoiler; you shall be without firm standing.' If one should interrupt him at the gāyatrī of the midday pavamāna, one should say: 'You have spoiled the downward prāṇa of the sacrifice, O sacrifice-spoiler; ruin shall destroy you.' Or one should say: 'Hemorrhage shall destroy you.' If one should interrupt him at the bṛhatī, one should say: 'You have spoiled the generative organ of the sacrifice, O sacrifice-spoiler; urinary obstruction shall destroy you.' Or one should say: 'You shall be without semen.' If one should interrupt him at the triṣṭubh, one should say: 'You have spoiled the navel of the sacrifice, O sacrifice-spoiler; a convulsion shall destroy you.' If one should interrupt him at the pṛṣṭha-sāmans, one should say: 'You have spoiled the indriya and vīrya of the sacrifice, O sacrifice-spoiler; the thunderbolt shall destroy you.'
1.253: If one should interrupt him at the gāyatrī of the ārbhava pavamāna, one should say: 'You have spoiled the prāṇa of the sacrifice, O sacrifice-spoiler; prāṇa shall leave you.' Or one should say: 'You shall be one whose life is cut short.' If one should interrupt him at the uṣṇih and kakubh, one should say: 'You have spoiled the two eyes of the sacrifice, O sacrifice-spoiler; you shall become blind.' If one should interrupt him at the anuṣṭubh, one should say: 'You have spoiled the speech of the sacrifice, O sacrifice-spoiler; speech shall depart from you.' Or one should say: 'You shall die of hunger.' If one should interrupt him at the jagatī, one should say: 'You have spoiled the ear of the sacrifice, O sacrifice-spoiler; you shall become deaf.'
1.254: These are the counter-declarations. He who knows thus does not come to grief concerning the sacrifice. He who speaks against him comes to grief. If someone should speak against him, he should say: 'I know this sacrifice fully, with all its limbs and body, completely. Whatever gap you find in it, I shall fill that with yourself.' He who speaks against one who knows thus comes to grief himself. This agniṣṭoma is Prajāpati, having placed this twenty-first yajñāyajñīya upon these nine bahiṣpavamāna verses, lying down, milking these doubled virāj meters -- five of fifteen each and five of seventeen each. These are five virāj meters of thirty-two each. He who knows thus milks from the virāj all desires. The sacrifice of one who knows thus does not leak, even when he speaks untruth. The sacrifice of one who speaks untruth leaks, and it putrefies. But the sacrifice of one who knows thus neither leaks nor putrefies. Therefore the Kuru-Pañcālas milk the doubled form, for it belongs to Prajāpati. Therefore others milk it differently, for they do not know it.
1.255: Having become circular, having become infinite, he lies down. In imitation of this, other divinities too are circular. The sun is circular, the moon is circular, the sky is circular, the atmosphere is circular, this earth is circular. Even that which is divine in a person is circular. Imitating his characteristics, one who knows thus wins a circular, great, infinite prosperity. This sacrifice is spread out upward following the person. Its feet are the bahiṣpavamāna. These four thighs and knees are the ājyas. What is below the navel is the three-metered midday chant. The pṛṣṭha-sāmans are the back itself. This is the ārbhava; this is the agniṣṭoma-sāman. Nagarin Dālbhya was asked by a brāhmaṇa: 'In what direction does the sacrifice extend?' He said: 'It is spread upward following the person.' Thus he spoke. He who knows this sacrifice as extending upward upon the self goes upward, ascending with offspring and cattle. And he who knows it in the reverse direction becomes one turned backwards. For him this bahiṣpavamāna, these ājyas, this midday chant, the pṛṣṭha-sāmans as the back itself, this ārbhava, and the yajñāyajñīya as the firm standing -- he becomes one turned backwards.
1.256: Of this there is this verse: 'What is its front is its rear, and what is its rear is its front. Like the crawling of a snake, I do not know of Śakala which is the front.' Śakala Gaupāyana went about measuring the sacrifice. He indeed could not understand which is the front of the sacrifice, which the rear, which the thinner, which the thicker. On this they say: 'From where is the sacrifice thinnest?' 'From where it is thickest.' 'From where is it thickest?' 'From where it is thinnest.' The sacrifice is thinnest toward the bahiṣpavamāna. This represents the feet. A person is thinnest toward the feet. Yet that is the thickest, for one goes with the feet. 'From where is it thickest?' 'From where it is thinnest.' The sacrifice is thinnest toward the yajñāyajñīya. This represents the head. A person is thinnest toward the head. Yet that is the thickest, for one goes seeing with the eyes. On this they say: 'If the sacrifice were extended upward, the gods alone would live, not humans. If it were extended downward, humans alone would live, not the gods.' The sacrifice is extended both upward and downward, and also sideways. If they should ask, 'In what direction is the sacrifice?' one should say 'upward,' and also 'downward,' and also 'sideways.' One should say 'in all directions.' The sacrifice of one who knows thus extends in all directions. The Kuru-Pañcālas engaged in a theological debate. They came upon a dog lying curled up. They said: 'Let our victory rest upon this.' The Pañcālas asked the Kurus: 'What is this, namely, the sacrifice?' They could not answer. Then Vasiṣṭha Caikitāneya said: 'Just as that one lies having placed this twenty-first yajñāyajñīya upon these nine bahiṣpavamāna verses, so this one lies having joined both ends. This sacrifice-ritual has its form in this manner of lying.' Therefore they maintain this one, though it seems purposeless.
1.257: Twice indeed the yajamāna is born: once from a couple, once from the sacrifice. That which is born from a couple is born for this world. That which is born from the sacrifice is born for the other world -- for the world of the gandharvas, for the world of the gods, for the heavenly world. The yajamāna is the sacrifice; the sacrifice is Soma the king. When they press Soma the king with the pressing stones in the havirdhāna, they make the yajamāna into semen. The udgātṛ is Prajāpati. He is Tvaṣṭṛ, the discharger of semen, the fashioner of forms. When he sings the retasyā at the bahiṣpavamāna, he pours the yajamāna as semen that has come into being. If he were to sing a verse without a sāman, only bone would be born, not flesh. If he were to sing a sāman without a verse, only flesh would be born, not bone. He sings the verse anointed with the sāman. Therefore a person is born covered with hair, skin, and flesh. In it he should not make a hiṃkāra. If he were to make a hiṃkāra, he would sever the semen with the thunderbolt of the hiṃkāra. For even if one were to split it with a hair, one would sever it. He should sing it flowing. If he were to sing it not flowing, offspring would be born without semen.
1.258: He sings a gāyatrī. Prāṇa is the gāyatrī. He interweaves two syllables of it. Thereby he interweaves the prāṇa and apāna. Therefore the prāṇa and apāna, being interwoven, accompany creatures back and forth without ceasing. He should sing it flowing. If he were to sing it not flowing, offspring would be born dead. Indeed, offspring are born dead in the channel of a bad udgātṛ. He sings a triṣṭubh. The eye is the triṣṭubh. He makes two syllables of it flash. Thereby he places the two eyes. Therefore the two eyes are as if yoked together. He should sing it flowing. If he were to sing it not flowing, offspring would be born blind. Indeed, offspring are born blind in the channel of a bad udgātṛ. He sings a jagatī. The ear is the jagatī. He makes four syllables of it flash. Thereby he places the ear -- two ears and two passages. Therefore, being in one place, one hears in all directions. Even when turned away, one hears what is spoken from behind. He should sing it flowing. If he were to sing it not flowing, offspring would be born deaf. Indeed, offspring are born deaf in the channel of a bad udgātṛ. He sings an anuṣṭubh. Speech is the anuṣṭubh. He sings it pushing it toward oneself, for the non-departure of speech. If he were to sing it pushing it away, he would blow away speech; speech would depart from him. By singing it pushing it toward oneself, he establishes speech in the self. He sings its two feet as both articulate and inarticulate. For by the articulate one enjoys speech; the inarticulate is its means of sustenance. He who knows thus enjoys speech and it sustains him. If he were to sing only the articulate, creatures would only speak and never be silent. If he were to sing the inarticulate, creatures would only be silent and never speak.
1.259: He should sing it with force, with rumble, with emphasis. The anuṣṭubh is indeed a meter that strikes. With the anuṣṭubh, with speech as the meter, the gods struck down the asuras. By singing it with force, with rumble, with emphasis -- the hostile rival is indeed evil -- he strikes down the hostile rival, the evil one, and attains prosperity for himself. He should sing it flowing. If he were to sing it not flowing, offspring would be born without tongues. Indeed, offspring are born without tongues in the channel of a bad udgātṛ. He sings a paṅkti. The seasons are the paṅkti. He sings it as an extended gāyatrī. Therefore offspring being born are stretched out. He should sing it flowing. If he were to sing it not flowing, offspring would fall prematurely. Indeed, offspring fall prematurely in the channel of a bad udgātṛ. Now the final tṛca is indeed the birth. With it he generates forth. Mind is the retasyā, prāṇa is the gāyatrī, the eye is the triṣṭubh, the ear is the jagatī, speech is the anuṣṭubh. He who, knowing that mind is the retasyā, sings the udgītha -- a great-minded, mindful one is born from him. He who, knowing that prāṇa is the gāyatrī, sings the udgītha -- he lives a full life. And he who is born from him lives a full life. He who, knowing that the eye is the triṣṭubh, sings the udgītha -- one with an unashamed face, seeing, with good vision, is born from him, one who is handsome.
1.260: Fit for the assembly. For thus the Kuru-Pañcālas knew it. Therefore among the Kuru-Pañcālas, a hero is born together with all heroic qualities. But when the outlying peoples formerly did not know this, therefore among the outlying peoples formerly a hero was not born together with all heroic qualities. But since those who know thus have associated with the outlying peoples and those who know thus perform sacrifices for them, from that time onward among the outlying peoples too a hero is born together with all heroic qualities. The Kuru-Pañcālas engaged in a theological debate. The Pañcālas asked the Kurus: 'What is it that we do in the sacrifice by which a hero is born among us together with all heroic qualities?' They could not answer. By this they were defeated. If they had replied: 'Because those who know thus exist among us, and those who know thus perform sacrifices for us, therefore a hero is born among us together with all heroic qualities' -- a hero is indeed born together with all heroic qualities from him who knows thus.
1.261: The Kurus. The morning pressing is gāyatra, the midday pressing is traiṣṭubha, the third pressing is jāgata. The anuṣṭubh is connected with them all. When, at the morning pressing being gāyatra, he sings a gāyatrī -- brahman is the gāyatrī, brahman is the morning pressing -- he places the brahman in its own seat. When he sings a triṣṭubh -- kṣatra is the triṣṭubh; these are the ornaments of the kṣatra: the elephant, the gold necklace, the mule-chariot, the horse-chariot, the gold plate, the bronze vessel -- he gathers these and anoints the brahman with them. When he sings a jagatī -- the viś is the jagatī; these are the ornaments of the commoners: cattle and horses, elephants and gold, goats and sheep, rice and barley, sesame and beans, ghee, milk, wealth, nourishment -- he gathers these and anoints the brahman with them. The anuṣṭubh is connected with them all.
1.262: Just as one would beat a quiver with arrows, so he gathers all these ornaments and anoints the brahman with them. Therefore they are to be sung apart. For from the time these are sung apart, the brāhmaṇas are being conquered. But when these were not sung apart, the brāhmaṇas were food-eaters. Dwelling in one-village settlements, Satyayajña Pauluṣi used to go about having churned from a single cart, as it were. But from the time these are sung apart, these ornaments are found among the brāhmaṇas. He sings a gāyatrī at the beginning and a gāyatrī at the end. Brahman is the gāyatrī. With brahman he encompasses all these ornaments on both sides. These ornaments, encompassed by brahman on both sides, attend upon him who knows thus. 'They should not be sung apart,' say the Pañcālas. 'These are self-separated, being the yoke-pieces. Pressing by pressing one ascends by singing them apart. But the bad reputation follows them -- they have sung apart!'
1.263: 'Let them stand,' they say. The morning pressing is gāyatra, the midday pressing is traiṣṭubha, the third pressing is jāgata. The anuṣṭubh is connected with them all. When, at the morning pressing being gāyatra, he sings a gāyatrī -- brahman is the gāyatrī -- he gives the brāhmaṇa a share in his own domain, the brahman. That one desires to take it from him. But it is his own, for he gives him a share in his own domain. When he sings a triṣṭubh -- kṣatra is the triṣṭubh -- he gives the kṣatriya a share in the brāhmaṇa's own domain. That one desires to take it from him. But it is his own, for he gives him a share in his own domain. When he sings a jagatī -- the viś is the jagatī -- he gives the vaiśya a share in the brāhmaṇa's own domain. That one desires to take it from him. But it is his own, for he gives him a share in his own domain. The anuṣṭubh is connected with them all. When he sings an anuṣṭubh -- the śūdra belongs to the anuṣṭubh -- he gives the śūdra a share in the brāhmaṇa's own domain. That one desires to take it from him. But it is his own, for he gives him a share in his own domain. If there is no visible protector, one seeks to obtain by simplicity alone. Therefore they should not be sung apart. For from the time these are sung apart, the brāhmaṇas are being conquered.
1.264: The morning pressing is gāyatra, the midday pressing is traiṣṭubha, the third pressing is jāgata. The anuṣṭubh is connected with them all. When, the morning pressing being gāyatra, he sings everything as gāyatra, he draws out a pure portion of the brahman alone. That pure portion of the brahman is drawn out for him. When the midday pressing being traiṣṭubha, he approaches it with the gāyatra, he gives the brāhmaṇa a share in the kṣatriya's own domain. That one wishes to give to him, out of faith, by service and attention. For when faith seizes a kṣatriya, it is a brāhmaṇa he then desires. He gives to him. When the third pressing being jāgata, he approaches it with the gāyatra, he gives the brāhmaṇa a share in the vaiśya's own domain. That one wishes to give to him, out of faith, by service and attention. For when faith seizes a vaiśya, it is a brāhmaṇa he then desires. He gives to him. The anuṣṭubh is connected with them all. When he sings an anuṣṭubh -- the śūdra belongs to the anuṣṭubh -- he gives the brāhmaṇa a share in the śūdra's own domain. That one wishes to give to him, out of faith, by service and attention. For when faith seizes a śūdra, it is a brāhmaṇa he then desires. He gives to him. He makes his own complete; he has a share in the kṣatra; he has a share in the viś. He goes to the intermediate position and is not defeated in the intermediate position. A hundred attendants stand around him, and even more, for one who, knowing thus, does not sing the yoke-pieces apart. If he should sing apart, he should do so knowing one thing. If he should not sing apart, he should not do so knowing the other.
1.265: He sings the retasyā. That pours the semen. That semen, once poured, he raises up with the gāyatrī, with a larger meter. He raises it up with the triṣṭubh, with a larger meter. He raises it up with the jagatī, with a larger meter still. Because he raises it up with progressively larger meters, therefore as one grows, one's vigor increases more and more. With the anuṣṭubh he brings it to completion, with a smaller meter. Therefore in later age one diminishes progressively, as it were. With the paṅkti he brings it to completion. Therefore in the fifth month embryos undergo transformation. With the anuṣṭubh, with speech, he brings it to completion. Therefore even of an old person they listen to the speech. The head of the sacrifice is the bahiṣpavamāna. That is it.
1.266: He sings a gāyatrī. Prāṇa is the gāyatra. The gāyatra is the head. Prāṇa has its seat in the head. He places the prāṇa in its own seat. He sings a triṣṭubh. The eye is the triṣṭubh. He brings it and yokes it to the head. Because he yokes it, the eye is as if yoked. He yokes it being from another seat. Therefore even during life the eye departs. He sings a jagatī. The ear is the jagatī. He brings it and yokes it to the head. Because he yokes it, the ear is as if yoked. He yokes it being from another seat, from the seat of the third pressing. Because he yokes it being from another seat, therefore even during life the ear departs. He sings an anuṣṭubh. Speech is the anuṣṭubh. He brings it and yokes it to the head. Because he yokes it, speech is as if yoked. He yokes it being from another seat, from the seat of the third pressing or from every seat. Because he yokes it being from another seat, therefore even during life speech departs.
1.267: On this they say: 'What is it that is done in the sacrifice by which other vital breaths depart even during life, but not prāṇa?' One should say: 'Because he sings the gāyatrī in its own seat, therefore as long as one lives, prāṇa does not depart.' Now the yoke-pieces have both distinguishings and identifyings. Mind is the retasyā, prāṇa is the gāyatrī, the eye is the triṣṭubh, the ear is the jagatī, speech is the anuṣṭubh. With the mind one distinguishes the well-disposed and the ill-disposed. With the prāṇa one distinguishes the fragrant and the non-fragrant. With the eye one distinguishes the handsome and the ugly. With the ear one distinguishes the pleasant to hear and the unpleasant to hear. With speech one distinguishes the sweet and the unsweet. These are the distinguishings here. He who knows thus becomes distinguished, becomes superior. These same are also the identifyings.
1.268: With his mind one should meditate upon the mind. When the gāyatrī is being chanted, one should meditate upon the prāṇa of whomever one desires with one's own prāṇa. When the triṣṭubh is being chanted, one should meditate upon the eye of whomever one desires with one's own eye. When the jagatī is being chanted, one should meditate upon the ear of whomever one desires with one's own ear. When the anuṣṭubh is being chanted, one should meditate upon the speech of whomever one desires with one's own speech. These are the identifyings. He who knows thus identifies with whomever he desires -- 'Let me identify with this one by this means.' Now he joins together these divine and human yoke-pieces. Mind is the human yoke; the waters are the divine yoke. When the retasyā is being chanted, one should join the mind with the waters. Meanwhile one should direct the yajamāna's mind. Prāṇa is the human yoke; Vāyu is the divine yoke. When the gāyatrī is being chanted, one should join prāṇa with Vāyu. Meanwhile one should direct the yajamāna's prāṇa. The eye is the human yoke; Āditya is the divine yoke. When the triṣṭubh is being chanted, one should join the eye with Āditya. Meanwhile one should direct the yajamāna's eye. The ear is the human yoke; the quarters are the divine yoke. When the jagatī is being chanted, one should join the ear with the quarters. Meanwhile one should direct the yajamāna's ear. Speech is the human yoke; the earth is the divine yoke. When the anuṣṭubh is being chanted, one should join speech with the earth. Meanwhile one should direct the yajamāna's speech. This is how the divine and human yoke-pieces are joined together. Having joined the divine and human yoke-pieces, one crosses beyond that death which is in the heavenly world. He who knows thus does not die again.
1.269: Now this is the joint statement of these great brāhmaṇas -- of Āruṇi, Jīvala Kārīrādi, Aṣāḍha Sāvayasa, and Indradyumna Bhāllaveya. Jīvala Kārīrādi and Indradyumna Bhāllaveya, being fellow students of Āruṇi the teacher, came together. They stayed in Aṣāḍha's house. Aṣāḍha said: 'O Āruṇi, although we studied brahmacarya together and learned together, by what have you surpassed us? You who are so dear to fame, so dear to sight, so dear to hearing -- by what have you attained this?' He said: 'I have meditated upon the yoke-pieces.' 'What have you meditated upon in the yoke-pieces?' 'The dear.' 'He who meditates upon the dear in them, what does he become?' 'He becomes dear to fame, dear to sight, dear to hearing.' 'Such indeed you are,' they said. Then they said to Jīvala Kārīrādi: 'You are the foremost of that half in which you are; even kings attend upon you from below. By what have you attained this?' He said: 'I have meditated upon the yoke-pieces.' 'What have you meditated upon in the yoke-pieces?' 'Prosperity.' 'He who meditates upon prosperity in them, what does he become?' 'He becomes the foremost of whatever half he is in; even kings attend upon him from below.' 'Such indeed you are,' they said. Then they said to Aṣāḍha Sāvayasa: 'You are indeed the village headman of the Śārkarākṣas. By what have you attained this?' He said: 'I have meditated upon the yoke-pieces.' 'What have you meditated upon in the yoke-pieces?' 'Lineage.' 'He who meditates upon lineage in them, what does he become?' 'Wherever he is born among kinsmen, he becomes the headman there.' 'Such indeed you are,' they said. Then they said to Indradyumna Bhāllaveya: 'Your fame goes above and beyond all others; you are one of wide renown. By what have you attained this?' He said: 'I have meditated upon the yoke-pieces.' 'What have you meditated upon in the yoke-pieces?' 'Glory.' 'He who meditates upon glory in them, what does he become?' 'His fame goes above and beyond all others; he becomes one of wide renown.' 'Such indeed you are,' they said.
1.270: They said to Āruṇi: 'You are our teacher. You speak first.' He said: 'I meditate upon the gāyatrī as the dear. Prāṇa is the gāyatrī; prāṇa is the dear. There is nothing dearer than prāṇa. He who thus meditates upon this gāyatrī as the dear -- just as prāṇa itself is dear to the self, so he becomes dear to fame, dear to sight, dear to hearing.' Then Jīvala Kārīrādi said: 'I meditate upon the triṣṭubh as prosperity. Kṣatra is the triṣṭubh; kṣatra is prosperity. He who thus meditates upon this triṣṭubh as prosperity becomes the foremost of whatever half he is in; even kings attend upon him from below.' Then Aṣāḍha Sāvayasa said: 'I meditate upon the jagatī as abundance and progeny. Abundance and progeny are the jagatī among the meters. He who thus meditates upon this jagatī as abundance and progeny attains abundance through offspring and cattle; wherever he is born among kinsmen, he becomes the headman there.' Then Indradyumna Bhāllaveya said: 'I meditate upon the anuṣṭubh as glory. Speech is the anuṣṭubh; speech is glory. Speech causes one's fame to travel above and beyond all others.'
1.271: Those were each thus by only one. But he who knows all these as one -- for all these become one -- becomes the foremost among his own people. On this Vāsiṣṭha Caikitāneya said later, having understood: 'These brāhmaṇas, while deliberating on the yoke-pieces, did not reach that yoke in which all these yoke-pieces are contained.' He spoke this about the retasyā. That which they call the one yoke, that is the retasyā -- the yoke of yokes -- food is that, the adornment of the yoke-pieces. The fragrant scent belongs to the gāyatrī; the handsome appearance to the triṣṭubh; the pleasant to hear to the jagatī. Whatever auspicious thing one says with speech belongs to the anuṣṭubh. He who provides them with food by endowing them with these qualities -- they, being fed, make him an eater of food, the foremost among his own people.
1.272: Three indeed are these oceans, namely the pavamānas: Agni, Vāyu, and yonder Āditya. One should sing the udgītha having covered oneself with them. For when Prajāpati sends rain and food to creatures, they are covered. The udgātṛ is that Prajāpati. When, about to sing the udgītha, he sings having covered himself with the pavamānas, he covers the yajamāna with food; he covers himself; he covers the creatures. 'Where I sing the udgītha, there the rain-god rains abundantly,' Kūṭa Śailana used to say. Where the rain-god rains abundantly, there the bull, the mule, the elephant, the gold necklace, the person are all fine. Indeed every excellent form is born there -- thus he spoke, knowing this. He who separates the gods and humans turns away from evil. The gods are the pavamānas; creatures are the pṛṣṭha and uktha chants. One should sing the udgītha with the pavamānas covered, indirectly, inarticulate. For the gods are as if covered, indirect, inarticulate. Creatures are the pṛṣṭha and uktha chants. One should sing them uncovered, directly, articulate. For humans are as if uncovered, direct, articulate.
1.273: He who sings the udgītha with the pavamānas enters the divine assembly, as it were. Just as one approaching a superior person -- learning, as it were; attending, as it were; bowing down, as it were. One should sing the udgītha with accomplished chants, being able, being supported. Just as one would greet one coming, just as one would give what is asked -- such is that. But he who sings the udgītha with unaccomplished chants, unable, being unsupported -- just as one would rebuff one coming, just as one would not give what is asked -- such too is that. Right here indeed is known both the success and the failure of the sacrifice. The pavamānas are like a river to be crossed. He who enters a river to be crossed unclothed gets smeared with mud or is injured. But he who enters clothed is not smeared with mud and is not injured. Just so the pavamānas should be treated with care. On this they say: 'While other stotras have stotriya-pratipads, why do the pavamānas have no stotriya-pratipads?' One should say: 'The pavamānas are the vital breaths; the pāvamānīs are also vital breaths. If one were to follow the pavamānas with pāvamānīs, one would wipe away the vital breaths in the forward direction.'
1.274: On this they say: 'An unchanted stotra, as it were, is that which is not followed by a stotriya. By what do their stotriya-pratipads become accomplished?' One should say: 'Speech is the anuṣṭubh; in speech all meters are contained. Because of the āgneya, aindra, and pavamāna, their stotriya-pratipads become accomplished.' These stomas called pavamānas are indeed the victorious ones. With the pavamānas the gods went forth conquering the asuras. Because the pavamānas go forward, it is as if one goes forth conquering. When they follow them with the anuṣṭubh -- speech is the anuṣṭubh; speech is the urger -- with speech one says: 'Go forth! Conquer! Advance! Do not retreat!' Just as one would say with speech: 'Go forth! Conquer! Advance! Do not retreat!' -- such is that. The gods are the pavamānas. The stotras belong to the gods. The gods are attached to them. Creatures are the pṛṣṭha and uktha chants. Those are the stotras of creatures. Creatures are attached to them. Because they chant the pavamānas going forward, therefore the gods go forward. The sun goes forward; the moon goes forward; the stars go forward; Agni goes forward burning. But because they chant the pṛṣṭha and uktha chants both forward and backward, therefore creatures too go both forward and backward. They set forth in the morning and return in the evening.
1.275: Pṛthu Vainya asked the divine vrātyas: 'The supreme foundation of the sacrifice, hidden, fashioned from the great atmosphere -- why do the pavamānas go forward? Why do the ukthyas roar back again?' 'The gods follow one path of the sacrifice, while humans live by another. Therefore the pavamānas go forward; therefore the ukthyas roar back again' -- thus they replied. He who knows the gods as having a share among humans, and humans as having a share among the gods, has a share among the gods and a share among humans. He goes to that world where the gods are; the deities do not depart from him. The gods are the pavamānas; creatures are the pṛṣṭha and uktha chants. Sāman is the gods; creatures are the meters. Because the pavamāna-sāmans go only forward, while the ṛc settles in afterwards, thereby the gods have a share among humans. And because in the returning chants, when the ṛc is settling in, the sāman settles in after it, thereby humans have a share among the gods. He who knows this thus has a share among the gods and a share among humans.
1.276: He who distinguishes the form of the gods from the form of humans turns away from evil. The gods are the pavamānas; creatures are the pṛṣṭha and uktha chants. Because they chant the pavamānas in pāvamānī verses only, therefore the gods are of one form, all bright. But because they chant the pṛṣṭha and uktha chants in verses of various forms and various deities, therefore creatures are of various forms -- white, red, and dark. He who thus distinguishes the form of the gods from the form of humans turns away from evil -- he who knows thus. He who knows the portion drawn out from the gods draws out a portion and becomes worthy of having a portion drawn out. The bahiṣpavamāna is indeed the portion drawn out from the gods. With a single hiṃkāra they chant it going forward. Therefore the drawn-out portion goes forward. This is the foremost among the gods. Its drawn-out portion is the creatures. He who knows thus draws out a portion from Prajāpati himself and becomes worthy of having a portion drawn out. Having extracted this essence of the sacrifice and directing it forward to Prajāpati, therefore those about to chant the bahiṣpavamāna go forth as if bent down, as if agitated. For such indeed is the approach to a superior being. Therefore during its chanting one should not speak. For who is worthy of criticizing the serving of a superior? He who criticizes the serving of a superior -- by whatever affliction he wishes to afflict that one, by that he destroys him. But when the adhvaryu then speaks the saṃpraiṣas, having offered the essence of the sacrifice to Prajāpati, he nourishes him.
1.277: He who knows the glory among the gods and the glory among humans attains glory among the gods and glory among humans. The pavamānas are the glory among the gods. They are the prāṇas. They make him known among the gods. The returning chants are the glory among humans. They are the apānas. They make him known among humans. This is the glory among the gods and the glory among humans. He who knows this glory among the gods and glory among humans attains glory among the gods and glory among humans. He who knows the measured and the unmeasured -- the measured and the unmeasured become abundant for him. The gods are the pavamānas; creatures are the pṛṣṭha and uktha chants. Sāman is the gods; creatures are the śastras. That is the same thing. These are all the gods, namely the stotras. Because the stotras are measured, therefore the gods are measured: eight Vasus, eleven Rudras, twelve Ādityas. But because the śastras are unmeasured, therefore creatures are unmeasured. We do not know how many brāhmaṇas, how many rājanyans, how many vaiśyas, how many śūdras there are. This is the measured and the unmeasured. The measured and the unmeasured become abundant for him who knows thus. 'Those going forward they call the one going backward; those going backward they call the one going forward. Indra, and what you and Soma have done -- these carry like yoked steeds from the pole of the chariot of space.' The pavamānas go forward. Their camasas face backward. They are the prāṇas. Therefore one can breathe in and out. The returning chants face forward. Their camasas face backward. They are the apānas.
1.278: He who knows the precedence of the pressings attains precedence and does not fall from precedence. The gāyatrī is the precedence of the morning pressing. When any other meter is employed there, it is called gāyatra. The triṣṭubh is the precedence of the midday pressing. When any other meter is employed there, it is called traiṣṭubha. The jagatī is the precedence of the third pressing. When any other meter is employed there, it is called jāgata. This is the precedence of the pressings. He who knows this precedence of the pressings attains precedence and does not fall from precedence. He who knows the houses of the gods becomes a householder and obtains houses. The meters are the houses of the gods. The gāyatrī has eight syllables; there are eight Vasus. The Vasus are householders through the gāyatrī. The triṣṭubh has eleven syllables; there are eleven Rudras. The Rudras are householders through the triṣṭubh. The jagatī has twelve syllables; there are twelve Ādityas. The Ādityas are householders through the jagatī. The Viśve Devas are householders through the anuṣṭubh. Indrāgnī through the pressings. He who knows these houses of the gods becomes a householder and obtains houses.
1.279: On this Heyapi Saumāpa used to say: Whether one sacrifices oneself or has another sacrifice for one -- he who knows how, just as at a great tīrtha one would satisfy cattle without crowding them with sand, so all the deities at each pressing in the meters, syllable by syllable, are satisfied without crowding -- he should know this. The gāyatrī has eight syllables; there are eight Vasus. The morning pressing is gāyatra, belonging to the Vasus. They are satisfied at the morning pressing in the meters, syllable by syllable, without crowding. The triṣṭubh has eleven syllables; there are eleven Rudras. The midday pressing is traiṣṭubha, belonging to the Rudras. They are satisfied at the midday pressing in the meters, syllable by syllable, without crowding. The jagatī has twelve syllables; there are twelve Ādityas. The third pressing is jāgata, belonging to the Ādityas. They are satisfied at the third pressing in the meters, syllable by syllable, without crowding. The Viśve Devas are through the anuṣṭubh.
1.280: When one who knows thus sacrifices or has another sacrifice performed, the gods have no power over him. Whether or not they come, whether they are about to partake or not, they come nonetheless. If he is pure, they partake. If impure, they merely look on. Yet even that is valuable -- that they look on. He who knows the vessel of the gods becomes the vessel-bearer among his own people. The brāhmaṇa is the vessel of the gods. He who knows thus becomes the vessel-bearer among his own people. Just as one desires to drink from a clean, pure vessel, so the gods desire to drink from a clean, pure brāhmaṇa.
1.281: Prajāpati created the gods. Death, as evil, followed after them. The gods, approaching Prajāpati, said: 'Why did you create us if you were going to send death, as evil, after us?' He told them: 'Gather the meters together. Enter them according to their proper seats. Then you will separate yourselves from death, from evil.' The Vasus gathered the gāyatrī. They entered it. It concealed them. The Rudras gathered the triṣṭubh. They entered it. It concealed them. The Ādityas gathered the jagatī. They entered it. It concealed them. The Viśve Devas gathered the anuṣṭubh. They entered it. It concealed them. The Maruts gathered the paṅkti. They entered it. It concealed them. The Sādhyas and Āptyas gathered the atichandas. They entered it.
1.282: Indrāgnī entered the pressings. Then death, as evil, could not discern them. How could death as evil have power over one whom it cannot discern? Death as evil does not find him who knows thus. The meters concealed them from death as evil. Because the meters concealed them from death as evil, that is the meter-ness of the meters. The meters conceal from death as evil him who knows thus. Whatever meter one who knows thus employs in performing priestly functions, in that he is then sheltered. By whatever meter they criticize him while performing, from that he is cut off. There is no affliction whatsoever for him who knows thus. He who criticizes him comes to grief. Then Ūrja Jānāyana asked Kapivana Bhauvāyana: 'Since the morning pressing is gāyatra, the midday pressing traiṣṭubha, the third pressing jāgata, what is this anuṣṭubh that, unfailing, approaches the openings of the pressings and carries the sacrifice to its end?' He said: 'Speech is the anuṣṭubh; from speech the sacrifice is woven. Just as one would keep returning and yoke again and again a good, strong bull tied with a rope for heavy or contrary labor, so they yoke this speech, the anuṣṭubh, at the openings of the pressings for the yoking. The openings of the pressings are as if contrary. This speech, unfailing, carries the sacrifice to its end.' He who knows the anuṣṭubh as being everywhere -- everywhere does merit accrue to him. The gāyatrī has eight syllables, the triṣṭubh eleven syllables, the jagatī twelve syllables; 'vāk' is the nidhana of the yajñāyajñīya. That makes thirty-two syllables -- an anuṣṭubh.
1.283: Now Keśī Dārbhya besieged Ahīnasa Āśvatthi from the position of purohita of Keśin Sātyakāmin. Ahīnasa was the elder; Keśī was younger. He said: 'O Keśin, knowing what have you brought me a rājanya?' He said: 'We meditate upon the anuṣṭubh as all the meters, upon the bṛhatī as cattle, as the sacrifice, as the heavenly world.' That moved him to tears. He was overcome with emotion. He said: 'Do not be moved. If you are moved because of the rājanya, here is your rājanya. We shall seek another rājanya.' He said: 'It is not because of the rājanya, O Keśin. It is the memory of brahmacarya that brought me to tears. For I practiced brahmacarya. We have heard this as if from old age -- that this young boy proclaimed it thus.' It was from this memory that tears came. That he said the anuṣṭubh is all the meters: the gāyatrī has eight syllables, the triṣṭubh eleven syllables, the jagatī twelve syllables; 'vāk' is the nidhana of the yajñāyajñīya. That makes thirty-two -- an anuṣṭubh of thirty-two syllables. This is what he meant by calling the anuṣṭubh all the meters. 'The bṛhatī as cattle' -- for the anuṣṭubh is the bṛhatī. It became the bṛhatī when installed among four-footed cattle. 'As the sacrifice' -- for he who is wealthy in cattle, the sacrifice approaches him. 'As the heavenly world' -- for the bṛhatī is the heavenly world. Then Saṃgamana Kṣaimi asked Satyayajña Pauluṣi, being sent by his teacher Āruṇi: 'O Satyayajña Pauluṣi, when the gāyatrī has been praised, the triṣṭubh is being praised, and the jagatī is unpraised, how do all of them, coming together, raise up the midday pressing?' He could not answer. By this he defeated him.
1.284: When the meters divided these worlds, the gāyatrī claimed this world, the triṣṭubh the atmosphere, and the jagatī the yonder world. Brahman is the gāyatrī, kṣatra is the triṣṭubh, viś is the jagatī. This triṣṭubh, in this atmosphere, was not satisfied with this meager production. She pierced the gāyatrī from the front. She heated her. The gāyatrī was afraid: 'She will carry away my seat.' She said to her: 'Homage to you! With what desire do you pierce me?' 'Give me a tribute,' she said. She gave her two eight-syllable feet. Then she crept up to the jagatī. She pierced her. She heated her. The jagatī was afraid: 'She will carry away my seat.' She said to her: 'Homage to you! With what desire do you pierce me?' 'Give me a tribute,' she said. She gave her a twelve-syllable foot. Then she crept back to this gāyatrī again. The gāyatrī said: 'I have given you a tribute. What more do you want?' 'Give me your entire self,' she said. 'Then what will remain for me?' 'Our food shall be the same. I shall place you in front.' 'So be it.' She placed her in front. She gave her her entire self. Because the gāyatrī gave her entire self, therefore a brāhmaṇa approaches a kṣatriya with his entire self. And because the jagatī did not give her entire self as it were, therefore the viś seems to move away from the kṣatra. Whatever the gāyatrī and jagatī gave to each other -- that became this bṛhatī. Therefore the bṛhatī has three eight-syllable feet and one twelve-syllable foot. This gāyatrī is yoked first: 'uccā te jātam andhasā.' She is at the end of the purohita's recitation.
