1.285: Then the bṛhatī, then the triṣṭubh. Thus the food is encompassed on both sides by brahman and kṣatra. The food, encompassed on both sides by brahman and kṣatra, attends upon him who knows thus. Therefore a brāhmaṇa should not harm a kṣatriya, nor a kṣatriya a brāhmaṇa. For their food is the same. Therefore when a brāhmaṇa attains greatness, it is a kṣatriya he seeks as a second protector of that food. When a kṣatriya, it is a brāhmaṇa. In the beginning the meters had four syllables and were incapable of carrying the sacrifice. Now, Soma was in the third heaven of Indra. The Agni-Gandharvas guarded him -- they are the fire-hearths; they are the venomous serpents. He was in golden cups. They would swoop down in the blink of an eye. These meters said: 'Let us fetch Soma; let us extend the sacrifice.' The jagatī flew up first, thinking herself the strongest, mightiest, most abundant, most powerful. From her, as she went, the Soma-guardians took three syllables. She returned with one syllable, bringing dīkṣā and cattle. Therefore whoever is wealthy in cattle is inclined toward dīkṣā. Then the triṣṭubh flew up. From her, as she went, the Soma-guardians took one syllable. She returned with three syllables, bringing tapas and dakṣiṇās. Therefore in the realm of the triṣṭubh, dakṣiṇās are given. Therefore they consider as performing tapas one who gives. Then the gāyatrī flew up. The anuṣṭubh, her mother, watched after her.
2.66: One should attain him (the sacrificer) together with his desires. Indeed, one who knows thus, even while undergoing diksha, attains all desires. Therefore, during the consecrations, one should not serve the king (Soma). For whatever observance he (the consecrated one) maintains, these worlds maintain that same observance, the metres maintain that observance, and Soma the king maintains that observance. Then he says: 'bhur bhuvah svah -- may I be rich in nourishment through nourishments, rich in heroes through heroes, rich in offspring through offspring.' Thereby he pronounces this unlimited benediction at the end: 'May tapas be my foundation.' Thereby he makes tapas his foundation. 'May shraddha be my imperishability' -- thereby he makes shraddha his imperishability. 'May satya be my householder' -- for he (the sun) is satya; he who shines yonder is satya. He is the uninjured deity. Thereby he makes this uninjured deity his householder. He is indeed of good observance, for if he were malicious, he would burn all this. Therefore the householder should be of good observance. If one should practise the observance of an unconsecrated person, or speak evil or blameless evil, or have sexual intercourse, or do anything falsely, he should worship this very Aditya with all these recitations. Thenceforth he is indeed consecrated. He, however, is at present unconsecrated. But whatever glory was in him when he was consecrated, that same glory is in him even now. They say: 'He who knows the distinguishing mark of the glory of the consecrated one -- he should either undergo diksha today or cause another to undergo diksha.' The black antelope skin is indeed the glory of the consecrated one, for it is the form of all the Vedas. The white parts are the form of the samans, the black parts are the form of the rcs -- or else the reverse. Those that are brownish-tawny are the form of the yajus formulas. Therefore, even if one wears it unseen, people desire to see him.
2.67: Those who enter the waters wearing the black antelope skin — who are cut off from this glory of the consecrated — they, having flooded their entire self, rise up together with evil. When the avabhṛtha-iṣṭi is concluded, then, having removed the black antelope skin, he should sprinkle it with water, saying: 'May I not be separated from indriya, from pre-eminence, from excellence.' Then he gives it to whomever he considers worthy of the gift. Then he sips water: 'You are the avabhṛtha of enjoyment, you are the avabhṛtha of eating, you are the avabhṛtha of what has been eaten.' Whatever impurity from the pressing (ṛjīṣa) remains attached here, this is its enjoyment. Then he enters the waters. For the waters are a downward refuge. He takes refuge in this downward refuge for the non-pursuit of evil. If the waters should flow, he should bathe against the current, saying: 'May they carry away my evil.' But all waters do flow. Those that are stationary and flow downward — they carry his evil downward. The husband and wife rub each other's backs. The waters are the vajra. With the vajra they strike away all evil. And they thereby perform the paired coupling for procreation, for the sake of progeny.
2.68: He says: 'O Unnetr, lead me up.' Vishnu is the Unnetr; the sacrifice is Vishnu. The sacrifice thus leads him up, freeing him from all evil. He rises up reciting: 'Up from darkness, beholding the higher light, we have reached the god among gods, Surya, the highest light.' This is the highest light -- he who shines yonder. He is the uninjured deity. Grasping this uninjured deity, he rises up. They make fresh, green, leafy udumbara logs as firewood. Having gone, he places a log on the ahavaniya, saying: 'You are fuel; may we prosper.' After placing it, he stands worshipping: 'I have followed the waters; we have mingled with their essence; I have come, O Agni, with nourishment -- unite me with splendour.' In the same way, the wife places a log on the garhapatya and stands worshipping. Taking the ajya vessel with the sruc ladle, going north of the agnidhra and the sadas, and sitting down west of the garhapatya, having placed the black antelope skin on her lap and taken four portions of ajya, she offers: 'May Agni bestow upon me indriya, pre-eminence, excellence -- svaha.' Whatever glory was in him when consecrated, that same glory is in him when he has risen. That black antelope skin he should not discard; he should make it a ladle-rest, or a board for grinding the offering, or store it away for a future consecration. This is the Kaishini diksha.
2.69: Mṛtyu and Prajāpati, when they sacrificed, competed through their sacrifices. At that time the implements of sacrifice were like the implements at present used in iṣṭis. That which is chanted (stuti) at the sacrifice, that which is recited (śastra), that which is performed (pracaryate) — that was Prajāpati's army. But that which is sung to the vīṇā, that which is danced, that which is done as idle pastime — that was Mṛtyu's army. Their two armies were equal — as great as one's, so great was the other's. They continued for a long time without gaining victory, for many years. Prajāpati desired: 'May I conquer Mṛtyu.' He saw this concord (saṃpad) in the sacrifice — this enumeration (saṃkhyāna). With that he conquered Mṛtyu. When he was defeated, his royal glory faded. He (Mṛtyu) retreated and took refuge in the patnīśālā (wives' hall).
2.70: Now, what was the yupa (sacrificial post) -- that became the staff of the vina. What were the ropes (rashana) -- those became the strings (upavana) of the vina. What was the dronakalasha (Soma vessel) -- that became the resonating body (suna) of the vina. What was the pressing skin (adhishavana carman) -- that became the leather of the vina. What were the pressing stones' supports (uparava) -- those became the hollow spaces (akasha) of the vina. What were the pressing stones (gravana) -- those became the plectra (vadana) of the vina. What were the seven metres increasing by four (syllables) -- those became the seven strings (tantri) of the vina. What were the stomas of the dasharatra -- those became the ten fingers (anguli). They say: 'There is no longer any rivalry, for the second sacrifice (that of Mrtyu) lost its glory. There is only one sacrifice.'
2.71: Now, regarding the enumeration itself. Of this year there are seven hundred and twenty day-and-nights (ahoratra). Those make eighty-nine (sets). And of its pressings (savana) there are one thousand and eighty-three. That many are the pavamana chants. This is celebrated in the verse: 'Eight times ninety are the day-and-nights of the year; one thousand and eighty-three are the pressings and all the pavamanas.' And of its stuta-shastras there are six thousand and sixty-two. And of its dhishniya fires that are distributed, there are ten thousand, eight hundred and thirty. This is celebrated in the verse: 'Six thousand and sixty-two are the stuta-shastras over the months, six; ten hundred, ten thousand, eight hundred dhishniyas in the year, and thirty.'
2.72: That many are the twinkling stars, that many are the lights, that many are the hymns of praise. He attains these twinkling stars, attains these lights, attains these hymns of praise, who knows thus. And of its stotriyas there are ninety thousand, three hundred and ninety-six. This is celebrated in the verse: 'Ninety thousand are his stotriyas, and three hundred, and ninety and six; praised by these, Prajapati goes forth, clothing himself in what has been and what will be, the universe.' And of its syllable-feet (pada) there are thirty-four thousand, seven hundred and fifty. This is celebrated in the verse: 'Four thousand, thirty ayutas, seven hundred, fifty feet -- such is the middle divine measure of the year praised by the sages.'
2.73: And of its syllables (akshara) there are one ayuta, sixty prayutas, two niyutas, nine (further) and seven thousand -- ten times ten. This is celebrated in the verse: 'One ayuta, sixty prayutas, two niyutas, nine, and seven thousand, ten times ten -- such is the supreme self of Prajapati.' This enumeration is infinite. This enumeration is immortal. This enumeration is well-done (sukrta). But what is without enumeration -- that is mortal. Therefore one should indeed enumerate. This is the army of the gods -- the enumeration. With a human army one strikes down him whom one wishes to overcome. So here one should strike down him whom one wishes to overcome with the divine army. Now there are three paths -- well-made, leading to the gods: the path of syllables, the path of feet, and the path of rcs. The wise ones carry the brahmana on these, where Mrtyu becomes his food. This is celebrated in the verse: 'Three paths, well-made, divine: of syllables, of feet, and of rcs. The wise carry the brahmana on these, where Mrtyu becomes his food -- licking the immortal syllable by syllable. Going to heaven from the divine sattra, the sages, having pursued action through tapas, having conquered death for the day, go departing. Knowing, burning, they go, destroying grief. When the wise ones, wingless, fly to where the immortal is found and death is not -- there the wise sages dwell.' The priests who know thus carry the sacrificer to worlds, cattle, and desires through their sacrifices. He enjoys stuta-shastra in the sacrifices; imperishable are his priests -- the adhvaryu, the hotr, and the samaga. For him the metres flow as honey-streams. He enjoys stuta-shastra in the sacrifices; imperishable are his priests -- the adhvaryu, the hotr, and the samaga.
2.74: Each single one of these syllables stands immersed (pibdamāna) in as much vital sap, food, honey, and immortality as this earth is great — in desire upon desire. Thus a brāhmaṇa who knows this lives for thousand upon thousand divine ages. When one (merit) is exhausted, he passes on to another, and then that other is again replenished. Therefore people wish to give to a brāhmaṇa who knows thus, thinking: 'Let us obtain from him vital sap, food, honey, immortality, desire upon desire.' Just as one might buy a plot of land or a land-measure, even so one buys a world from a brāhmaṇa who knows thus. And just as in a purchase the fields revert to the landowner, so the worlds revert to the world-possessor. Even if a brāhmaṇa who knows thus were to accept the entire filled earth, a single syllable of his would be greater.
2.75: Janaka of Videha performed a sacrifice with great dakshinas (fees). The brahmanas of the Kuru-Pancalas came together for it. Having penned a thousand cows, he said: 'Brahmanas, let the most learned in brahman among you drive these away.' Vajasaneya (Yajnavalkya) said: 'Drive these toward me, dear friend.' They said to him: 'Are you then the most learned in brahman among us?' He said: 'Homage to the most learned in brahman. We are simply desirous of cows.' They said: 'Who will question us?' He (Vidagdha) said: 'I, Vidagdha Shakalya.' They put him forward and went. Seeing him approach, (Yajnavalkya) said: 'Have the brahmanas made you a firebrand-remover, Shakalya?' He said: 'If we are a firebrand-remover, we shall indeed question you.' He asked: 'How many gods are there, Yajnavalkya?' He said: 'Three and thirty, and three and three hundred, and three and three thousand -- as many as are invoked in the nivid.'
2.76: 'How many gods are there really?' 'Three.' 'Yes,' he said. 'How many gods are there really?' 'Two.' 'Yes,' he said. 'How many gods are there really?' 'One.' 'Yes,' he said. 'Which are the three and thirty, and the three and three hundred, and the three and three thousand?' 'Those are merely their powers (mahiman),' he said. 'There are indeed just thirty-three.' 'Which are the thirty-three?' 'Eight Vasus, eleven Rudras, twelve Adityas, and Indra and Prajapati -- (these make) thirty-three.' 'Which are the Vasus?' 'Agni, Prthivi, Vayu, Antariksha, Aditya, Dyaus, Candrama, and the Nakshatras -- these are the Vasus. For in these all wealth (vasu) is deposited. Therefore they are the Vasus.' 'Which are the Rudras?' 'The ten pranas in a person,' he said. 'The self (atman) is the eleventh. When they depart and leave, they cause weeping (rodayanti). Therefore they are the Rudras.' 'Which are the Adityas?' 'The twelve months are the year,' he said. 'These are the Adityas. For they go along taking up (adadana) all this. Therefore they are the Adityas.' 'Which are the three?' 'These worlds alone.' 'Which are the two?' 'Day and night.' 'Which is Indra and which is Prajapati?' 'Speech itself is Indra; mind is Prajapati.' 'Which is the one deity?' 'Prana (breath).' He (Yajnavalkya) said: 'You have asked me beyond the deity that should not be questioned further. You are mortal before even having received hospitality. Your limbs will not even reach home.' And indeed it was so. He died accordingly. His pallbearers carried away the limbs along the road, mistaking them for something else. Therefore one should not speak ill (of a knower).
2.77: Brahman and Subrahman were in these worlds -- in this realm. Then Subrahman departed upward. That Aditya yonder is brahman; and speech itself is subrahman. The gods enclosed brahman here on the altar, within the vedi, through the sacrifice. Even now brahman is enclosed on the altar, within the vedi, through the sacrifice. They ask: 'Since other priestly functions are performed within the vedi, how is it that the Subrahmaiya is performed outside the vedi?' One should reply: 'The utkara (refuse heap) is the self of the vedi; the Subrahmaiya is the manifest brahman. When he approaches the utkara and then calls the Subrahmaiya, thereby it is made to be within the vedi.' He calls 'Subrahmanyom, Subrahmanyom, Subrahmanyom' three times. Speech is the Subrahmaiya. Thus he first lays hold of this speech. And speech is brahman; the essence of speech is the omkara.
2.78: 'First comes prana, then speech,' they say. But that is not so. Just as if one were to pour honey and then sprinkle parched grains over it -- that would be something different. Such is this. Therefore one should call simply 'Subrahmanyom.' 'Indra, come!' -- thereby he invites Indra to the sacrifice. 'O possessor of the Hari steeds, come!' -- 'Come with your two Haris,' is what he says to him. Prana and apana are his two Haris; they carry (harati) all this. Also day and night are his two Haris; they carry all this. 'O Medhatithi, (in the form of) a ram' -- for Medhatithi, having become a ram, drank the king (Soma). 'O Vrshanashva, (in the form of) a woman (mena)' -- for having become the wife of Vrshanashva, Maghavat (Indra) dwelt in his family. 'O leaper upon the cow (gaura)' -- for thence, having become a gaura bull, he leapt into the ocean. 'O lover of Ahalya' -- for he was the lover of Ahalya Maitreyi. 'O Kaushika brahmana, O you called Kaushika' -- for when he fought the great battle with the Asuras, he set aside the Vedas. He learned them again from Vishvamitra.
2.79:
This was devised by Āruṇi of good renown. Therefore one should not disregard his tradition. Therefore one should call: 'O Kauśika brāhmaṇa, O you called Kauśika.' 'O gods, O brāhmaṇas, come, come!' — thereby he invites both the gods and the brāhmaṇas. Those who are thus invited desire good will toward him. Therefore, having invited someone, one should not fail in one's promise, lest he speak ill of one. They ask: 'What is the metre of the Subrahmaṇyā?' One should say: 'Triṣṭubh, for triṣṭubh belongs to Indra.' They ask: 'What is the deity of the Subrahmaṇyā?' One should say: 'It belongs to Indra, for by it one invites Indra.' Or one may say: 'It belongs to the Viśve Devāḥ, for by it one invites all the gods.' They ask: 'Since other priestly functions have stotras and śastras, where is the stotra of the Subrahmaṇyā, where is its śastra?' One should say: 'All that is ṛc, sāman, and yajus is in this very one. Thereby the Subrahmaṇyā has a stotra and a śastra.' They ask: 'What is the Subrahmaṇyā like — is it like an ṛc, or like a sāman, or like a yajus?' One should say: 'It is neither an ṛc, nor a yajus, nor a sāman. It is like everything. For brahman is like everything.' The Subrahmaṇyā that he calls when the king (Soma) is bought, and this one that he calls standing at the utkara while the dakṣiṇās are being led away — these are the same. Also, what was the outward journey (prāyaṇa), that would be the return journey (udayana). And: 'Indra has come — let me not be silenced.' This is a cow with five calves, five-footed. For what they say — 'animals belong to groups of five (pāṅkta)' — this is that. Some call it according to the deity: all-Agni for the agniṣṭut, all-Indra for the indrastoma, all-Viśvedeva for the vaiśvadeva, undefined for the undefined. But that is not always so, for there is no restriction on brahman. One should call it as it naturally falls — one should call it as it naturally falls.

[2.79 continued = 2.80 preamble] Now this is the Upaśada sacrifice. Kaśyapa desired: 'May offspring and cattle settle close to me; may I be abundantly born with offspring and cattle.' He saw this sacrifice, brought it, and sacrificed with it. Thereupon offspring and cattle settled close to him, and he was abundantly born with offspring and cattle. Offspring and cattle settle close to him, and he is abundantly born with offspring and cattle, who knows thus. Its bahiṣpavamāna has the trivṛt (stoma). The trivṛt is deficient. Deficiency is procreation. 'May that which is procreation cause me to procreate.' Its stomas are increasing by one. 'For progeny from uninterrupted procreation.' Since in this one a single stotriyā each is added (upaśīyate), therefore it is called Upaśada. Its stomas are of both kinds — odd and even. This is the divine pair (mithuna), the procreative one — that the stomas are of both kinds. 'May that divine pair, that procreation, cause me to procreate.' Both bṛhat and rathantara are its two sāmans. This is the divine pair, the procreative one — that both bṛhat and rathantara (are used).
2.80:
Its two prescribed samans in the pavamanas are the two 'birth-chants' of Vasishtha (vasisthasya janitre). This is the divine pair, the procreative one -- the two birth-chants of Vasishtha. 'May that divine pair, that procreation, cause me to procreate.' Its agnishtomasaman is the savimsha (twenty-fold). That is the viraj. Food is viraj. In the viraj, in food, the sacrifice is finally established. They say: 'The stoma has reached the viraj, which is not procreative; one should not sacrifice with this.' But others say: 'It is indeed procreative. For food is viraj. From what is eaten, seed is shed. From seed, offspring are born. Therefore it is procreative.' Or they say: 'One should make the acchavaka's (chant) eighteen-fold, the arbhava twenty-fold, and the agnishtomasaman twenty-one-fold.' For the twenty-one-fold stoma establishes itself at the end of the stotras in a well-known manner.

[Continued:] Now this is the Shada sacrifice. Aditya (the Sun) desired: 'May I, shaking off all evil, ascend to the heavenly world.' He saw this sacrifice, brought it, and sacrificed with it. Thereupon he, shaking off all evil, ascended to the heavenly world. He shines yonder, free from evil. Shaking off all evil, one ascends to the heavenly world, who knows thus.
2.81: Now this is the Punastoma. This rshi, having come, was unable to rise, because of demonic food. Poison (gara) had entered him -- either because he had betrayed a friend (Mitra) or because he had eaten the food of enemies. He said to Indra: 'I cannot rise.' He (Indra) saw this sacrifice, brought it, and caused him to sacrifice with it. Its morning pressing (pratahsavana) has the trivrt, and its third pressing (trtiyasavana) has the trivrt. The trivrt is deficient; from the deficient, (evil) drips away. With these two trivrt pressings, just as one might wash a leather bag by tying it on both sides, so he washed away all his evil. From here upward and from here downward he struck away evil. He strikes away evil upward from here and downward from here, who knows thus. But he had been so completely emptied that he could not rise. He considered: 'How shall I restore this one?' He caused him to sacrifice again with this very rite. Thereby he restored him. That is its quality of being a Punastoma (re-chanting). Its madhyamdina savana (midday pressing) has the twelve-fold (stoma). Twelve are the months, and the months make the year. Through the year he restored his emptied self from the middle. He became as if he had never been drained. Just as a child with two sets of teeth would have vitality, so his vitality was restored. He who thinks himself poisoned, or one who has accepted what should not have been accepted, or one who has eaten the food of one whose food should not be eaten -- he should sacrifice with this.
2.82: Its prstha is the brhat. This world is the rathantara; yonder world is the brhat. This (world) is near, as it were; that (world) is drained, as it were -- it is for (restoring) the draining. On the verses 'pra yo ririkse ojase' (he who would empty himself for strength), the gara-brahmasaman is performed -- for the removal of poison. But others say: 'Those wish-fulfilling (cows) that existed -- they were seized and brought by means of this very sacrifice.' Its morning pressing has the trivrt; its third pressing has the trivrt; its midday pressing has the twelve-fold. The jagati has twelve syllables. Cattle are the jagati; cattle are the wish-fulfilling ones. With brahman (the trivrt), he seized them from both sides and brought them. One of them, Shravana, was milking. They brought her down to the earth. People seek her with a plough of wood -- the vish (commoners) seek her through trade, the brahmans seek her through brahmacarya (studentship), the kshatra (warriors) seek her through service to the king. The three that remained -- those they made into the uktha chants. This sacrifice is an ukthya. This is indeed the first among the ukthya sacrifices; thereafter the agnishtomas follow in succession. He who desires cattle should sacrifice with this. In that case, both brhat and rathantara should be used. For cattle are of two kinds: some belong to the rathantara, others to the brhat. Those animals that have teeth on one side only belong to the rathantara; those with teeth on both sides belong to the brhat. This is for obtaining both kinds of cattle. It is an ukthya, for cattle are the uktha chants -- for the obtaining of cattle.
2.83: Now these are the Viraj and Svaraj (sacrifices). The gods desired: 'May we obtain the viraj, food; may we be established in the viraj, in food.' They saw this viraj sacrifice, brought it, and sacrificed with it. Thereupon they obtained the viraj, food, and were established in the viraj, in food. One obtains the viraj, food, and is established in the viraj, in food, who knows thus. He is indeed a viraj (sovereign ruler) who does not covet another's world. By this sacrifice one conquers that (coveted world). Its ten stotras have the trivrt; these make three virajes and two with the fifteen-fold. That is viraj. These make four virajes altogether. Food is viraj -- for the obtaining of viraj, food. Its prstha is the rathantara. This world is the rathantara. This world is viraj. He who encompasses her the most, he rules (virajati). Food is viraj -- for the obtaining of viraj, food. Its prescribed saman in the midday pavamana is 'indus samudram urviya vibhati' -- the saman of the earth.
2.84: They also desired: 'Having obtained the viraj, food, may we go to the world of svaraj (self-rule).' They saw this Svaraj sacrifice, brought it, and sacrificed with it. Thereupon, having obtained the viraj, food, they went to the world of svaraj. Having obtained the viraj, food, one goes to the world of svaraj, who knows thus. He is indeed a svaraj (self-ruler) who does not covet another's world. By this sacrifice one conquers that. Its ten stotras have the trivrt; these make three virajes. The fifteen-fold and the seventeen-fold make two. That is anushtubh. The anushtubh has indeed attained the svaraj world among the metres. Prajapati is anushtubh. Prajapati is svaraj. Food is viraj. Established in the viraj, in food, they went to the world of svaraj. Established in the viraj, in food, one goes to the world of svaraj, who knows thus. Its prstha is the brhat. Yonder world is brhat. Yonder world is svaraj. By this they went to yonder world of svaraj. By this one goes to yonder world of svaraj, who knows thus. Its prescribed saman in the arbhava pavamana is not 'indus samudram urviya vibhati'; rather the saman of heaven, with a single syllable (ekarava), is used. For yonder world is as if nearer. They thereby complete the stoma in its proper form.
2.85: Now this is the Rshabha sacrifice. Indra desired: 'May I be the bull (rshabha) of all creatures; may I attain bull-hood.' He saw this sacrifice, brought it, and sacrificed with it. Thereupon he became the bull of all creatures and attained bull-hood. One becomes the bull among one's own people and attains bull-hood, who knows thus. For the bull is prosperity (shri). He thereby attained prosperity. The bull is the king among men. The bull (rshabha) is the bull among cattle. The stallion (ashvavrsha) is the bull among mares. The he-goat (basta) is the bull among goats. The ram (vrshni) is the bull among ewes. The black antelope (krshna) is the bull among spotted deer. The rshya deer is the bull among the rohita deer. These are the forms of prosperity. One attains prosperity, who knows thus. It has the fifteen-fold (stoma). The vajra is fifteen-fold. The vajra is Indra. This sacrifice belongs to Indra; it is made with his characteristic mark. Its midday (pressing) has the twenty-one-fold. That is its summit (kakubh). Those who are pre-eminent in these worlds are the summits. Agni is the summit of this world, Vayu of the atmosphere, Aditya of heaven. He becomes one of the summits, who knows thus. Its prstha is the brhat. Indra is brhat. The prsthas are prosperity. Thereby they complete Indra with prosperity. Its two prescribed samans in the pavamanas are 'rshabhah pavamana' and 'rshabho vaidanvata.'
2.86: Now this is the Vyoman sacrifice. The gods desired: 'May we be vyoman (space/heaven); may we go to the vyoman world.' They saw this sacrifice, brought it, and sacrificed with it. Thereupon they became vyoman and went to the vyoman world. One becomes vyoman and goes to the vyoman world, who knows thus. He is indeed vyoman who does not covet another's world. By this sacrifice one conquers that. It has the seventeen-fold (stoma). Prajapati is seventeen-fold. Prajapati is vyoman. 'Through vyoman may we become vyoman; through vyoman may we go to the vyoman world.' Both brhat and rathantara are its samans. Neither of these two alone is vyoman. Both together are vyoman. 'Through vyoman may we become vyoman; through vyoman may we go to the vyoman world.' Its arbhava pavamana has the twenty-one-fold, or the agnishtomasaman. Twelve months, five seasons, these three worlds, and yonder Aditya -- that is twenty-one. He is indeed vyoman. Those gods who are on this side of Prajapati -- he is their (vyoman). 'Through vyoman may we become vyoman; through vyoman may we go to the vyoman world.' Its two prescribed samans in the pavamanas are 'arko devanam parame vyoman' and 'arkasya devah parame viyoman.'
2.87: Now these are the Udbhid and Valabhid (sacrifices). One desiring offspring should sacrifice with both of these. Their stomas are the trivrt and pancadasha -- having seven and seventeen. The deficient and the excess are a pair (mithuna), procreative. One is deficient, the other is in excess. From a deficient-and-excess pair, offspring and cattle are born. That makes procreation. Therefore one desiring offspring should sacrifice with both. But others say: 'One desiring brahmavarcas should sacrifice with both.' For one of these attains six gayatris and the other attains six. Brahman is the gayatri. These two sacrifices have attained the gayatri. Therefore one desiring brahmavarcas should sacrifice with both. Others say: 'One desiring long life should sacrifice with both.' For prana is the gayatri. These two sacrifices have attained the gayatri. Therefore one desiring long life should sacrifice with both. Others say: 'One desiring splendour (tejas) should sacrifice with both.' For tejas is the gayatri. These two sacrifices have attained the gayatri. Therefore one desiring splendour should sacrifice with both. Others say: 'One desiring heaven should sacrifice with both.' For the six gayatris make four brhatis. The brhati has attained the heavenly world. Therefore one desiring heaven should sacrifice with both. Others say: 'One desiring cattle should sacrifice with both.' For the six gayatris make three jagatis. Cattle are the jagati.
2.88: The gods saw these two — the Udbhid and Valabhid. With them they pressed forward. With the Valabhid they broke open the cave (vala). With the Udbhid they released the cows. The Valabhid has the trivṛt and pañcadaśa (stomas) with the rathantara as its sāman, and utsedha as its brahmasāman. The Udbhid has the sapta and saptadaśa with the bṛhat as its sāman and niṣedha as its brahmasāman. Just as a sharp spike or a pointed stake — such is the trivṛt stoma. Just as one would pierce with a spike or sharp point and then strike with a mallet — so with this trivṛt stoma they pierced the cave (vala), and with the fifteen-fold (pañcadaśa) vajra they broke it open. Or they say: 'The trivṛt stoma is Agni. The fifteen-fold is the vajra. Just as one would heat a stone with fire and then split it with a mallet, so with this trivṛt-Agni-stoma they heated the cave and broke it with the fifteen-fold vajra.' The (cows) stood inside, finding no path. They pushed them up with the utsedha. Now this Udbhid has the sapta-saptadaśa. Of these, the seven are the seven kinds of cattle, and the saptadaśa is Prajāpati, the seventeen-fold. Seeing their father Prajāpati, they came forth rejoicing. Having come forth, they spread far and wide. The gods were afraid of losing them. Having pushed them up with the utsedha and restrained them with the niṣedha, they kept them within themselves. He who desires cattle should sacrifice with both of these. With the Valabhid one breaks open the cave; with the Udbhid one releases the cows.
2.89: Now these are the Satrājit and Pṛtanājit (sacrifices). The gods and the Asuras competed over Prajāpati, the year, and over these worlds. The gods desired: 'May we conquer the army (pṛtanā).' They saw this Pṛtanājit sacrifice, brought it, and sacrificed with it. Thereupon they conquered that army. Because they conquered the army (pṛtanā), that is the Pṛtanājit's quality of being Pṛtanājit. One conquers the army and the hostile rival, who knows thus. It has the twenty-four-fold (stoma). Twenty-four half-months make the year. From the year they conquered them. Its pṛṣṭha is the rathantara. This world is the rathantara. From this world they conquered them. Its twelve stotras correspond to the twelve months of the year. Month by month they conquered them from the year. Its twenty-four stuta-śastras correspond to the twenty-four half-months of the year.
2.90: Those (Asuras), defeated, took refuge with the twenty-five-fold — with their father Prajāpati and with yonder world. (The gods) desired: 'Let us now conquer them completely (satrā).' They saw this Satrājit sacrifice, brought it, and sacrificed with it. Thereupon they conquered them completely. Because they conquered completely (satrā), that is the Satrājit's quality of being Satrājit. One completely conquers the hostile rival, who knows thus. It has the twenty-five-fold (stoma). Twenty-four half-months make the year. Prajāpati is the twenty-five-fold. From the year and from Prajāpati they conquered them. Its twelve stotras correspond to the twelve months of the year. Month by month they conquered them from the year. Its twenty-four stuta-śastras correspond to the twenty-four half-months. Successively, half-month by half-month, they conquered them from the year. Thereupon the gods prevailed and the Asuras were defeated.
2.91:
Others say: 'Prajāpati, desiring offspring, saw this Pṛtanājit sacrifice, brought it, and sacrificed with it.' Its stotriyas number two hundred and eighty-eight (2 × 88 × 100?). That is deficient. From deficiency comes procreation, for a woman is deficient by two (compared to a man) — from that she procreates. That makes procreation. It has the twenty-four-fold. Twenty-four half-months make the year. The year is procreation. That makes procreation. Its twelve stotras correspond to the twelve months of the year. The year is procreation. That makes procreation. Its twenty-four stuta-śastras correspond to the twenty-four half-months. The year is procreation. That makes successive procreation. One is born abundantly with offspring and cattle, who knows thus.

Prajanya (the rain-god) desired: 'May these offspring of mine have food.' He saw this Satrājit sacrifice, brought it, and sacrificed with it. It has the twenty-five-fold. Twenty-four half-months make the year. The twenty-five-fold is food. Through the twenty-five-fold he gave them food. Its stotriyas number three hundred. That is virāj. Food is virāj. Thereby he gave them food. His offspring became possessed of food. He who desires: 'May I be born abundantly with offspring and cattle; may these offspring of mine have food' — he should sacrifice with both of these.
2.92: Now these are the Viraj and Svaraj. Two brothers should sacrifice with this rite. Those who desire: 'May one of us attain kingship, and the other self-rule (svarajya)' -- this sacrifice attains both the viraj and the anushtubh. Kingship is viraj. Self-rule is anushtubh. Through viraj the younger one attains kingship; through anushtubh the elder attains the world of self-rule. Both brhat and rathantara are its samans. Kingship is rathantara. Self-rule is brhat. Through rathantara the younger one attains kingship. Through brhat the elder attains the world of self-rule. Its stotras include three with the trivrt, three with the pancadasha, three with the saptadasha, and three with the ekavimsaha. Through the trivrt the younger one attains kingship; through the pancadasha the elder attains the world of self-rule.
2.93: They say: 'This sacrifice is for one who already is (bhūta), not for one who desires to become; for the stoma reaches the virāj and the anuṣṭubh, which are non-procreative. The virāj is a complete, full metre, like something already accomplished. And the anuṣṭubh is the end of the metres.' But others say: 'This sacrifice is for both — for one who already is and for one who wishes to become.' Its stomas are trivṛt-pañcadaśa and saptadaśa-ekaviṃśa — deficient-and-excess, paired, procreative. One is deficient, the other in excess. From a deficient-and-excess pair, offspring and cattle are born. Trivṛt and pañcadaśa — that is a procreative pair. Saptadaśa and ekaviṃśa — that is a procreative pair. 'May that procreative pair cause me to procreate.' And since both bṛhat and rathantara are its sāmans — that is the divine pair, the procreative one: both bṛhat and rathantara. 'May that divine pair, that procreation, cause me to procreate.' Through this sacrifice, procreation is most abundantly attained when it reaches the virāj. For food is virāj. From what is eaten, seed is shed. From seed, offspring are born.
2.94: The opening verse to be used is 'yuvam hi stha svahpati.' This is the opening verse for two who sacrifice together. But others say the opening verses should be 'pavasva vaco agriya' and 'upasmai gayata nara.' These are indeed the opening verses for two who sacrifice together. For here both samans are performed. Therefore these two opening verses should be used. In this sacrifice they arrange these samans: the gaushukta, ashvasukta, havishmate, and havishkrte. Those samans that come in pairs are the samans for two who sacrifice together. The viraj-svaraj is placed in the anushtubh of the arbhava pavamana. For this sacrifice is the Viraj-Svaraj.
2.95: Now this is the Jyeshtha sacrifice (sacrifice of pre-eminence). He who desires pre-eminence (jyaishthya) should sacrifice with this. This sacrifice attains eight times the twenty-one-fold. Twelve months, five seasons, these three worlds, and yonder Aditya -- that is twenty-one. This is the foremost pre-eminence. 'May he who is the foremost pre-eminence lead me to foremost pre-eminence.' Its pavamanas have the seventeen-fold. Prajapati is seventeen-fold. Prajapati is the foremost pre-eminence. 'May he who is the foremost pre-eminence lead me to foremost pre-eminence.' Its stotras increase progressively. They lead him to ever-increasing glory, to the ever-higher heavenly world. Its prstha is the brhat. Prosperity (shri) is brhat; pre-eminence is brhat. Yonder world is brhat; yonder is pre-eminence. Thereby they establish him in the middle, in prosperity, in pre-eminence. Its twelve stotras correspond to the twelve months of the year. The year is the foremost pre-eminence. 'May he who is the foremost pre-eminence lead me to foremost pre-eminence.' Its twenty-four stuta-shastras correspond to the twenty-four half-months. The year is the foremost pre-eminence. 'May he who is the foremost pre-eminence lead me to foremost pre-eminence.'
2.96: Now this is the Durasha sacrifice. Having sacrificed with the full-moon offering, he offers a caru (rice dish) to Aditya. He gives therein a gold piece of a hundred manas to the Sun in the forenoon. He gives therein a silver piece of a hundred manas to the Moon in the afternoon. Thus he sacrifices during the two halves of the day -- the forenoon and afternoon -- during this latter half of the month. On the new-moon day, the diksha takes place, followed by the pressing. This is the course (ayana) of the Sun and Moon. The Sun and Moon desired: 'May we through the same sacrifice attain the same prosperity. May one of us conquer the day, and the other the night.' They saw this sacrifice, brought it, and sacrificed with it. Thereupon through the same sacrifice they attained the same prosperity. One of them conquered the day, the other the night. This equal prosperity of theirs consists in that he sacrifices in the same world, in both forenoon and afternoon. The forenoon is day, the afternoon is night -- for conquering both day and night. He sacrifices in both the bright and dark fortnights. The bright fortnight is day, the dark fortnight is night -- for conquering both day and night. The (gifts) are of a hundred manas each.
2.97: The stoma is seventeen-fold. Prajapati is seventeen-fold. Prajapati is the one who leads to the heavenly world. 'May he lead me to the heavenly world.' Its dakshina consists of a hundred oxen. A man lives a hundred years, has a hundred powers, a hundred strengths -- for the attainment of that power and strength. They are all draught-oxen, for with a draught animal one goes wherever one wishes to go. They are all choice ones, for the heavenly world is choice, as it were -- for the attainment of the heavenly world. One person gives this; another should give seventeen times seventeen nishkas (gold coins) at each stotra. The gods and the Asuras competed in the sacrifice. Whatever the gods did in the sacrifice, the Asuras did the same. The gods said: 'Whatever we do in the sacrifice, the Asuras imitate. Let us sacrifice with the Gold-Soma.' At that time gold existed only among the gods, not among the Asuras. The gods had seized the Asuras' gold. They sacrificed with the Gold-Soma. At each stotra they gave seventeen times seventeen nishkas. The Asuras could not match this. They said: 'The gods have done something hard to match (durasha).' That is the Durasha's quality of being Durasha. Thereupon the gods prevailed and the Asuras were defeated. One prevails oneself, and his hostile rival is defeated, who knows thus. Its two prescribed samans in the pavamanas are the Shukra and Candra, for this sacrifice belongs to the Sun and Moon.
2.98: Now this is the Apaciti sacrifice (the sacrifice of honour/reverence). Prajapati created offspring. Once created, they did not honour him. He desired: 'May I attain honour (apaciti) among these offspring.' He saw this sacrifice, brought it, and sacrificed with it. Thereupon they honoured him. And Indra -- the gods did not honour him. He ran to Prajapati: 'My gods do not honour me.' (Prajapati) assigned to him this Apaciti sacrifice. He brought it and sacrificed with it. Thereupon the gods honoured him. And Darbha Shataniki -- the Pancalas did not honour him even though he was their king. Even boys would call him 'Darbha, Darbha.' He had these two brahmanas: Ahina Ashvatthi and Keshin Satyakami. They came to him. He sat down, pondering. They said to him: 'What is the prince pondering?' He said: 'I have reason to ponder. The Pancalas do not honour me though I am their king. Even boys call me Darbha, Darbha. That is what I ponder.' They said to him: 'There is a sacrificial rite called the Apaciti. Let us perform it for you.' 'So be it.' They performed it for him.
2.99: Of this sacrifice, the morning pressing attains the gayatri, the midday pressing attains the trishtubh, the third pressing attains the jagati. Since the morning pressing attains the gayatri -- the gayatri has eight syllables, eight are the Vasus -- through the gayatri metre, Prajapati and Indra attained honour among the Vasu gods at the morning pressing. Since the midday pressing attains the trishtubh -- the trishtubh has eleven syllables, eleven are the Rudras -- through the trishtubh metre, Prajapati and Indra attained honour among the Rudra gods at the midday pressing. Since the third pressing attains the jagati -- the jagati has twelve syllables, twelve are the Adityas -- through the jagati metre, Prajapati and Indra attained honour among the Aditya gods at the third pressing. The gayatri has eight syllables, the trishtubh eleven, the jagati twelve. 'Vak' is the nidhanam of the yajnayajniya. That makes thirty-two. The anushtubh has thirty-two syllables. The anushtubh belongs to the Vishve Devah. Through the anushtubh metre, Prajapati and Indra attained honour among the Vishve Devah at the third pressing. These are all the gods. These are all the classes of gods.
2.100: The brahmana belongs to the gayatri. The kshatriya belongs to the trishtubh. The vaishya belongs to the jagati. The shudra belongs to the anushtubh. Since the morning pressing attains the gayatri -- through the gayatri metre Darbha Shataniki attained honour among the brahmana class of men. Since the midday pressing attains the trishtubh -- through the trishtubh metre Darbha Shataniki attained honour among the kshatriya class of men. Since the third pressing attains the jagati -- through the jagati metre Darbha Shataniki attained honour among the vaishya class of men. The gayatri has eight syllables, the trishtubh eleven, the jagati twelve. 'Vak' is the nidhanam of the yajnayajniya. That makes thirty-two. The anushtubh has thirty-two syllables. The shudra belongs to the anushtubh. Through the anushtubh metre Darbha Shataniki attained honour among the shudra class of men. These are all the classes of men. These are all the human groups.
2.101: They ask: 'What is the support (vidhṛti) of this sacrifice, and what is its honour (apaciti)?' One should reply: 'The fact that from one pressing to the next it does not remain the same — since a different pressing arises each time — that is the support and the honour of this sacrifice.' They say the opening verse should be 'davidyutatyā ruce.' This is a leading (gaṇinī) opening verse, of many forms. 'Groups (gaṇa), as it were, follow one who attains prosperity.' But others say the opening verses should be 'pavasva vāco agriya' and 'upāsmai gāyatā nare' — these are the leading opening verses of many forms. For here both sāmans are performed. Therefore these are the opening verses to be used. Both bṛhat and rathantara are used. Brahman is rathantara; kṣatra is bṛhat. Both brahman and kṣatra are the form of honour. The Bhadra and Śreyasī are used — for through bhadra (auspiciousness) the gods became auspicious, and through śreyas (excellence) they attained prosperity. The Saha and Mahasī are used — for through saha (force) the gods overcame the Asuras, and through mahas (greatness) they became great. The Bharga and Yaśasī are used — for through bhargas (splendour) the gods became splendid, and through yaśas (glory) they attained glory. The Utsedha and Niṣedha are used — for through utsedha the gods pushed up what was to be raised, and through niṣedha they restrained what was to be restrained. The Sadoviśīya and Viśoviśīya are used — for through sadoviśīya the gods settled among the people, and through viśoviśīya they became kings of every community. The Dharma and Vidharmaṇī are used — for through dharma the gods became dharma, and through vidharman they struck away what was contrary to dharma. In this sacrifice, two-footed atichanda (hypermetric) verses are used — both the longest and the shortest metres. Cattle are the metres. Therefore cattle of both kinds — the largest and the smallest — attend upon the prosperous one. Cattle of both kinds attend upon him, who knows thus. Its dakṣiṇā is a chariot drawn by four horses. All the horses are worth a hundred. He (the sacrificer) wears a gold necklace (niṣka) and a trembling ornament (prāvepa), all ornaments; he is radiant. His cover is of tiger skin, his shield of leopard skin, his quiver of bear skin. The one who mounts it wears sword-armour, fully armed, with an armed charioteer, surrounded, with two guards posted. The charioteer wears a niṣka and a garland. This is the form of splendour; this is the form of honour. In this sacrifice he gives wealth that commands honour. 'May I, honoured, give honoured wealth.'
2.102: Now these are the Abhibhu (the overpowering) and the Vinuitti (the expelling). The gods and Asuras competed. The gods desired: 'May we overpower these Asuras.' They saw this Abhibhu sacrifice, brought it, and sacrificed with it. Thereby they overpowered the Asuras. Because they overpowered (abhyabhavan), that is the Abhibhu's quality of being Abhibhu. One overpowers the hostile rival, who knows thus. Its two outer stomas are trivrt. Brahman is trivrt. Through brahman they overpowered them. Through brahman one overpowers the hostile rival, who knows thus. Those (Asuras), defeated, took refuge with the thirty-three-fold, with their father Prajapati. As one might go around the greatest, most excellent fortress -- thus they desired: 'Let us now expel them.' They saw this Vinuitti sacrifice, brought it, and sacrificed with it. Thereupon they expelled them. Because they expelled (vyanudanta), that is the Vinuitti's quality of being Vinuitti. One expels the hostile rival, who knows thus. Its two middle stomas are trivrt. Brahman is trivrt. Through brahman they expelled them.
2.103: Others say: 'The trivrt stoma is Agni. Just as one would burn assembled enemies with fire, so with this trivrt-Agni-stoma they scorched them, crushing them from both sides with the two thirty-three-folds.' They (the Asuras), scattered, crept toward this (world), saying: 'Let us approach this one.' Thereupon the gods prevailed and the Asuras were defeated. One prevails oneself, and his hostile rival is defeated, who knows thus. Others say: 'One desiring the office of purohita should sacrifice with the Abhibhu.' Its two outer stomas are trivrt, and the two middle are thirty-three-fold. Brahman is trivrt; kshatra is thirty-three-fold. Through brahman he encompasses kshatra on both sides. When brahman encompasses kshatra, one attains the office of purohita. He attains the purodha; they place him at the front. Others say: 'One desiring to be an intimate counsellor should sacrifice with the Vinuitti.' Its two middle stomas are trivrt, and the two outer are thirty-three-fold. Brahman is trivrt; kshatra is thirty-three-fold. Through kshatra he encompasses brahman on both sides. When kshatra encompasses brahman, one stands in the intimate position. He holds the intimate position and is not dislodged from it.
2.104: Of this Abhibhu, the two outer stomas are trivrt and the two middle are thirty-three-fold. Their stotriyas number sixty-six. Of these, sixty are the seat (asandi). The four are the legs of the seat. The two represent the sacrificer himself -- he is two-footed, enthroned upon this royal seat. He attains kingship, who knows thus -- either in this world or in the other. Others say: 'He whose two trivrats are in the middle -- that is the seat.' For a seat that is curved in the middle is, as it were, depressed in the middle. Like a seat depressed in the middle, he is (seated). And a seat that is curved in the middle is more comfortable. He attains kingship, who knows thus -- either in this world or in the other. Others say: 'These two sacrificial rites are like a return journey.' For six stomas rise upward in each. With six he goes upward to the heavenly world; with six he descends back from there. He goes to the heavenly world and stands again in this world. And he knows: 'He who I was there, I am this one here' -- for he is born again in this world.
2.105: Of these two, the trivrats are together, the pancadashas together, the saptadashas together, the ekavimshatas together, the trinavas together, the trayastrimshatas together. Just as one walking would step on two feet at a time, even so one is established in the heavenly world through these two. Others say: 'These two sacrificial rites are meant for those seeking investigation (anvishti).' He who would sacrifice with a year-long or non-year-long fire-altar (agnicayana) should sacrifice with these two. Instantly his year becomes investigated -- season by season, month by month, half-month by half-month. Since the stomas of the six-day prsthya cycle are used -- the prsthas are the seasons; the seasons are the year -- thereby season by season. Since there are twelve stotras -- twelve months make the year -- thereby month by month. Since there are twenty-four stuta-shastras -- twenty-four half-months make the year -- thereby half-month by half-month.
2.106: Now this is the Manustoma. Manu desired: 'May I be born abundantly with offspring and cattle; may I create the races of Manu (manavi prajah).' He saw this sacrifice, brought it, and sacrificed with it. Thereupon he was born abundantly with offspring and cattle. He created these races of Manu. He who desires: 'May I be born abundantly with offspring and cattle; may I create the races of Manu' -- he should sacrifice with this. Its stomas follow the six-day prsthya cycle. The prsthas are the seasons. The seasons are the year. The year is procreation. 'May that which is procreation cause me to procreate.' Its stomas are of both kinds -- odd and even. That is the divine pair, the procreative one -- that the stomas are of both kinds. 'May that divine pair, that procreation, cause me to procreate.' Its stomas are interwoven (vyatishakta). From an interwoven pair, offspring and cattle are born -- for the sake of progeny. Both brhat and rathantara are its samans. That is the divine pair, the procreative one -- that both brhat and rathantara (are used). 'May that divine pair, that procreation, cause me to procreate.' Its pavamanas have the chandoma (metre-characterized) samans. Cattle are the chandoma. Cattle are procreation. 'May that which is procreation cause me to procreate.' Its brahmasaman is the Manava. Brahman is the brahmasaman. The brahmasaman serves the desire of the sacrificer. In its own seat they satisfy the sacrificer with desires. It is an ukthya, for offspring and cattle are the uktha chants. Offspring and cattle are procreation.
2.107: Others say: 'This is indeed one of the rites for those seeking investigation.' He who would sacrifice with a year-long or non-year-long fire-altar should sacrifice with this. Instantly his year becomes investigated -- season by season, half-month by half-month, month by month, night by night. Since the stomas follow the six-day prsthya cycle -- the prsthas are the seasons; the seasons are the year -- thereby season by season. Since there are fifteen stotras -- fifteen are the nights of a half-month -- thereby half-month by half-month. Since there are thirty stuta-shastras -- thirty are the nights of a month -- thereby month by month. Since there are three hundred stotriyas -- so many are the nights of the year -- thereby night by night. Instantly his year becomes investigated, who knows thus. Prajanya desired: 'May I go to the heavenly world.' He saw this second Manustoma, brought it, and sacrificed with it. Its stomas follow the six-day prsthya cycle -- the prsthas are the seasons; the seasons are the year; the year is the heavenly world -- for the attainment of the heavenly world. Its stomas ascend progressively -- for the heavenly world is above -- for the attainment of the heavenly world. Both brhat and rathantara are its samans -- they are heavenly -- for the attainment of the heavenly world. Its pavamanas have the chandoma samans -- cattle are the chandoma; cattle are the heavenly world -- for the attainment of the heavenly world. Its brahmasaman is the twenty-five-fold (pancavimsha). Food is the twenty-five-fold. The brahmasaman serves the desire of the sacrificer. In its own seat they satisfy the sacrificer with food. It is an ukthya, for offspring and cattle are the uktha chants.
2.108:
Others say: 'This is indeed one of the rites for those seeking investigation.' From there (i.e. from the ukthya) they proceed into the night. Through the atirātra the gods went upward to the heavenly world. Having reached the heavenly world, they remained all together, unsettled. Not knowing the directions, they desired: 'May we know the directions.' For the heavenly world is beyond day and night. They saw these four stotras above the night. They chanted with them. Having chanted with them, they knew the directions. Thereupon, wherever they wished in the heavenly world, they settled. Wherever one wishes in the heavenly world, one settles, who knows thus — so said Jānaśruteya.

But Vaitahavya said: Prajāpati created cattle. Once created, they ran away from him. He tried to stop them with the agniṣṭoma. They ran past that. He tried to stop them with the ukthya. They ran past that. He tried to stop them with the ṣoḍaśin. They ran past that too. He pursued them with the paryāyas, circuit by circuit. Because he went circuit by circuit (paryāya), that is the paryāyas' quality of being paryāya. With the Āśvina rite he enclosed them. Of those enclosed, just as small fish might slip through between the meshes — so the small cattle slipped through. He desired: 'Having seized them, may I keep them in myself.' He saw these four stotras above the night. He chanted with them. Having thereby seized them, he kept them within himself. Because he seized and kept (āptva ayacchat), therefore it is called Aptoryāma.
2.109: The stotriyas number sixty-two. Of these, sixty are the viraj. Cattle are the viraj. The two represent the sacrificer himself -- he is two-footed, encompassed from beyond. This is for the guarding of cattle, so cattle do not fall away. His wealth is not carried off, who knows thus. Others say: 'This is a single stotra that is divided into four: six (stotriyas) are chanted for the Hotr, six for the Maitravaruna, six for the Brahmanacchamsin, six for the Acchavaka. But the Hotr alone should do the recitation.' These make twenty-four stotriyas. Twenty-four half-months make the year. The year is Prajapati. Prajapati is encompassed from beyond -- for the guarding of cattle, so cattle do not fall away. His wealth is not carried off, who knows thus. Others say: 'The stomas should be simply trivrt, pancadasha, saptadasha, ekavimsaha.' For all sacrificial rites conclude with the twenty-one-fold. And the twenty-one-fold is the firm, established stoma. Therefore the twenty-one-fold stoma should be used at the end.
2.110: On (the verse) 'abhi tva vrshabha sutah' -- that is the Arshabha. 'Abhi' is the form of the rathantara. Thereby they do not depart from the form of the rathantara. 'Yad adya kac ca vrtrahan' -- the Shakala with himkara is the form of the vamadeya. Thereby they do not depart from the form of the vamadeya. 'Tam indram vajayamasi' -- the Edavasu-nidhana, with the nidhanam outside (bahirnidhana), is the form of the brhat. Thereby they do not depart from the form of the brhat. And cattle are wealth (vasu) -- for the obtaining of cattle. Having seized them as 'this wealth,' he approaches. 'Idam vishnur vicakrame' -- this is the samkshara of the ida. Cattle are the samkshara of the ida -- for the obtaining of cattle. This is what calls them: 'Come, Ida! Come, Ida!' Cattle are Ida. Therefore these additional uktha chants should be used. But Tandya said: 'Prajapati created cattle. Once created, they departed from him. He said to Agni: "Restrain these for me." He did not restrain them with the Jarabodha saman. He said to Indra: "Restrain these for me." He did not restrain them with the Satrasahiya. He said to Ishana Deva: "Restrain these for me." He did not restrain them even with the Margiyavena. He said to Vishnu: "Restrain these for me." He restrained them on the Vaishnavi (verses) with the Varavantiya itself. Because he restrained (avarayata), that is the Varavantiya's quality of being Varavantiya.'
2.111: Now this is the Gosava sacrifice. One desiring heaven should sacrifice with this. It has the thirty-six-fold (stoma). The brhati has thirty-six syllables. The brhati is the heavenly world, imperial sovereignty (samrajya). Established in the brhati, in the heavenly world, in imperial sovereignty, they proceed. The brhati pervades all these worlds with its syllables: with ten syllables it pervades this world, with ten the atmosphere, with ten yonder world, with four the directions, with two the day and night. 'As she pervades these worlds, so may I pervade these worlds.' At each stotra, four trivrats are attained. Other stomas attain the trivrt; the trivrt is the heavenly world. Just as with a swift four-yoked horse one would go wherever one wishes -- so one goes to the heavenly world with this. Both brhat and rathantara are its samans -- they are heavenly, for the attainment of the heavenly world. It is an ukthya, for offspring and cattle are the uktha chants; offspring and cattle are the heavenly world -- for the attainment of the heavenly world. Its dakshina consists of twelve thousand (cows). Twelve months make the year; the year is the heavenly world -- for the attainment of the heavenly world. Its observance (vrata) is: he should go to his mother, go to his sister, go to a woman of his gotra (clan). He should drink water lapping it up; he should tear grass with his teeth. Wherever excrement overtakes him, there he should void it. He conquers the world of the bull (anadvan). Janaka of Videha wished to sacrifice with this. The brahmanas sat around him. He asked: 'What is the stoma?' Sudakshina said: 'The Kshaimas say it is simply the trivrt.' 'What is its observance, what is its dakshina?' He told him that. He (Janaka) said: 'I can venture the dakshina, but I cannot venture the observance.' Therefore he did not dare to sacrifice with it. Punyakesha Yaudheni, a Shaibya king, sacrificed with it. Excrement overtook him right in the assembly hall. Having just risen, he said: 'This was indeed an old man's sacrifice. Only an old man should sacrifice with this rite, for everything is permitted to an old man.' This is indeed the Old Man's Sacrifice.
2.112: Now this is the Vivadha sacrifice (the carrying-yoke sacrifice). One desiring cattle should sacrifice with this. It attains six panktis and ten stotriyas. The six panktis are cattle. The ten stotriyas are the milking of cattle -- that is their milk. These two (groups of) five make two pankti groups of five. And those are the cattle. Its morning pressing has the twenty-fold, the midday pressing has the ten-fold, the third pressing has the twenty-fold. Just as a carrying-yoke (vivadha) is heavy on both sides -- such is it, for the midday pressing has the ten-fold, which is the carrying-yoke's quality. In the twenty-folds on either side, the front half and the rear half receive the larger share. Therefore cattle provide the larger share with their front half and rear half. They carry with the front half; they are milked and procreate with the rear half. One obtains both -- the milk of cows and the milk of oxen -- who knows thus. In this case, both brhat and rathantara should be used, for cattle are of two kinds: some belong to the rathantara, others to the brhat. Those with teeth on one side only belong to the rathantara; those with teeth on both sides belong to the brhat -- for obtaining both kinds of cattle. It is an ukthya, for cattle are the uktha chants -- for the obtaining of cattle.
2.113: Now this is the Sadyahkri (same-day purchase). Those (priests) who had been chosen did not withdraw. They came and performed the sacrifice. For them they brought this speech -- a white mare, manifest, yoked with horses -- (saying): 'Accept this.' They said: 'This is better than us; we will carry this up.' She, angered (that they did not accept her), became a two-faced lioness and rose upward, saying: 'They did not accept me.' She stood between both gods and Asuras, seizing whomever she reached of the gods and whomever of the Asuras. The gods addressed her from one side, the Asuras from the other. As she was turning toward the gods, she said: 'What would be mine if I were to turn toward you?' 'The first offering before Agni shall reach you.' They assigned her a place in the sacrifice. She is this uttaravedi (high altar).
2.114: They uphold Agni; they set up the uttaravedi. Thereby the first offering of Agni reaches her (the uttaravedi). Then for these (priests) they brought this Aditya -- a white horse, manifest, yoked with horses -- (saying): 'Now accept this.' They said: 'This one too is better than us; we will carry this one up.' The gods and the rshis came together and said: 'Sacrifices without dakshinas will be cut off. Accept this.' This is proclaimed in the verse: 'The Adityas, O singer, brought the horse as dakshina to the Angirases. That, O singer, they did not bring back. That, O singer, they did not accept.'
2.115:
'Ādityas, Rudras, Vasus implored: "Accept this gift, O Aṅgiras. This gift is great and broad. May the gods give you a boon. May that be well-understood by you. May it be yours day by day. Accept it back!"' — thus they made them accept it. Thereupon the Ādityas first went to the heavenly world; the Aṅgirases were left behind.

He who has a hostile rival, who is bewitched, who is challenged — he should sacrifice with this. His evil and his rival are left behind; he goes to the heavenly world. The altar is a barley field. For it is already prepared. The uttaravedi is of barley. With barley the Ādityas joined (ayuvata) the sacrifice of the Aṅgirases. Because they joined the sacrifice with barley (yava), that is the barley's quality of being barley. With barley one yokes (joins) the sacrifice of the hostile rival.

The sacrificial post is a khalevala (pillar), pointed like a sphya, belonging to Indra — for conquering. Together with the animal offerings they achieve completeness. The Soma-purchasing (animal) is a male bull — for a male is strength: 'May we have strength.' It is a breeding bull (sāṇḍa) — for having Indra's quality. The vasatīvarī waters are from a pot, from the house of the one who was born first. For those are already collected. Having eaten food with ghee, he lies down. Whatever that consecration-food is that he eats — that is his food eaten. He lies between the thighs of his dear wife, without lying down (fully). That is his austerity. He lies down with gold placed in his mouth. Gold is truth. Thereby truth is spoken for him.
2.166: Its pṛṣṭha is the vāmadevya, which is svāra. They arrange all the sāmans as svāra. The svara is indeed prāṇa. By means of the prāṇas the udgātṛ thereby breathes out the prāṇas of the sacrificer. On this they say: at the stotra of the yajñāyajñīya, one should lie down behind the gārhapatya, wrapping oneself to the right. From there one goes to the heavenly world. But on this they also say: that would be a disruption of the sacrifice, if one were to lie down before its completion. Only when all the ritual acts have been completed, when they would move about freely -- then one should lie down behind the āhavanīya, wrapping oneself to the right. From there one goes to the heavenly world. That sun yonder is the āhavanīya. He is indeed the heavenly world.
2.167: Now this is the manustoma. Manu and Yama, the two sons of Vivasvat, were there. Manu desired: 'May the gods grant me sovereignty for this world, and Yama for the other world.' He saw this sacrifice. He brought it. He sacrificed with it. Thereupon the gods granted him sovereignty for this world, and Yama for the other world. This sacrifice has the sadaśa stoma. The sadaśa is indeed a pair of tongs. Just as one would seize something with tongs and carry it away, so with these tongs he seized him and carried him from this world to the other world. Its pṛṣṭha is the rathantara. The rathantara is indeed the vajra. With the vajra he struck him forth from this world to the other world. He who has a rival, he who would practice sorcery, he who would compete -- he should sacrifice with this. With the vajra he thereby strikes his rival forth from this world to the other world.
2.168: Of Jamadagni: the seven-day rite called vidratham, 'the eyes.' Those sāmans which have the character of sorcery -- those are they. They complete the stoma with their corresponding form. But they also say: one desiring food should sacrifice with this. Everyone else indeed exceeds the virāj in chanting. But he who chants with ten does not exceed the virāj. The virāj has ten syllables. The virāj is food. It is for the securing of food from the virāj. Its pṛṣṭha is the rathantara. The rathantara is indeed food -- for the securing of food. In it they arrange these sāmans: the arka, the arkapuṣpa, and the dairghaśravasa -- those sāmans which have the form of food, those are they.
2.169: Now this is the gāyatra. In it, the trivṛt is in the morning pressing, the pañcadaśa is the ājya of the acchāvāka, the saptadaśa is the midday pressing, and the ekaviṃśa is the third pressing. The sacrifice is ukthya, with both sāmans. One desiring the office of purohita should sacrifice with this. That the trivṛt is in the morning pressing and the pañcadaśa is the ājya of the acchāvāka—the trivṛt is brahman, the pañcadaśa is kṣatra; thus he places brahman before kṣatra. Therefore the brāhmaṇa is the purohita of the kṣatriya. And thereby he makes kṣatra follow in the path of brahman. The saptadaśa is the midday pressing. The saptadaśa is the viś. This purohita is indeed a second king. He who attains prosperity reaches the lordship and sovereignty of the viś. 'May I attain the lordship and sovereignty of the viś'—therefore the saptadaśa is the midday pressing.
2.170: Its pṛṣṭha is the rathantara; the bṛhat is the brahmasāma. The rathantara is indeed brahman. The bṛhat is kṣatra. Thus he places brahman before kṣatra. Therefore the brāhmaṇa is the purohita of the kṣatriya. And thereby he makes kṣatra follow in the path of brahman. It is ukthya. The ukthāni are indeed the viś. This purohita is indeed a second king. He who attains prosperity reaches the lordship and sovereignty of the viś. 'May I attain the lordship and sovereignty of the viś'—therefore it is ukthya. And thereby he makes the viś a yoke-companion for both brahman and kṣatra. They become ekaviṃśa. Twelve months, five seasons, three worlds, and that sun yonder—ekaviṃśa is the twenty-first. He is indeed śrī (prosperity). Thus at the end he ascends to that very śrī. And the entire chant resolves into the gāyatrī. The gāyatrī is indeed brahman. When one attains brahmavarcas, then one obtains the office of purohita. When one obtains the office of purohita, one should desire: 'May I be irremovable from this office of purohita, may I not be displaced.' From this very arrangement, one should make the pañcadaśa the ājya of the brāhmaṇācchaṃsin. The trivṛt is brahman. The pañcadaśa is kṣatra. Thus he grasps kṣatra on both sides with brahman.
2.171: Now this is the catuṣṭoma of Gautama. Gautama desired: 'May I secure cattle, may I reach an abundance of cattle.' He saw this sacrifice. He brought it. He sacrificed with it. Thereupon he secured cattle and reached an abundance of cattle. He who desires 'May I secure cattle, may I reach an abundance of cattle' should sacrifice with this. He indeed secures cattle and reaches an abundance of cattle. The parallel is 2.273. Its bahiṣpavamāna is in groups of four. Horses are four-footed and four-hoofed. Those animals that are four-footed and four-hoofed—those he secures by this. The ājyas are in groups of eight. Animals are eight-hoofed. Those animals that are eight-hoofed—those he secures by this. The midday pressing is twelve. The jagatī has twelve syllables. Cattle belong to the jagatī. That which is small among cattle is of the jagatī character. That he secures by this. The pṛṣṭhas are sixteen. Animals have sixteen parts, both those we know and those we do not. Those he secures in their totality by this. The ārbhava pavamāna is twenty. These are two decades, two virāj measures making twenty. Animals established on twenty are of virāj character. Those animals established on twenty that are of virāj character—those he secures by this. Then the agniṣṭomasāma is twenty-four. Four-footed animals are of virāj character. Those four-footed animals of virāj character—those he secures by this. And twenty-four half-months make a year. By the year he grasped them and placed them in himself. This is neither purely an agniṣṭoma nor an ukthya, but something of both. And these are neither domestic animals nor wild, but something of both.
2.172: Now this is the apūrva. One desiring offspring should sacrifice with this. Prajāpati desired: 'May I be abundant in offspring and cattle, may no one be prior to me.' He saw this sacrifice. He brought it. He sacrificed with it. Thereupon he became abundant in offspring and cattle. No one was prior to him. He who desires 'May I be abundant in offspring, may no one be prior to me' should sacrifice with this. He indeed becomes abundant in offspring and cattle; no one becomes prior to him. It has the trivṛt stoma with the rathantara sāman. For there is no stoma prior to the trivṛt, no pṛṣṭha prior to the rathantara, no deity prior to Prajāpati. Because these are without precedent, therefore it is called apūrva (without precedent). And indeed, Prajāpati is mind. There is nothing whatsoever prior to mind. Therefore too it is apūrva. It is trivṛt. The trivṛt is deficient. Procreation is deficient—'may that which is procreation cause me to procreate.' Its pṛṣṭha is the rathantara. The rathantara is procreation—'may that which is procreation cause me to procreate.' It has twelve stotras. Twelve months make a year. The year is procreation—'he who is procreation, may he cause me to procreate.' It has twenty-four stuta-śastras. Twenty-four half-months make a year.
2.173: Now this is the pañcaśāradīya. The Maruts desired: 'May we be the strongest, mightiest, most numerous, and most powerful among the gods; may we win the heavenly world.' They saw this sacrifice. They brought it. They sacrificed with it. Thereupon they became the strongest, mightiest, most numerous, and most powerful among the gods; they won the heavenly world. He who knows thus becomes the strongest, mightiest, most numerous, and most powerful among his own people, and wins the heavenly world. This sacrifice belongs to the viś. The Maruts are the viś; therefore it is the sacrifice of the viś. With this, Aikyāva Kāṇḍama sacrificed. He became the strongest, mightiest, most numerous, and most powerful among his own people, and he may or may not have won the world. It has the saptadaśa stoma. Prajāpati is the saptadaśa. Prajāpati is procreation -- 'may that which is procreation cause me to procreate.' Its pṛṣṭha is the rathantara. The rathantara is procreation -- 'may that which is procreation cause me to procreate.'
2.174: For it, five autumn-calves and five oxen go about consecrated. Because there are five autumn-calves and five oxen consecrated, therefore it is called pañcaśāradīya. And they are all speckled. They say the Maruts have Pṛśni as their mother. And it is recited in a verse: 'The Maruts have Pṛśni as their mother.' Therefore they are speckled. Vigor is indeed the offering of the gods. That they seized for themselves. Because it belongs to the Maruts, they place vigor in themselves. 'For the Maruts who are victorious,' 'for the Maruts who are well-achieving,' 'for the Maruts who have their own power,' 'for the Maruts indeed, for the Maruts,' 'for the Maruts who are aggressive' -- because it is 'for the Maruts who are victorious,' whatever desire one would conquer, for that desire one should sacrifice. Because it is 'for the Maruts who are well-achieving,' whatever desire one would fully attain, for that desire one should sacrifice. Because it is 'for the Maruts who have their own power' -- all this indeed belongs to the Maruts as their own. By this he gratifies them with what is their own. Being gratified, they bestow their own in return.
2.175: The Maruts are indeed many, both those we know and those we do not. 'May they all be pleased with us.' Because it is 'for the Maruts who are aggressive,' whatever desire one would fully enjoy as well-attained, for that desire one should sacrifice. One should offer these animals: when about to consecrate, one at the upavasatha, one at the setting-free, one at the anūbandhyā, one at the udavasānīyā -- thus one should offer these animals. But some say: this should be a five-day rite. One should offer these animals day after day in it. Then Udara Śāṇḍilya asked the elder Ekayāvan: 'As they dispute about this -- some say it should be a five-day rite, others say it should be an ukthya with saptadaśa stoma as a one-day rite -- what is it?' He replied: 'They taught us that it is an ukthya with saptadaśa as a one-day rite.' But some say: this should be an atirātra. 'How could a sacrifice wrapped up for five years not be an atirātra?' That is not so. Some say it should be an agniṣṭoma. 'From the agniṣṭoma all sacrifice-forms are extended.' But that too is not so. It should be an ukthya with saptadaśa. The ukthya belongs to the viś. The saptadaśa belongs to the viś. This is the sacrifice of the viś. Therefore it should be an ukthya with saptadaśa. Its prescribed sāmans in the pavamānas are the marutām arka and the marutām saṃstobha.
2.176: Now this is the abhijit. The gods and the asuras were contending in these worlds, in this domain. The gods desired: 'May we conquer these worlds, may we conquer the asuras, the rival competitors.' They saw this sacrifice. They brought it. They sacrificed with it. By it they conquered these worlds; they conquered the rival competitors, the asuras. Because they conquered these worlds, that is the abhijit-nature of the abhijit. He who knows thus conquers these worlds and conquers the rival competitor who hates him. Its stomas increase by three. These worlds are three -- for the conquering of these worlds. They are fourfold -- the four directions -- for the conquering of the directions. They chant with abhivatī verses, for the conquering of these very worlds.
2.177: Its pṛṣṭha is the rathantara; the vāmadevya is the maitrāvaruṇa sāman; the bṛhat is in the ārbhava pavamāna. This world is the rathantara. The atmosphere is the vāmadevya. The yonder world is the bṛhat. This is for the conquering of these worlds without interruption. In it they arrange these as covert pṛṣṭha-forms, as a counter-advance to the viśvajit. Indra slew Vṛtra. Having slain Vṛtra, darkness enveloped him from these worlds. He desired: 'May I drive away this darkness.' He saw this abhijit sacrifice. He brought it. He sacrificed with it. Its stomas increase by three. These worlds are three. By it he drove away the darkness from these worlds. But darkness still enveloped him from a fourth world. He desired: 'May I indeed drive away this darkness.' He saw this viśvajit sacrifice. He brought it. He sacrificed with it. Its stomas increase by four. The directions are four. The directions are indeed a fourth world. By it he drove away the darkness from the fourth world as well. Then, having driven away darkness and evil from all these worlds, he ascended to the heavenly world.
2.178: Now this is the viśvajit. Indra conquered the asuras. But he did not conquer their possessions. The gods made him sacrifice with this. He conquered everything. Indra is the viśvajit (all-conquering). Because he conquered with this ritual skill, that is the viśvajit-nature of the viśvajit. The gods, fearing the asuras, gathered up all this and ascended upward to the heavenly world. He who, gaining everything, does not give everything, is one who lets everything perish. But he who, gaining everything, gives everything, is one who possesses everything. He indeed gains everything, for he gains all the stomas, all the grahas, all the pṛṣṭhas, and all the metres. Its stomas increase by four. With three he conquers these worlds. The fourth—by it all the directions become filled. The combination of pṛṣṭhas has different deities, with that conjunction, with that form. On this they say: 'Those who begin with pāvamānī verses go as if on a pathless way.' 'Of thee, Pavamāna, O sage'—this is the reversal. They return to the path. The four do not come from the six.
2.179: Now these āprī ājyas are present. Prajāpati created creatures. Having created them, he felt diminished. He saw these āprī ājyas. By them he gratified himself, for the avoidance of exhaustion. He who gives everything feels as if diminished. Because these āprī ājyas are present, the sacrificer thereby gratifies himself, for the avoidance of exhaustion. On this they say: 'Those who combine these different forms, different deities, and different metres in the same sacrifice-type are as if they are thriving with vital breaths.'
