Because the bṛhat occurs in gāyatrī verses—the bṛhat is prāṇa, the gāyatrī is prāṇa—they are thereby fulfilled with vital breaths. If one were to employ the pṛṣṭhas in order and make the revatī verses last, these verses are of the character of the revatīs—and so they do not depart from the form of the revatīs. On this they say: 'Those who make a trayastriṃśa stoma out of the agniṣṭomasāma depart from it.' These verses have twenty-one syllables. Thereby they do not depart from the agniṣṭomasāma. On this they say: 'Those who make the bṛhat the agniṣṭomasāma depart from the yajñāyajñīya.' Because the verse 'O Agni, thou art the hotṛ of sacrifices' occurs, thereby they do not depart from the form of the yajñāyajñīya, and they do not depart from the third pressing.
2.180: He puts on a reddish skin. The beloved form of cattle is the red. He is thereby fulfilled with the beloved form of cattle. He digs -- the spade overcomes misfortune. The knife is sharp on both sides -- it cuts food for him from both sides. He dwells three nights in the forest; whatever forest-food there is, he secures it by those nights. Three nights not in the village nor in the forest -- whatever food is neither of the village nor of the forest, that he secures by those. Three nights among people -- the food of people he secures by those. Three nights among kinsmen -- the food of kinsmen he secures by those. They amount to twelve. Twelve months make a year. From the year he secures food. He puts on a reddish skin. This skin of cattle was originally that of humans -- the skin of cattle was once the skin of humans. The cattle could not endure sun, rain, gadflies, and mosquitoes. They went to the human and said: 'Let this skin of yours be ours, and ours be yours.' 'What would come of that?' 'We would be your food,' they said. 'This is your garment,' and they gave him a garment. Because he puts on a reddish skin, he is fulfilled with his own corresponding form. In this way, cattle in the other world do not eat him. For cattle do eat a person in the other world. Therefore one should not be naked in the vicinity of a cow.
2.181: It has a tail, for completeness. And indeed, all creatures are of Prajāpati. But whichever of his sons is most wretched, he moves the father's heart. 'He goes to a kind of wretchedness who gives everything'—this reaches the understanding of Prajāpati. The nights he spends in the forest should be spent under an udumbara tree. When Prajāpati distributed nourishment and food to his creatures, the remainder that was left over became this tree. The udumbara is nourishment and food. The forest-food of him who gives everything goes away. He who is inclined toward Prajāpati—whatever food there is, that he secures by those nights. The nights that are neither in the village nor in the forest should be spent among the niṣādas. The niṣādas are indeed neither in the village nor in the forest. Through these he partakes of both kinds of food, both the domestic and the wild. He who is inclined toward Prajāpati—whatever food there is, that he secures by those nights. The nights spent among people should be spent with a vaiśya or a rival. The vaiśya or the rival is indeed the jana (people) of a brāhmaṇa. The food belonging to people departs from one who gives everything. He who is inclined toward Prajāpati—whatever food there is, that he secures by those nights. The nights spent among kinsmen should be spent with a king. The king is indeed the sajana (kinsman).
2.182: Prajāpati created creatures. He created this entire universe by this very viśvajit. Because these introductory verses are of various forms, therefore this entire creation is of various forms. This is indeed the yoking of the pṛṣṭhas. The rathantara is yoked with the upavatī. The sarasvata is with the vairāja; the sārasvatī with the mahānāmnī; the brāhmaṇaspatya with the vairūpa; the sāvitrī with the revatī; the āgnipāvamānī with the bṛhat. Because these introductory verses are of various forms, the pṛṣṭhas are thereby yoked at the morning pressing. They ascend to them at the midday pressing. On this they say: 'Those who begin with pavasvinī verses are as if thriving with vital breaths.'
2.183: Now these are the sarasvata and the sarasvatī. This is the divine couple, the procreative pair -- sarasvat and sarasvatī. 'May we procreate through the divine couple.' On this they say: 'The male's verse should be done first, the female's after. Thus offspring is born without presumption for the sacrificer.' But on this they also say: 'Vāyu is the seed-placer. Sarasvatī is procreation. These two beget Sarasvat from the couple.' And also: 'The cow in heat runs first, the bull follows after -- therefore the sarasvatī should be done first.' But indeed, the sarasvata should be done first. Then this brāhmaṇaspatya introductory verse occurs. When Prajāpati created creatures without brahman, those creatures of his, once created, perished. He saw this brāhmaṇaspatya introductory verse. By it he created creatures endowed with brahman. Those creatures of his, once created, did not perish.
2.184: Now the sāvitrī-prasavas: 'Since we have come upon diverse, various-formed introductory verses, may this be impelled by Savitṛ for us.' Then the āgnipāvamānīs: 'Those who begin with pāvamānī verses go as if on a path.' Agni is the path-maker among the deities. By whatever fire one goes, one goes making a path. Because this āgnipāvamānī introductory verse occurs, by Agni the path-maker they return to the path. 'Of thee, Pavamāna, O sage' -- this is the reversal. By this they return to the path. He who knows the draft-animal of the pṛṣṭhas becomes the draft-animal among his own. The vairāja is indeed the draft-animal of the pṛṣṭhas.
2.185: On this they say: 'It is as if an inversion of rank is being made when the bṛhat and rathantara are on both sides and the pṛṣṭhas are in the middle. It is as if a lesser person were seated above while a greater person sits below -- such is the situation when the bṛhat and rathantara are on both sides with the pṛṣṭhas in the middle.' The bṛhat and rathantara should be in the middle, with the pṛṣṭhas on both sides, to avoid the inversion of rank. But also: 'It is as if a greater person were seated above while a lesser person sits below -- such is the situation when the bṛhat and rathantara are in the middle with the pṛṣṭhas on both sides.' But then one would lack the full set of pṛṣṭhas. For the vairāja was the first among all the pṛṣṭhas to attain its yoking. Moreover, even if one were to ask the very last person going, 'Have you employed the pṛṣṭhas?' -- he would say 'no.' The bṛhat and rathantara should be on both sides with the pṛṣṭhas in the middle, for the completeness of the pṛṣṭha-set. This world is the rathantara, the yonder world is the bṛhat. Cattle are the pṛṣṭhas. With these two worlds on both sides he encloses the cattle, to prevent their departure.
2.186: Also, as father and mother are the bṛhat and rathantara, so the pṛṣṭhas are as sons. When a son transgresses, the father is his pacifier, the father is his restrainer. Because the bṛhat and rathantara are on both sides, it is for pacification and restraint. Also, just as two parents would lie on either side of their children, so the bṛhat and rathantara are on both sides with the pṛṣṭhas in the middle. Just as bulls or stud-animals might stand in an uproar, so it is when these pṛṣṭhas are in the middle. Because the śyaita and naudhasa are on both sides: the naudhasa is brahman. Having first pacified them with brahman, they make them śyaita-like with the śyaita above. Therefore the naudhasa should come first, the śyaita above. On this they say: 'When the pṛṣṭhas have the same endings, by what means are they made dissimilar?' The vairāja and the mahānāmnī, the vairūpa and the revatī -- by this explanation they are made dissimilar. And also because these have intermediate endings, thereby indeed. In it there are three saptadaśa stomas. Their stotriyas total fifty-one. That is the morning pressing of the cow. One hundred and eighty-nine is the night; that is the night of the jyotiṣṭoma. Thus a procreative couple is made. He who knows thus procreates through the couple. The total stotriyas of both come to two hundred and forty-two. That is the virāj. The virāj is food.
2.187: The gods and asuras were competing. At that time these ritual skills were in Prajāpati alone, not in the other gods. Indra ran to Prajāpati: 'Let me conquer the asuras.' To him he gave that skill. He fastened it upon him. Just as one would fasten a gold ornament, a jewel, or a garland, so he put it on his body. From that he fashioned this skill, these pṛṣṭhas. And the other radiance that was there, by which that one (the sun) shines—the asuras, recognizing this form, saying 'Our father strikes us, our father strikes us,' were defeated. Thereupon the gods prevailed, and the asuras were defeated.
2.188: On this they say: one should not over-chant. Just as one having put on a gold ornament, a jewel, or a garland would then take it off again -- such is the case when one over-chants in an agniṣṭoma. But one conquers one of the two halves of the universe. Day and night are indeed the entire universe. Also, just as having climbed to the top of a great tree, one cannot find a way to climb back down -- such is the case when it becomes an agniṣṭoma. Because the stotras become descending, this is the complete foundation, the atirātra -- by it one stands firm in this foundation. On this they say: 'Those who proceed to an atirātra verily fall away from this world.'
2.189: Also, just as one would fasten a chariot-wheel onto an axle, so having gathered all these ritual skills, they establish them in brahman -- those who make the bṛhat the agniṣṭomasāma in gāyatrī verses. Therefore the bṛhat should be made the agniṣṭomasāma in gāyatrī verses. On this they say: one should give a thousand, for that is everything. But they also say: whatever the stotriyas of this viśvajit amount to, that much one should give -- that is everything. But on this they also say: whatever property one has, one should give that. What one withholds is sin. Just as it flows from a couple, so sin flows from him who withholds from all that.
2.190: Now this is the vājapeya. One desiring food should sacrifice with the vājapeya. This is indeed a 'food-drink'—the vājapeya. Food is vāja. When a cow, horse, or person becomes well-nourished with food, then it becomes vājin (vigorous). One desiring brahmavarcas should sacrifice with the agniṣṭoma, for the agniṣṭoma is brahman—for the attainment of brahmavarcas. One being consecrated is consecrated for the sake of seasons and cattle. Of it, five stotras should be made catustriṃśa (thirty-four), including all the pavamānas and both brahmasāmans. That many are the stotriyas of the ukthya. They attain the desire that is in the ukthya. Yet they do not depart from the agniṣṭoma. This is neither purely an agniṣṭoma, nor an ukthya, nor a ṣoḍaśin, nor an atirātra. It is a sacrifice of surplus. Prajāpati is surplus. This is a prājāpatya sacrifice, for the attainment of Prajāpati. Its bṛhat occurs with the śipiviṣṭavatī verses. Cattle are śipiviṣṭas (radiant ones). The bṛhat is kṣatra. This world belongs to the kṣatriya. 'May the cattle follow the world of this world-possessing sāman'—thus. They chant the bṛhat in triṣṭubh verses. The triṣṭubh is kṣatra—'may one being consecrated be consecrated with kṣatra.' They chant in vaiṣṇavī verses. Viṣṇu is the highest among deities—'may one being consecrated be consecrated with the highest deity.' They run a race for the attainment of Prajāpati and for the winning of the heavenly world. Seventeen runners race. Prajāpati is the saptadaśa.
2.191: On this they say: they should race during only three chanted stotras. The prāṇa, apāna, and vyāna are three—for their continuity, for their non-interruption. But they say: they should race during one chanted stotra only. For what is this prāṇa, apāna, and vyāna but one? But on this they say: they should race during all chanted stotras, to prevent the mixing of these two races. On this they say: 'Those who make them race outside the vedi depart from the sacrifice. The sacrifice extends only as far as the inner vedi. They should race only within the vedi.' But they say: one should place one's foot on the chariot-platform and then bring it back. That counts as both having raced and not having raced. But on this they say: they should indeed race. By the rathantara one wins the divine race; by the chariot, the human one. Therefore they should race, for the winning of both races. The brahman priest, seated on the chariot-wheel, sings the sāman: 'We have reached the prize, O prize-winners.' 'They have reached the prize, the prize-winners.' The immortal prize is food—for the securing of food. He sings while it is in motion. For by being in motion one reaches where one wishes to go. Birds he gives as gifts. The world is indeed distributed by means of birds. All this belongs to Prajāpati. This is a prājāpatya sacrifice, for the attainment of Prajāpati. He gives seventeen of each kind of bird, seventeen by seventeen. Prajāpati is the saptadaśa. This is a prājāpatya sacrifice, for the attainment of Prajāpati. Its morning pressing is undefined. Prajāpati is undefined. This is a prājāpatya sacrifice, for the attainment of Prajāpati. The midday pressing is vāja-possessing, for this is the vājapeya. The third pressing is distinguished, for this is the most distinguished of sacrifices. 'High is thy birth, O drop'—the gāyatrīs of the midday pavamāna have andhas (plant-pressing). Food is indeed andhas.
2.192: In those, the gāyatra is the one whose brahmana has been expounded. Then the āmahīyava. The āmahīyava belongs to Prajāpati. This is a prājāpatya sacrifice, for the attainment of Prajāpati. Then the saindhukṣita, a kṣatra-sāman—'may one being consecrated be consecrated with kṣatra.' Then the abhīka, which has the form of consecration. 'O purifying Soma, to thy stream'—this is a bṛhatī of double form. This has both sāmans ascending. Therefore, in making these, they serve both the bṛhat sāman and the rathantara sāman. In those, the raurava is the recognized agniṣṭomasāma—recognized as the agniṣṭomasāma, lest there be obstruction. Then the vājajit: food is vāja—for the securing of food. Then the dairghaśravasa: the dairghaśravasa is food—for the securing of food. Then the arkapuṣpa: the arka is food—for the securing of food. Then the samanta: 'may prosperity be all around him.' Then the yaudhājaya with three endings, for the arrangement of the pressings. Then the auśana as the final sāman—'by the final sāman may he reach the final heavenly world.' Then the rathantara: the rathantara is brahman; this is a brahmasava (brahman-consecration)—'may one being consecrated be consecrated with brahman.' Then the vāmadevya: 'may it follow the auspicious, peaceful, well-pressed one.' And also: cattle are the vāmadevya—for the securing of cattle. Then the naudhasa as the recognized brahmasāma—recognized as the brahmasāma, lest there be obstruction. Then the kāleya: 'may there be happiness for the well-pressed one.' And also: 'may the well-pressed one attain the proper time.' And: 'may it destroy him who is his enemy.' 'Of thy intoxication, most excellent'—the gāyatrīs of the ārbhava pavamāna contain andhas (plant-pressing). Food is indeed andhas.
2.193: In those, the gāyatra is the one whose brahmana has been expounded. And the saṃhita: the saṃhita is brahman; this is a brahmasava—'may one being consecrated be consecrated with brahman.' Then the vājadāvarīs: vāja is food; these are food-givers—for the securing of food. Then the vāravantīya: 'may chosen prosperity attend him.' And also: the vāravantīya is cattle—for the securing of cattle. Then the sabha: 'may there be a splendid assembly for the well-pressed one.' And also: 'may the well-pressed one attain an assembly.' Then the śrudhīya, a sāman of renown: 'may auspicious renown attend him who has been well-pressed.' 'Of your andhas, the foremost'—the anuṣṭubhs contain andhas. Food is indeed andhas. By that they fulfill the well-pressed one with food. In those, the nānada. For the ṣoḍaśī is performed here too. Then the viśoviśīya, a kṣatra-sāman—'may one being consecrated be consecrated with kṣatra.' And also: this well-pressed one is the viśa (people); 'may he be king of the viś.' Then the śyāvāśva, a sāman of the heavenly world—for the attainment of the heavenly world. Then the āndhīgava: the āndhīgava is food—for the securing of food. Then the kāva: 'may there be happiness for the well-pressed one.' And also: 'may the well-pressed one attain the kāva.' Then the yajñāyajñīya as the recognized agniṣṭomasāma—recognized as the agniṣṭomasāma, lest there be obstruction. And also: the yajñāyajñīya is preeminence; by it he establishes him in preeminence. Then the sākamaśva, a sāman of the heavenly world—for the attainment of the heavenly world. Then the saubhara, the splendor of the bṛhat—'may what we have chanted with the bṛhat be full of splendor.' Then the nārmedha as the atirātra-sāman—the atirātra-sāman, lest there be obstruction. And also: the nārmedha is cattle—for the securing of cattle. Then the ṣoḍaśī: 'whatever desire is in the ṣoḍaśin, may that be attained for me.' Then the bṛhat occurs with the śipiviṣṭavatī verses. The bṛhat is mind. Mind is Prajāpati. This becomes the seventeenth of the stotras. The seventeenth is this stoma. Prajāpati is the saptadaśa. Prajāpati is the heavenly world. That is śrī (prosperity).
2.194: Now this is the rājasūya. Varuṇa desired: 'May I be consecrated to the sovereignty of all the gods.' He saw this sacrifice. He brought it. He sacrificed with it. Thereupon he was consecrated to the sovereignty of all the gods. He who knows thus is consecrated to preeminence among his own. Its pavamānas have the catustriṃśa stoma. The svarāj (self-ruling) is the world of the gods. Sovereignty is the catustriṃśa. Thus at the very opening of the pavamāna they fulfill the well-pressed one with the world of sovereignty. The introductory verse is anuṣṭubh. Speech is the anuṣṭubh. Speech is indeed kṣatra—'may one being consecrated be consecrated with kṣatra.' Then these five supplementary verses are of different deities. For the one being consecrated is consecrated to sovereignty over five classes of men: the king, the rājanya, the sūta, the grāmaṇī, and the śūdra-servant. They raise the trivṛt stoma above, and the āgneyī above the supplementary verses. Therefore the brāhmaṇa woman is to the kṣatriya worthy of respect, not to be harassed, not to be punished, not to be killed.
2.195: On this they say: the kṣatriya should not teach this. For a chief does as he pleases. If he should say 'Make the trivṛt stoma, the āgneyī among the supplementary verses,' then the kṣatriya, without offense against brahman, would overcome the brāhmaṇas, would kill without the guilt of embryo-slaughter. But on this they say: the kṣatriya who would teach this—him would the one free from brahman-offense overcome, him would he kill without embryo-slaughter guilt. On this they say: 'Those who depart from the trivṛt stoma depart from the vital breaths.' One should sacrifice first with a trivṛt agniṣṭoma. The trivṛt is prāṇa. Thereby they do not depart from the vital breaths. Brahman comes first, then kṣatra is consecrated. But they say: the apaciti is a sacrifice-form. One should sacrifice with that. Because its two stotras are trivṛt, thereby they do not depart from the vital breaths. And the well-pressed one attains honor. But on this they say: because this introductory verse is vāyavya (belonging to Vāyu), and Vāyu is prāṇa, thereby they are fulfilled with the vital breaths. 'O Vāyu, brilliant one, I approach thee'—it contains the word śukra (brilliant). 'He who is consecrated shines like a light among other men—may this consecrated one be as a light.' 'The foremost of honey at the divine offerings'—thus they make this mādhavya (honey-offering) primary. 'Come, O Soma, to the drinking'—this is saumī (related to Soma), and thereby is made pāvamānī. 'O desirable god, with thy teams'—the desirable is kṣatra; the teams are the viś. Thereby they make the viś follow in the path of kṣatra.
2.196: Now these are the sarasvata and the sarasvatī. This is the divine couple, the procreative pair—sarasvat and sarasvatī. 'May we procreate through the divine couple.' On this they say: 'The male's verse should be done first, the female's after. Thus for the consecrated one a royal son is born without presumption.' But on this they say: 'Vāyu is the seed-placer. Sarasvatī is procreation. These two beget Sarasvat from the couple.' And: 'The cow in heat runs first, the bull follows after—therefore the sarasvatī should be done first.' But indeed, the sarasvata should be done first. Then the brāhmaṇaspatya: 'may kṣatra be consecrated in brahman.' Then the sāvitrī: 'may one be consecrated impelled by Savitṛ.' Then these two are both introductory verses, for both sāmans are performed here. The abhivan has nine verses, as a form appropriate to consecration. And thereby they do not depart from the trivṛt stoma. The savat has six verses: 'may he be consecrated by those who speak harmoniously.' The vṛṣaṇvat occurs: 'may this bull be consecrated as one being consecrated.' It has four verses—for the securing of four-footed cattle. The two-footed verses occur covertly—for the securing of two-footed beings.
2.197: 'Now when the night is adorned' -- an anuṣṭubh is the last verse. That udgātṛ who would conduct a king in the rājasūya should, already in the bahiṣpavamāna, seat him on the divine throne of the gods. The introductory verse is anuṣṭubh, and the final verse is anuṣṭubh. Of these two, there are sixty-four syllables. Of those, sixty form the throne. And the four that remain are the feet of the throne. Thus the udgātṛ seats the king on the divine throne of the gods already in the bahiṣpavamāna. Both sets of ājyas occur, for both sāmans are performed here. On this they say: only one set should be done -- those of the bṛhat. The ājyas are the share of the viś -- 'may kṣatra be consecrated through the viś, together with the vājinī.' But on this they say: both sets should be done. For the one being consecrated is consecrated to sovereignty over all. Therefore both sets should be done. They are pañcadaśa. Kṣatra is the pañcadaśa.
2.198: After emerging from the avabhṛtha bath, he fastens upon the consecrated one a garland of twelve lotuses. The waters were the wives of the gods. They desired a mate. The gods approached them. They conceived. From that, lotuses were born. Truth is indeed the waters. Truth is the dīkṣā. The lotuses are the truth of truth. By the truth of truth he fastens the ardor of the dīkṣā upon him. The garland has twelve lotuses. Twelve months make a year. The year is the sacrifice. He thereby fastens the sacrifice upon him. But they say: it should have seven lotuses. The metres are seven. The sacrifice is woven by the metres. He thereby fastens the sacrifice upon him. But they say: it should have six lotuses. Six seasons make a year. The year is the sacrifice. He thereby fastens the sacrifice upon him. But they say: it should have five lotuses. The sacrifice is fivefold. Animals are fivefold. Animals are the sacrifice. He thereby fastens the sacrifice upon him. But they say: it should have four lotuses. The directions are four. Four-footed are the animals. Animals are the sacrifice. He thereby fastens the sacrifice upon him. But they say: it should have three lotuses. The sacrifice has three pressings. He thereby fastens the sacrifice upon him. But on this they say: it should have twelve lotuses. For in twelve, all these are contained.
2.199: Now these are the saṃsṛpa iṣṭis. When Varuṇa had been consecrated, the waters struck off his lustre with the sixteenfold. It fell in this direction. It became fourfold: one quarter Bhṛgu, one quarter Sarasvatī, one quarter the daśapeya, one quarter the śrāyantīya. Therefore the hotṛ of this sacrifice is a Bhārgava; the waters of Sarasvatī are for the consecration-bath; the daśapeya is the sacrifice; and the śrāyantīya is the brahmasāma. The gods said: 'Let us recover this lustre.' They saw these saṃsṛpa iṣṭis. By them they crept toward it. Because they crept toward it, that is the saṃsṛpa-nature of the saṃsṛpas. They recovered it by means of Viṣṇu as deity and with ten offerings. Therefore the tenth offering of this sacrifice is dedicated to Viṣṇu. For they recovered it on the tenth day. One is consecrated on the tenth day, for they recovered it on the tenth. There are ten cups. For they recovered it on the tenth. Ten by ten follow each cup in procession. For they recovered it on the tenth. He who is uninterrupted in soma-drinking for ten generations is the performer of the rite. For they recovered it on the tenth. Having recovered it, they kindled it with fire. The Maruts blew. With the śrāyantīya they cooked it. That is the śrāyantīya-nature of the śrāyantīya. Its bahiṣpavamāna occurs in the verses of the yajñāyajñīya. The verses of the yajñāyajñīya belong to Agni.
2.200: The śrāyantīya is the brahmasāma. By it they cook it together. The yajñāyajñīya is inserted in anuṣṭubh verses. The yajñāyajñīya is speech. The anuṣṭubh is speech. By speech they fulfill it. The vāravantīya is the agniṣṭomasāma. The vāravantīya is vigor and power. By vigor and power they fulfill it. The stoma is saptadaśa. Prajāpati is the saptadaśa. Prajāpati is the recoverer of what is lost—'by him who recovers what is lost, may we recover this lustre.' The sacrifice is the agniṣṭoma, or rather the agniṣṭoma is the year. By the year he grasped them and placed them in himself. In it, the appropriate gifts are distributed: a garland for the udgātṛ, a gold plate for the hotṛ, a pair of shining ornaments for the adhvaryu, twelve barren cows for the brahman, a milk-cow for the maitrāvaruṇa, a bull for the brāhmaṇācchaṃsin, a pair of garments for the neṣṭṛ and potr̥, a jar of barley for the acchāvāka, and an ox for the agnīdh.
2.201: The garland is made of paṭara-leaves. For the sun yonder is like a paṭara-leaf. And the gold plate that is for the hotṛ—three things are joined together in alignment: the gold plate, the āhavanīya fire, and that sun yonder. Because these are joined together, it is for fulfillment. Fulfillment comes to him who knows thus. The pair of shining ornaments for the adhvaryu—they say it is as if calling the two by their form. And because those two move about conspicuously, that which is similar in appearance is made. The twelve barren cows for the brahman—barren cows are procreation; the brahman is procreation—that which is similar in nature is made. The milk-cow for the maitrāvaruṇa—the milk-cow gives milk; Mitra and Varuṇa share in milk—that which is similar in nature is made. The bull for the brāhmaṇācchaṃsin—the brāhmaṇācchaṃsin belongs to Indra; the bull belongs to Indra—that which is similar in nature is made. The pair of garments for the neṣṭṛ and potṛ—garments belong to the Maruts; the neṣṭṛ and potṛ belong to the Maruts—that which is similar in nature is made. And also: the Maruts are abundance; a cloth is made of abundant threads. The jar of barley for the acchāvāka—the acchāvāka recitation is like a fixed, traditional priestly portion. They nourish it with these very barley grains. It is a large jar, the form of security—'may the priestly portions be released.' The ox for the agnīdh—an ox carries burdens; Agni carries offerings to the gods—that which is similar in nature is made. Because these gifts are distributed according to their proper forms, it is for fulfillment.
2.202: After emerging from the avabhṛtha, he shaves only his beard, not his head-hair. Śrī (prosperity) is the head. Śrī goes to the head of the consecrated one. If he were to shave his head-hair before the year, he would shave off śrī, not the hair. But if he shaves his head-hair after the year, he has first enclosed śrī in himself by the year—then he shaves only the hair, not the śrī. Therefore he should shave afterwards. It is an atirātra. The form of hair is the atirātra. Those that are white are the form of day; those that are black, of night. Therefore it is an atirātra. Its stomas go in reverse order. He who is being consecrated ascends to one who is greater. Its morning pressing is ekaviṃśa. Twelve months, five seasons, three worlds, and that sun yonder—ekaviṃśa is the twenty-first. He is Varuṇa. This is the varuṇasava. He now ascends to one greater. He who, having ascended to one greater, then does not submit to him—that one brings upon him whatever affliction he desires. Because these stomas go in reverse order, they are as if submitting, as if acknowledging. Then the stomas come back again in ascending order. He who, having ascended to one greater, then submits to him—'go forth, do not withdraw,' he says to him. Because the āśvina rites rise upward again, it is as if one said to the submitting one 'go forth, do not withdraw'—such is that. Its rathantara occurs at the midday pressing. This earth is the rathantara.
2.203: Now this is the dvirātra. One desiring offspring should sacrifice with this. Its stomas are of both kinds, odd and even. This is the divine procreative couple, that the stomas are of both kinds. 'May that divine procreative couple cause me to procreate.' Its two sāmans are the bṛhat and rathantara. This is the divine procreative couple, that both the bṛhat and rathantara are present. 'May that divine procreative couple cause me to procreate.' Its brahmasāma is pañcaviṃśa. The puruṣa is pañcaviṃśa. The desire of the sacrificer is indeed the brahmasāma. He thereby brings his offspring to the state of being a complete person. Its stotriyas number seven hundred and twenty. That many are the days and nights of the year. Days and nights indeed generate everything.
2.204: Now this is the triṣṭoma agniṣṭoma. One desiring a firm footing should employ this. On this they say: 'He who is consecrated verily falls away from this world.' Because it is triṣṭoma -- these worlds are three -- he thereby gains a firm footing in these worlds. And this sacrifice-form is called dhṛti ('steadiness'). Its pṛṣṭha is the rathantara. With this king Abhipratāraṇa sacrificed. He made it the conclusion of a dvirātra. Then the brāhmaṇa said to him: 'The Kurus will lose Kurukṣetra. Bring forth offspring, not territories.' And so these Kurus, having lost Kurukṣetra, wander about. The Salvas are now in Kurukṣetra.
2.205: Now these are the nākasads. The gods ascended to the heavenly world. Having reached the heavenly world, they were all together, unsettled. They desired: 'May we settle in the nāka, the heavenly world.' They saw these nākasada stomas. They brought them. They sacrificed with them. Thereupon they settled in the nāka, the heavenly world. Because they settled in the nāka, that is the nākasada-nature of the nākasads. 'He settles in the nāka, the heavenly world, who knows thus' -- so Jānaśruteya used to say. And Vaitahavya used to say: 'In the beginning Prajāpati created the gods in this very world.' And then these upper worlds were trackless, like wilderness forests, without chariot-roads, like tracts of jungle, impassable.
2.206: The Vasus went first. With three trivṛt stomas they cleared this world; with one pañcadaśa they settled in it. With three trivṛt stomas they cleared this intermediate world; with one saptadaśa they settled in it. With three trivṛt stomas they cleared the yonder world; with one ekaviṃśa they settled in it. Just as the sharp edge of an axe, or as Agni among deities -- so is this trivṛt stoma. Just as one would go forward hewing with an axe, leveling behind with fire -- so the Vasus cleared these worlds with the trivṛt stoma. Because they cleared these worlds, therefore these stomas are called prayuta ('clearing'). Just as one would settle in comfort on a fire-cleared, easy path -- so the Vasus went. Therefore they are the most excellent among the gods.
2.207: Then the Rudras saw a more prepared path. With one trivṛt alone they cleared this world. With three pañcadaśas they settled in it. With one trivṛt alone they cleared this intermediate world. With three saptadaśas they settled in it. With one trivṛt alone they cleared the yonder world. With three ekaviṃśas they settled in it. Just as on a prepared path there is still some roughness -- making that smooth behind, so the Rudras went. Then the Ādityas went with four trivṛts, four pañcadaśas, three saptadaśas, and one ekaviṃśa, by an already-made path. Then the Aṅgirasas went separately with three trivṛts, three pañcadaśas, three saptadaśas, three ekaviṃśas, and with the virāṭsvarāja. The Maruts went with the triṇava alone. The Maruts are vigor and strength. They are of the triṇava. Thus they went by vigor and strength. The Viśve Devas went with the trayastriṃśa alone. Having reached the heavenly world, they were all together, unsettled.
2.208: These Viśve Devas saw this trayastriṃśa stoma. They brought it. On this they say: 'Who are the Viśve Devas?' One should say: 'These five groups of deities, having combined, become the Viśve Devas.' These Viśve Devas sacrificed together with this trayastriṃśa stoma. There are indeed thirty-three deities and thirty-three divine worlds. Thereupon they sat in the nāka, the heavenly world. They said: 'We have sat in the heavenly world, in the nāka.' Because they said 'We have sat in the heavenly world, in the nāka,' that is the nākasada-nature of the nākasads. He who knows thus sits in the nāka, the heavenly world. In these, both pairs of sāmans occur, for by both one goes where one wishes to go.
2.209: Now these are the ṛtuṣṭomas. The seasons desired: 'By a common sacrifice may we attain a common fulfillment, may we secure food, may we be renewed again and again.' They saw these sacrifices. They brought them. They sacrificed with them. Spring sacrificed first. He sacrificed with this. He attained this very fulfillment, secured this food, attained this renewal which belongs to spring. Then winter observed: 'With that very means by which the earlier ones sacrificed and succeeded, with that same means shall I too sacrifice.' He sacrificed with this very same rite.
2.210: Of all these, this is indeed the most flourishing. For upon reaching this, a cow, horse, or person attains this very form. This is the endless, imperishable, ever-renewing divine cycle that revolves back and forth. He who knows this wins this endlessness, this imperishability, this renewal. He who desires 'By a common sacrifice may I attain a common fulfillment, may I secure food, may I be renewed again and again' should sacrifice with these. He should sacrifice with the first in spring. He attains this very fulfillment, secures this food, attains this renewal which belongs to spring. He should sacrifice with the second in summer. He should sacrifice with the third in the rains. He should sacrifice with the fourth in autumn. He should sacrifice with the fifth in the cool season. He should sacrifice with the sixth in winter.
2.211: Thus he becomes one with the seasons. He attains the seasons, and his merit becomes imperishable and uninterrupted. Ever-renewed, he goes on flourishing by merit. They have five stotriyas each. The sacrifice is fivefold; animals are fivefold; food is fivefold; the seasons are five; whatever is fivefold -- this is the fulfillment of all that, its attainment. Each one of them has sixty stotriyas. That many are the nights of a season. Thereby they do not over-chant the seasons. All of them together have three hundred and sixty stotriyas. That many are the nights of the year. By the nights they thus attain the year. On this they say: 'The pṛṣṭhas should be employed in succession.' One should proceed from pṛṣṭha to pṛṣṭha. For the pṛṣṭhas are the seasons. The first should have the rathantara sāman, then the bṛhat, then the vairūpa, then the vairāja, then the śakvarī, and the last should have the revatī sāman. But on this they say: the pṛṣṭhas would be interrupted by the dīkṣā, upasat, and sutyā days. If one were to do so, the pṛṣṭhas would seem far apart. One pṛṣṭha should follow upon another. This is the subordinate set of the bṛhat and rathantara, these sāmans. The bṛhat and rathantara serve for the continuity of an interrupted sacrifice. Therefore they should use only the bṛhat and rathantara. The first should have the rathantara sāman, then the bṛhat, then the rathantara, then the bṛhat, then the rathantara, and the last should have the bṛhat.
2.212: Now these are the stomas of the directions. The directions desired: 'By a common sacrifice may we attain a common fulfillment, may we gain a firm footing, may we win the endless heavenly world.' They saw these sacrifices. They brought them. They sacrificed with them. This eastern direction sacrificed first. It sacrificed with this. It attained this very fulfillment, this firm footing, and won this endless heavenly world. This is the endless heavenly world won by it. Even if one were to travel eastward for many days, one would never find its end. Then the upward direction observed: 'With that very means by which the earlier ones sacrificed and succeeded, with that same means shall I too sacrifice.' It sacrificed with this very same rite. It attained this very fulfillment, this firm footing, and won this endless heavenly world. This is the endless heavenly world won by it.
2.213: On this they say: 'The pṛṣṭhas should be employed in succession.' One should proceed from pṛṣṭha to pṛṣṭha. For the pṛṣṭhas are the directions. The first should have the rathantara sāman, then the bṛhat, then the vairūpa, then the vairāja, then the śakvarī, and the last should have the revatī sāman. But on this they say: the pṛṣṭhas would be interrupted by the dīkṣā, upasat, and sutyā days. If one were to do so, the pṛṣṭhas would seem far apart. One pṛṣṭha should follow upon another. This is the subordinate set of the bṛhat and rathantara, these sāmans. The bṛhat and rathantara serve for the continuity of an interrupted sacrifice. Therefore they should use only the bṛhat and rathantara.
2.214: They are each pañcaviṃśa. Of that, the twenty is the virāj. And the four are these four directions. And the five that make up the pañcaviṃśa -- this is the upward direction. The directions milk heaven, or heaven milks the directions. He who knows thus obtains the milking of the directions and of heaven. He who desires 'By a common sacrifice may I attain a common fulfillment, may I gain a firm footing, may I win the endless heavenly world' should sacrifice with these. He should prepare a large sacrificial ground. He should order five halls to be built: one here, one here, one here, one here, and one in the middle. He should sacrifice with the rathantara sāman. He attains this very fulfillment, this firm footing, and wins this endless heavenly world. Then he should sacrifice with the bṛhat sāman, then with the rathantara, then with the bṛhat, then with both sāmans. He attains this very fulfillment, this firm footing, and wins this endless heavenly world, and he attains this very direction. He becomes one with the directions. He attains the directions. His heavenly world won thereby is endless.
2.215: Now these are the ṛṣiṣṭomas. The ṛṣis won the heavenly world by toil, austerity, and observance of vows. They desired: 'Having established offspring in this world, may we go to the heavenly world.' Vasiṣṭha desired: 'May my offspring be foremost and chief.' He saw this trivṛt stoma. He brought it. He sacrificed with it. The trivṛt is the foremost, the chief of stomas. Thereupon his offspring became foremost and chief. Even now these Vasiṣṭhas among the Kurus consider themselves foremost and chief. He who knows thus becomes foremost and chief. Then Bharadvāja desired: 'May my offspring be the strongest and mightiest.' He saw this pañcadaśa stoma. He brought it. He sacrificed with it. Vigor and strength belong to the pañcadaśa of stomas. Thereupon his offspring became the strongest and mightiest. Even by that road by which the Kurus formerly did not go, the Bharadvājas went. And people cry out: 'The might of the Bharadvājas!' Therefore a Bharadvāja alone, when authorized, may strike a punishable person in the assembly.
2.266: On this they say: 'Which one should be seized [for sacrifice]?' They say: 'The one that is famous from afar should be seized.' For he is famous by his vigor. This sacrifice is vigor. And they also say: 'Whichever of a yoked pair draws more, that one should be seized.' For by vigor indeed he draws more of the two. This sacrifice is vigor. On this, Saunaka Kapeya and Abhipratarina came to an agreement: 'What kind should be seized?' They determined upon a dark-tawny one. When a white one has something black, or a black one has something white -- that is the one marked by Prajapati. Prajapati's eye swelled. It flew away. It entered the waters. From that the horse came into being. Because it swelled (asvayat), therefore it is 'horse' (asva). Because it swelled (svayat) and came into being, therefore it putrefies. Because it entered the waters, therefore it is white (svetana). Of white color? Of a white one indeed, the eye has something black, or of a black one something white. That is the one marked by Prajapati.
2.267: With this, Mundibha Udanyu sacrificed, the king of the Udanyus. Then Yavakri Saumastambi sat down opposite the place of praise. For him they made these opening verses: 'This Pusan, wealth, Bhaga.' He said: 'O Sambana, do not stand there! Mundibha has given away his vital breaths.' That was indeed his counter-utterance at that time. Now Mundibha Udanyu was versed in the threefold knowledge. He said: 'Sweep my offering-hall and havirdhana for me, smear my cups with clay and set them down. When this brahmana counter-speaker dies, then I shall sacrifice.' They smeared his cups with clay and set them down. Now Yavakri Saumastambi was powerful and full of brahman-splendor. Whatever woman he spoke to, whatever woman desired him -- she died. And whoever did not desire him also died. He spoke to the wife of Yajnavacas Rajastambhayana. She considered: 'Whether I desire him or not, I shall die anyway. Let me desire him then. Let me die having done a pleasing thing for a brahmana.' She said to him: 'Stand there.'
2.268: Her husband came upon her while she was adorning herself and weeping. He said to her: 'Why are you adorning yourself? Why do you weep?' She said: 'Because you will die' -- thus [she lamented] for her own self. 'Yavakri has spoken to her,' [she explained]. He said: 'Bring ghee from here.' He purified it with two strainers and made an offering: 'She who protects us today, the refined one of Bharadvaja, the fire-beloved fuel-stick, O Agni, bring her to me, svaha!' He raised up for her an iron-fisted gandharva, a jealous one. He said to him: 'Your wife has gone to Yavakri. Go there!' When he arrived, he spread himself before her. She laughed. He said: 'A woman should certainly not laugh before a stranger. Yet you laugh?' 'How so?' [she asked]. 'Because you will die,' [he replied]. She seized his foot and said: 'Surely you will not cook a woman who walks in the manner of humans.' For her feet were hairy underneath. They were either lying together or not lying together. Then the iron-fisted gandharva strode forth.
2.269: 'Is there [anything] or not?' he said. 'Whatever paternal property you have, cut off all of it before sunrise. Whether she be that one or not.' He then undertook to cut in that manner. They said: 'Yavakri has gone mad! Let us bind him!' 'No,' said the father. 'My son acts as driven by the gods. He alone knows what is best in this.' Now a deaf carpenter in the village did not hear the one being restrained. He went past while striking. [Yavakri asked:] 'Who will be the one to rub us with grass?' He struck him down -- so say some. The sun rose upon him while he was still striking. When [the sun] had risen, that very gandharva struck him down -- so say others. However he died, so it was.
2.270: Then Mundibha, having become aware, said: 'Break open my offering-hall and havirdhana! Let the brahmanas make me sacrifice! That brahmana counter-speaker is indeed dead.' They did so for him. Then the Saumastambi, having become aware, came. He sat down at the place of praise in the same way. They made for him the same opening verses: 'This Pusan, wealth, Bhaga.' He said: 'This princeling did not know this sacrificial rite. He certainly did not slay my son by what he said. Just so much was the lifespan of his son.' He cursed him thus: 'This princeling shall die! These Mundibhas shall be destroyed!' They indeed wander about as ruined ones, calling themselves Gotamas. The opening verses of this [rite] are those beginning with 'Tarat sa mandi dhavati' -- they contain [the word] 'tarad' (crossing). Therefore the horse is best at crossing a path. They contain [the word] 'dhavad' (running). Therefore the horse attains the path by running. The verse has one foot [more].
2.271: [The parallel is 2.173.] Its bahispavamana consists of groups of four. Horses indeed have four feet and four hooves. Those animals that have four feet and four hooves -- those he secures by this. The ajya [chants] are in groups of eight. Animals indeed have eight hooves. Those animals that have eight hooves -- those he secures by this. The midday [pressing] is twelve-fold. The jagati indeed has twelve syllables. Animals are [of the nature of] jagati. Whatever is small among animals, that is of the jagati. That he secures by this. The arbhava pavamana is twenty-fold. These two are the ten-by-ten viraj meters that make twenty. Animals that have twenty supports are of the viraj. Those animals that have twenty supports and are of the viraj -- those he secures by this. Then the agnistoma-saman is twenty-four-fold. Animals that have four feet are of the viraj. Those animals that have four feet and are of the viraj -- those he secures by this.
2.272: Having enclosed it, they make the offering on this second day. This second day is of the ekavimsa [stoma], of a single stoma. Twelve months, five seasons, three worlds, and yonder sun -- that is twenty-one. Just as a firmly driven post [stands firm], so among the stomas the ekavimsa -- just as one would drive in a firm post, so is this for the sake of non-falling. On that day they offer these animals: the elephant as the highest, the flea as the lowest. Having carried fire around some, they seize some and release others. The mahanamni verses form its prstha. For no other prstha than the mahanamnis is able to raise up these animals. They have seven feet, for there are seven domestic animals -- for the securing of domestic animals. There is one foot beyond the seventh -- for the securing of wild animals. Its brahma-saman is the Parthurasman, complete with the syllable 'atha.' For 'athag, athag' is how a horse runs. And indeed a broad rein is able to drive a horse. It is of five-fold [structure], for the brahma-saman of the mahanamnis is five-fold. Its sacrificial posts are twenty-one, each twenty-one forearms [in height]. For no fewer than twenty-one posts are able to raise up these animals. Six are of bilva wood, twelve of khadira, and those of putudru on both sides of the fire -- for putudru is the body of Agni. The rajjudala [makes] twenty-one. That is the agnistha. Prajapati among trees is the rajjudala. Its fruits are his phalluses. And the sap is his semen. Prajapati is the father, Aditya the son. The atiratra has all stomas, for conquering and surpassing all worlds. For all this is indeed the stomas.
2.273: Now this is the Govinata Chandomapavamana. One desiring cattle should sacrifice with this. Its stomas are of both kinds -- odd and even. Those stomas that are odd, domestic animals follow them; those that are even, wild animals. The chandomah [rite] is indeed animals. He who, knowing thus, sacrifices with the Chandomapavamana, secures both kinds of animals -- both domestic and wild. Then this second day has the ekavimsa prstha. The very praise of the ekavimsa that was given before -- 'let it not be unattained' -- that is the brahmana of this. On that they offer this sacrificial horse. Because this has the ekavimsa prstha, therefore it is Govinata. The third day has the trayastrimsa punarabhyavarti. Thirty-three are the deities, thirty-three the divine worlds. Having attained these finally, they ascend. Now, of this [rite], the agnistoma has fifteen, the ukthya has fifteen, the atiratra has fifteen. One desiring royal power should sacrifice with this. The horse is royal power, fifteen is royal power, the sacrifice is royal power. This is made to bear those marks. With this Rsabha Yajnatura sacrificed, the Svaitna king, desiring sole sovereignty. Having sacrificed, he became the sole sovereign of the Svaitnas. He who desires prosperity should sacrifice with this. Prosperity is royal power. This is a sacrifice of royal power.
2.274: Now of this [rite], the day has trivrt [stoma] and the night has pancadasi; the day has trivrt and the night has pancadasi; the day has trivrt and the night has pancadasi. One desiring heaven should sacrifice with this. Datva Sautemanasa and Mitravid Damstradyumna were the two brahmacari students of Pratidarsa Vaibhavata, the Svaitna king. Of the two, Mitravid Damstradyumna performed the teacher's work. He used to tend the cows. The other one only pursued his studies. Coming together in the evening, they would say to each other: 'Come, friend, let us place our hands upon what has been recited. Come, friend, let us place our hands upon what has been well-practiced.' Then the teacher instructed Datva Sautemanasa. Coming together in the evening, they said to each other: 'Come, friend, let us place our hands upon what has been recited. Come, friend, let us place our hands upon what has been well-practiced.' 'No,' he said. 'Either I have been released beyond you, or I am one instructed by the teacher.' He spent that night pierced by displeasure. The next day, he went out following the cows.
2.275: She [a cow/goddess] said: 'I know what displeases you. Let it not displease you. Come, I shall tell you the path leading to the gods.' 'So be it.' She told it to him. They came together in the evening. He said: 'Datva, come here! You who claim to be instructed by the teacher -- do you see the path leading to the gods?' 'No,' he said. 'But I see it,' [said Mitravid]. 'Tell it to me then.' 'So be it.' He told it to him. Therefore indeed a princeling is more tender-hearted than a brahmana.
2.276: 'No,' he said. 'Either by giving or by exertion does one go on this path, knowing it. Come, let us either give or exert ourselves.' Mitravid Damstradyumna went for a year with the Go-Ayus [rite]. He went to the heavenly world from above, at the end of the year. But Datva Sautemanasa, having sacrificed with just this three-night [rite], went to the heavenly world. By sacrificing with this, one goes to the heavenly world. With this, Hiranyada Veda sacrificed, desiring a pair. He had abundance of cattle and horses, elephants and gold, goats and sheep, rice and barley, sesame and beans.
2.277: Now this is the Antarvasu. Khandika Haudbhari and Kesi Darbhya competed among the Pancalas. Khandika overcame Kesi. Kesi, defeated by Khandika, went to Uccaihsravas Kauvayeya, the Kauravya king, his mother's brother. He said to him: 'Arrange a sacrifice for me.' 'So be it,' he said. 'How shall I arrange it for you?' 'So that one prospers in this world and also in yonder [world].' 'So that in this [world],' he said. He said: 'Does he then ask about a sacrifice who does not know the companion of the sacrifice?' 'How so?' 'For it is for yonder world that the sacrifice [exists], not for this one.' 'No,' he said. 'Khandika Haudbhari has overcome me. Arrange [the sacrifice] for me so that I may overcome him.' He arranged for him this Antarvasu. The agnistoma has all stomas. From here upward the middle day has the chandoma arrangement: the twenty-four-fold for the morning pressing, forty-four-fold for the midday pressing, forty-eight-fold for the third pressing. The atiratra has all stomas, in the latter [position], in reverse. Twenty-four is brahman, forty-four is royal power, forty-eight is the people. By these two all-stoma [rites], having enclosed this threefold people, he placed it in himself.
2.278: Thereupon he drove out Khandika. Then Kesi Darbhya prospered, and Khandika was destroyed. One prospers by oneself, and his rival enemy is destroyed, who knows thus. He who desires prosperity should sacrifice with this. By these two all-stoma [rites], having enclosed this threefold people, he places it in himself. He attains prosperity thereby. Now this is the Paraka. One desiring heaven should sacrifice with this. Its chants are directed away, its recitations are directed away, its prsthas are directed away, its hymn-arrangements are directed away -- everything is directed away. With this, Uccaihsravas Kauvayeya sacrificed, the aged Kauravya king. Having sacrificed, he went to the heavenly world. By sacrificing with this, one goes to the heavenly world. Now, of the [rite] whose agnistoma has jyotis, whose ukthya has jyotis, whose atiratra has jyotis -- one desiring splendor of brahman should sacrifice with this. He shines as if with light who attains splendor of brahman. One attains splendor of brahman by this and becomes possessed of brahman-splendor. Now, of the [rite] whose agnistoma has seventeen, whose ukthya has seventeen, whose atiratra has seventeen -- that is Prajapati's horse-sacrifice. One desiring progeny should sacrifice with this. Now, of the [rite] whose agnistoma has twenty-one, whose ukthya has twenty-one, whose atiratra has twenty-one -- that is Aditya's horse-sacrifice.
2.279: Now these are the four-night [rites]. Atri desired: 'May four heroes be born to me.' He saw this four-night sacrifice. He brought it forth. He sacrificed with it. Then four heroes were born to him. Four heroes are born to one who knows thus. The Atri seers desired: 'May four heroes each be born to us.' They saw this four-night sacrifice. They brought it forth. They sacrificed with it. Then four heroes each were born to them. Four heroes each are born to one who knows thus. Up to four, indeed, is the procreative pairing; beyond four, the pairing moves away. One and one, two and two, three and three, four and four -- this is the procreative pairing up to four. For there are no [names for] 'five and five.' 'Let that procreative pairing procreate for me.' Its pavamanas have the twenty-four-fold [stoma]. Twenty-four half-months make a year. The year is procreation. 'Let it procreate for me.' Its saman songs are both brhat and rathantara. This is the divine procreative pairing -- when both brhat and rathantara are [present]. 'Let that divine procreative pairing procreate for me.' Its stomas are of both kinds -- odd and even. This is the divine procreative pairing -- when stomas are of both kinds.
2.280: The upper stomas are odd. The seasons are odd. The seasons procreate all this. 'Let those that procreate all this procreate for me.' They go releasing a stoma from day to day. They take up a stoma from day to day. A hero is what the stoma is. Day by day they go procreating vigor, day by day they take up vigor, ascending syllable by syllable. The first day has a one-syllable ending, the second a two-syllable ending, the third a three-syllable ending, the fourth a four-syllable ending -- 'may there be a hero standing foremost in syllables, a hero standing foremost in syllables.' The agnistoma-samans are different [each day], for the sake of distinct vigor among heroes. For that one is not a hero who is a hero after another's vigor. He indeed is a hero who is a hero after his own vigor.
2.281: On this they say: 'They should make the yajnayajniya the agnistoma-saman day after day. The yajnayajniya is pre-eminence. Let the hero not fall from pre-eminence.' If the chants were the same, one would attain only equal vigor. If they were unequal, one would place vigor singularly in the foremost ones. When the pavamanas are equal, one attains only equal vigor thereby. But when they go ascending by the later chants, they go placing greater vigor in the foremost ones. This [rite] extends over only two nights. For those who would make the rathantara the prstha on the third day with the verses 'Abhi tva vrsabha suta' -- because of 'abhi tve,' they do not depart from the form of the rathantara. The third day extends with a gayatri that is ayatayamna (not previously used). 'Kas tam Indra tvavasav' is the Maitravaruna-saman. Their syllables correspond here. The gayatris are gayatris, the kakubhs are kakubhs, the brhatis are brhatis. The meters are joined according to their proper places. The first day extends with gayatri, the second with tristubh, the third with jagati, the fourth with anustubh. For those who would make the brhat the prstha on the fourth day with the verses 'Tam tva yajnebhir imaha' -- because of 'tam tve,' they do not depart from the form of the brhat. The fourth day extends with an anustubh that is ayatayamna. 'Vayam u tvam apurvya' is the Maitravaruna-saman. Their syllables correspond here. The gayatris are gayatris, the kakubhs are kakubhs, the brhatis are brhatis.
2.282: The first day is an agnistoma, the second an ukthya, the third has the sodasi, the fourth an atiratra. The days have different kinds of vigor, for the sake of distinct vigor among heroes. Then Taksaka Bhagirathi, the Matsya king, used to say: 'The four-night [rite] encompasses the prsthas. For it extends over [only] two nights by means of the brhat and rathantara.' They should make the vairupa and the mahanamni on the third day -- these are of the rathantara type. The third day is of the rathantara type. They should make the vairaja and the revati on the fourth day -- these are of the brhat type. The fourth day is of the brhat type. 'Thus indeed the four-night [rite] encompasses the prsthas,' he used to say. On this they say: 'Which of the sacrifices is the tallest, which the broadest?' One should answer: 'The three-night [rite] is the tallest of sacrifices.'
2.283: Now this is [the rite] of Jamadagni. One desiring progeny should sacrifice with this. Jamadagni desired: 'May I be abundant in offspring and cattle.' He saw this four-night sacrifice. He brought it forth. He sacrificed with it. Then he became abundant in offspring and cattle. Indeed, two descendants of Aurva in his lineage did not recognize each other: 'Who are you? Who are you?' So abundantly did he procreate here. Therefore even now two grey-haired descendants of Aurva, meeting, do not recognize each other. One becomes abundant in offspring and cattle who knows thus. They say its consecration should last a year. The year is procreation. This sacrifice is procreation. Thus that procreation is effected. Therefore one desiring progeny should sacrifice with this. And they say [the consecration] should be forty-eight [days]. The jagati has forty-eight syllables. Animals are [of the nature of] jagati. This sacrifice is of the jagati type. Therefore one desiring cattle should sacrifice with this. And they say [it should be] thirty-six. The brhati has thirty-six syllables. The brhati has attained the heavenly world. That is its heavenly [aspect]. Therefore one desiring heaven should sacrifice with this. These are three jagati [verses]. Jagati verses have three feet. Therefore they do not depart from the form of the jagati. And they say [the consecration] should be twenty-four. Twenty-four half-months make a year, [and] procreation. This sacrifice is procreation. Thus that procreation is effected. Therefore one desiring progeny should sacrifice with this. That has one jagati foot.
2.284: On this they say: 'Since the first day extends with gayatri, the second with tristubh, the third with jagati, the fourth with anustubh -- why then is this sacrifice declared to be of the jagati type?' One should answer: 'Just as one who has many horses or many mules goes yoking one after another -- even so, [in] this [rite], the jagati, ascending one meter after another, goes.' And further, because the sum of syllables in these chants when combined corresponds to the jagati, therefore it is called 'of the jagati type.' And because the jagati is the foremost of meters, therefore by its pre-eminence people name [things] after it; they who know thus name [things] after it. This [rite] extends over [only] two nights. For those who would make the rathantara the prstha on the third day with the verses 'Abhi tva vrsabha suta' -- because of 'abhi tve,' they do not depart from the form of the rathantara. The third day extends with a gayatri that is ayatayamna. 'Kas tam Indra tvavasav' is the Maitravaruna-saman. Their syllables correspond here. For those who would make the brhat the prstha on the fourth day with the verses 'Tam tva yajnebhir imaha' -- because of 'tam tve,' they do not depart from the form of the brhat. The fourth day extends with an anustubh that is ayatayamna. 'Vayam u tvam apurvya' is the Maitravaruna-saman.
2.285: This four-night [rite] indeed has interchanged meters. The earlier connection is of the brhati in the prsthas, the later of the gayatri; the earlier is of the usnik and kakubh in the arbhava, the later of the anustubh. Because these meters are made interlocking -- from an interlocking pairing, offspring and cattle are born -- it is for the sake of procreation. And indeed a person is interlocked with evils. By interlocked meters one drives away the interlocked evils. On this they say: 'What does the four-night [rite] follow?' One should answer: 'Day-and-night.' The four-night [rite] follows day-and-night. By the first day one covers the day; by the second, the mark of the day; by the third day, one covers the night; by the fourth, the mark of the forenoon. Day-and-night procreate all this. 'Let those that procreate all this procreate for me.' And one should say: 'The four-night [rite] follows the directions.' Four directions, and an upward fifth direction. Four are these days, and the night is the fifth. By this one prospers in the directions. Established in the directions, one goes to the heavenly world. Its upasad offerings include rice-cakes. A rice-cake is a womb, ghee is seed. When, having offered the rice-cake, one pours ghee over it, one thereby establishes seed in the womb. One whose seed is deposited in order procreates -- who knows thus. And to whatever deities one offers oblations, in those one prospers.
2.286: Now this is the [rite] of Prajapati. One desiring progeny should sacrifice with this. Prajapati desired: 'May I be abundant in offspring and cattle.' He saw this four-night [rite]. He brought it forth. He sacrificed with it. Its stomas are trivrt and pancadasa, saptadasa and ekavimsa. These are unequal-and-surplus pairings, procreative. One is less, the other more. From an unequal-and-surplus pairing, offspring and cattle are born. Trivrt and pancadasa -- that is a procreative pairing. Saptadasa and ekavimsa -- that is a procreative pairing. 'Let that procreative pairing procreate for me.' Then he became abundant in offspring and cattle. One becomes abundant in offspring and cattle who knows thus. And they say he feared the loss of those created offspring. He saw this four-night sacrifice. He brought it forth. He sacrificed with it. Its agnistomas are on both sides, and the ukthyas in the middle. The agnistoma is brahman; offspring and cattle are the ukthyas. By brahman, on both sides, he encloses offspring and cattle against loss.
2.287: Now this is Vasistha's Samsarpa (Creeping). Vasistha desired: 'May I creep through these worlds.' He saw this four-night sacrifice. He brought it forth. He sacrificed with it. Then he crept through these worlds. Because he crept through these worlds, that is the samsarpa-nature of the Samsarpa. One who knows thus creeps through these worlds. Three-by-three stomas, yoked, carry the day. Three-by-three [yoked] are the best for drawing among yoked [animals]. One goes by the yoked one to where one wishes to go. And they say: 'One desiring offspring should sacrifice with this.' For stoma is joined to stoma. From a joined pairing, offspring and cattle are born -- for the sake of procreation indeed.
2.288: Now this is Gautama's Catustoma (Four-stoma). People did not have faith in Gautama. 'A royal seer is untrustworthy,' [they said]. He desired: 'May they have faith in me; may I be trustworthy.' He saw this four-night sacrifice. He brought it forth. He sacrificed with it. Then they had faith in him; he became trustworthy. One becomes trustworthy who knows thus. This is the Krtastoma (Won-stoma). Faith is the 'won.' For whatever people carry away having won by a throw [of dice], they carry it by faith. Therefore it is Krtastoma. This is the four-ascending [rite] of the Angirases. By two-ascending the Adityas went to the heavenly world; by four-ascending, the Angirases. One goes to the heavenly world by four-ascending who knows thus. On this they say: 'This sacrificial rite is liable to falling; one might go to yonder world before [one's full] lifespan.' But because the chants descend again, therefore it is not liable to falling, and thereby it grants long life. Now this is the Visvajyotis (All-light). The gods desired: 'May we all be as light.' They saw this four-night sacrifice. They brought it forth. They sacrificed with it. Then they all were as light. Therefore, even now, when people see any of the gods, they say: 'We have seen [one who shines] as light.' He who desires splendor of brahman should sacrifice with this. He shines as if with light who attains splendor of brahman.
2.289: Now these are the five-night [rites]. Five are the conquests. By five utterances the gods conquered this [world]. 'Let me conquer as much as the gods conquered.' The sacrifice is five-fold. 'Let me attain the full measure of the sacrifice.' Animals are five-fold. 'Let animals in the full measure of animals stand by me, secured.' Food is five-fold. 'Let food in the full measure of food stand by me, secured.' Five are these days. Five seasons make a year. The year is Prajapati, the sacrifice. By this one attains the year, Prajapati, the sacrifice. The gods and asuras competed. They did not conquer either by clubs or by arrows. They said: 'We do not conquer by clubs or arrows. Let our victory in speech, in brahman, prevail.' They considered: 'Whichever of us first speaks against the others, those shall overcome them.' The gods said to the asuras: 'You speak first.' 'You indeed,' [said] the asuras to the gods.
2.290: 'One is ours,' said the gods. 'One is ours,' said the asuras. The gods said 'mind'; the asuras said 'speech.' The gods by mind wrested away the speech of the asuras. Therefore what one grasps by mind, that one speaks by speech. 'Two are ours,' said the gods. 'Two are ours,' said the asuras. The gods said 'inbreath and outbreath'; the asuras said 'day and night.' The gods by inbreath and outbreath wrested away day and night from the asuras. Therefore one who lives long breathes through many days and nights. 'Three are ours,' said the gods. 'Three are ours,' said the asuras. The gods said 'these three worlds'; the asuras said 'the three knowledges.' The gods by these worlds wrested away the three knowledges from the asuras. Therefore these knowledges are proclaimed in these very worlds. 'Four are ours,' said the gods. 'Four are ours,' said the asuras. The gods said 'four-footed animals'; the asuras said 'four directions.' The gods by the four-footed animals wrested away the four directions from the asuras. Therefore, in whatever direction one leads an animal, one goes through it all as conquered, ordained, and established. 'Five are ours,' said the gods, [meaning] the seasons. But the asuras could not find [anything for five]. For there is nothing that is 'fivefold' [in their domain]. The asuras, finding no basis, merely repeating 'five, five,' were defeated. Then the gods prevailed, and the asuras were defeated.
2.291: These utterances are indeed victory. The five-night [rite] is victory. One who knows thus conquers and conquers. This is the complete victory over all; this is the complete defeat of all. The gods conquered all the asuras by this. The asuras were completely defeated. One completely conquers the rival enemy; one's rival enemy is completely defeated, who knows thus. Now these [rites] are continuous. Just as pieces of wood are strung together by a strap in a chariot or wagon, so the five-night [rite] is strung together by stomas. The stomas are indeed the vital breaths. A person is held together by the vital breaths. One who knows thus is held together. Two-by-two stomas, yoked, carry each day. Two-by-two [yoked] are the best for drawing among yoked [animals].
2.292: Now, the [rite] whose three-day [portion] has jyotis, go, and ayus [stomas], with a twenty-five-fold mahavrata, and an atiratra of all stomas -- one desiring food should sacrifice with this. On this they say: 'The mahavrata should not be approached before [the completion of] a year.' Because the three-day [portion] has jyotis, go, and ayus: this world is jyotis, this [middle world] is go, yonder [world] is ayus. These worlds are the seasons, the year. The seasons, the year, cook all food. They approach the food that is conquered through the year. The mahavrata is twenty-five-fold. Food is twenty-five-fold -- for the securing of food. The atiratra has all stomas, for conquering and surpassing all worlds. For all this is indeed the stomas. 'Those stomas which we have approached one by one, having attained them together and established in them, let us attain the surplus.' Now the [rite] whose three-day [portion] has trivrt, pancadasa, and saptadasa, with a twenty-five-fold mahavrata, and an atiratra of all stomas -- one also desiring food should sacrifice with this. On this they say: 'The mahavrata should not be approached before a year.' Because the three-day [portion] has trivrt, pancadasa, and saptadasa: this world is trivrt, this [middle world] is pancadasa, yonder [world] is saptadasa. These worlds are the seasons, the year. The seasons, the year, cook all food. They approach the food conquered through the year. The mahavrata is twenty-five-fold. Food is twenty-five-fold -- for the securing of food. The atiratra has all stomas, for conquering and surpassing all worlds. For all this is indeed the stomas.
2.293: Now, the [rite] whose three-day [portion] has jyotis, go, and ayus, with a twenty-four-fold day, and an atiratra of all stomas -- whoever desires 'let my mahavrata be approached indirectly' should sacrifice with this. Because a twenty-four-fold day occurs, thereby his mahavrata is approached indirectly. Whatever himkara, prastava, or pratihara occurs therein, by that his twenty-five-fold [nature] is attained. The atiratra has all stomas, for conquering and surpassing all worlds. For all this is indeed the stomas. 'Those stomas which we have approached one by one, having attained them together and established in them, let us attain the surplus.' Now, the [rite] whose three-day [portion] has trivrt, pancadasa, and saptadasa, with a twenty-four-fold day, and a jyotis atiratra -- whoever also desires 'let my mahavrata be approached indirectly' should sacrifice with this. Because a twenty-four-fold day occurs, thereby his mahavrata is approached indirectly. Whatever himkara, prastava, or pratihara occurs therein, by that his twenty-five-fold [nature] is attained.
2.294: Now, the [rite] whose two jyotis agnistomas are on both sides, with go and ayus ukthyas in between, and a jyotis atiratra in the middle -- one desiring progeny should sacrifice with this. This is the complete procreation, namely the jyotis atiratra. And procreation is from the middle of the self. Because the jyotis atiratra is in the middle, one thereby places procreation in the middle of the self; one becomes abundant and procreates. The go and ayus ukthyas are in between. The ukthyas are animals. Their chant-verses number five hundred. By animals on both sides one encloses the progeny. The two jyotis agnistomas are on both sides. The jyotis agnistoma is brahman. By brahman on both sides one encloses progeny and cattle against loss. One whose wealth is not carried away knows thus. Now, the [rite] whose two jyotis agnistomas are on both sides, with go and ayus ukthyas in between, and a jyotis agnistoma in the middle -- whoever desires 'may one possessed of brahman-splendor be born among my offspring' should sacrifice with this. Radiance, brahman-splendor, is the jyotis agnistoma. Procreation is from the middle of the self. Because the jyotis agnistoma is in the middle, one thereby places radiance in the middle of the self; one possessed of brahman-splendor is born among his offspring. The go and ayus ukthyas are in between. The ukthyas are animals. Their chant-verses number five hundred. By animals on both sides one encloses the offspring possessed of brahman-splendor. The two atiratra [rites] are on both sides. These two worlds are the atiratra rites. By these two worlds on both sides one encloses progeny and cattle against loss.
2.295: Now these are the six-night [rites]. The Maruts desired: 'May we be the strongest, most powerful, most numerous, most vigorous of the gods; may we conquer the heavenly world.' They saw this six-night sacrifice. They brought it forth. They sacrificed with it. Then they became the strongest, most powerful, most numerous, most vigorous of the gods, and conquered the heavenly world. One becomes the strongest, most powerful, most numerous, most vigorous among one's own and conquers the heavenly world, who knows thus. They go with a prsthya six-day [rite]. Prosperity, eminence, is the prsthas. One thereby ascends to prosperity, eminence, the prsthas. Strength, vigor, is the prsthas. By strength, by vigor, they go. The prsthas are the heavenly world. They go ascending to the heavenly world. Their consecration is at the submersion in the Sarasvati.
2.296: The offering-hall and havirdhana have wheels; the sacrificial post has a mortar-shaped base -- for ascending and for non-falling. For whatever they were doing there [otherwise], that would be their falling. They go with the samyaparasa [measurement rods]. These are indeed the steps to the heavenly world, the samyaparasas. By the steps of the heavenly world they go, stepping. They go striking and shouting. This is the form of strength -- what is struck and shouted. They go along the Sarasvati. Speech is Sarasvati. Speech is the path of the gods. By the path of the gods they go. They go upstream. The heavenly world is as if upstream. They attain the heavenly world. They go eastward and northward. The heavenly world is as if east and north. They go ascending to the heavenly world. They go up to the rapids, to the source. This is the end of speech -- the rapids, the source. Where the end of speech is, there is the heavenly world.
2.297: When the Maruts set out with this [rite], Indra and Agni took note: 'If they accomplish this, the Maruts will become all this.' They won from them a thousand as the prize for attending the session. That caused dissension among them. It was their evil that was ground apart. Therefore one should not desire a share of the disputed property, lest one partake of evil. Of those two [Indra and Agni], they yielded out of awe. They stood up [saying]: 'We have not given.' These are their surpluses, which they achieve by completing [the rite]: that one [surplus]. When one of them dies, that is one. When ten cows become a hundred, that is one. When a hundred cows become a thousand, that is one. With this, the Sthura householders of Purayu [sacrificed]. The [warriors] of the Trikarta or Salva tribes, having risen up against them with invasions, conquered them. They slew their householder. They sat down around this slain householder, lamenting. But Dhruvagopa recognized him [as one] running with the Sankasina. He ascended from the ahavaniya fire upward to the heavenly world. He said: 'Do not lament! The one you lament -- he has ascended from the ahavaniya upward to the heavenly world.' Those among them who were slain there went to the heavenly world. But those who survived are even now like the poorer ones among the Sthuras. They were indeed of worse villages than before.
2.298: Now, the [rite] whose visvajit and abhijit atiratra rites are on both sides, with go, ayus, go, ayus -- four ukthyas in the middle -- those desiring cattle should approach this. This world is the abhijit, yonder [world] is the visvajit. These four ukthyas are in the middle. The ukthyas are animals. Their chant-verses number a thousand. By this world and by yonder world on both sides they enclose these animals; they secure animals. They have abundant cattle. By this indeed Indra and Agni won a thousand from the Maruts. [See also 2.249: Indra won a thousand from the Maruts.] Agni is the abhijit, Indra the visvajit. These four ukthyas are in the middle. The ukthyas are animals. Their chant-verses number a thousand. It was this thousand that Indra and Agni enclosed on both sides and carried off. By these two worlds and by Indra and Agni on both sides, animals stand enclosed for one who knows thus. Their bathing [place] is at Parinada, on the western end of Kuruksetra, for the consecration. They go eastward through the middle of Kuruksetra. This is the divine place of sacrifice of the gods -- Kuruksetra. They go by the divine place of sacrifice of the gods. Their avabhrtha [final bath] is in the Yamuna. The Yamuna is the heavenly world. They attain the heavenly world. Now, the [rite] whose three-day upward and three-day downward [portions] -- whoever desires 'may I go to the heavenly world and stand established in this world' should sacrifice with this. By the three-day upward, one goes to the heavenly world; by the three-day downward, one descends from there. One goes to the heavenly world and stands established in this world.
2.299: Now these are the seven-night [rites]. Seven are the meters. The meters are all speech. He attains sovereignty and lordship over speech who attains prosperity. 'Let me attain sovereignty and lordship over speech.' Therefore one sacrifices with a seven-night [rite]. Seven are the vital breaths. By the vital breaths, offspring and cattle stand enclosed. 'By those vital breaths by which offspring and cattle stand enclosed, let offspring and cattle stand enclosed for me.' By the vital breaths the gods went to the heavenly world. 'By those vital breaths by which the gods went to the heavenly world, let me go to the heavenly world by those vital breaths.' Seven are the chief vital breaths. These vital breaths whose evils are driven away are the chief ones. 'By the measure of those vital breaths whose evils are driven away, let me extend the sacrifice.' The seven sages, desiring prosperity, performed austerities. They saw this seven-night sacrifice. They brought it forth.
2.300: They were day-sharers. Then for them, one prospered by a day, one by a day, one by a day. The most blessed of those who attain prosperity, the most meritorious of worlds, is that which the seven sages attained by this. 'That most blessed of those who attain prosperity, that most meritorious of worlds which the seven sages attained by this -- may I attain that.' Therefore one sacrifices with a seven-night [rite]. Seven are these days, seven the stomas. The day carries the stoma; a day, a stoma; a day, a stoma. This is the abundance of the days -- that different stomas carry them. That very abundance of the days is created.
