And all seven together correspond to twenty-one: trivrt and trayastrimsa -- those two [make] twenty-one; pancadasa and trinava -- those two [make] twenty-one; saptadasa and pancavimsa -- those two [make] twenty-one; ekavimsa itself is twenty-one. Twelve months, five seasons, three worlds, and yonder sun -- twenty-one. They who sacrifice with the seven-night [rite] attain the same world as this [sun]. One who has attained this -- his world prospers.
2.301: With this, Janaka of Videha wished to sacrifice. When the brahmanas of Kuru-Pancala said to him: 'Let us make you sacrifice with this rite, let us make you sacrifice with that rite,' he would say: 'No. No brahmana among you was more learned in brahman than the one who arranged this sacrificial rite for me. Make me sacrifice with this alone.' 'So be it.' They made him sacrifice with it. This is indeed a prsthya six-day [rite] with the twenty-five-fold mahavrata. From that they approach the night. Prosperity, eminence, is the prsthas. One thereby ascends to prosperity, eminence, the prsthas. Food is the mahavrata -- for the securing of food. On this they say: 'The mahavrata should not be approached before a year.' Because the prsthya six-day [rite] occurs -- the prsthas are the seasons. The seasons are the year. The year cooks all food. They approach the food conquered through the year.
2.302: On this they say: 'This is the equivalent of day-and-night: the agnistoma is this day.' And they also say: 'The agnistoma is the year. The year is Prajapati. The mahavrata is of Prajapati. The chants of the agnistoma-saman should be of the twenty-five-fold [type].' 'Then they should descend to the night with the twenty-one-fold ukthya.' Thus these two sacrificial rites are differentiated. Now, the [rite] whose prsthya six-day has [specific] stomas, with the mahavrata on the fifth day, [followed by] a visvajit-stoma atiratra with all prsthas -- with this Kratu Kariradri sacrificed, desiring prosperity and food. The self is the prsthya six-day; food is the mahavrata. 'I shall place food from the middle of the self; I shall be an eater of food, the foremost among my own.' And the year is the prsthya six-day. 'May food enclosed by the year stand by me.' The atiratra is a visvajit with all prsthas. Prosperity, eminence, is the prsthas. 'I shall ascend to prosperity, eminence, the prsthas.' But the brahmana told him: 'As long as he lives, he will be an eater of food, the foremost among his own. But he will die headless.' The head of the sacrifice is the mahavrata. 'Just as if one were to cut off a head and press it against the armpits -- it would not rejoin. Such is what he has done,' he said. 'Those who cut off his head will know.' And it was so. As long as Kratu Kariradri lived, he was an eater of food, the foremost among his own. But he died headless. Those who cut off his head were indeed the Bharyakas, who knew [what they were doing].
2.303: Now this is the Babara-seven-night [rite]. Its first day has the saptadasa [stoma], then trivrt, then pancadasa, then ekavimsa, then trinava, then trayastrimsa, with a jyotis atiratra. With this he sacrificed, desiring prosperity and food. The self is the prsthya six-day. The mouth is trivrt; food is saptadasa. 'I shall first place food from the mouth; I shall be an eater of food, the foremost among my own.' And the year is the prsthya six-day. 'May food secured before the year stand by me.' The jyotis atiratra -- this is the well-known divine ford, the jyotis atiratra.
2.304: [They said:] 'Govarna Khalateya will become worse. He has placed food first from the mouth. When food reaches the mouth, then indeed it putrefies: if he vomits, it putrefies; if he spits, it putrefies. Having putrefied the food, he will become worse.' And so it was. He became worse. They told him: 'Your own pupil Govarna Khalateya -- he it was who made the counter-utterance against you.' He said to him: 'You must make me sacrifice with this [rite].' 'So be it,' he said. 'I shall repair you as one would repair a broken chariot or wagon.' He made the trivrt the first day, then pancadasa, then ekavimsa, then trinava, then trayastrimsa, then saptadasa, then saptadasa. Two saptadasas in succession. With this, Govarna Khalateya made his teacher sacrifice. The self is the prsthya six-day; food is the saptadasa. 'Food will ascend upward from the self. That food, secured, will stand above you -- from saptadasa to saptadasa, from food to food.'
2.305: These Babaras among the Kurus are merely traders. He used to wander about at the divine place of sacrifice [saying]: 'This he sacrificed, this he did not sacrifice.' Now, the [rite] whose three-day [portion] has jyotis, go, and ayus, with a visvajit, all-prstha ukthya, a sodasi, a chandomapavamana, an all-stoma ukthya, a twenty-five-fold mahavrata, and a jyotis atiratra -- whoever desires 'having approached the abhiplava, having approached the prsthas, having approached the chandomas, may the mahavrata be approached for me' should sacrifice with this. Having approached the abhiplava, having approached the prsthas, having approached the chandomas, the mahavrata becomes approached for him. Food is the mahavrata -- for the securing of food. On this they say: 'The mahavrata should not be approached before a year.' Because the prsthya six-day occurs -- the prsthas are the seasons. The seasons are the year. The year cooks all food. They approach the food conquered through the year. The mahavrata is twenty-five-fold. Food is twenty-five-fold -- for the securing of food. The jyotis atiratra -- this is the well-known divine ford, the jyotis atiratra.
2.306: The prsthya six-day. Now, whatever is the seventh day: its bahispavamana is twenty-four-fold; the later chants are thirty-three-fold; the mahavrata is twenty-five-fold; the agnistoma-saman is thirty-four-fold. 'The sacrifice becomes as it were exhausted when this prsthya six-day is exhausted.' Because the bahispavamana of the seventh day is twenty-four-fold -- twenty-four half-months make a year. The year is the sacrifice. They thereby take hold of the sacrifice at the opening of the sacrifice. And the gayatri has twenty-four syllables. By gayatris one extends anew a sacrificial rite that has been attained. Therefore another gayatri is able to raise up an attained sacrificial rite. The later chants are thirty-three-fold. Thirty-three are the deities -- for the attainment of the deities. The mahavrata is twenty-five-fold. Food is twenty-five-fold -- for the securing of food. The agnistoma-saman is thirty-four-fold. Thirty-three are the deities. Prajapati is the thirty-fourth. In the deities together with Prajapati, one thereby finds establishment at the end of the sacrifice. Again, the prsthya six-day. Now whatever is the seventh day: its pavamanas are twenty-four-fold; the [stoma is] a thirty-three-fold punarabhyavarti; the mahavrata is twenty-five-fold; the agnistoma-saman is thirty-four-fold. 'The sacrifice becomes as it were exhausted when this prsthya six-day is exhausted.' Because the pavamanas of the seventh day are twenty-four-fold -- 'let the sacrifice be taken hold of at the opening of each pressing.' The punarabhyavarti is thirty-three-fold. Thirty-three are the deities -- for the attainment of the deities. The mahavrata is twenty-five-fold. Food is twenty-five-fold -- for the securing of food. The agnistoma-saman is thirty-four-fold. Thirty-three are the deities. Prajapati is the thirty-fourth.
2.307: Again, the prsthya six-day. Now whatever is the seventh day: it is entirely twenty-four-fold; the mahavrata is twenty-five-fold; the agnistoma-saman is thirty-four-fold. 'The sacrifice becomes as it were exhausted when this prsthya six-day is exhausted.' Because the seventh day is entirely twenty-four-fold -- 'let the sacrifice be taken hold of by the entire day.' The mahavrata is twenty-five-fold. Food is twenty-five-fold -- for the securing of food. The agnistoma-saman is thirty-four-fold. Thirty-three are the deities. Prajapati is the thirty-fourth. In the deities together with Prajapati, one thereby finds establishment at the end of the sacrifice. Again, the prsthya six-day. Now whatever is the seventh day: its pavamanas are chandoma rites; the punarabhyavarti is thirty-four-fold; the mahavrata is twenty-five-fold; the agnistoma-saman is thirty-three-fold. Whoever desires 'having approached the prsthas, having approached the chandomas, may the mahavrata be approached for me' should sacrifice with this. Having approached the prsthas, having approached the chandomas, the mahavrata becomes approached for him. Food is the mahavrata -- for the securing of food. The punarabhyavarti is thirty-four-fold. Thirty-three are the deities. Prajapati is the thirty-fourth. In the deities together with Prajapati, one thereby finds establishment. The mahavrata is twenty-five-fold. Food is twenty-five-fold -- for the securing of food.
2.308: Now these two are the Samsangyas (Interlocking rites). Jamadagni was the purohita of the Mahenas. He surpassed them in wealth. They then tried to harm him. He considered: 'How might I slay seven times seven of their domestic animals in a single day?' He saw these two sacrifices. He brought them forth. He sacrificed with them. Then he slew seven times seven of their domestic animals in a single day. This is an unbroken thunderbolt. He used to set them in motion with just this unbroken thunderbolt. Just as one crushes sesame seeds with a pressing-stone, so he used to see them crushed and scattered at dawn. He who has a rival, who practices sorcery, who competes -- let him sacrifice with these two. With this unbroken thunderbolt, one destroys the hated evil rival. And they say: 'One desiring offspring should sacrifice with these two.' For stoma is joined to stoma; from a joined pairing, offspring and cattle are born -- for the sake of procreation indeed. Now this seventh day has the saptadasa [stoma]. On that they approach the mahavrata. Prajapati is saptadasa.
2.309: Now these are the eight-night [rites]. Brhaspati desired: 'May I be radiant, possessed of brahman-splendor; may I attain glory among the gods.' He saw this eight-night sacrifice. He brought it forth. He sacrificed with it. Then he became radiant, possessed of brahman-splendor, and attained glory among the gods. For he attained that glory when he obtained the office of purohita among them. One becomes radiant, possessed of brahman-splendor, and attains glory among the gods, who knows thus. And they say: 'One desiring long life should sacrifice with this.' Prana is the gayatri. This sacrifice corresponds to the gayatri. Therefore one desiring long life should sacrifice with this. And they say: 'One desiring cattle should sacrifice with this.' The gayatri has eight syllables. Animals have eight hooves. Animals are the gayatri. This sacrifice corresponds to the gayatri. Therefore one desiring cattle should sacrifice with this. Prajapati desired: 'May I go from this world to yonder world.' He saw this eight-night sacrifice. He brought it forth. He sacrificed with it. Then from this world he reached (asta) yonder world. Because he reached (asta) yonder world from this world, that is the eight-night-nature (astaratra) of the eight-night [rites]. One who knows thus reaches from this world to yonder world. On this they say: 'What does the eight-night [rite] follow?' One should answer: 'The directions.' The eight-night [rite] follows the directions. Four directions and four intermediate directions. The upward direction is the ninth. Eight are these days, the night is the ninth.
2.310: Now, the [rite] whose jyotis agnistoma is [first], then the prsthya six-day, then the jyotis agnistoma -- whoever desires 'may I be radiant, possessed of brahman-splendor in this world; may I go to the heavenly world; may I be radiant, possessed of brahman-splendor in yonder world' should sacrifice with this. Because the jyotis agnistoma is in front -- radiance and brahman-splendor is the jyotis agnistoma -- thereby one becomes radiant, possessed of brahman-splendor in this world. The prsthya six-day occurs -- the heavenly world is the prsthya six-day; by this one goes to the heavenly world. The jyotis agnistoma is at the end -- radiance and brahman-splendor is the jyotis agnistoma -- thereby one becomes possessed of brahman-splendor in yonder world. Now, the [rite] whose jyotis atiratra is [first], then the prsthya six-day, then the jyotis atiratra -- whoever desires 'may I stand established in this world; may I go to the heavenly world; may I stand established in yonder world' should sacrifice with this. Because the jyotis atiratra is in front -- this is the complete establishment, the jyotis atiratra -- thereby one becomes established in this world. The prsthya six-day occurs -- the heavenly world is the prsthya six-day; by this one goes to the heavenly world.
2.311: Now, the [rite] whose jyotis agnistoma is [first], then the prsthya six-day, then the jyotis atiratra -- whoever desires 'may I be radiant, possessed of brahman-splendor in this world; may I go to the heavenly world; may I stand established in yonder world' should sacrifice with this. Because the jyotis agnistoma is in front -- radiance and brahman-splendor is the jyotis agnistoma -- thereby one becomes radiant, possessed of brahman-splendor in this world. The prsthya six-day occurs -- the heavenly world is the prsthya six-day; by this one goes to the heavenly world. The jyotis atiratra is at the end -- this is the complete establishment, the jyotis atiratra -- thereby one becomes established in yonder world. Now, the [rite] whose jyotis atiratra is [first], then the prsthya six-day, then the jyotis agnistoma -- whoever desires 'may I stand established in this world; may I go to the heavenly world; may I be radiant, possessed of brahman-splendor in yonder world' should sacrifice with this. Because the jyotis atiratra is in front -- this is the complete establishment, the jyotis atiratra -- thereby one becomes established in this world. The prsthya six-day occurs -- the heavenly world is the prsthya six-day; by this one goes to the heavenly world.
2.312: Now this is the prsthya six-day [rite] with the twenty-five-fold mahavrata and an atiratra of all stomas. One desiring food should sacrifice with this. On this they say: 'The mahavrata should not be approached before a year.' Because the prsthya six-day occurs -- the prsthas are the seasons. The seasons are the year. The year cooks all food. They approach the food conquered through the year. The mahavrata is twenty-five-fold. Food is twenty-five-fold -- for the securing of food. The atiratra has all stomas, for conquering and surpassing all worlds. For all this is indeed the stomas. 'Those stomas which we have approached one by one, having attained them together and established in them, let us attain the surplus.' Now, the [rite] whose prsthya six-day has [specific] stomas, with a twenty-four-fold day, and a visvajit, all-prstha atiratra -- whoever desires 'let my mahavrata be approached indirectly' should sacrifice with this. Because a twenty-four-fold day occurs, thereby his mahavrata is approached indirectly. Whatever himkara, prastava, or pratihara occurs therein, by that his twenty-five-fold [nature] is attained. The atiratra is a visvajit with all prsthas. Prosperity, eminence, is the prsthas.
2.313: Now, the [rite] whose prsthya six-day has a jyotis agnistoma in the middle and a jyotis atiratra at the end -- whoever desires 'may one possessed of brahman-splendor be born among my offspring' should sacrifice with this. The self is the prsthya six-day. Radiance, brahman-splendor, is the jyotis agnistoma. Procreation is from the middle of the self. Because the jyotis agnistoma is in the middle, one thereby places radiance in the middle of the self; one possessed of brahman-splendor is born among his offspring. The jyotis atiratra is at the end -- this is the well-known divine ford, the jyotis atiratra. 'By the well-known divine ford, unharmed, may we unharmed attain the surplus.' Now, the [rite] whose visvajit and abhijit agnistomas are on both sides, with a six-day [rite] having jyotis on both sides in the middle -- one desiring cattle should sacrifice with this. These four ukthyas are in the middle. The ukthyas are animals. Their chant-verses number a thousand. And these four agnistomas are on both sides. The jyotis agnistoma is brahman. By brahman on both sides one encloses cattle against loss. One whose wealth is not carried away knows thus. However, one might become just like what one is when one sacrifices. One should sacrifice with this when one considers oneself as if at the end of prosperity.
2.314: Now these are the nine-night [rites]. One desiring radiance should sacrifice with this, the nine-night [rite]. The trivrt stoma is radiance. This sacrifice corresponds to the trivrt stoma. Therefore one desiring radiance should sacrifice with this. And they say: 'One desiring brahman-splendor should sacrifice with this.' The trivrt stoma is brahman. This sacrifice corresponds to the trivrt stoma. Therefore one desiring brahman-splendor should sacrifice with this. And they say: 'One desiring long life should sacrifice with this.' The trivrt stoma is prana. Nine are these days, the night is the tenth. Nine are the vital breaths in a person, the navel is the tenth. By this one conquers the vital breaths, one attains the vital breaths. On this they say: 'What does the nine-night [rite] follow?' One should answer: 'The directions.' The nine-night [rite] follows the directions. Four directions, four intermediate directions, the upward direction is the ninth, the downward the tenth. By this one prospers in the directions.
2.315: Now, the [rite] whose [days are] jyotis, go, ayus as a three-day, jyotis, go, ayus as a three-day, jyotis, go, ayus with an atiratra -- whoever desires 'may I ascend these worlds without interruption' should sacrifice with this. Because the three-day has jyotis, go, and ayus -- this world is jyotis, this [middle world] is go, yonder is ayus -- one ascends these worlds without interruption by this. The three-day has an atiratra as the topmost. These worlds are indeed of this nature: this world is like an agnistoma, this [middle world] is like an ukthya, yonder is like an atiratra. Just as these worlds are of this nature, so one ascends them. Now, the [rite] whose [days are all] jyotis, go, ayus with atiratra; jyotis, go, ayus with atiratra; jyotis, go, ayus with atiratra -- whoever also desires 'may I ascend these worlds without interruption' should sacrifice with this. Because the [day] has jyotis, go, ayus with atiratra -- this world is jyotis, this [middle world] is go, yonder is ayus -- one ascends these worlds without interruption by this. The three-day periods end with atiratra. And these worlds are indeed of this nature: this world is like an agnistoma, this [middle world] is like an ukthya, yonder is like an atiratra.
2.366: The sandhisama -- by that it would not have been chanted, they say. Moreover, speech (vac) is the rathantara, speech is the asvina, speech alone is able to pervade speech in return -- 'this speech pervades speech in return,' they say. This indeed is the second recognized sandhisama, namely the jarabodhiya. Since it is the second recognized sandhisama, by that it would not have been chanted, they say. Now the srudhiya is renowned. The samavrkti and the sandhisaman-songs [belong together]. Since we have plucked with these two sandhisaman-songs, 'may our cattle follow us to the world of the world-possessing saman,' they say. Now the nanadagaurivita songs are world-possessing saman-songs: 'may they follow us to a greater world,' they say. They say the asvina should be recited three times only. For speech consists of three hundred [syllables]. These are three worlds. If at an atiratra within an atiratra one were to recite the asvina, that would be a disruption of speech. One should instruct the hotr: 'Recite the asvina-kratus at other atiratras only; the asvina should be recited three times only' -- at the beginning, in the middle, and at the end. This indeed is the complete asvina -- that which corresponds with the rathantara.
2.367: Now these are the enya [nights], the single black ones. These nights are indeed called enya. In them is the black one for the sake of pairing, for procreation. He who knows thus is born forth through pairing. They approached him from the front and from above. If they approached from the front, they would be led by a male, they say. If from above -- for from behind a male deposits seed in the female, for the depositing of seed. Prajapati indeed was at first created alone in this [world], seeing no other second. He considered: 'I indeed was born first; I am the best. Is there anyone else here besides me?' He looked about. He saw something standing above him to the north, greater than himself. He asked it: 'What are you, that you are this?' 'I am brahman,' it said. It said: 'We two indeed were born first; we two are the best. Let us unite together.' 'No,' it said. 'You are seized by evil (papman).' 'Where then is that evil of mine?' 'It is in your head.' 'Remove it from me.' 'So be it.' It removed it. It placed it on the neck. It pressed it together. That was like something gripped: 'Remove it.' It removed it. It placed it at the waist. It pressed it together. That was like something gripped: 'Remove it completely.' It removed it. It placed it at the ankles. It pressed it together. That was like something gripped: 'Remove it completely.' It removed it. 'Stand upon it with your feet.'
2.368: That is the protuberance (ucchlarikha) from the foot. 'Split it into three,' [brahman said]. He split it into three. His prosperity (sri) became thus: a third cattle, a third sleep, a third shadow. Therefore these are most abundant for the most excellent one -- most abundant cattle, greatest shadow, and he sleeps [most]. He considered: 'The evil that he told me of -- let me cut it off entirely.' He divided this very brahman eightfold. The gayatri has eight syllables. The gayatri is brahman. He divided brahman itself eightfold. With eight trivrt-[stomas] he drove it away. With eight pancadasa-[stomas] he drove it further away. With eight saptadasa-[stomas] he drove it further away. With eight ekavimsa-[stomas] he drove it further away. With eight trinava-[stomas] he sent it to the farthest end. With eight trayastrimsas he cut it off completely. This is the atiratra at the end. His evil is thus driven away. Moreover, this is Prajapati, he who shines yonder [the sun]. He shines with evil removed. He has no shadow. Therefore one should neither stand upon a shadow nor urinate upon it -- for that grasps evil. Having slept, one should sip water -- for that removes evil. One should kill an attended cow -- for that removes evil. Those who, knowing thus, undergo these nights rise up having removed all evil. They are, however, likely to become without cattle -- for cattle are the uktha-chants, for he drove away cattle there too. Those who desire prosperity should undergo these nights. Indeed formerly the brahmins of the Kuru-Pancalas, desiring prosperity, used to undergo these. But they were without cattle, unfortunate. Their cattle were among their kinsmen. Therefore when one returning from a cow-expedition arrived, they would anoint a cow for him. Thus they gave to one who should not be given to. Thereupon Satyayani said: 'One should not speak of that which is a censure. When one is unwilling, prosperity comes; when one is unwilling, cattle and wealth come. Thinking, we shall become possessors of cattle and shall be the best and shall obtain, they should undergo these nights.' So it is indeed. The vaisvanara is the concluding atiratra.
2.369: They offer an animal victim to Savitr on the day before. Savitr is the impeller of the gods. Impelled by Savitr, they thus undertake the year. For who is worthy to undertake this without being impelled? On the next day they offer one to Prajapati. The gods have Prajapati as the best. They thereby gratify the deities starting from the best. He who, having propitiated the best, then seeks the others -- all those are indeed propitiated for him. And he who, having propitiated the best, then also attends to the others -- all those become satisfied and pleased for him. The gods have Prajapati as the best. They thereby gratify all the deities starting from the best. [The animal is] white, hairy, a hornless steer, with a dewlap, with uneven legs on one side. That it is white and hairy -- that is the form of sheep. That it is a hornless steer -- that is the form of horses. That it has a dewlap -- that is the form of spotted [animals]. That it has uneven legs on one side -- that is the form of cattle. That it is a goat -- that is the form of goats. This is the sacrificial essence of all cattle. With the sacrificial essence of all cattle they thereby gratify Prajapati, the father, the year. He, gratified, gratifies them.
2.370: Indra perceived this astaka [the eighth-day rite]. He consecrated himself for it. He attained it with a single one. Because he attained it with a single one, that is the ekastaka-nature of the ekastaka. Those who, knowing thus, consecrate themselves, attain everything; they all become equinoctial, for he became the visuvat [equinox]. If they were to consecrate themselves before [the proper time], they would consecrate themselves in the world of the ancestors and would be short-lived. If they were to consecrate themselves after, it would be as if one wished to flee and might or might not catch [the quarry] -- such is that. They should consecrate themselves on this very astaka. It would be like mounting a chariot that has been brought up, or like sitting upon a kneeling elephant. 'In what direction do the sattra-performers [go]?' they ask. One should say: 'Neither hither nor yonder -- like the sun yonder.' For these are neither hither nor yonder. They are crosswise. Nor is the sun yonder going hither or yonder -- he is crosswise. For six months he goes southward, for six northward.
2.371: Having undertaken this, they go toward Indra, Prajapati, the inspired one, the station, the rtupatra [vessel of seasons], all the gods, and the heavenly world. They turn along the circuit of this one. They turn along the circuit of this one; they are released following its release. This is its censure: that they go to the pressing day during the waning [moon], they reach the culmination during the waning [period]. They should perform twelve consecrations (diksa) and three upasad days. By that they obtain both. But they say: 'By the upasad alone they obtain both.' But this is the censure: that they enter the concluding bath in the cold season without welcoming the waters. The frost, as it were, rouses anger. He who would pronounce a curse upon those who have done thus -- 'so indeed they shall be' -- so it would be. On the new-moon night of Phalguna, after that, they should consecrate themselves on the fifth or sixth day.
2.372: The cows indeed first sat in a sattra, seeking to obtain food. They rose on the tenth month, thinking: 'We have obtained all food.' These have horns. But their [companions] said, as it were: 'Let us complete all these twelve months.' They exceeded the duration. In the twelfth month, their horns fell off. These are the hornless ones. Therefore sattra-performers shave their topknots in the twelfth month, for at that time they become similar to the cows. They rose up, having obtained -- having completed the sattra, having completed all food. Therefore these [cows] rise in all twelve months, even in the rainy and winter seasons. For their sattra-conquest was secured, it was obtained for them. Those who, knowing thus, complete twelve months, rise up having obtained all food. The sattra is indeed its own daksina. By the first two upasad days they touch the hair, by the second two the skin, by the third the flesh, by the fourth the bone, by the fifth the marrow. Whatever of theirs remains untouched there, that they touch by the sixth.
2.373: Prajapati indeed was here at first. He desired: 'May I become many; may I be reproduced; may I attain abundance.' He performed tapas. He perceived this vaisvanara introductory atiratra. From that he generated day and night. He was established in day and night. Since this vaisvanara is the introductory atiratra, from that they generate day and night and are established in day and night. He desired: 'Let me be further reproduced.' From this very vaisvanara introductory atiratra he fashioned a three-day sequence -- from the agnistoma he fashioned jyotis [light], from the ukthya-days the cow, from the night ayus [life]. From that three-day sequence he generated the three seasons; he was established in the three seasons. Since this three-day sequence exists, from it they generate the three seasons and are established in the three seasons. He desired: 'Let me be further reproduced.' He extended this very sadaha [six-day period] beyond. There became two sadahas. From those two sadahas he generated twelve months; he was established in the twelve months. Since these two sadahas exist, from them they generate twelve months and are established in the twelve months. He desired: 'Let me be further reproduced.' He extended these very two sadahas beyond. There became four sadahas. From those four sadahas he generated twenty-four half-months; he was established in twenty-four half-months.
2.374: Having generated this entire world, he placed it upon himself by means of the prsthya sadaha. Since this prsthya sadaha exists, by means of it they generate the entire world and place it upon themselves. The prstha-chants are eminence. In eminence they are thereby established. The prstha-chants are prosperity (sri). In prosperity they are thereby established. The prstha-chants are preeminence. In preeminence they are thereby established. The prstha-chants are splendor (tejas). In splendor they are thereby established. The abhiplava sadaha is indeed the cattle; the prsthya [sadaha] is the self (atman). When, having performed the abhiplava sadaha, they then perform the prsthya, they place the person (purusa) over the cattle. Therefore the person stands above, as it were ruling over the cattle. The abhiplava sadaha is indeed the root; the prsthya is the tip. When, having performed the abhiplava sadaha, they then perform the prsthya -- therefore plants are born from the root and ripen from the tip. These five sadahas exist. The sacrifice is fivefold (pankta). In as much as is the measure of the sacrifice, in that they are established. That amounts to a month. The year is the month. Month by month they thereby attain the year.
2.375: There is the introductory day (prayaniya). By the introductory day the gods went forth to the heavenly world. Because they went forth (prayan), that is the prayaniya-nature of the prayaniya. Since there is the introductory day, it is for the attainment of the heavenly world. Therefore the priest should desire to be [present] on this day, for it is nearest to the heavenly world. It is a caturvimsa [twenty-four-fold stoma]. Twenty-four half-months make the year. By half-months they thereby attain the year. Some sattra-performers, having performed this day alone, stood up saying: 'We have attained the year.' Those who would be like ones undertaking desire after desire should, having performed this day alone, rise up. Their year is indeed attained. Its laudatory verses (stotriya) number three hundred and sixty. That many are the nights of the year. Night by night they thereby attain the year. They say: 'If the year-long performers sit pressing soma for the days of the year, by what means do they attain the nights?' One should say: 'As many as are the stotriyas of the introductory day, that many are the nights of the year. By the stotriyas of the introductory day itself they attain the nights; then they sit pressing soma for the days.' It is an ukthya, for the uktha-chants are cattle -- for the securing of cattle. Its laudations (stotra) number fifteen. Fifteen are the nights of a half-month. By half-months they thereby attain the year. The stuta-sastras number thirty. Thirty are the nights of a month.
2.376: They say: 'The acchavaka is like a resting place of the hotra-chants. If they were to conclude with the acchavaka, they would be like ones who have chanted to a resting place.' Its brahmasaman is the traikakubha; the acchavaka-saman is the udvamsiya. The traikakubha is power and virility; the udvamsiya has the form of all prstha-chants. In power and virility and in the form of all prstha-chants they are established at the end. That indeed was praised. They say the agnistoma alone should be performed. Its laudations number twelve. Twelve months make the year. This is the most excellent portion (parvan) of the year -- the month. 'By the most excellent portion of the year may our year be commenced,' [they think]. They are, however, likely to become without cattle, for the uktha-chants are cattle, since they depart for them. They should make three laudations forty-eight-fold, both pavamana-chants and the brahmasaman. That many are the stotriyas of the ukthya. They obtain that desire which [comes] from the ukthya, or they do not depart from the agnistoma. The agnistoma is eminence; the hotr is eminence. With eminence they thereby join eminence; through eminence they are established in eminence.
2.377: They say: 'The jagati meter is like a raft -- not for the starting [of the sattra].' Instead, they should make six thirty-six-fold laudations and six twenty-four-fold ones. The brhati has thirty-six syllables. The brhati is the heavenly world, sovereign rule. In the brhati, in the heavenly world, in sovereign rule they are established as they go. The brhati pervades all these worlds with its syllables -- with ten syllables it pervades this world, with ten the midspace, with ten the yonder world, with four the directions, with two the day and night. 'May we pervade these worlds with that which pervades these worlds,' [they think]. Therefore they should make six thirty-six-fold laudations and six twenty-four-fold ones. Moreover, twenty-four half-months make the year. By half-months they thereby attain the year. 'These drops have flowed forth' (ete asrgram indavah) -- they should use a multi-form opening verse, for many are those who consecrate themselves. But they say: 'Pavasva vaco agriya alone should be the opening verse. That is the multi-form one. In it are all forms.' There are two opening verses, for two saman-melodies are composed here. It is as if one would harness two most powerful, strongest, most capable of conveyance, fit for completing a long journey. They arrange all prstha-forms in order, as one would arrange calves after their father and mother -- such is that. 'O Agni, harness those [horses] of yours' (tad agne yunksva hi ye tave) -- on these [verses] the agnistomasaman should be used, they say. For the vaisvanara fire is the year. 'For his yoking, O god, the steeds are good' -- for success. 'They draw swiftly' -- for completion. They say: 'Those who displace the yajnayajniya from the rk-verses of the yajnayajniya -- they approach sacrifices with sacrifice.' And those who cause the yajnayajniya to rest upon the rk-verses of the yajnayajniya -- they are established in sacrifice by means of sacrifice.
2.378: The abhivarta is the brahmasaman. By the abhivarta the gods prevailed over these worlds. Because they prevailed (abhyavartanta), that is the abhivarta-nature of the abhivarta. Since the abhivarta is the brahmasaman, it is for prevailing over these worlds. Prajapati is the abhivarta; the creatures are the meters. This Prajapati goes placing the embryo among the creatures. They chant on different [verses] with the same saman. That is the depositing of seed. Therefore the same [male] deposits seed in many [females]. The subsequent rk-verses are the same, but the saman differs. That is the bringing forth of seed deposited. Therefore the same [female] gives birth to many. They chant on different [verses] with the same saman, for they see different things as they go. The subsequent rk-verses are the same, but the saman differs. Those worlds which they ascend to by means of pragatha-chants going forth, from those they descend by means of saman-melodies coming back. This world is the rk; that yonder world is the saman. When those going from here proceed grasping the saman, they proceed grasping that yonder world. If they were to abandon [the chant] before the visuvat, they would be cut off from that yonder world. When those coming from yonder proceed grasping the rk, they proceed grasping this world.
2.379: Once on the far side they transpose the abhivarta. Therefore embryos are turned around as they are born. Formerly, indeed, by the fifth month the barhata pragatha-chants were completed. By combining the two meters usnih and tristubh, they could complete five months. By combining the two meters anustubh and pankti, they could complete six months, with one meter left over. Then they should use the jagati at the visuvat. For the visuvat is of the jagati. In its own seat they thus establish the visuvat. The meters are the heavenly world. They thereby ascend to the heavenly world. They say: 'But it is as though they cut up the meters when they join these two by two. Where will today's day and tomorrow's day come together? They should employ all seven with four additional ones.' They should use the gayatri on the first day, the usnih on the second, the anustubh on the third, and complete five months with the brhati. They should use the pankti on the first day and complete six months with the tristubh, with one meter left over. Then they should use the jagati at the visuvat. For the visuvat is of the jagati. In its own seat they thus establish the visuvat. The meters are the heavenly world.
2.380: They say: 'It is as though they yoke meters that are not worn out, since they are [already] worn out. They use them all again and again. They should determine which pragatha-chants they consider most potent and employ thirty-six.' The brhati has thirty-six syllables. The brhati is the heavenly world, sovereign rule. In the brhati, in the heavenly world, in sovereign rule they are established as they go. They release six, and recirculate with thirty, for the sake of freshness. But they say: 'Thirty-three alone should be employed.' Thirty-three are all the deities. In all the deities they are thereby established as they go. They release three, and recirculate with thirty, for the sake of freshness. But they say: 'Thirty alone should be employed.' The viraj has thirty syllables; food is the viraj. In the viraj, in food they are thereby established as they go. But they say: 'Twenty-four alone should be employed.' Twenty-four half-months make the year. By half-months they are thereby established in the year as they go. But they say: 'Twelve alone should be employed.' Twelve months make the year. By months they are thereby established in the year as they go. But they say: 'Six alone should be employed.' Six are the seasons. In the seasons they are thereby established as they go. But they say: 'Four alone should be employed.' Four are the directions; four-footed are the cattle. In the directions and in the cattle they are thereby established as they go. But they say: 'Three alone should be employed.' Three are these worlds. In these worlds they are thereby established as they go. But they say: 'Two alone should be employed -- the two pragatha-chants of the syaita and naudhasa.' For the pragatha-chants of the syaita and naudhasa are the most potent.
2.381: He who travels a path that turns back does not reach [the goal]. But he who travels one that does not turn back -- he indeed reaches it. Those who employ two travel as though on a return path. 'Only the traistubha and barhata ones should be employed.' The traistubha and barhata belong to the midday pressing. On a day when there is the rathantara, they should use the barhata on it; on a barhata day, the traistubha. Neither their traistubha pragatha-chants are exhausted nor the barhata ones. The meters are yoked according to their proper places; they sit through the year without staleness. But they say: 'Two alone should be employed -- the two pragatha-chants of the syaita and naudhasa.' These are indeed the most potent. These are the two straight paths to the heavenly world -- the brhat and rathantara. Of these two, the straight paths are the syaita and naudhasa. Day after day they thereby go straight to the heavenly world. Of this there is the verse: 'When a person strays from the great path of universal course, he, having crawled, exhausted, wishes to reach the same course again.' Those who employ other pragatha-chants take a bypath from the great path. But those who employ the pragatha-chants of the syaita and naudhasa -- they go straight to the heavenly world day after day.
2.382: The other saman-chants of the anustubh are food. The anustubh is the food of the other saman-chants. Those saman-chants that have approach and departure [movements] are the food of the anustubh. Those who impair them -- to them this speech, unadorned, unsatisfied, unrecognized, speaks: 'Unharmonious, faithless, they sacrificed with the seasons -- they did not obtain.' But those who fulfill them -- to them this speech, satisfied, pleased, recognized, speaks: 'Harmonious, faithful, they sacrificed with the seasons -- they obtained.' The sujnana is performed daily. The udgatr should indeed consecrate the sattra-performers for the sattra, so that he may lead them to the divine realm of the gods. For one who has ascended to the divine realm of the gods does not fear, does not hunger, does not thirst, does not suffer distress. The sujnana is indeed the divine realm of the gods. They say: 'Those who perform the sujnana daily depart, as it were, from this world.' On a day when there is the rathantara, they should perform the pauskala on it; on a barhata day, the sujnana. But they say: 'In the sujnana indeed are all these forms.'
2.383: Its closing phrase (nidhana) has seven syllables. Seven are the vital breaths (prana). By the vital breaths they go forth prospering, being established in the vital breaths. The gaurivita is performed daily. The gaurivita is the surplus of brahman. These days of the ahina [multi-day rite] are surplus. By the surplus they thereby go placing the surplus essence of brahman. The plava is performed daily, for the attainment of the heavenly world. With 'e' they float over the midspace. 'May we cross over all misfortunes' -- whatever in the sacrifice is ill-chanted, ill-recited, or disordered, that they cross over. Its closing phrase has twelve syllables. Twelve months make the year. In the months, in the year, they are established as they go. They say: 'Those who perform these distant sadahas depart, as it were, from this world.' They should use the oka-ending on the sixth day. This world is a person's dwelling (oka). In this very world they are thereby established. They also call it 'the conjunction of the dispatched.' Day and night are the dispatched. For these two travel as though dispatched. And these sadahas are cut apart, as it were, when they reach the sixth day. Just as one would join a horse with a harness, so they join the sadahas by means of this saman as they go. They say: 'Should the oka-ending be used in the prsthya sadaha?' 'No,' one should say. For the sadahas begin with the prsthya and end with it. It is this very [goal] they seek to reach -- by the abhivarta, by the plava, by the sujnana. If they were to use it here, it would be like a repetition of the procedure.
2.384: Svarbhanu the Asura struck the sun (Aditya) with darkness. The gods and the seers healed him. They perceived these svara-chants. With those they restored him. Because they restored him (asprnvan), that is the svara-nature of the svara-chants. Since these svara-chants exist, by them they restore the sun. Whatever a man does for the gods, the same the gods do for him. Since these svara-chants exist, by them they restore themselves. The svara-saman days have the saptadasa [seventeen-fold stoma]; the visuvat has the ekavimsa [twenty-one-fold]. Prajapati is the saptadasa; the sun yonder is the ekavimsa. Thereby they convey the son around the father. For the father is dearest to the son, and the son is dearest to the father. If one were to place another stoma there, yonder [sun] would burn him with its splendor; the vaisvanara fire would seize the offspring. These six svara-saman days exist. Six are the seasons. In the seasons they thereby convey him around; therefore he goes south for three seasons and north for three. Just as a son follows after his fathers -- so it is, for the sake of non-kinship-violation. For a son follows his fathers without kinship-violation. Now these two, the visvajit and abhijit, stand on both sides as the power of the stomas. By their power they support the visuvat. They say: 'It is as though it is a cutting of the stomas -- the stomas go from the trayastrimsa to the saptadasa.' Having employed the abhijit before the prsthya sadaha, whatever day in the middle of the prsthya sadaha is the saptadasa day, that they should arrange beyond the trayastrimsa.
2.385: They say: 'The prsthya sadaha is the self (atman); the saptadasa is food. If they were to remove the saptadasa from the prsthya sadaha, they would place the food outside; they would become hungry.' It should be employed from the trayastrimsa only. Thirty-three are all the deities. All the deities, having united with Prajapati, may or may not attain [the goal]. Thus they go from the even to the even. It is eminence from eminence. For the father is eminence. Moreover, it is an ascent. For the father is greater than the son. They are ukthya-days -- the uktha-chants are cattle -- for the securing of cattle. They say: 'It is as though the sacrifice is performed in reverse when the visvajit and abhijit are agnistomas, the visuvat is an agnistoma, and the svara-saman days are ukthya.' All should be agnistomas, they say. The agnistoma is eminence; the visuvat is eminence. With eminence they thereby join eminence; through eminence they ascend to eminence. The agnistoma is splendor; the visuvat is splendor. With splendor they thereby join splendor; through splendor they ascend to splendor. Now, the Kamyakiyas -- sattra-performers who were householders without iron pillars -- sat for a sattra. Indradyumna Bhallabeya urged them on, [saying]: 'I will have my son chant at the visuvat.' He arrived when one day of their prsthya sadaha remained unchanted. He said: 'You have done well in that you have not yet chanted. But if I had been here, I would have arranged the visuvat differently.' 'How would you have arranged it, sir?' his son asked.
2.386: The prsthya sadaha is the self; the svara-saman days are the offspring. Offspring indeed goes forth from us. We are speech. Speech goes forth from us. Since this abhijit stands in between, it is as though the procreative power stands concealed. When they arrange it before the prsthya sadaha, [they think:] 'May we be reproduced from unconcealed procreation.' Those are saptadasa [seventeen-fold]. Prajapati is the saptadasa. Prajapati is the progenitor of offspring. 'By him who is the progenitor of offspring, may we be reproduced,' [they think]. Those are of the anustubh meter. Speech is the anustubh; speech is procreation. 'From the procreative power of speech may we be reproduced,' [they think]. Those are ukthya-days. The uktha-chants are cattle; food is cattle. 'By cattle, by food, may we not obstruct offspring,' [they think]. Thereupon Satyayani said, disregarding Bhallabeya's statement: 'It should be employed at the abhijit itself. This prsthya sadaha is itself the abhijit -- by its seat. Those stomas which belong to the prsthya sadaha are these very ones. In the prsthya sadaha there is one saptadasa. But in this [arrangement] there are three saptadasas. They thereby make this stoma and generate from it. Since they generate saptadasa from saptadasa, saptadasa from saptadasa, saptadasa from saptadasa -- and beyond the visvajit there are three saptadasas -- those two groups, having united, generate. There is their establishment.'
2.387: The visuvat has the ekavimsa [twenty-one-fold stoma]. The ekavimsa is the visuvat of this universe. Twelve months, five seasons, three worlds, and the sun yonder -- [that is] twenty-one. In all of this he is established. 'May we be established in all of that in which he is established,' [they think]. Therefore the opening verse (pratipad) is a vayavya anustubh. Speech and wind -- these two are the ones who have consumed among the deities. 'By those two, who have consumed among the deities, may we consume him,' [they think]. If wind did not blow, yonder [sun] would burn this [world] with its splendor; the vaisvanara fire would seize the offspring. The opening verse is vayavya -- for the pacification of the vaisvanara fire, to prevent burning. They say: 'Those who begin with the pavamani verses are diminished, as it were, in vital breaths.' Since this opening verse is vayavya -- wind is breath (prana) -- by the vital breaths they thereby prosper. 'O Vayu, bright, I come to you' -- it contains the word 'bright' (sukra). For this is the bright day. 'The first of the divine libation' -- thereby those who are foremost become possessors of brahmanical splendor. 'Come to the soma-drinking' -- it is of Soma; thereby the pavamani is performed. 'Desirable, O god, with the Niyutvat team' -- yonder [sun] is desirable, food is the niyutvat; by food they thereby approach him. He who approaches a superior bringing [an offering], him [the superior] welcomes. But he who approaches him without bringing -- him he does not welcome. Since this opening verse is a vayavya anustubh, [they think:] 'May he welcome us; may we prosper toward him.' But they say: 'Pavasva vaco agriya alone should be the opening verse.' For this speech is extended as these worlds. Its far end shines as the sun yonder. Since the opening verse is 'pavasva vaco agriya,' by this they thereby approach him. Just as one would grasp oneself by one's own self, so they grasp him by his own form.
2.388: Svarbhanu the Asura struck the sun with darkness. The gods and the seers healed him. They perceived these divakirtya saman-chants. By those they dispelled his darkness. Since these divakirtya chants exist, by them they dispel the darkness of the sun. Whatever a man does for the gods, the same the gods do for him. Since these divakirtya chants exist, by them they dispel their own darkness. The bhraja and abhraja are at the opening of the pavamana-chants. By those they dispel the darkness from his limbs. The mahadivakirtya is the prstha; the vikarna is the brahmasaman. By those they dispel the darkness from his middle. The dasastobha and bhasa are the agnistomasaman. By those they dispel the darkness from his head. The sun yonder is the ekavimsa visuvat; its rays are the divakirtya-chants. Since these divakirtya-chants exist, by them they surround him and complete him. They ask: 'By what do the year-long performers [reach] the heavenly world?' 'The head of heaven, the spoke of the earth' -- the agnistomasaman, one should say. By that, they say. Yonder is the head of heaven, he who shines -- by this they ascend to him. They say: 'Those who ascend to him depart, as it were, from this world.' 'The spoke of the earth,' it says -- this world is the spoke of the earth; in this very world they are thereby established. The dasastobha and bhasa are the agnistomasaman; the viraj has ten syllables; food is the viraj -- for the securing of food. 'They are likely to carry away the food for another by the head, those who use the agnistomasaman in these head-bearing [verses],' they say. It should be done with the anustubh only. Speech is the anustubh; food is the dasastobha.
2.389: The visuvat is the self of the year. The wings are on both sides. Whatever they travel by with the abhivarta, and whatever they descend by from yonder with the pragatha -- both of those they should use at the visuvat. In the self they thereby place the wings, for flying to the heavenly world. Therefore wings are placed on the self. They say: 'Those who employ distant sadahas, distant stomas, distant prstha-chants, and distant meters depart, as it were, from this world.' Since beyond the visuvat they employ hither-facing sadahas, they are thereby established in this very world. 'Today-today, tomorrow-tomorrow, we [see] him; yesterday you, yesterday, O men -- where are you? where are you?' and 'Indra, threefold shelter' -- these are continuity-pragatha-chants, for the continuity of today and tomorrow. Today and tomorrow they extend for them. They sit through the year without staleness. They say: 'On these verses with the word avat, one should chant in the form of the day -- Indra kratum na a bhara.' 'As a father to sons, teach us in this course, O much-invoked; may we, the living, attain the light.' Those who attain the culmination of the year -- they indeed attain the living light. 'May not unknown hostilities or evil ones assail us. With you, O hero, we cross over the streams, the perpetual waters' -- these perpetual streams are the seasons, months, half-months, days-and-nights, and dawns of the year.
2.390: They threw Sakti Vasistha into the fire for Saudasa. Being thrown in, he said: 'Indra kratum na a bhara -- as a father to sons...' Only this much was his utterance. Then they threw him into the fire. Then Vasistha arrived. He said: 'What did my son say while being thrown in?' They told him: 'Indra kratum na a bhara -- as a father to sons... -- only this much was his utterance; then they threw him into the fire.' He said: 'Teach us in this course, O much-invoked; may we, the living, attain the light. Had my son reached this second half-verse, they would not have thrown him into the fire, and he would have lived out his full life. [The deity] whom he approached was the most powerful of the deities. Those sattra-performers who chant with this pragatha of my son will prosper,' [he said]. This pragatha is accompanied by prosperity and success. Those who have chanted with it become prosperous. The gaus [cow-stoma] and ayus [life-stoma] are the two stomas, for stability. The deficient and the excessive ones exist for procreation. The vairaja ones exist for the securing of food. The prsthya sadaha exists. Prosperity and eminence are the prstha-chants. They ascend upward to prosperity and eminence through the prstha-chants. The chandoma-chants exist -- cattle are the chandoma-chants -- for the securing of cattle. There is a tenth day; the viraj has ten syllables; food is the viraj -- for the securing of food. 'This one of a thousand measures' (ayam sahasramanava) -- before the silent praise (tusnim stoma), these should be chanted in the atichanda [super-meters], they say. For the atichanda exceeds other meters. Those who are year-long performers exceed others. In their own meter they are thereby established. The saman has a single-syllable closing phrase. Speech consists of a single syllable. By the tenth day they thereby bring speech to fulfillment. The closing phrase is 'goh' -- the cow is complete food -- for the securing of complete food. They say: 'The anustubh would be over-chanted if they were to do this here. Lest we over-chant speech' -- by mind they chant with the silent praise. Here indeed it is fulfilled through speech.
2.391: They deliberate: 'Should one release [days] or not?' They say: 'One should indeed release.' He who travels without releasing either cuts off or diminishes. Those who do not release travel as though without releasing. Since they release the full-moon days, it is for well-being. He who pours into what is full -- it either spills or breaks apart. The year is full. Since they release the full-moon days, it is for well-being. From the deficient, seed is shed; from the deficient, offspring are born. The year is full. Since they release the full-moon days, they thereby make a place for seed, for procreation. From the deficient, vital breaths stir. The year is Prajapati. The full-moon days are his vital breaths. Since they release the full-moon days, they thereby release the vital breaths of Prajapati. The gods are indeed full-moon-stoma ones.
2.392: On the day they release, they should offer in the forenoon a rice-cake on eight potsherds to Indra Vasumant. At midday they should offer a rice-cake on eleven potsherds to Indra Rudravant. In the afternoon they should offer a rice-cake on twelve potsherds to Indra Adityavant. The pressings are the share of these deities; they do not depart from them by the pressing, nor do the deities come between. The year is Prajapati. The full-moon and new-moon days are his inbreath and outbreath. Since they release both the full-moon and new-moon days, they thereby release the inbreath and outbreath of Prajapati. After the year they perform a twenty-four-night [rite]. But they say: 'Only the full-moon days should be released.' The full-moon day is the share of the prana; the new-moon day is the share of the apana. One lives by the prana which is external and by the apana which is internal. Since they release the full-moon days, [they think:] 'May we live by the prana which is external and the apana which is internal.'
2.393: Thereupon Aruni said to Asadha Savayasa, who was about to release: 'The hotr sits in a breach of the year.' He replied: 'Do not speak to us thus. We shall perform a twelve-night rite after it.' He sang to him in response: 'Whether greater or lesser, it is not equal to that. A potsherd, being different, is placed to fill a hole' -- 'this is what I think of the hotr's [suggestion]. I consider the year of those who release to be with a breach. Moreover, their sattra is performed in a different manner, as it were. The sacrifice is like a hare. He who tries to catch a hare by releasing it does not catch it. But he who tries to catch it without releasing -- he indeed catches it. Those who release are trying to catch it by releasing.'
2.394: Thereupon Kusurbinda Auddalaki said: 'One should both release and not.' Whatever day they release, on the next day they should recollect and chant additionally. Thereby it is both released and not. Or, however many days they release, they should count those and perform them after the atiratra. Thereby it is both released and not. Thereupon Bhallabeya said: 'Only the uktha-chants should be released.' Offspring and cattle are the uktha-chants. They thereby release offspring and cattle. It would be like inflating a leather bag by blowing through the foot-opening -- such is that. They say: 'One should indeed release.' The year is Prajapati. The full-moon and new-moon days are his vital breaths, his joints. Just as a leather bag, inflated, would burn [if overheated], so indeed this Prajapati burns the cattle when one does not release. Since they release the full-moon and new-moon days, they thereby extend his limbs, vital breaths, and joints -- 'may the blazing year not harm us,' [they think]. 'Him the maidens urge on' -- on the next day they should make an opening verse. It would be like re-inflating an exhaled leather bag -- such is that.
2.395: Thereupon Sirinda Palali said: 'One should release only on the new-moon day.' On the new-moon day the deities released. 'Let us release following the release of the deities,' [they think]. These two are indeed the sacrificially pure nights of the year -- the full-moon and new-moon. If they were to release both the full-moon and new-moon, they would impair the year with the sacrificial essence. The agnistoma should be performed as trivrt only. Since its laudations are fewer, thereby it is both released and not. Thereupon Nagari Janasruteya said: 'One should employ the ekatrika alone.' Since its laudations are, as it were, curtailed, thereby it is both released and not. Thereupon Aruni said: 'By these two stomas, the gau and ayus alone, it is both released and not.' Of these two, that whose laudations are fewer -- by that it is released; that whose laudations are excessive -- by that it is unreleased.
2.396: The adhvaryu, having taken the cup (graha), runs. He says: 'Turn around.' They, having hummed (himkrtya), go forth eastward and, standing beyond the agnidhra [fire-house], chant with the observance of all the gods. By that they announce to all the gods: 'We have obtained.' Going forth eastward and looking at the catvala [pit], they chant with the krosa [cry]. When the gods had gone to the end, their power and virility departed. They secured it with the krosa [cry]. Those who attain the culmination of the year go to the end, as it were. Since they chant with the krosa, it is for the securing of power and virility. Now the anukrosa [response-cry]. When the gods went to the heavenly world with a three-day rite, on their third day the vairupa broke apart. Of this there is the verse: 'The gods, gathering around the vairupa, stood upon it, but it broke apart for them. Humming, having ascended again, they all shook off the decay of their bodies.' Of this there is the verse: 'When the vairupa was struck down, having hummed, they stood together on the course. The gods produced that chant as the divine station. By it the gods went to immortality.'
2.397: In this eastern direction he placed a descendant of Vidhatr named Pura. His purohita was Pracina Ahuryayana. In this southern direction he placed a descendant of Vidhatr named Adharahi. His purohita was Daksina Ahuryayana. In this western direction he placed a descendant of Vidhatr named Pasca. His purohita was Praticina Ahuryayana. In this northern direction he placed a descendant of Vidhatr named Uttarahi. His purohita was Udicina Ahuryayana. The gods said to Vidhatr: 'If you thus preside over this, then watch over us so that we may go safely to the heavenly world.'
2.398: At the Indra-pressings of soma, with 'ho ye hu ho' he urged them on. They said: 'You have expelled us from the sattra altogether. Watch over us so that you share with us in this world.' By this very anukrosa, 'indra ho iha ve ho,' he shared with them in this world. Thereby they were established in both these worlds. By the krosa they went to the heavenly world; by the anukrosa they were established in this world.
2.399: They chant near the side of the fire, for the success of the sattra. By that they announce to Agni and the gods: 'We have obtained.' On both sides of the havirdhana [soma-cart shed], with the vrata-wings. For they consecrate themselves for the heavenly world who [consecrate] for a year. Since they chant with the vrata-wings, they thereby place wings upon themselves for flying to the heavenly world. Therefore wings are placed upon the self. They chant with the prana and apana -- with the prana in front, with the apana behind. They say: 'Those who omit or do not omit a part of the sacrificial ritual are diminished, as it were, in prana and apana. For who knows whether they omit or not?'
2.400: They chant with the sloka and anusloka -- with the sloka in front, with the anusloka behind. Since they chant with the sloka, [they think:] 'May we secure auspicious fame (sloka).' And since with the anusloka: 'May auspicious fame (anusloka) follow us who have risen up.' They chant with the nihnava and abhinihnava -- with the nihnava in front, with the abhinihnava behind. They say: 'Those who [complete] the year ascend to a higher state.' Since they chant with the nihnava, they conceal themselves from the higher [state] and ascend by means of the abhinihnava. 'Whatever we have done here, may we not be denied that,' [they think]. Thus their [accomplishment] is approved. They chant the interior chant with the saman of the Angirases, which has a twenty-six-syllable closing phrase.
2.401: They chant that as a pancavimsa [twenty-five-fold stoma], so as not to over-chant the observance -- or indeed they chant with the stoma that corresponds to the meter of the observance: 'Tawny are your mustaches, O Indra, and tawny your two steeds. You the singers praise, men [who seek] forest-cattle. Prosper, O Indra, bhung!' 'O Maghavan Indra, bhung!' 'Indra Trasaraputa... hip, again and again, trasara-hip.' Only this much of his body was un-praised -- the hips. They praise him with the un-praised limb. Those who have chanted with this become prosperous. They ask: 'For whom are the parimada embellishments made?' Just as a bull in autumn -- so is the mahavrata. As one would offer an autumn bull with ribbons of speech, so they offer the mahavrata with the parimada embellishments, so that the observance does not diminish. Moreover, just as a person has hair, nails, body-hair, and a beard, so has this [rite] these [embellishments].
2.402: The stotriyas of the mahavrata number eighty-seven; the parimadas are thirteen. That makes a hundred. A person lives a hundred years, has hundredfold power, hundredfold virility -- for the securing of that power and virility. Now they utter these speeches. The minor parimadas are: the karkari [lute], the alabu [gourd-instrument], the vakra [curved flute], the kapisirshni [monkey-headed instrument], the aisiki [reed instrument], the apaghatalika [clapper], the vina, the kasyapi [tortoise-lute], and the bhumidundubhi [earth-drum] struck with a bull's hide, and the vana with a hundred strings. Drums are stretched in the midspace and sound. Pots are carried around. They utter these speeches for the securing of all speech -- for the completeness of speech: 'May we secure complete speech,' [they think]. Since the earth-drum, struck with a bull's hide, sounds -- the bull is the highest speech -- for the securing of the highest speech. The drums stretched in the midspace sound -- whatever speech is in the midspace, that they thereby secure. The pots are carried around -- this is the manifest form of heaven, the head. Whatever speech is in the yonder [world], that they thereby secure.
2.403: They pair a magadha [bard] and a pumscalu [courtesan] to the south of the altar, for the sake of pairing, for procreation. He who knows thus is born forth through pairing. They stretch an arya and a sudra one on top of the other upon the catvala pit, on a goatskin or bull's hide. Of the two, the arya is within the altar area, the sudra outside. With the arya they overpower the sudra. The gods and the asuras contended over the sun yonder. The gods wrested it from the asuras. Since with the arya they overpower the sudra, thereby they wrest it from the hated rival. Of the two, one reviles and the other praises. He who reviles purifies them. And he who praises -- in those purified he places power and virility. They run a race. The race is the highest greatness -- for the securing of the highest greatness. They are armored. The highest splendor is in one who is armored. They thereby place the highest splendor in themselves. Full pots are carried around the marjaliya [fire] -- for the securing of fullness. 'Hai maha, hai maha!' -- for the securing of greatness. 'This is honey, this is honey!' -- for what is done for the gods as the mahavrata is, as it were, honey. The highest essence is in honey -- for the securing of the highest essence. They say: 'They are likely to be consumed, if women singing hai maha, this is honey were to go around.' They should go around singing 'hai maha' only. Thereupon Satyayani said: 'When something exists, when it is -- then it is consumable. How can the non-existent be consumed?'
2.404: Those about to chant take their positions. The adhvaryu, about to chant with the trivrt head and the gayatra, sits down in front of the havirdhana. The maitravaruna, about to chant with the pancadasa wing and the brhat, sits down on the south. The brahmanacchamsin, about to chant with the saptadasa wing and the rathantara, sits down on the north. The nestr, about to chant with the ekavimsa tail and the bhadra, sits down in the midst of the wives. The udgatr, about to chant with the pancavimsa self and the rajana, ascends the audumbari post -- for the udumbara is vigor and food -- for the securing of vigor and food. He addresses it: 'The brhat and rathantara are your fore-feet; the vairupa and vairaja are your hind-feet. The recited meters are the bedding; the yajus-formulas are the weaving. [Be] a comfortable seat, a well-seated seat, a good seat.' Its descent-verse is: 'May great heaven and earth favor this our sacrifice; may they fill us with nourishment.'
2.405: They all hum together at once. Therefore a person is born as a unified being. They hum following the udgatr's hum. Therefore the limbs follow the will of the self. The adhvaryu chants with the trivrt head and the gayatra. Thereby he places the head. Therefore this head is placed. [The verses go] in outward direction without return. Therefore this head goes only outward [i.e., cannot turn back]. He chants with nine [verses]. Therefore this head neither fattens when [the body] fattens nor wastes when it wastes. The maitravaruna chants with the pancadasa wing and the brhat. Thereby he places this arm. Therefore this arm is placed. [The verses go] in outward direction without return. Therefore this arm bends and extends. Though being three, he makes them fifteen. Therefore this arm fattens when [the body] fattens and wastes when it wastes. Power is the brhat; power is the pancadasa. Since he chants with the pancadasa wing and the brhat, therefore this arm [the right] has more power. Therefore birds circle following the right wing. The brhat is the male form; the rathantara is the female. Since they chant with the brhat on this side and the rathantara on that, therefore a man lies to the right of a woman. The brahmanacchamsin chants with the saptadasa wing and the rathantara. Thereby he places this [other] arm. Therefore this arm is placed. [The verses go] in outward direction without return. Therefore this arm bends and extends. Though being three, he makes them seventeen.
2.406: The nestr chants with the ekavimsa tail and the bhadra. Thereby he places the tail [lower body]. Therefore the tail is placed. [The verses go] in outward direction without return. Therefore the lower body bends and extends. Though being three, he makes them twenty-one. Therefore the lower body fattens when [the body] fattens and wastes when it wastes. The udgatr chants with the pancavimsa self and the rajana. Thereby he places the self (torso). Therefore this self is placed. [The verses go] in outward direction without return. Therefore this torso bends and extends. Though being three, he makes them twenty-five. Therefore this torso fattens when [the body] fattens and wastes when it wastes. With each individual chant-portion they join together with the udgatr. By those the udgatr chants. In the self they thereby place the limbs. Therefore the limbs are placed in the self. With the last portion he chants for the self.
2.407: Prajapati, having created the creatures, fell apart. The deities departed from him. Indra alone among the deities did not abandon him. He said to Indra: 'How may I again come into being from this [state]?' 'What do you have?' he said. 'I have these two -- prana and apana.' Since he said 'I have' (sto), that is the stoma-nature of the stoma. 'Praise him, for he is ours, the best' -- he who knows thus [understands the stoma]. He said to him: 'Praise [me] with these two for a year. When you have praised [me] with these two for a year, at the end of the year all the deities will come together to you.' He praised with these two for a year. When he had praised with them for a year, at the end of the year all the deities came together, extracting the essence of the directions, the essence of the waters, the essence of the Veda, the essence of food, saying: 'For the great observance we bring [offerings]; for the great observance we bring [offerings].' Since they said 'for the great observance (mahavrata) we bring,' that is the mahavrata-nature of the mahavrata. They offered it to him. He made it into an observance. They rejoiced: 'He performs the mahavrata!' 'He performs the mahavrata!' Therefore on this day they particularly rejoice. For the deities rejoiced on it.
2.408: Food is the observance (vrata). Food eaten from the middle nourishes. But they say: 'Those who perform the mahavrata at the visuvat, in the middle of the year, secure only half the food.' The year cooks complete food. It should be performed at the end of the year, for the securing of complete food. Thereupon Nagari Janasruteya said: 'Fierce indeed is this middlemost -- the mahavrata. When something fierce is middlemost, it scatters all others and lies alone. It should therefore be performed at the end of the year, for the securing of complete food.' It has the pancavimsa [twenty-five-fold stoma]. Twenty-four half-months make the year. Food is the twenty-fifth; food is thereby placed upon the twenty-fifth.
2.409: They say: 'The tavassavya alone should be the prstha-chant of this day.' When the gods first collected the observance, that became the tavassavya. Since the tavassavya is the prstha-chant of this day, they thereby approach the observance directly. But they say: 'The stobhas, verse-divisions, prastavas, pratiharas, deities, and closing-phrases of the tavassavya are difficult to discern. The vamadevya alone should be the prstha-chant of this day.' The vamadevya is the self (atman) of the observance. It would be as if one, having come home, did not find it -- such would be the case if one were to use any prstha-chant other than the vamadevya for this day. They say the svara is not suitable as a prstha. Instead, the rajana alone should be the prstha-chant of this day. By the rajana, Prajapati attained kingship, sovereignty, and lordship over creatures. 'By that with which Prajapati attained kingship, sovereignty, and lordship over creatures, by that may we attain kingship, sovereignty, and lordship over creatures,' [they think]. The vamadevya is, as it were, a veiled form of the rajana. The rajana is the prstha-chant. The vamadevya is the maitravaruna-saman. The five-closing-phrase vamadevya is the brahmasaman.
2.410: It has five stobhas, five verse-divisions, five deities, five pratiharas, and five closing-phrases. Whatever sacrifice is fivefold, whatever cattle are fivefold, whatever food is fivefold, whatever five seasons there are, whatever is five by five -- this is the success, the attainment of all that. The five-closing-phrase vamadevya is the brahmasaman. For fivefold indeed they chant with the observance. It occurs in the atichanda [super-meters], in [the verses] 'In the Trikadrukas, the buffalo, the barley-drink.' The atichanda surpasses other meters. Those who are year-long performers surpass others. In their own meter they are thereby established. And in the atichanda [verses] it is ehivat and ihavat [containing 'come here' and 'here'] -- that is the form of the end. For this day is the end. They say: 'The ilanda alone should be the agnistomasaman of this day.' By the ilanda, Prajapati bestowed sustenance (ira) and food upon creatures. 'By that with which Prajapati bestowed sustenance and food upon creatures, may we secure sustenance and food and rise up,' [they think]. It also occurs in the atichanda [verses]. The atichanda surpasses other meters. Those who are year-long performers surpass others.
2.411: It occurs in the vaisvanariva [verses]. For this is the laudation of the vaisvanara. It occurs in eighty-syllable [verses] -- 'may we attain.' Like the ocean, inexhaustible, as it were, is this meter -- the atichanda: 'may we be like those who have chanted inexhaustibly, like the ocean.' It is in the paksin [bird] form. For the observance of this day is the bird. 'E -- the observance,' he says -- food is the observance -- for the securing of food. 'E svar,' he says -- for the attainment of the heavenly world. 'E sakuna,' he says -- becoming birds (sakuna), they thereby attain the heavenly world. They say: 'The varavantiya alone should be the agnistomasaman of this day.' Prajapati created cattle. They, once created, ran away from him. By the varavantiya, having broken off a branch of the varana tree, he blocked them. Because he blocked (avarayata) them, that is the varavantiya-nature of the varavantiya. Therefore they call the varana tree medicinal. Since the varavantiya is the agnistomasaman of this day -- 'whatever we have secured by this ritual, may the varavantiya keep it standing firm for us to the end.' It is ehivat and ihavat -- that is the form of the end. For this day is the end. But they say: 'Whatever speech no other speech surpasses -- that should be the agnistomasaman of this day. No other speech surpasses the yajnayajniya. The yajnayajniya is speech. For how could speech surpass speech?'
2.412: Food is the observance; prosperity is the observance. By the observance food is eaten; by the observance prosperity is attained. They say it should be performed at the end of the year. 'The observance is better recognized from where it arises,' [they say]. It has the pancavimsa [twenty-five-fold stoma]. Twenty-four half-months make the year; food is the twenty-fifth. Food is thereby placed upon the twenty-fifth. A person is indeed twenty-five-fold. 'This is the laudation of the person,' they say: ten fingers of the hands, ten toes of the feet, four foreparts, and the self -- twenty-five. Or: six joints here, six here, six here, six here, and the self -- twenty-five. Or: twelve ribs here, twelve here, and the self -- twenty-five. They thereby fashion and generate a person. They say: 'This entire day should be performed with the twenty-five-fold [stoma].' The head should employ the pancavimsa, one wing the pancavimsa, the other [wing] the pancavimsa, the tail the pancavimsa, the self the pancavimsa -- 'thus we attain the observance limb by limb.' Therefore Arjjisva Vatavata said: 'This being one single laudation, they should divide it fivefold and chant -- with five for the head, five for one wing, five for the other, five for the tail, five for the self.' Thus it is one and yet twenty-five-fold, without excess.
2.413: Thereupon Aruni said: 'The other limbs are not equal to the self, nor is the self equal to the other limbs. A person is formed by the unequal; by the unequal he is made whole. Therefore this should be employed as it actually is.' Brahman is the head of the observance; heaven and earth are the wings; the midspace is the self; the saman is the tail. The gayatra is the head of the observance, for that is brahman. The brhat and rathantara are the wings, for those are heaven and earth. The vamadevya is the self, for that is the midspace. The yajnayajniya is the tail, for that is the saman. They chant the head with nine [verses]. Nine are the vital breaths of a person. This is the measure of the vital breaths, the prosperity of the vital breaths. On that they place the gayatrasaman. The prana is the gayatrasaman. They thereby place the prana in the head. Therefore the prana is established in the head. Moving backward, they chant with 'arkasirsa.' These are the neck -- as though singing while surveying these worlds. Thereby they join the head and the self. Therefore the head and the self are joined.
2.414: With this trivrt stoma they proceed toward the rathantara. Brahman is the trivrt; food is the rathantara. By brahman they thereby sustain food. On that they place the pancadasa stoma.
