The pancadasa is the thunderbolt (vajra); food is the rathantara. With the thunderbolt they thereby seize the food. With this pancadasa they proceed toward the saptadasa. The pancadasa is the thunderbolt; the saptadasa is the vis [common people]. With the thunderbolt they thereby sustain the vis. On that they place the brahmasaman. The brhat is the ksatra [ruling power]. They thereby place the ksatra upon the vis. Therefore the ksatriya is placed upon the vis. They say: 'One should chant with the brhat from this side and the rathantara from that.' 'This entire [world] is, as it were, directed toward the prana,' [they say]. Thereupon Kija Vakayani said: 'Those who chant with the brhat from this side and the rathantara from that -- they place the food behind. For food, when obtained, approaches from the south. Therefore one should chant with the brhat from this side and the rathantara from that, for the securing of food.' Moreover, it is this pancadasa stoma that is directed [toward the prana]. Therefore birds circle following the left wing. With this entire [chant] they come to the ekavimsa as their foundation. Twelve months, five seasons, three worlds, the sun yonder -- twenty-one. In all of this he is established.
2.415: On that they place the bhadra saman. Offspring is the bhadra. Offspring is born from the tail [lower body]. From the tail they thereby place procreative power for the production of offspring. 'There too the sreyas should be used,' they say. Offspring is the bhadra; offspring is that which is sreyas [better]. He who knows thus sees offspring of offspring. They say: 'One should chant all at once with the entire [chant], making it in the likeness of a bird.' When a bird takes flight, it flies with all its limbs yoked in different directions. And when it alights, it draws all its limbs together into itself and alights. Since they chant all at once with the entire [chant], it is like a bird flying, yoking all limbs in different directions -- such is that. And since they come together with each individual chant-portion, it is like a bird alighting, drawing all its limbs together into itself -- such is that. Thereupon Bhallabeya said: 'On this day no one other than the udgatr should officiate with his saman-chant. This day is prosperity; the udgatr is prosperity. If on this day another were to officiate with the udgatr's saman-chant, he would censure the uncensured, he would ascend over one who should not be ascended over; it would be an evil seizure. He should have [the rite] concluded with the surrounding arrangement; he should come together with each individual chant-portion.' But here they note [the concern] that a twenty-nine-fold stoma results. They say: 'A twenty-nine-fold stoma results -- a lowing cow follows its own herd; O Janamejaya, the cows of the thousand-sacrificer separate into splinter-groups' -- it is as though these go to their respective places. The head goes to its place in the head, the wings to the wings, the tail to the tail, the self to the self.
3.26: They say that the vāsiṣṭha is more to be performed. The pārtha is kṣatra, the somasāman pair is kṣatra, this day is kṣatra, because it is aindra it is kṣatra, because it is traiṣṭubha it is kṣatra, because it is bārhata it is kṣatra, because it has pañcadaśa stoma it is kṣatra. Kṣatra becomes singular in nature. When the yauktāśva comes first and the vāsiṣṭha comes after, these two are ṛṣi-sāmans, indeed for the flourishing of brahman. 'Let brahman be yoked with kṣatra,' thus it should be. Vasiṣṭha, defeated and his sons slain, desired: 'May I abound in offspring and cattle.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereupon he abounded in offspring and cattle. This is the sāman of procreation. He who knows thus abounds in offspring and cattle. Because Vasiṣṭha saw it, therefore it is called vāsiṣṭha. Now the bṛhat: the bṛhat is eminence. They go forth established in eminence. Now the vāmadevya: the vāmadevya is cattle. Those who have praised with it become rich in cattle. Now the śyaita — it is the wedding of the two sāmans, the continuity of the sacrifice. It is a single day, for 'tvām idā hyo nara' is the verse used.
3.27: Among those is the mādhucchandasa. Madhucchandas Vaiśvāmitra desired: 'May I be foremost, chief, and possessed of brahmavarcas.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereupon he became foremost, chief, and possessed of brahmavarcas. 'May we be foremost, chief, and possessed of brahmavarcas' — with this intention they sit in the sattra. They indeed become foremost, chief, and possessed of brahmavarcas. Because Madhucchandas Vaiśvāmitra saw it, therefore it is called mādhucchandasa. It becomes the acchāvāka-sāman with il̥ā. Cattle are il̥ā.
3.28: 'yas te mado vareṇya' — the gāyatrī verses of the ārbhava pavamāna are 'mad-containing' (madvatī). Rasa is indeed madhu. The third pressing is like something that has been milked. Because these are mad-containing, they place rasa in it, and with this they cause it to swell. 'tenā pavasvāndhase' — they are andhas-containing, for andhas is food — indeed for the obtainment of food. 'jaghnir vṛtram amitriyam' — this is the traiṣṭubha form. That which contains slaying is of triṣṭubh form, for triṣṭubh is kṣatra. 'saṃmiślo aruṣo bhuvas sūpasthābhir na dhenubhir' — this bārhata day is as if rushing forward, for the bṛhat is yonder. Because it says 'sūpasthābhir,' it is for the stability of the day. Among those the gāyatra has been explained. Now the hāviṣmata with paristobha and external nidhana, is bārhata. Therefore it is performed on the bārhata day. Haviṣmat and Haviṣkṛt were two Āṅgirasas. When the Āṅgirasas went to the heavenly world, these two were left behind. They desired: 'May we fly up after them to the heavenly world.' They performed austerities. They saw these two sāmans. They praised with them. Having praised, as Haviṣmat and Haviṣkṛt, they flew up after them to the heavenly world. Cattle indeed are the haviṣmat ones, cattle are the haviṣkṛt ones. It was cattle that they obtained by means of these two. Having sacrificed with those cattle, they went to the heavenly world. These two are sāmans pertaining to cattle and leading to heaven.
3.29: These two sāmans are indeed the proclamation and the declaration of the offering. When the hāviṣmata is on the second day, they say to the gods 'We are haviṣmat (possessing offerings).' For they are indeed like those possessing offerings at that time. When the hāviṣkṛta is on the ninth day, they declare to the gods 'We have made offerings.' For they are indeed like those who have made offerings at that time. These two sāmans are indeed the proclamation and the declaration of the offering. Because Haviṣmat and Haviṣkṛt, the two Āṅgirasas, saw them, therefore they are called hāviṣmata and hāviṣkṛta. 'pavasva madhumattama' — the kakubh verses are madhu-containing. Rasa is indeed madhu. The third pressing is like something milked. Because these are madhu-containing, they place rasa in it, and with this they cause it to swell. 'indrāya soma ṛtuvittamo madaḥ / mahi dyukṣatamo madaḥ' —
3.30: Among those is the sīdantīya. The gods desired: 'May we be seated in the heavenly world.' They saw this sāman. They praised with it. Thereupon they were seated in the heavenly world. 'May we be seated in the heavenly world' — with this intention they sit in the sattra. They are indeed seated in the heavenly world. Because the gods were seated in the heavenly world by means of this sāman, therefore it is called sīdantīya. They also call it śaṃku. For with a peg (śaṃku) one makes firm what is dadhiṣeṇya (a prop). This day is like the dadhiṣeṇya, namely the second day. Therefore it is performed on the second day. 'indram accha sutā ime vṛṣaṇaṃ yantu harayaḥ / śruṣṭe jātāsa indava svarvidaḥ' — by the ṛc the first day is made svar-possessing; by the sāman, this one.
3.31: Among those is the sujñāna. The asuras wished to confuse the gods by entering among them in the very form that the gods had. Those gods who practiced svādhyāya in the forest — the asuras sought to destroy them by assuming the form of those who were in the village. And those who were in the village — the asuras sought to destroy them by assuming the form of those who practiced svādhyāya in the forest. They sought to confuse them utterly. The gods became aware of this. They said: 'Let us make a recognition-sign.' They made a recognition-sign: 'Whomever we ask while he is walking, "Toward whom do you walk?" — he should say, "I walk toward Indra."' Whomever they asked while walking — 'Toward whom do you walk?' — he said, 'I walk toward Indra.' 'You are well-known (sujñāna)!' they would say. That is the sujñāna-nature of the sujñāna. This is the sāman of mutual recognition. Through it, recognition comes to him — that he may be recognized by whomever he desires — he who knows thus. And whoever, when asked, did not reply in this manner, they struck him down. Thus it is also a slayer of the rival. He who knows thus slays the hostile rival. The gods, going to the heavenly world, were afraid of being unrecognized. They desired: 'May recognition become manifest for us.' They saw this sāman. They praised with it. Thereupon recognition became manifest for them. He who knows thus attains manifest recognition. 'ayaṃ pūṣā rayir bhagas somaḥ punāno arṣati / patir viśvasya bhūmano vy akhyad rodasī ubhe' — this is for the linking of both days. Just as one would lead along a band of travelers hanging on to each other, so this day leads these two days, the bārhata and the rāthantara.
3.32: Now the krauñca. Kruṅ the Āṅgirasa found the second day to be like something enviable. The second day is indeed like something enviable. Therefore it is performed on the second day. There was originally just one day. Kruṅ the Āṅgirasa desired: 'May I fashion a day from a day.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereupon he fashioned a day from a day. When this sāman is used, it fashions a day from a day. Kruṅ the Āṅgirasa used to separate day from day. When they say 'Kruṅ separated milk,' it is not milk that he separated. It was Kruṅ the Āṅgirasa who separated day from day. Kruṅ the Āṅgirasa, desiring heaven, performed austerities. He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereupon he reached the heavenly world. This is a sāman leading to heaven. He who knows thus reaches the heavenly world. It has il̥ā above, which is the form of the bṛhat on the bārhata day. Thereby it is fulfilled in form. Because Kruṅ the Āṅgirasa saw it, therefore it is called krauñca. 'vṛṣā matīnāṃ pavate vicakṣaṇa' — the verses are vṛṣan-possessing.
3.33: Among those is the yāma. The second day is like a twin (yama). Just as twins would be born, so is this day. By twins, Yama pushed aside his rivals from the worlds and ascended to his own world. He who knows thus pushes aside the hostile rival from the world and ascends to his own. By mantra the ṛṣis became possessed of bonds (nidāna); by the yāma, Yama. 'May we become possessed of bonds' — with this they sit in the sattra. They indeed become possessed of bonds. Bonds are the household, bonds are offspring, bonds are cattle, bonds are the heavenly world. He who knows thus becomes possessed of bonds. The gods sent Yama Vaivasvata to yonder world. He desired: 'May I have bonds in this world.' He saw these yāma sāmans. He praised with them. Thereby he became possessed of bonds in this world. He who knows these sāmans has these as his bonds in this world, these as his offspring. Through these very sāmans he becomes possessed of bonds in this world and attains the sonship of Yama. They say: 'These are sāmans with a deity of the fathers. One is liable to go to the other world before full age' — āyur they chant as a stobha, or else vāyur. Thus one does not go to the other world before full age. Because Yama saw them, therefore they are called yāma. Now the yajñāyajñīya has been explained. 'ehy ū ṣu bravāṇi te agna itthatarā giraḥ / ebhir vardhāsa indubhiḥ' —
3.34: Among those is the sākamaśva, a sāman leading to heaven, for the attainment of the heavenly world. 'evā hy asi vīrayur' — evā is the form of the bṛhat. This is a bārhata day. Among those is the āmahīyava, a sāman belonging to Prajāpati. Prajāpati is kṣatra. This day belongs to Prajāpati. It is placed among gāyatrī verses. Brahman is the gāyatrī. This day is kṣatra. When the āmahīyava is among gāyatrī verses, they elevate brahman with glory. For one who is of brahman nature, there comes a radiance as of kṣatra — he who knows thus. This is why there is a radiance as of kings among brāhmaṇas — because the uktha with the brahma-sāman is in the gāyatrī verses. It occurs among verses that say the same thing. When many declare the same thing, that is truth. 'evā hy asi vīrayur evāśūra uta sthiraḥ / evā te rādhyaṃ manaḥ' — this is for the growth of truth. 'indraṃ viśvā avīvṛdhann' — for this bārhata day is one of growth. Among those the āṣṭādaṃṣṭra has been explained. It becomes the acchāvāka-sāman with il̥ā. Cattle are il̥ā. In cattle they find their foundation.
3.35: 'davidyutatyā ruce' — the opening verses are: 'davidyutatyā ruce' is the form of the gāyatrī. 'pariṣṭobhantyā kṛpe' is of the triṣṭubh. 'somāś śukrā gavāśira' is of the jagatī. These are the forms of the meters; by meters the third pressing is performed. The third day is like the third pressing. These are gāyatrī verses with jagatī coloring, fulfilled by the form of the day. Where they fulfill with the day's form, there prosperity comes. Prosperity comes to him who knows thus. 'ete asṛgram indava' is the anurūpa verse. Verses containing 'eta eta' point back to what preceded. By the path traversed by bṛhat and rathantara, by the stomas trivṛt and pañcadaśa, they proceed. They say: 'With the stotriya they take hold of the form of the sāman, with the anurūpa of the day.' The stotriya and the anurūpa are present. The stotriya is prāṇa, the anurūpa is apāna.
3.36: They are go-possessing, il̥ā-possessing, horse-possessing, cattle-possessing — for the obtainment of cattle. For cattle belong to this day. They are viśva-containing, for this day belongs to the Viśvedevas. They are antarikṣa-containing, for this day belongs to the antarikṣa. They are āśīr-containing (blessing-containing), for this day is one of blessings. And indeed, āśīr (a milk mixture) is offered at the third pressing. This day partakes of the third pressing. The hymn has five verses — for there are five directions — for the support of the directions. Or because the sacrifice is fivefold (pāṅkta), for the undertaking of the sacrifice. There is a concluding verse (paryāsa) for the sustaining of people and the stability of cattle. The stoma is saptadaśa (seventeen-fold). Prajāpati is saptadaśa.
3.37: 'agnināgnis sam idhyata' — the ājya is āgneya (belonging to Agni), which is rāthantara. For this day is rāthantara. When fire is kindled by fire, the vairūpa is kindled by the rathantara, or the rathantara by the vairūpa. The wife of Saujātasya Arāṭi said: 'When my husband considered that I had only two pṛṣṭha-sāmans, then this donkey brayed upon the audumbarī post!' They say: 'This is a rāthantara day. Therefore the rathantara should be made the pṛṣṭha for this day.' But others say: 'Who indeed would be such a person — who would have only himself and no cattle? The self is the bṛhadrathantara pair. Cattle are the pṛṣṭha-sāmans. Therefore the vairūpa alone should be the pṛṣṭha for this day.'
3.38: 'mitraṃ huve pūtadakṣam' — the ājya is maitrāvaruṇa (belonging to Mitra-Varuṇa). 'hu' is the form of the rathantara. This is a rāthantara day. 'varuṇaṃ ca riśādasaṃ dhiyaṃ ghṛtācīṃ sādhante' — this is for accomplishment indeed. 'ṛtena mitrāvaruṇāv ṛtāvṛdhāv ṛtaspṛśā / ṛtuṃ bṛhantam āśāthe // kavī no mitrāvaruṇā' — 'vī' occurs, which is the form of the antarikṣa. This day partakes of the antarikṣa. 'indreṇa saṃ hi dṛkṣasa' — the ājya is aindra (belonging to Indra). Both 'sam' and 'vī' are forms of the antarikṣa. From here this world extends upward, and that one extends downward from there. 'Sam' and 'vī' — that is the antarikṣa. 'saṃjagmāno abibhyuṣā mandū samānavarcase' — the third and fourth days are indeed alike. The fourth day is born from the third. 'ād aha svadhām anu punar garbhatvam erira' — here they cause the embryonic state to arise again on the third day, for the birth of the fourth day. 'dadhānā nāma yajñiyam // vīl̥u cid ārujatnubhir guhā cid indra vahnibhiḥ / avinda usriyā anu' — the verses are go-possessing, cattle-possessing, for the obtainment of cattle.
3.39: 'to huve yayor idam' — the ājya is aindrāgna (belonging to Indra-Agni). They invoke the sacrifice as speech: 'papre viśvaṃ purā kṛtam' — for now it is as if done before. 'indrāgnī na mardhataḥ // ugrā vighaninā mṛdha' — 'vī' occurs, which is the form of the antarikṣa. This day partakes of the antarikṣa. 'hatho vṛtrāṇy āryā hatho dāsāni satpatī / hatho viśvā apa dviṣaḥ' — this is of Vṛtra-slaying character, for conquest at the end. For by this three-day period the gods first conquered. Because this Vṛtra-slaying ājya is performed at the end, it is for conquest indeed, for the slaying of the rival. He who knows thus conquers and slays the hostile rival. The stoma is saptadaśa. Prajāpati is saptadaśa.
3.40: 'uccā te jātam andhase' — the gāyatrī verses of the midday pavamāna are andhas-possessing — andhas being food — for the obtainment of food. 'divi sad bhūmy ā dada' — the verses are dyāvāpṛthivī-related (pertaining to heaven and earth). The vairūpa day is as if without foundation. Because these are heaven-and-earth-related, they establish the day with them. 'sa na indrāya yajyave varuṇāya marudbhya' — the verses contain the Maruts. The midday's form is marutvat (Marut-possessing). They do not depart from the form of the midday. 'enā viśvāny arya ā' — the verses are viśva-containing. For this day belongs to the Viśvedevas. Among those the gāyatra has been explained. Now the vaiṣṭambha. The gods ascended upward to the heavenly world. They did not see the directions. They desired: 'May we see the directions.' They saw these two sāmans. They praised with them. Thereupon they saw the directions. They stepped upon them. The directions collapsed as it were. With these two nidhanas, as one would pin down a hide with pegs, so they pinned them down. The directions held firm. When these two sāmans are used, it is for the support of the directions and for the winning of the heavenly world. The directions are this day, the directions are these sāmans. When these two sāmans are performed on this day, they take hold of the directions directly. 'abhi somāsa āyava' — 'abhi' occurs, which is the form of the rathantara.
3.41: Whatever the gods did in the sacrifice, the asuras did the same. The gods said: 'Whatever we do in the sacrifice, the asuras do the same. Come, let us mix the three Vedas with the gāthā.' They cast down the three Vedas mixed with the gāthā. The asuras said: 'This that they mixed with the gāthā is of no use. Come, let us abandon it.' They abandoned it. From it, so abandoned, the gods drew out the rasa (essence) of the gāthā. That became the pragātha. Because they drew out the rasa of the gāthā, that is the pragātha-nature of the pragātha. It is indeed an essence drawn forth. When one who knows thus praises with the pragātha, his praise becomes full of rasa. Now originally the ṛc and the gāthā were alike in appearance, and each surpassed the other. They ran a race. The ṛc drew out the rasa from the gāthā. That became the pragātha. Because it drew out the rasa from the gāthā, that indeed is the pragātha-nature of the pragātha. It is indeed an essence drawn forth.
3.42: In it the gods saw the virāj as food, as a couple, as procreation. Because the first verse has four feet and the two that follow have three feet, these amount to ten syllables. The virāj has ten syllables; food is the virāj. This is what they saw. Now, because the first is called bṛhatī and the latter two are called pragātha, that is the couple, the means of procreation. In this same way Vasiṣṭha saw the virāj as food, the couple up to four as procreation. Because the first verse has four feet and the three that follow have two feet, these amount to ten syllables. The virāj has ten syllables; food is the virāj. This is what he saw. One, that makes one; two, that makes three; three, that makes six; four, that makes ten. That is the virāj. One and one, two and two, three and three, four and four — this is the pairing up to four, the means of procreation.
3.43: Among those is the paurumudga. The il̥ā-possessing pavamāna is performed with the paurumudga. Purumudga the Āṅgirasa, desiring cattle, performed austerities. He saw this sāman. He praised with it. He reached this il̥ā. Cattle are il̥ā. Thereupon he obtained cattle. This is a sāman pertaining to cattle. He who knows thus obtains cattle and becomes rich in cattle. Because Purumudga the Āṅgirasa saw it, therefore it is called paurumudga. The gods and the asuras were contending over the ocean. The gods desired: 'May we first submerge the asuras in the ocean.' They saw this sāman. They praised with it. Thereby they first submerged the asuras. Because they first submerged them, that is the paurumudga-nature of the paurumudga. He who knows thus first submerges the hostile rival. And whoever among them surfaced, they struck him down with this very sāman. Thus it is a slayer of the rival. Why do they call this sāman 'demon-slaying'? Because they slew them as if they were demons.
3.44: Now the gautama. It fulfills both aspects — bārhata by its prastāva, rāthantara in its rising. Gotama Rāhūgaṇa desired: 'May I be one who conquers.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereupon he became a conqueror. 'May we be conquerors' — with this they sit in the sattra. They indeed become conquerors. Whatever ṛṣis were after Gotama and whatever were before him — both groups honor Gotama as a ṛṣi. For he saw this greatness. Both those who are his later ancestors and those who are his earlier ones honor him — he who knows thus.
3.45: Now the antarikṣa. When the gods had departed, the asuras pierced the space between. The gods desired: 'May we, having conquered from the antarikṣa by means of the antarikṣa, ascend to the heavenly world.' They saw this sāman. They praised with it. Thereupon, having conquered from the antarikṣa by means of the antarikṣa, they ascended to the heavenly world. That is the antarikṣa-nature of the antarikṣa. This is a sāman of conquest leading to heaven. He who knows thus conquers and goes to the heavenly world. The antarikṣa is this day. The antarikṣa hovers. Because it has a nidhana in the middle, it is for stability. It has paristobha on both sides of the pada — this is the form of the antarikṣa. For the antarikṣa is propped on both sides by these two worlds. Therefore this sāman should not be omitted from this day. The antarikṣa is this day, the antarikṣa is this sāman. He who has praised ascends the day by means of it.
3.46: Now the kāṇva. Kaṇva the Nārṣada, wandering long without a foundation, desired: 'May I find a foundation.' He saw this sāman. He did not see its nidhana. He heard the buzzing of a biting fly. He saw it then. He praised with it. Thereupon he found his foundation. This is a sāman of foundation. He who knows thus finds a foundation. Because Kaṇva the Nārṣada saw it, therefore it is called kāṇva. 'tisro vāca īrayati pra vahnir' — the final verses are triṣṭubh, which is the form of the third day. Thereby three days are arranged. 'ṛtasya dhītiṃ brahmaṇo manīṣām / gāvo yanti gopatiṃ pṛcchamānās somaṃ yanti matayo vāvaśānāḥ' — the verses are go-possessing, cattle-possessing, for the obtainment of cattle.
3.47: Among those is the saṃkrośa. The gods, crying out together (saṃkrośamānāḥ), went to the heavenly world. Because they went to the heavenly world crying out together, that is the saṃkrośa-nature of the saṃkrośa. This is a sāman leading to heaven. He who knows thus goes to the heavenly world crying out. They also call it 'the saṃkrośa of the Aṅgirasas.' The Aṅgirasas desired: 'May we go to the heavenly world in mutual accord, crying out together.' They saw this sāman. They praised with it. Thereupon they went to the heavenly world in mutual accord, crying out together. Because they went to the heavenly world crying out together, that is the saṃkrośa-nature of the saṃkrośa. This is a sāman leading to heaven. He who knows thus goes to the heavenly world in mutual accord, crying out. For śrī is the heavenly world. He who knows thus attains śrī and goes to the heavenly world. Because the Aṅgirasas saw it, therefore it is called 'the saṃkrośa of the Aṅgirasas.' These two sāmans are indeed a call and a response — 'he ye ho vā hā ho' — with this the Aṅgirasas called. 'hā vā o vā' — with this they answered back.
3.48: 'yad dyāva indra te śataṃ śataṃ bhūmīr uta syuḥ / na tvā vajrin sahasraṃ sūryā anu' — that which contains 'hundred' and 'thousand' is the form of the jagatī. This is a jāgata day. 'na jātam aṣṭa rodasī' — these are the two worlds (rodasī). These it addresses. 'ā paprātha mahinā vṛṣṇyā vṛṣan viśvā śaviṣṭha śavase' — the verses are viśva-containing. For this day belongs to the Viśvedevas. 'asmaṃ ava maghavan gomati vraje vajriñ citrābhir ūtibhir' — the verses are go-possessing, cattle-possessing, for the obtainment of cattle. For cattle belong to this day. Among those is the vairūpa. The saṃvatsara (year) is virūpa (of diverse forms), the ṛtus are virūpa, the half-months are virūpa. It has five nidhanas because there are five seasons; it has twelve nidhanas because there are twelve months; it has directional stepping because of that; it is viśva-containing because this day belongs to the Viśvedevas. The vairūpa is procreative. When it is performed on the third day, it is for the birth of the fourth day. When the gods came into being, then the asuras came into being. The gods became horses; the asuras could not follow. Thereupon the gods prevailed, the asuras were defeated.
3.49: Because it contains a horse (aśva), it is for the repelling of the rival. The third day has adhyardhel̥ā (one-and-a-half il̥ā), and the fifth has adhyardhel̥ā. Just as a frog would croak 'iṭ,' so one should croak 'iṭ' there. Thereby the fifth day is made unworn (ayātayāma). All speech is obtained on the third day.
3.50: Among those is the vaiṣṭambha. By the vaiṣṭambha the gods, having propped (niṣṭabhya) the hostile rival away from these worlds, sat in the sattra in their own abode. He who knows thus, by the vaiṣṭambha, having propped the hostile rival away from these worlds, sits in the sattra in his own abode. It is actually called naiṣṭambha (the propping away). It is called vaiṣṭambha. This day is like the belly. The vaiṣṭambha is food. When the vaiṣṭambha is performed here, they place food in the middle of the self. That is why a person is as if propped up by food. 'taraṇir it siṣāsatī' — the stotriya verses are well-known. Among those is the raurava, which is three-formed, with three paristobhas, being the form of the third day. Agni is the ruru; Rudra is Agni. From whomever cattle depart, it is Agni who makes them depart. And to whomever cattle come, it is Agni who brings them. The sattra-performers are āgneya (belonging to Agni); their own deity obtains cattle for them. It becomes the acchāvāka-sāman with il̥ā. Cattle are il̥ā.
3.51: 'tisro vāca ud īrata' — the gāyatrī verses of the ārbhava pavamāna are the form of the third day. Thereby three days are arranged. 'gāvo mimanti dhenavaḥ / harir eti kanikradat' — the verses are go-possessing, cattle-possessing, for the obtainment of cattle. For cattle belong to this day. 'abhi brahmīr anūṣate' — 'abhi' occurs, which is the form of the rathantara. This is a rāthantara day. 'rāye samudrāṃś catura' — this is for the birth of the fourth day. 'asmabhyaṃ soma viśvata' — the verses are viśva-containing. For this day belongs to the Viśvedevas. 'ā pavasva sahasriṇa' — that which contains 'hundred' and 'thousand' is the form of the jagatī. This is a jāgata day. Among those the gāyatra has been explained. Now the pāṣṭhauha. Pāṣṭhauhī the Āṅgirasa, desiring heaven, performed austerities. He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereupon he reached the heavenly world. This is a sāman leading to heaven. He who knows thus reaches the heavenly world. Because Pāṣṭhauhī the Āṅgirasa saw it, therefore it is called pāṣṭhauha. These two sāmans are indeed a call and a response — 'ho ye ho vā hā ho' — with this the Aṅgirasas called. 'hā o vā o vā' — with this they answered back. These two sāmans are a call and a response, leading to heaven, for the attainment of the heavenly world. 'ā sotā pari ṣiñcate' — the verses are pari-containing, which is the form of the anta (end).
3.52: Among those is the vācassāma (sāman of speech). The gods sat in a sattra for deliverance. They did not know the heavenly world on this third day. They desired: 'May we know the heavenly world on this third day.' The sāman of speech, being chanted, floated toward them. They saw it. They praised with it. They prospered by it. They knew the heavenly world on this third day. This is a sāman of prosperity leading to heaven. He who knows thus prospers and goes to the heavenly world. That which is auspicious and peaceful in speech — that is the sāman. They say 'he speaks sāman' — meaning he speaks well. When this sāman is used, they pacify the three-day period with what is auspicious and peaceful in speech. Because the sāman of speech floated toward them as they chanted, therefore it is called vācassāma. 'sakhāya ā ni ṣīdata punānāya pra gāyate' — the verses are pra-containing (forward-moving) and pari-containing (encompassing). The pra-containing are for the birth of the fourth day. The pari-containing are the form of the end — for this is the concluding day — 'whoever is the end, let him be the end.'
3.53: Among those is the śaukta. The Aṅgirasas, having performed austerities, grieved. They desired: 'May we dispel grief.' They saw this sāman. They praised with it. By it they dispelled grief. Because they dispelled grief (śuc), that is the śaukta-nature of the śaukta. He who knows thus dispels grief. Śukti the Āṅgirasa, desiring heaven, performed austerities. He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereupon he reached the heavenly world. This is a sāman leading to heaven. He who knows thus reaches the heavenly world. Because Śukti the Āṅgirasa saw it, therefore it is called śaukta. 'sutāso madhumattamās somā indrāya mandina' — the verses are mad-containing. Rasa is indeed madhu. The third pressing is like something milked. Because these are mad-containing, they place rasa in it, and with this they cause it to swell. 'pavitravanto akṣaran devān gacchantu vo madā' — they place rasa and madhu progressively therein. 'indur indrāya pavata iti devāso abruvan / vācas patir makhasyate viśvasyeśāna ojasā' — the verses are viśva-containing. For this day belongs to the Viśvedevas. 'sahasradhāraḥ pavate samudro vācamīṅkhaya' — that which contains 'hundred' and 'thousand' is the form of the jagatī.
3.54: Among those the gaurivīta has been explained. Now the tvāṣṭrī-sāman — it is a sāman of hero-begetting. A hero is born to him who knows thus. And it is for the birth of the fourth day. For the tvāṣṭrī-sāman is procreative. It has a nidhana in the middle, for stability. Those who approach the ārmava pavamāna are like people floating toward an ocean without a shore to grasp. Because it has a nidhana in the middle, it is for stability. And it is as if 'seated,' which is the form of the end. For this day is the end of the heavenly worlds. The third day is performed for the heavenly world. Because it is as if 'seated,' it prevents overstepping the heavenly world. 'pavitraṃ te vitataṃ brahmaṇas pata' — the verses are pavitra-possessing (filter-containing). For the antarikṣa is like a filter stretched out. This day partakes of the antarikṣa. 'prabhur gātrāṇi pary eṣi viśvata' — the verses are viśva-containing. For this day belongs to the Viśvedevas. 'ataptatanūr na tad āmo aśnute śṛtāsa id vahantas saṃ tad āśate' — this is for attainment. 'tapoṣpavitraṃ vitataṃ divas pade śocanto asya tantavo vy asthiran / avanty asya pavītāram āśavo divaḥ pṛṣṭham adhi tiṣṭhanti tejasā' — this is for the attainment of the heavenly world. 'arūrucad uṣasaḥ pṛśnir agriya ukṣā mimāti bhuvaneṣu vājayuḥ / māyāvino mamire asya māyayā nṛcakṣasaḥ pitaro garbham ā dadhuḥ' —
3.55: Among those is the ariṣṭa, for the safety of the sacrifice. Whatever in the sacrifice is badly praised, badly recited, or goes awry — it is for its safety. The sacrifice is indeed unharmed by it. The gods were extending this twelve-night rite. Whatever was about to be harmed, they made unharmed by the ariṣṭa. That is the ariṣṭa-nature of the ariṣṭa. He who knows thus crosses over evil and attains glory by means of the ariṣṭa twelve-night rite. It has three il̥ā — cattle are il̥ā; cattle belong to this day — for the obtainment of cattle in succession. Three il̥ās are indeed a remedy. Three il̥ās are what is auspicious and peaceful in speech. Because three il̥ās occur on the third day, thereby they pacify the three days. Now the yajñāyajñīya has been explained. 'pra maṃhiṣṭhāya gāyate' — the pramaṃhiṣṭhīya has been explained. 'taṃ te madaṃ gṛṇīmasī' — the verses are mad-containing. They place rasa and madhu progressively in the third pressing. Among those the hārivarṇa has been explained. Its nidhana has four syllables — for cattle have four feet.
3.56: 'śrudhī havaṃ tiraścyā' — this is the tairaśca. Some gods and ṛṣis conquered the upper worlds. Then Tiraści the Āṅgirasa desired: 'May I conquer the crosswise worlds — those through which these waters flow, through which this wind blows, through which yonder sun travels, through which the moon and stars go.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereby he conquered the crosswise worlds — those through which these waters flow, through which this wind blows, through which yonder sun travels, through which the moon and the stars go. By other rites one conquers the upper worlds. But by this one conquers the crosswise worlds — both types of worlds together. He who knows thus conquers both the crosswise and the upper worlds. Tiraści the Āṅgirasa desired: 'May I obtain both heroes and cattle.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. 'śrudhī havaṃ tiraścyā indra yas tvā saparyati / suvīryasya gomato rāyas pūrdhi mahaṃ asi' — thus he obtained both heroes and cattle. This is the obtainment of heroes and cattle. He who knows thus obtains both heroes and cattle. Because Tiraści the Āṅgirasa saw it, therefore it is called tairaśca. The gods, going to the heavenly world, were followed by demons. They desired: 'May we slay the demons.' They saw this sāman. Making it into a divine fortress, they built it crosswise around. Thereby they slew the demons. This is the slaying of demons. He who knows thus slays demons and evil. It becomes the acchāvāka-sāman with il̥ā.
3.57: When the sacrifice is completed, the meters are completed, the deities are completed, these worlds are completed — then by what is the fourth day extended? they ask. 'By speech,' one should reply. Speech alone remains over, the imperishable three-syllabled. That extends the next three days. When they say 'the sacrifice is completed' — the first day partakes of the morning pressing, the second of the midday pressing, the third of the third pressing — thereby the sacrifice is completed. When they say 'the meters are completed' — the morning pressing is gāyatra, the midday pressing is traiṣṭubha, the third pressing is jāgata — thereby the meters are completed. When they say 'the deities are completed' — the morning pressing belongs to the Vasus, the midday pressing to the Rudras, the third pressing to the Ādityas — thereby the deities are completed. When they say 'these worlds are completed' — the morning pressing is this world, the midday pressing is this middle world, the third pressing is yonder world — thereby these worlds are completed. The imperishable three-syllabled remains over. That extends the next three days. He who knows Indra on the fourth day becomes possessed of indriya (power). Speech indeed is Indra on the fourth day.
3.58: These are the verses: 'pra ta āśvinīḥ pavamāna dhenava' — they are pra-containing (forward-moving) on the prāyaṇīya (opening) day. For this day is one of leading forth. 'Pra' is the form of the gāyatrī. By the form of the gāyatrī they set forth. 'divyā asṛgran payasā dharīmaṇī' — they approach the form of the virāj. For this is a vairāja day. 'prāntarikṣāt sthāvarīs te asṛkṣate' — the verses are antarikṣa-containing. For the third day partakes of the antarikṣa. This day was born from the third. 'ye tvā mṛjanty ṛṣiṣāṇa vedhasa' — they wipe clean, as it were, one newly born. And indeed one is born by means of this day. 'ubhayataḥ pavamānasya raśmaya' — from both three-day periods they beget. They beget forward from the first, they extend from the latter. 'dhruvasya sataḥ paryanti ketava' — the day is the fixed point, the meters are the rays. Just as one would yoke weary oxen, turning them around, in the yoke — so they yoke the meters with a double turning on the completed days. 'yadi pavitre adhi mṛjyate harir' — they wipe clean, as it were, one newly born. And indeed one is born by means of this day. 'sattā ni yonau kalaśeṣu sīdatī' — this addresses Soma directly. 'viśvā dhāmāni viśvacakṣa ṛbhvasaḥ prabhoṣ ṭe sataḥ paryanti ketava' — Agni indeed is the master of these worlds. They beget him from the morning pressing. Him, once begotten, they churn at the midday pressing.
3.59: These are jagatī verses with gāyatrī coloring, fulfilled by the form of the day. Where they fulfill with the day's form, there prosperity comes. Prosperity comes to him who knows thus. The junction point of this transition has not been perceived. The first three-day period ends in jagatī. They enter this day with the jagatī. From the same level they reach the same level. He who knows the stoma as yoked by four — four yoked together carry him. The first three-day period ends in jagatī. They enter this day with the jagatī. Just as one would yoke a lead horse for the lead-horse yoke — so they hitch the latter three-day period to the former. Four yoked together carry him who knows thus. Indeed, he who has scattered meters has gathered meters, and he who has gathered meters has scattered meters. The first three-day period ends in jagatī; they enter this day with the jagatī — from the same level they reach the same level. The second three-day period ends in triṣṭubh; they enter the third three-day period with the triṣṭubh — from the same level they reach the same level. They cross over where the third three-day period ends; they enter the tenth day with the gāyatrī — from the same level they cross over going. This indeed is the gathering of meters. 'pavamāno ajījanad' — the anurūpa verse is birth-containing. They beget the ṣoḍaśin from the morning pressing. The one begotten, they take hold of at the third pressing. They say: 'With the stotriya they take hold of the day's form, with the anurūpa of the sāman.' The stotriya and the anurūpa are present. The stotriya is prāṇa, the anurūpa is apāna.
3.60: 'divaś citraṃ na tanyatuṃ jyotir vaiśvānaraṃ bṛhad' — they approach the form of the bṛhat. For this is a bārhata day. 'pavamānasya te rasaḥ, pavamāna rasas tave' — it repeatedly touches the same, which is the vairāja form. For the form of the vairāja is anutunna (pricked). 'pra yad gāvo na bhūrṇaya' — they approach the prāyaṇīya form. For this is a prāyaṇīya day. 'tveṣā ayāso akramur ghnantaḥ kṛṣṇām apa tvacam' — evil is the dark skin. They dispel it by this. 'suvitasya vanāmahe ati setuṃ durāyyam' — the first three-day period is the bridge difficult to cross; they cross over it by this. 'śṛṇve vṛṣṭer iva svanaḥ pavamānasya śuṣmiṇaḥ / caranti vidyuto divi' — they approach the form of the bṛhat. For this is a bārhata day. 'ā pavasva mahīm iṣaṃ gomad indo hiraṇyavat / aśvavat soma vīravat' — the verses are go-possessing, cattle-possessing, for the obtainment of cattle. 'pavasva viśvacarṣaṇa ā mahī rodasī pṛṇā / uṣās sūryo na raśmibhiḥ' — the verses are sūrya-containing (sun-containing). For this is a solar day — ekaviṃśa, the foundation, vairāja. 'pari naś śarmayantyā dhārayā soma viśvataḥ / sarā raseva viṣṭapam' — the verses are pari-containing, which is the form of the end. For this is the concluding day. 'āśur arṣa bṛhanmate pari priyeṇa dhāmnā / yatra devā iti bruvan // pariṣkṛṇvann aniṣkṛtam ayaṃ sa yo divas pari' — pari pari occurs, which is the form of the end. For anuṣṭubh is the end of the meters.
3.61: The hymn has six verses — for there are six seasons — for the completion of the seasons. They ask: 'If a six-verse hymn is the form of the sixth day, why is it performed on the fourth day?' One should reply: 'There are six seasons making the year; the year is Prajāpati, the sacrifice. Having completed the year, Prajāpati, the sacrifice, they begin again at the opening of the pressing. For the sacrifice is as if completed with this first three-day period.' The second six-verse hymn comes after. They take hold of the sacrifice progressively by it. 'hinvanti sūram usraya' — they urge on, as it were, one newly born. And indeed one is born by means of this day. 'svasāro jāmayas patim' — these are the sisters, these are the kinswomen. 'mahām induṃ mahīyuva' — these are the 'magnifying ones.' They indeed urge him on. 'pavamāna rucāruce' — with 'rukmat' (golden) they approach the solar form. For this is a solar day — ekaviṃśa, the foundation, vairāja. 'ā pavamāna suṣṭutiṃ vṛṣṭiṃ devebhyo duvaḥ / iṣe pavasva saṃyatam' — the concluding verse is for the sustaining of people and the stability of cattle. The stoma is ekaviṃśa (twenty-one-fold). Twelve months, five seasons, three worlds, and yonder sun — that is ekaviṃśa. When combined, they become twelve jagatī verses. Those are twenty-four gāyatrīs. Those are eighteen anuṣṭubhs.
3.62: 'janasya gopā ajaniṣṭa jāgṛvir' — the ājya is āgneya (belonging to Agni), containing birth. He is indeed the guardian of the people. They beget him from the morning pressing. Him, once begotten, they churn at the midday pressing. 'Agni' — they declare him by name. 'sudakṣas suvitāya navyasa' — the dakṣas (powers) are the prāṇas (vital breaths). They place the prāṇas in him. 'ghṛtapratīka' — ghṛta is Agni's beloved abode; they bring him to fulfillment by his beloved abode. 'bṛhatā divispṛśe' — they approach the form of the bṛhat. For this is a bārhata day. 'dyumad vi bhāti bharatebhyaś śucir' — these peoples are indeed Bharata's; he shines brightly for them. Also because the Bharatas first sacrificed with him. Also because he carries the oblation to the gods, therefore he is Bhārata. 'tvām agne āṅgiraso guhā hitam' — 'tvām' is the form of the bṛhat. This is a bārhata day. 'anv avindañ chiśriyāṇaṃ vanevana' — for they found him settled in tree after tree. 'sa jāyate mathyamānas saho mahad' — they first churn him at the morning pressing. Him, once begotten, they generate at the midday pressing. 'tvām āhus sahasas putram aṅgira' — for they generate him by force, as it were. 'yajñasya ketum' — he is indeed the banner of the sacrifice.
3.63: 'prathamaḥ purohitam' — they place him in front, for when they establish the fire... 'agniṃ naras triṣadhasthe sam indhata' — these peoples are 'the men'; they kindle him. 'indreṇa devais sarathaṃ sa barhiṣi sīdan ni hotā yajathāya sukratur' — the hymn belongs to all the gods. For this day belongs to all the gods, being ānuṣṭubha. All the gods belong to the anuṣṭubh. 'ayaṃ vāṃ mitrāvaruṇe' — the ājya is maitrāvaruṇa. This is a re-announcement of the offering. The sacrifice is as if complete when this first three-day period is completed. 'yan nu vo paro yajña' — thus they announce to the gods. Therefore the sacrificer should make this the second ājya. 'rājānāv anabhidruhā dhruve sadasy uttame / sahasrasthūṇa āsāte' — they approach the thousand-fold form of the virāj. For this is a vairāja day. 'tā samrājā ghṛtāsutī' — the verses are samrāj-containing (sovereign-containing). For they have attained sovereignty, as it were.
3.64: Dadhyañc Ātharvaṇa was brilliant and possessed of brahmavarcas. However many asuras looked upon him, they would fall down headless. He ascended to the heavenly world. When Indra was hard-pressed by the asuras, he said: 'Where might that Dadhyañc be?' They told him: 'He has ascended to the heavenly world, O Lord.' He said: 'Is nothing of his left here?' They told him: 'There was this horse-head with which he told the Aśvins the knowledge of the gods, but we do not know where it went.' 'Search for it,' he said. They searched for it. 'icchann aśvasya yac chiraḥ parateṣv apaśritam / tad vidac charyaṇāvati' — Śaryaṇāvat is the name of a lake at the hind part of Kurukṣetra. Having found it, they took it up and gave it to him. He used to hold it openly before the asuras.
3.65: With those very bones he slew ninety-nine of the asuras. This is a rival-slaying victory. 'indro dadhīco asthabhir vṛtrāṇy apratiṣkutaḥ / jaghāna navatīr nava' — He who knows thus conquers and slays the hostile rival. 'atrāha gor amanvata nāma tvaṣṭur apīcyam / itthā candramaso gṛhe' — the verses are go-possessing, cattle-possessing, for the obtainment of cattle. 'iyaṃ vām asya manmana' — the ājya is aindrāgna (belonging to Indra-Agni). 'indrāgnī pūrvyastutiḥ / abhrād vṛṣṭir ivājani' — they say 'ajanī' (was born). For indeed one is born by means of this day. 'śṛṇutaṃ jaritur havam indrāgnī vanataṃ giraḥ / īśānā pipyataṃ dhiyaḥ // mā pāpatvāya no narendrāgnī mābhiśastaye / mā no rīradhataṃ nide' — this is the removal of evil. The stoma is ekaviṃśa. Twelve months, five seasons, three worlds, and yonder sun — that is ekaviṃśa.
3.66: 'pavasva dakṣasādhana' — the gāyatrī verses of the midday pavamāna are dakṣa-possessing (power-possessing). By them they empower the day, empower the meters, empower the deities, empower the ṣoḍaśin. 'devebhyaḥ pītaye hare marudbhyo vāyave mada' — the verses contain the Maruts. The midday's form is marutvat (Marut-possessing). They do not depart from the form of the midday. 'saṃ devaiś śobhate vṛṣe' — the verses are vṛṣan-possessing, which is the form of the bṛhat. This is a bārhata day. 'pavamāna dhiyā hito 'bhi yoniṃ kanikradad' — the verses are yoni-containing. From the yoni, offspring and cattle are born. Birth-containing is the form of this vairāja day. 'dharmaṇā vāyum ā ruhe' — the verses are vāyu-containing — vāyu is peace — for peace, so there may be no scorching. Among those the gāyatra has been explained. Now the caturṇidhana (four-nidhana) of the Ātharvanas is the form of the fourth day. The Ātharvans, ascending above to the heavenly world, did not find a foothold there. They desired: 'May we stand firm in the heavenly world.' They saw this sāman. They praised with it. Thereupon they stood firm in the heavenly world. It has four nidhanas for stability — 'as if standing on four feet,' they say. This is a well-established sāman leading to heaven, a sāman of foundation. He who knows thus goes to the heavenly world and stands firm in the heavenly world. It is also a remedy and an expiation. They make an expiation as if making a remedy. By this they make the expiation a remedy. It is anutunna (pricked), for from what is anutunna, offspring and cattle are born. The anutunna is the form of this vairāja day. Thereby it is fulfilled in form.
3.67: Now the nidhanakāma (desire-in-the-nidhana) vairāja. The virāj is as it were neither svāra, nor nidhana-possessing, nor il̥ā-possessing, nor vainarksamama. It has vāṅnidhana (speech-nidhana). Speech is the virāj — for the grasping of the virāj. They take hold of the virāj directly by it. Desires are in its nidhana. Whatever desire one desires, one should meditate on it while approaching its nidhana. That desire is fulfilled for him. Because desires are in its nidhana, therefore it is called nidhanakāma vairāja. The gods, desiring heaven, performed austerities. They saw this sāman. They praised with it. Thereupon they reached the heavenly world. This is a sāman leading to heaven. He who knows thus reaches the heavenly world. The nidhana of this is that fire which they churn during the vairāja stotra. 'tavāhaṃ soma rāraṇe' — 'tava' is the form of the bṛhat. This is a bārhata day. 'sakhya indo divedive / purūṇi babhro ni caranti mām ava paridhīṃr ati taṃ ihi' — for they have gone beyond. 'tavāhaṃ naktam uta soma te divā duhāno babhra ūdhanī' — these are the two udders. These it addresses; these milk all desires for him. 'ghṛṇā tapantam ati sūryaṃ para' — the verses are sūrya-containing. For this is a solar day — ekaviṃśa, the foundation, vairāja. 'śkunā iva pratime' — for they have flown beyond. Among those the āṣṭādaṃṣṭra has been explained. It has il̥ā above, which is the form of the bṛhat on the bārhata day.
3.68: Now the ābhīśava — it is a grasping of the sacrifice and a running back. It grasps the fifth day and runs back to the third. The gods, by means of the twelve-day rite, ascended upward to the heavenly world. Their ungrasped sacrifice began to fall apart. They saw these two sāmans. With them, as if grasping a chariot by its reins (abhīśu), they grasped it and ascended to the heavenly world. Having gone to the heavenly world, they said: 'These two sāmans have been like reins (abhīśu) for our sacrifice.' That is the ābhīśava-nature of the ābhīśava pair. These two are sāmans leading to heaven. He who knows thus reaches the heavenly world. Therefore both of these sāmans should be performed. Just as one would drive with a single rein — that is when only one is used; just as one would drive with both reins — that is when both are used. Therefore both should be performed. The other one should be on the ninth day. Abhīśu Śyāvāśvi desired: 'May the gods be satisfied with my soma.' He saw these two sāmans. He praised with them. Thereupon the gods were satisfied with his soma. Of these two, there is as it were a spilling — just as one would satisfy. With 'e e' they satisfy while holding back. These two are sāmans of satisfaction. He who knows thus — the gods are satisfied with his soma.
3.69: Now the caturṇidhana (four-nidhana) of the Āṅgirasas is the form of the fourth day. Also, by it the four days are arranged. The Aṅgirasas, desiring cattle and heaven, performed austerities. They saw this sāman. They praised with it. Reaching the il̥ā in the middle, they went upward to svar. Cattle are il̥ā; the heavenly world is svar. Having established themselves on cattle, they went to the heavenly world. This is a sāman pertaining to cattle and leading to heaven. He who knows thus obtains cattle and goes to the heavenly world. Because the Āṅgirasas saw it, therefore it is called the caturṇidhana of the Āṅgirasas. Now these verses occur — 'punāno akramīd abhī' — they are 'going-beyond' verses. For they go beyond these worlds. From the world of the Āditya indeed — these are the worlds; this is the first three-day period. The fourth day is the world of the Āditya. They go beyond it. 'ā yonis aruṇo ruhad' — the verses are yoni-containing. From the yoni, offspring and cattle are born. Birth-containing is the form of this vairāja day. 'gamad indro vṛṣā suta' — the verses are vṛṣan-possessing, which is the form of the bṛhat. This is a bārhata day. 'nū no rayiṃ mahām indro 'syabhyaṃ soma viśvata' — the verses are viśva-containing. For this day belongs to the Viśvedevas. 'ā pavasva sahasriṇam' — they approach the thousand-fold form of the virāj. For this is a vairāja day. They say: 'Those triṣṭubhs that place the ending in the gāyatrī verses — they depart from the pavamāna's end.' The verses are vṛṣan-possessing. The vṛṣan form is the form of the triṣṭubh. Thereby they do not depart from the triṣṭubh at the pavamāna's end. Among those the satrāsāhīya has been explained. It becomes the uktha-sāman — cattle are uktha — for the obtainment of cattle. They ask: 'The satrāsāhīya being an uktha-sāman, why is it performed in the pavamāna?' The first day partakes of the morning pressing, the second of the midday pressing, the third of the third pressing. This day partakes of the uktha. When the satrāsāhīya is performed here, they move it from the uktha. They fulfill it in its own abode. Being fulfilled by its own abode, it becomes fruitful in its own realm. Also, the uktha is cattle, the self is the midday.
3.70: The vairāja is procreative. When it begins with a pair of stobhas, it is for the procreation of a pair. The three former stobhas mean 'from the stobha, from the real, offspring are born'; the three latter are prāṇa, apāna, and vyāna. These are established by it. He utters the pada three times. A pair arises from two coming together. What is born from the pair is the third. He pushes (anunudati) three times. From what is pushed, offspring and cattle are born. He who knows thus abounds in offspring and cattle. He props (viṣṭabhnoti) ten times — he thereby places the bones. Nine stobhas follow, the nidhana being the tenth — the virāj has ten syllables, food is the virāj — for the obtainment of food through the virāj. They churn fire. Agni is the virāj. They thereby beget the virāj as food. They churn after making the hiṃkāra. For from the hiṃkāra, seed is placed. And they beget the virāj as food from the middle of the sāman. They churn on the thigh of the udgātṛ, the prastotṛ and the pratihartṛ supporting, so the virāj may not be cut off. They churn on the right thigh. Therefore this half of a person is more powerful. They churn upon a chip (śakala), for peace. They churn upon avakā grass, so there may be no scorching. The fire once born, they carry it to the āgnīdhra hearth. From there they distribute the fires. But others say it should be carried to the āhavanīya, for the sacrificer is the āhavanīya, and this fire is offspring.
3.71: They say: 'They carry food away from that from which they carry the fire.' One should address it with the mantra: 'punar ūrjā ni vartatva punar agna iṣāyuṣā / punar naḥ pāhy aṃhasaḥ // saha rayyā nivartasvāgne pinvasva dhārayā / viśvapsnyā viśvatas pari' — thus one places food back in oneself. With what he begins the prastāva, with that they approach the nidhana. Therefore yonder sun goes back and forth. From where it rose today, from there it will rise tomorrow. Now the vāmadevya: cattle are the vāmadevya. Those who have praised with it become rich in cattle. 'viśvāḥ pṛtanā abhibhūtaraṃ naras sajūs tatakṣur indraṃ jajanuś ca rājasa' — the verses are birth-containing.
3.72: Among those is the traiśoka. The vairāja sacrifice is as if torn apart. They prop it up with the traiśoka, as with a larger meter. These worlds, being together, split into three. They grieved, as one split into three would grieve. The gods said: 'Let us remove the three griefs (śuc) of these three worlds.' They saw this sāman. They praised with it. Thereby they removed the three griefs of the three worlds. Because they removed the three griefs of the three worlds, that is the traiśoka-nature of the traiśoka. He who knows thus removes grief. They deposited those griefs in the eunuch, the gambler, and the harlot. Therefore these, pierced by grief, grieve. These do not arise for him who knows thus. Therefore one should not speak about these people, lest one share in their grief. Kaṇva the Nārṣada found the daughter of Akhagasya the asura. From her two sons were born to him, Triśoka and Nabhāka. She, angered, went to her kinsmen. He followed after her. The asuras smeared him with an ointment and said: 'Know the dawn here, if you are a brāhmaṇa.' The Aśvins became aware of this. The Aśvins are the liberators of the bound among the gods. They came to him unseen and said: 'When these two strike the vīṇā and fly over and over above, then know the dawn.' Those others kept rising in the night saying: 'Get up, dawn has broken, yoke the ploughs!' 'It has not yet dawned!' he would say. Then those two struck the vīṇā and flew over and over above.
3.73: They said: 'This one was a brāhmaṇa, a ṛṣi. His wife is hard to take away. Come, let us give her to him.' They gave her to him. She said: 'They will again try to kill you by stealth. Do not sit on that seat.' They departed. They placed for him a golden seat in the shade. Carelessly, he sat upon it. It, becoming a stone slab, wrapped itself around him. The two sons, Triśoka and Nabhāka, became aware: 'The asuras have wrapped our father in a stone slab!' They came. Nabhāka desired: 'May I outwit them.' He saw the Nābhāka sūkta. With it he praised her (the stone).
3.74: Just as a string would be visible in a gem — so it was. Then Triśoka desired: 'May I split it open.' He saw this sāman. With it he split it in two. Its edge turned back. He desired it to live. He touched it saying 'o yi jīva (live!).' It lived. For him it was as if dark. He desired: 'May it be as daylight for him.' He touched it saying 'o yi diva (day!).' For him it became as daylight. He desired: 'May I send him to the heavenly world.' He sent him to the heavenly world saying 'o yi divam.' For one whose edge, as it were, is turning, one should approach the nidhana with 'o yi jīva.' For one for whom there is darkness, as it were, one should approach the nidhana with 'o yi diva.' For one who desires heaven, one should approach the nidhana with 'o yi divam.' These are the nidhanas of this sāman; these are the desires. He obtains these very desires.
3.75: 'yo rājā carṣaṇīnām' — for it is as if they attained kingship at that time. 'yātā rathebhir adhriguḥ / viśvāsāṃ tarutā pṛtanānāṃ jyeṣṭhaṃ yo vṛtrahā gṛṇe' — for it is as if they attained preeminence at that time. 'indraṃ taṃ śumbha puruhanmann avase yasya dvitā vidhartari / hastena vajraḥ prati dhāyi darśato mahān devo na sūryaḥ' — the verses are sūrya-containing. For this is a solar day — ekaviṃśa, the foundation, vairāja. Among those is the pṛśni. Speech is pṛśni. They seek speech by this day. They find it. They also call it 'the pṛśni of Bharadvāja.' Bharadvāja desired: 'May I abound in offspring and cattle.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereupon he abounded in offspring and cattle. He said: 'Pṛśni has indeed been abundant in offspring and cattle.' That is the pṛśni-nature of the pṛśni. This is a sāman of procreation. He who knows thus abounds in offspring and cattle. Also, by this very sāman Bharadvāja obtained food in the pṛśni. This is a sāman for the obtainment of food. He who knows thus obtains food and becomes the chief food-eater among his own. Because Bharadvāja saw it, therefore it is called 'the pṛśni of Bharadvāja.' It becomes the acchāvāka-sāman with il̥ā. Cattle are il̥ā.
3.126: He (Tvaṣṭṛ) said: 'The gods are sitting at Kurukṣetra, performing the sacrifice without its head. They do not attain the desire that is in the sacrifice. The head of the sacrifice was cut off. That which Dadhyañc Ātharvaṇa perceived — go to him. He will teach it to you. Then you two will also become soma-drinkers.' Now, as to what is said 'the head of the sacrifice was cut off' — that is yonder sun (āditya). And that is indeed the pravargya. The two (Aśvins) went to Dadhyañc Ātharvaṇa. They said to him: 'O ṛṣi, we have come to you.' 'For what desire?' 'We wish you to teach us the head of the sacrifice.' 'No,' he said. 'Indra also perceived that. He told me: if you tell this to another, I will cut off your head. Therefore I am afraid.' 'Then teach it to us by means of a horse's head.' 'So be it,' he said. 'But let me see you two conversing.' The two set aside their own head and fastened on the horse's head, and they sat conversing, singing a sāman, reciting an ṛc and pronouncing a yajus.
3.127: Indra became aware of this — 'He has told them.' He rushed up and cut off this horse-head. Then what was his own head, these two Aśvins (the manīṣins) refitted. They came to the gods who were performing the sacrifice without a head. They said to them: 'You are performing the sacrifice without a head. You do not attain the desire that is in the sacrifice.' 'Who knows the head of the sacrifice?' 'We do.' 'Then refit it.' 'Take a graha for us two.' They drew the āśvina graha for them. They said: 'You two are the adhvaryus; you two, knowing this, shall refit the head of the sacrifice.' 'So be it.' The two became the adhvaryus.
3.128: Now Cyavana Bhārgava, having become young again, came to Śaryāta Mānava. He had him perform a sacrifice in the eastern region. He (Śaryāta) gave him a thousand (cows). With that he performed the sacrifice. This indeed is what Cyavana Bhārgava, having praised with this sāman, became young again, obtained a young woman as wife, and sacrificed with a thousand. These are the desires in this sāman; one attains these very desires. Whatever desire one has when one praises with this sāman, that desire is fulfilled for him. And also, with this very sāman Cyavana Bhārgava — whatever food he desired, that arose for him from the spring of Sarasvatī. This is therefore the sāman for the attainment of food. One obtains food, one becomes the foremost food-eater among one's own people — he who knows thus. Now, because Cyavana Bhārgava perceived it, it is called cyāvana. 'The child, the prāṇa of the great ones' — with this they approach the form of cattle. For the young one belongs to the cattle. In those (verses), the krośa (sāman), the bhrātṛvya-slaying sāman with Indra. One slays the hostile rival; Indra comes to his invocation — he who knows thus. It has the form of aila — for cattle are ilā.
3.129: 'Purify yourself for the winning of nourishment' — these are anuṣṭubh verses containing the word vāja — for vāja is food — for the attainment of food. 'Pressed through the filter, for Indra, O Soma, for Viṣṇu, sweetest for the gods' — these are vaiṣṇava verses — for the sacrifice is Viṣṇu — for the undertaking of the sacrifice. In those, the gaurivīta has its brāhmaṇa stated elsewhere. Now the eight-ilā pada-stobha obtains the fresh portion of the ilā verses. Through it, single-hoofed animals stand (remain) for him. Prajāpati, while ruling over creatures, was seized by evil (pāpman) from the head. He desired: 'May I drive away the evil.' He perceived these pada-stobhas. He praised with them. With them he drove away the evil. One should sing them as if casting off. For casting off, as it were, he drove away the evil from the head first.
3.130: In the beginning, the ṛṣis had mantras and progeny but not cattle. They desired: 'May we obtain cattle.' They perceived cattle in the course of a year. They released them with this eight-ilā pada-stobha. They said: 'There is still more.' They released them with the six-ilā. They said: 'There is still more.' They released them with the four-ilā. They said: 'There is still indeed more.' They released them with the two-ilā. These pada-stobhas are indeed cattle. Cattle are this day. One who knows thus becomes rich in cattle. And all of them are in the jagatī metre. Cattle are the jagatī. Therefore, through this, single-hoofed animals also stand (remain) for him, because all are in the jagatī.
3.131: Now the pārtha with double repetition is the form of the second day, for this is the second day by its foundation. Now the ṛṣabha is in the śakvarī metre. Cattle are the simā verses, cattle are the revatī verses, cattle are the ṛṣabha. They release the bull among the cattle for pairing, for procreation. One who knows thus is born through pairing. And it is supported by two deities each. Therefore the bull, when mounting, is two-footed. These deities have four syllables each. Therefore it mounts with two feet and generates four-footed ones. One who knows thus is born forth with progeny and cattle. It is also in the pāṅkta metre. It is performed on the pāṅkta day for the completeness of form. 'The drop, the prize-winner, purifies itself, rich in cattle' — these are rich in cattle, having the form of cattle, for the attainment of cattle. For cattle are this day. In those, the dāśaspatya has its brāhmaṇa stated elsewhere. And the yajñāyajñīya has its brāhmaṇa stated elsewhere. 'O Agni, may we thrive' — these are verses with ā (āvatī). This sacrificial section (the uktha) is cut off, as it were, from the main body of the sacrifice.
3.132: In those (verses), the saṃjaya sāman. The gods and asuras contended for these worlds. In this world, the gods desired: 'May we win these worlds together, may we conquer the asuras, the rivals in the contest.' They perceived this sāman. They praised with it. With it they won these worlds together; they conquered the rivals, the asuras, in the contest. Because they won these worlds together (sam-ajayan), that is the saṃjaya-ness of saṃjaya. One wins these worlds together, one conquers the hostile rival in the contest — he who knows thus. It is in the pāṅkta metre; it is performed on the pāṅkta day — for cattle are pāṅkta. Cattle are this day — for the attainment of cattle. They also call it 'the vāmadevya with a double hiṃkāra.' Cattle are the simā verses, cattle are the revatī verses, cattle are the vāmadevya with a double hiṃkāra. Those who have praised with this become rich in cattle. And also, whatever seems harsh (krūra) on this day beyond the śakvarī verses, that they pacify with these two hiṃkāras. Now the saumitra is a bhrātṛvya-slaying sāman with Indra.
3.133: Three (ṛṣis) perceived the sacred formulation (brahman): Nodhas one third, Sumitra one third, Bṛhadukhta one third. Because the naudhasaṃ is performed in the first triad of days (triyaha), thereby the first triad of days is made endowed with brahman. And because the bārhadukthaṃ is performed in the third triad of days, thereby the third triad of days is made endowed with brahman. By these sāmans, these triads of days are made endowed with brahman. It has a five-syllable nidhana, being the form of the fifth day, for the continuity and non-interruption of the five-night period.
3.134: In those (verses), the mahāvaiśvāmitra. With this, Viśvāmitra obtained greatness, abundance, and supremacy over cattle. This is the sāman pertaining to cattle. One obtains greatness, abundance, and supremacy over cattle — he who knows thus. Indra wished to slay Vṛtra. He ran to Viśvāmitra: 'O ṛṣi, I run to you; instruct us here.' Viśvāmitra perceived this sāman. He praised with it. Then Indra slew Vṛtra. He said: 'Great indeed was this of Viśvāmitra.' That is the mahāvaiśvāmitra-ness of mahāvaiśvāmitra. It is also a bhrātṛvya-slayer. One slays the hostile rival — he who knows thus. It has three ilā — for cattle are ilā. Cattle are this day — for the attainment of supremacy over cattle. Three ilā are indeed a healing remedy. This is the auspicious and peaceful aspect of speech: three ilā. Because the three-ilā is on the fifth day, by that they pacify both the triṇava stoma (and the day). The seasons (ṛtus) departed from the sun (āditya). He desired: 'May the seasons not depart from me; may they revolve around me.' He perceived this sāman. He praised with it. 'Hehayā o hā o hā' — these are like his horses (hayā), as it were. Then the seasons did not depart from him. Then they returned to him. They entered him. He mounted upon them. He, mounted upon the seasons and the year, shines in this glory. Because Viśvāmitra perceived it, it is called mahāvaiśvāmitra. It is an aila sāman, the acchāvāka-sāman. For cattle are ilā.
3.135: 'The light of the sacrifice purifies itself, the dear honey' — this is the form of the simā verses. For the form of the simā verses and the revatī verses is the same. The revatī verses were fashioned from the simā verses. 'Light,' it says — it places light in the creatures. 'Light' and 'purifies' — this is the form of the gāyatrī. The gāyatrī is extended on this day. 'Father of the gods, the progenitor, the brilliant one' — Prajāpati is the father of the gods, he is the progenitor, he is the brilliant one. This day belongs to Prajāpati. 'He holds the treasure, dear to the two (worlds), the most intoxicating, the exhilarating, the powerful juice' — for this day is the essence (rasa). The essence that flowed beyond from the fifth day, that became this day. This is indeed that essence. 'The prize-winner rushes forth, bellowing toward the vessel' — they release the bull in the revatī verses at the morning pressing for the sake of procreation. 'Lord of heaven, hundred-streamed, wise' — whatever is hundredfold or thousandfold, that is the form of the jagatī. This day is jāgata. 'Friend of Mitra, he sits in the seats, polished by the waters, by the rivers, the bull' — these are vṛṣaṇvat (bull-possessing), the form of bṛhat. This day is bārhata. 'At the head of rivers, purifying himself, he flows; at the head of speech, foremost, he goes among the cows; at the head of nourishment, he shares great wealth; self-armed, O Soma, you are pressed by the pressers' — the word agra (foremost) recurs again and again. For this day is foremost — the bārhata. The trayastriṃśa is foremost among stomas.
3.136: These are jagatī verses, of jagatī character, completed in the form of the day. Where they complete with the form of the day, there it succeeds. It succeeds for him who knows thus. 'The prize-winners have been sent forth' — this is a corresponding (anurūpa) verse, speaking of what has been accomplished. These (verses) recount what was accomplished during the six-day period. Now they say: 'With the stotriya they begin the form of the day; with the anurūpa of the sāman (they complete it).' There are both stotriya and anurūpa. The stotriya is the prāṇa (in-breath); the anurūpa is the apāna (out-breath). Having prospered through prāṇa and apāna, they proceed. 'Rich in cattle, rich in horses, bright, rich in heroes, the swift ones' — these are rich in cattle, possessing cattle, for the attainment of cattle. For cattle are this day. 'Cleansing themselves by those devoted to truth, being polished in the two hands, they purify themselves in the sheep's wool filter' — this is the form of the revatī verses. 'They (bestow) all wealth upon the worshipper' — these are viśvavat (all-possessing). For this day is vaiśvadeva. 'O Soma, the heavenly and the earthly ones purify themselves, and the atmospheric ones' — with this he lays hold of these worlds. These worlds are Prajāpati. This day belongs to Prajāpati.
3.137: 'Purify yourself, O god-invoked one, beyond' — this is the beginning of the ten-verse hymn. 'He goes beyond what is at hand,' they say. This day is like something that has arrived. 'O Soma, rush through the filter; O drop, enter Indra, the bull' — these are vṛṣaṇvat (bull-possessing), the form of bṛhat. This day is bārhata. 'Open wide your great expanse' — these are āvatī (containing ā), for the sake of not overstepping. For at this point they are approaching the end, as it were. 'Lest we overstep the six days in going forward.' 'They milked the dear honey' — the milked milk of the mahānāmnī verses became the revatī verses. The milked milk of the mahānāmnī verses is the revatī verses. 'You who are great, the great ones follow you' — the trayastriṃśa stoma is great. That is what the revatī verses (celebrate). 'The waters flow, the rivers; when you will be clothed with cows / The ocean is purified in the waters, the pillar, the support of heaven; Soma on the filter, longing for us' — these contain the word water (āpas). For this day pertains to water. 'The tawny bull has bellowed' — these are vṛṣaṇvat, the form of bṛhat. For this day is bārhata. 'Great like Mitra to behold' — for great is this day, being the trayastriṃśa. 'O Indra, your praises are polished by those eager for action, with which you adorn yourself for exhilaration / You, for exhilaration, the fierce ones, the world-maker, we invoke, for your great praise / O drop, you are the winner of cows, the winner of men, the winner of horses, the winner of prizes; you are the self of the sacrifice, the primeval one' — these are rich in cattle, possessing cattle, for the attainment of cattle. For cattle are this day. 'For us, O drop, (send) the Indriya power; purify yourself with a stream of honey; like Parjanya with rain.'
3.138: 'O Soma, conquer and win' — this is the second ten-verse hymn. Progressively by means of this they obtain the virāj and food. The ṛṣis, desiring prosperity, perceived a dear utterance of speech: 'Make us more prosperous, make us more prosperous.' They praised with it. Then they all prospered. All indeed prosper who, knowing thus, praise with this ten-verse hymn. They place the udarka (final chant) at the end. The sixth day is the essence (rasa). For the sake of not letting the essence escape. Just as one would smear the dam of waters, even so they place the udarka at the end for the sake of not letting the essence escape. It has four verses, for the attainment of four-footed cattle. Implicitly, two-footed (verses) are present, for the attainment of two-footed animals. It is like a nārāśaṃsī verse, being divided, for the attainment of both desires — both the divine and the human. They place the udarka at the end. The sixth day is the essence.
3.139: Now, Taranta and Purumīḍha, the sons of Vitadaśva, having received much from the two Dhvasras, the Puruṣantis, felt as if they had swallowed poison. They kept touching the gift with their finger (in aversion). They desired: 'May this gift of ours become as if un-received, as if taken but not accepted.' They perceived this four-verse hymn. With it they made restitution. Then their gift became as if un-received, as if taken but not accepted. One who, having received a gift, should desire 'May this gift of mine become as if un-received, as if taken but not accepted' — he should make restitution with this four-verse hymn. His gift becomes as if un-received, as if taken but not accepted. 'These soma juices have been sent forth' — these are the final verses, speaking of what has been accomplished. They recount what was accomplished during the six-day period. 'You purify yourself to win the nourishment of cows, the strength, flowing around' — these have the word pari (around), being the form of the end. For this is the final day. 'And for us the nourishment rich in cows, pour forth all (blessings) that flow around, being praised by Jamadagni' — the paryāsa (concluding verse) serves for the support of progeny and the stability of cattle. The trayastriṃśa stoma is used. Thirty-three are all the deities.
3.140: Now they say: the five days are paired (mithuna), but the sixth day is unpaired. Because 'this praise for the worthy Jātavedas' is an āgneya ājya, thereby the sixth day is made paired. 'Like a chariot, let us assemble this for our meditation' — with this it is made paired. On the earlier days, it is the hotṛ who pronounces the blessing for the sacrificer; on this day, it is the udgātṛ. He who pronounces a blessing by means of a sāman — for him, as it were, there is a diminishing. Because the verse 'O Agni, in your friendship, may we not be harmed; O Agni, in your friendship, may we not be harmed' is used, thereby they apply a remedy, an expiation. Thus there is no diminishing for him. They place the udarka at the end. The sixth day is the essence — for the sake of not letting it escape.
3.141: 'At your rising, O Sun' — this is the maitrāvaruṇa. For at this point the stomas, the metres, the seasons, and the grahas (soma cups) revolve back. And because 'prati vām' (toward you two) is said, therefore food is assigned in accordance with the seasons. The other stotras have the udarka at the end. But this one does not have the udarka at the end. The maitrāvaruṇa pair are prāṇa and apāna. If they were to place the udarka at the end, they would obstruct prāṇa and apāna. Therefore, by not placing the udarka at the end, it is for the release of prāṇa and apāna. 'Shatter all hostile ones' — this is an aindra (belonging to Indra). 'Drive away the obstructions, smite the foes' — it has a smiting quality. This is the sacrificer's own stotra, that of the brahman priest. In its own seat, they smite all evil of the sacrificer. 'Bring that desirable wealth, of which everything is in your keeping; the giver of abundance knows it. Bring that desirable wealth. / What is in the weak, O Indra, what in the firm, what has been deposited in the sides — bring that desirable wealth.' Cattle are the desirable wealth. Food too is cattle. With this the udgātṛ pronounces the blessing for the sacrificer. They place the udarka at the end.
3.142: 'For you are the priests of the sacrifice' — this is an aindrāgna. Here he chooses Indra and Agni for the priestly office; here, having entrusted the sacrifice to Indra and Agni, he is freed. For him there is no diminishing; he becomes as before. Indra and Agni carry out the priestly office from this point onward. 'This intoxicating honey has been pressed for you by the stones, O men; be aware of it, Indra and Agni' — this is the form of the śakvarī and revatī verses. 'The two war-chariot drivers, Vṛtra-slayers, undefeated; be aware of it, Indra and Agni' — this is vārtraghna (Vṛtra-slaying), for the sake of final victory. With this six-day rite the gods conquered at the beginning. Because this vārtraghna is placed at the end of the ājya, it is for the sake of victory, for the slaying of the rival. One conquers, one slays the hostile rival — he who knows thus. They place the udarka at the end. The sixth day is the essence — for the sake of not letting it escape. Just as one would smear the dam of waters, even so they place the udarka at the end for the sake of not letting the essence escape. The trayastriṃśa stoma is used. Thirty-three are all the deities.
3.143: 'For Indra, O drop, accompanied by the Maruts' — the gāyatrī verses of the midday pavamāna have the form of bṛhat. This day is bārhata. 'The wise ones adorn you, the firm support, all around' — these have the word pari (around), being the form of the end. For this is the final day. 'The flavor for you, O Mitra, O Aryaman' — for this day is the essence (rasa). The essence that flowed beyond the fifth day — that became this day. This is indeed that essence. 'May Varuṇa drink, O sage, the Maruts of the pavamāna' — these are marutvat (accompanied by Maruts). The form of the midday pressing is marutvat. They do not depart from the form of the midday pressing. In those, the gāyatra has its brāhmaṇa stated elsewhere. Now the revatī verses. Cattle are the simā verses. Cattle are the revatī verses. Cattle are this day. Those who have praised with this become rich in cattle. The śakvarī verses were burning with their splendor. They were not to be touched due to their splendor. The gods feared their burning. They said: 'These will burn up everything. Let us pacify them.' They took two (syllables) from the foot of one and placed them in another — whichever had two removed became two-footed, whichever had one (added) became one-footed. Thus this became the three-footed gāyatrī. This is indeed the same metre. They divided it in two — for abundance and for pacification.
3.144: From the fifth day the gods fashioned the sixth day. They sought a foundation for it. They found the revatī verses as its foundation. One who knows thus has a (firm) foundation. Cattle are the revatī verses. The midday pressing is the self (ātman). When they bring the revatī verses to the midday pressing, they establish cattle in the self. Cattle are the revatī verses. The midday pressing is the self. When they bring the revatī verses to the midday pressing, they establish milk in the self. Semen is the revatī verses. The midday pressing is the self. When they bring the revatī verses to the midday pressing, they establish semen in the self. Semen is the revatī verses. Semen too is the raivata sāman. The finales (nidhanas) of the raivata sāman are indeed thunderbolts.
