3.145: Milk is the revatī verses. Cattle are the fifth day. If they (the revatī verses) were separated by another sāman, the milk would be separated from the cattle. Because they are not separated, the milk is not separated from the cattle. Now they say: 'This is like a repetition (jāmi), since these ilā verses approach with ilā verses.' 'One should begin with the iṣovṛdhīya, for the avoidance of repetition,' (they say). But then the milk would be separated from the cattle. By the thunderbolt, the semen would be scattered; one should begin with the revatī verses, lest the semen be scattered by the thunderbolt. And thus the milk is not separated from the cattle. The ṛc is this world; the sāman is that world. That world is the divine territory — the pavamāna. When they divide (the revatī verses) in two, thereby they attain both worlds. Water is the revatī verses. Water too is the raivata sāman. If they were to make the raivata the pṛṣṭha in the revatī verses, they would sink in the fathomless, they would have no footing. Therefore the vāravantīya is made the pṛṣṭha — for the sake of having a footing. The pṛṣṭhas are, as it were, worn out when they reach the sixth day. In what is worn out, no semen remains.
3.146: They make the cattle be with their calves. When cattle are with their calves, then they yield desires. The revatī verses become wish-fulfilling for him. One should have them be together during the pavamāna if one desires: 'May they be wish-fulfilling for me in the other world.' They become wish-fulfilling for him in the other world. One should have them be together during the return-chants (abhyāvartins) if one desires: 'May they be wish-fulfilling for me in this world.' They become wish-fulfilling for him in this world. One should have them be together in both if one desires: 'May they be wish-fulfilling for me in both worlds.' They become wish-fulfilling for him in both worlds. Now they say: 'The calves should be on one side, the mothers on the other.' 'Waters flow northward. These cattle are waters.' But others say: 'The calves should be on this side, the mothers on that.' When a father sends his son forth, he sends him northward. Therefore the calves should be to the north, the mothers to the south. The gods and asuras contended over the cattle. The gods stood to the north with the calves; the asuras to the south with the mothers. The mothers broke through and ran to the calves. The gods held the calves. Having gathered them together, they took them from the asuras. With them they ran northward. One wrests cattle from the hostile rival — he who knows thus.
3.147: Now the vājadāvarī verses. Food is vāja, food is dāvarī. These are for the attainment of food. Prajāpati created creatures. He created them without food. They ran to Prajāpati. He gave them food with this very sāman. They said: 'He has given us vāja (nourishment, food).' That is the vājadāvarī-ness of vājadāvarī. This is the sāman for the attainment of food. One obtains food, one becomes the foremost food-eater among one's own people — he who knows thus. Now the sauparṇa — it is for the undertaking of the sacrifice. The sacrifice departed from the gods. Having become a great eagle (suparṇa), it flew about. The gods desired: 'May we seize the sacrifice.' They perceived this sāman. They praised with it. With it they seized the sacrifice. The sacrifice, as it were, departs at the sixth day. Therefore the sauparṇa is used here — for the undertaking of the sacrifice. And because they seize it (the sacrifice) having become a great eagle flying about with this sāman, therefore it is called sauparṇa. They also call it krauñca. Kruṅ the Āṅgirasa found this day, as it were, desirable (īṣya). This sixth day is as if desirable. Therefore it is performed on the sixth day. It has the form of aila — for cattle are ilā.
3.148: Now the iṣovṛdhīya, with an external nidhana, belonging to bṛhat. Therefore it is performed on the bārhata day. The sixth day is nourishment (iṣ); the seventh is growth (vṛdhi). The seventh day was born from the sixth. Because of the word iṣa (nourishment), they generate the seventh day from the sixth. Because of the word vṛdha (growth), they indeed grew at that point. Prajāpati created creatures. He created them hungry (without food), again and again hungry. Hungry, they ate one another. Prajāpati considered: 'How may these creatures of mine not go hungry?' He perceived this sāman. With it he touched them, saying 'iṣovṛdhīya.' They, touched by his nourishment, grew. That is the iṣovṛdhīya-ness of iṣovṛdhīya. His household, touched by nourishment, grows — he who knows thus. It has a four-syllable nidhana — for four-footed are the cattle.
3.149: 'Being cleansed with good hands, you utter speech in the ocean, wealth' — the ru sound is the form of the revatī verses. 'Tawny, abundant, much-desired, you flow as pavamāna / Purifying yourself in the sheep's wool filter, pavamāna, the bull has bellowed in the forest' — these are vṛṣaṇvat (having a bull), the form of bṛhat. This day is bārhata. 'O Soma, purifying, you flow with the divine expiation, anointing yourself with cows' — these are rich in cattle, possessing cattle, for the attainment of cattle. For cattle are this day. In those, the sauṣāva with repetition, the form of the simā verses. The revatī verses are the repetition (abhyāsa) of the simā verses. They also call it 'Vasiṣṭha's pippalī.' Vasiṣṭha, defeated and with his sons slain, desired: 'May I be abundant with progeny and cattle.' He perceived this sāman. He praised with it. Then he became abundant with progeny and cattle. He said: 'This was a pippalī (fruit, means of growth) by which I was born forth.' That is the pippalī-ness of pippalī. Indeed its name is pippala, and it is called pippalī. Progeny is pippala, cattle are pappala, the heavenly world is pappala. Because the pippalī is used, it is for the attainment of all this. Because it has upward ilā, it has the form of bṛhat; on the bārhata day. Thereby it is complete in form.
3.150: Now the aukṣṇorandhra. Ukṣṇo Randhra Kāvya desired: 'May I ascend to the heavenly world upstream through the waters; may I see my paths in the waters, the way down.' He perceived this sāman. He praised with it. Then he ascended to the heavenly world upstream through the waters, and saw his own paths in the waters, the way down. He was indeed Raumaṇvata. He is Uśanas Kāvya. He ascended to the heavenly world upstream along the Yamunā itself. This is a sāman leading to heaven. One ascends to the heavenly world upstream through the waters — he who knows thus. Because Ukṣṇo Randhra Kāvya perceived it, it is called aukṣṇorandhra.
3.151: Now the vājajit. Food is vāja. When a cow, a horse, or a person is well-nourished, then it becomes vigorous (vājin). The gods and asuras contended for food. The gods desired: 'May we wrest the food of the asuras.' They perceived this sāman. They praised with it. Saying 'vājijigīva' (we have conquered the prize), they wrested the food of the asuras. That is the vājajit-ness of vājajit. This is the sāman for the attainment of food. One obtains food; one wrests the food of the hostile rival — he who knows thus. It has a five-syllable nidhana. The sacrifice is pāṅkta (fivefold). Those who reach this day go beyond the measure of the sacrifice, as it were.
3.152: Now the varuṇa-sāman. Varuṇa the king was, as it were, at a feast with the other deities. He desired: 'May I be consecrated for the kingship of all the gods.' He dwelt as a brahmacārin with Prajāpati for a hundred years. Prajāpati told him this sāman: 'This is my royal form. Go. The gods will make you king.' He approached the gods. Seeing him coming, the gods descended to meet him. He told them: 'Do not descend to meet me. You are indeed my brothers. As you are, so am I.' 'No,' they said. 'We see in you the form of our father Prajāpati.' They descended to meet him. They set down for him this royal throne. On it they anointed him: the Vasus for kingship (rājya), the Rudras for paramount kingship (vairājya), the Ādityas for self-sovereignty (svārājya), the Viśvedevas for supreme sovereignty (sāmrājya), the Maruts for complete dominion (sārvavaśya), the Sādhyas and Āptyas for supreme overlordship (pārameṣṭhya). This is the sāman of the consecration of royal splendor. One attains splendor, one obtains kingship — he who knows thus.
3.153: Now the goṣṭha (cattle-pen). This is the cattle-pen of the śakvarī and revatī verses. The cattle-pen is for the support of cattle. Cattle are supported in it. One who knows thus becomes rich in cattle. Prajāpati created cattle. Having been created, they fled from him. He tried to restrain them with the first day. He did not restrain them. He did not restrain them with the second. He did not restrain them with the third. He did not restrain them with the fourth. He did not restrain them even with the fifth. On the sixth day he restrained them with this very sāman. He said: 'This sāman has become a cattle-pen (goṣṭha) for my cattle.' That is the goṣṭha-ness of goṣṭha. This is the sāman pertaining to cattle — the goṣṭha. Cattle are supported in it. One who knows thus becomes rich in cattle. It sounds, as it were, like a settling down: 'ihā ilā ihā ilā' ('here, o refreshment, here, o refreshment'). With this he kept them in check from the front. 'Him, this one, the ten fingers cleanse, the mother of rivers' — whatever has the character of a river or ocean, that is the form of this day. This day has the character of waters. Now they say those triṣṭubh verses go beyond the pavamāna ending, those who place (the triṣṭubhs) at the end of the gāyatrīs. These are sūryavat (having the word 'sun'). And also they are aindrī (relating to Indra). Therefore the triṣṭubh verses do not go beyond the pavamāna ending. In those, the vāmadevya as if present here (ihavat). Cattle are the simā verses. Cattle are the raivata. Cattle are the vāmadevya as if present here. Those who have praised with this become rich in cattle, as it were.
3.154: 'May the rich ones be at our feast' — these are revatī verses. From the same (level), they approach the same. The gāyatrī verses are the last of the midday pressing. These are gāyatrī. Just as one would cross from level ground to level ground, so is this. At this point, other metres are exhausted (yātayāman), but the gāyatrī alone is not exhausted. The stotras are exhausted, the śastras are exhausted, the vaṣaṭkāra is exhausted. The five days have the seasons (ṛtu); the sixth is without seasons. Because the hotrā priests make the ṛtuyāja offerings with separate vaṣaṭkāras, thereby the sixth day is made to have seasons. In those, the vāravantīya. This is the splendid raivata quality — the vāravantīya among the revatī verses. One becomes splendid; a splendid raivata son is born to him — he who, knowing thus, praises with the vāravantīya among the revatī verses. This is the great (bṛhat) raivata quality — the vāravantīya among the revatī verses. The sixth day is bārhata. Thereby the sixth day is made bārhata. Agni Vaiśvānara is indeed the pṛṣṭhya six-day rite. The seasons are the pṛṣṭhas. The year is the seasons. The year is Agni Vaiśvānara. When unpacified, he is liable to burn up.
3.155: Prajāpati created this sacrifice, the pṛṣṭhya six-day rite. Once created, it ran away. He tried to restrain it with the rathantara. He did not restrain it. He did not restrain it with the bṛhat. He did not restrain it with the vairūpa. He did not restrain it with the vairāja. He did not restrain it with the mahānāmnī verses. He did not restrain it even with the revatī verses. He restrained it only with the vāravantīya. Because he restrained it, that is the vāravantīya-ness of the vāravantīya. Therefore the vāravantīya is the pṛṣṭha — for the restraining of the sacrifice. Prajāpati created this sacrifice, the pṛṣṭhya six-day rite. He sought a foundation for it. He found the waters. In them he sought a foundation with the rathantara. He did not find it. In them with the bṛhat he did not find it. In them with the vairūpa he did not find it. In them with the vairāja he did not find it. In them with the mahānāmnī verses he did not find it. In them with the revatī verses he did not find it. In them he found it only with the vāravantīya. Because he found it, that too is the vāravantīya-ness of the vāravantīya. By this they establish the sacrifice on this (earth). Agni Vaiśvānara is indeed the vāravantīya. And the revatī verses are waters. Because the vāravantīya is placed among the revatī verses, therefore waters have fire as their womb. The pṛṣṭhas are, as it were, worn out when they reach the sixth day. In what is worn out, no semen remains.
3.156: And Abhipratāraṇa said this while lying down, aged. His sons were dividing his inheritance. There was a sound. 'What sound is that?' They told him: 'Your sons, sir, are dividing your inheritance.' He said: 'I have heard well about this in the brāhmaṇa of the pṛṣṭhas — that while one is still alive, his sons approach the inheritance.' 'I have heard well about this.' This is like approaching the inheritance while one is still alive — that the vāravantīya is made the pṛṣṭha while the raivata day is still present. Now the other they do in the pavamāna. Then Gaireya Āṣāḍhi asked Aṣāḍha Bhāllabeya: 'Since there are so many sāmans, how is it that they did not suffice for the sixth day?' He replied: 'Six kings like you among the Pañcālas would be worth knowing.' He said: 'You know so little; we know differently from that.' Because this pṛṣṭha without a (proper) pṛṣṭha — the vāravantīya — is made, thereby this day is (shown to be) insufficient. Then Naitanava asked Āruṇi: 'Āruṇa Āruṇi, why do the mahānāmnī verses, being without semen, generate? And why do the revatī verses, being endowed with semen, not generate?' 'Because with ra — ' he began to utter 'semen' (retas) with the first syllable. But he could not answer. By that he defeated him. Had he answered, he would have said: 'Semen is the revatī verses, and semen is the raivata sāman. There is no semen from semen. At this point the pṛṣṭhas reach the end in the form of offspring (putra). When they reach the sixth day, they do not generate further beyond. Therefore the mahānāmnī verses, being without semen, generate. Therefore the revatī verses, being endowed with semen, do not generate.' Now the vāmadevya. Cattle are the vāmadevya. Those who praise with this become rich in cattle. 'Of beautiful form, for our aid' — with the abhyārambha (seizing upon), six-footed (verses) are on the sixth day.
3.157: In those, the ṛṣabha raivata. Cattle are the simā verses, cattle are the revatī verses, cattle are the ṛṣabha. They release the bull among cattle for pairing, for procreation. One who knows thus is born through pairing. They make the front half and the major portion share. Therefore cattle eat and carry (burdens) mostly with their front half and hind half. With the front half they are milked; with the hind half they generate offspring. One obtains both — the milk of cows and the milk of oxen — he who knows thus. 'Both, O Indra, the two worlds' — six-footed (verses) are on the sixth day. Thereby the six days are arranged. The gods and asuras contended for the cattle. The asuras drove the cattle into the ocean. The gods desired: 'May we wrest the cattle of the asuras.' They perceived these brands (aṅkas), these verses: 'The divine mother has given birth, the auspicious mother has given birth.' This is the form of a brand. With those they branded and recovered those cattle. These are the verses pertaining to cattle.
3.158: In those, the śyena saṃpāraṇa, performed to prevent loss. The eagle (śyena) is fit to carry this day across. The gods, desiring heaven, performed austerities. They perceived this sāman. They praised with it. It carried them to the heavenly world, having become an eagle. Having gone to the heavenly world, they said: 'This sāman, having become an eagle, carried us to the heavenly world.' That is the śyena-ness of śyena. This is a sāman leading to heaven. One reaches the heavenly world — he who knows thus. With this sāman, Indra, having become an eagle, drove away the asuras. That too is the śyena-ness of śyena. It is a bhrātṛvya-slayer. One slays the hostile rival — he who knows thus. It has the form of aila; it is the acchāvāka-sāman. For cattle are ilā.
3.159: 'The self-sounding one, dwelling on the mountain, flows around' — the gāyatrī verses of the ārbhava pavamāna have the word pari (around), being the form of the end. For this is the final day. 'Soma has streamed on the filter; in intoxications, you are everything' — these are madvatī (containing the word mada). Rasa (essence) is indeed mada (intoxication). The third pressing is, as it were, a milked-out (thing). Because these are madvatī, they place rasa (essence) in this, they cause it to swell thereby. 'You are the inspired one, you are the poet' — the word tvam (you) occurs, which is the form of bṛhat. This day is bārhata. 'The honey born from the plant; in intoxications, you are everything / In you all the concordant ones' — these are viśvavat (all-possessing). For this day is vaiśvadeva. 'The divine ones have partaken of the drink; in intoxications, you are everything' — they place the udarka at the end. The sixth day is the essence — for the sake of not letting it escape. In those, the gāyatra has its brāhmaṇa stated elsewhere. Now three vaidanvata sāmans. Śaryāta Mānava performed a sacrifice in the eastern region. The ṛṣis officiated for him. Both gods and men drank soma together. Soma was drunk for the last time by both gods and men together. In that (sacrifice), Cyavana Bhārgava drew a graha for the Aśvins. Indra held down that cup, saying: 'What is this unknown cup that is being offered?'
3.160: The gods became angry; the ṛṣis became angry. The ninety-nine Maruts stood with spears in hand, saying: 'Now we shall overturn all this, and all this shall be destroyed.' Then the ṛṣis invoked Mada Āsura (the demon of intoxication). He, tearing through, pressed his head against the sky. There was a great battle. Agni said to Indra: 'Let your anger subside. These are better than us. If you anger them, nothing here will remain. From them indeed we were born.' Agni dissipated his anger. Indra, his anger gone, fled together with the gods. Their sacrifice became without Indra, without gods. They desired: 'May our sacrifice have Indra, may it have the gods.' Vidanvān Bhārgava perceived these sāmans. He praised with them. With them he invoked Indra. With them he dissipated his anger. Then Indra returned to their sacrifice. Then he did not depart from them. Then their sacrifice had Indra and had the gods. These sāmans are endowed with Indra and with gods. His sacrifice has Indra and has gods; Indra returns to his sacrifice; Indra does not depart from his sacrifice — he who knows thus. Cattle are indeed the vidanvat verses; these are the vidanvatī verses. In them they release the ṛṣabha vaidanvata for pairing, for procreation. One who knows thus is born through pairing.
3.161: Now the demon of intoxication (mada āsura) became afraid. He said: 'Do not summon me in vain; distribute me (into various things).' They said, thinking 'we shall drink,' 'Let us bring surā (liquor), from Varuṇa's drink.' In that (liquor), there was none of any kind of ailment — 'these are the powers of Soma.' 'Go there, O Mada.' This demon of intoxication (mada āsura) is deposited in surā (liquor).
3.162: In those, the dīrgha, one of the bṛhat group. By this, this day is made bārhata. The gods, desiring heaven, performed austerities. They perceived this sāman. They praised with it. It, having become long (dīrgha), carried them to the heavenly world. Having gone to the heavenly world, they said: 'This sāman, having become long, carried us to the heavenly world.' That is the dīrgha-ness of dīrgha. This is a sāman leading to heaven. One reaches the heavenly world — he who knows thus. 'That friend for intoxication for you' — these are madvatī (containing mada). Progressively they place the essence (rasa) and intoxication (mada) in the third pressing. 'Sing to the purifying one; like a child, may they sweeten (him) with offerings and praises' — with this they approach the form of cattle. For the young one (śiśu) belongs to the cattle. 'Like a calf with its mothers, the drop, being impelled, is anointed' — these are rich in cattle, possessing cattle, for the attainment of cattle. For cattle are this day. 'The divine hero, the intoxication, adorned with hymns, all around' — these have the word pari (around), being the form of the end.
3.163: Now the kārṇaśravasa. Indra, having struck Vṛtra with the thunderbolt, not thinking he had struck him down, became disoriented. His ears closed up. He could not hear with them. He desired: 'May I not be deaf; may I hear with my ears.' He perceived this sāman. He praised with it. Then he became not deaf and heard with his ears. He said: 'I have heard with my ears (aśrauṣaṃ karṇābhyām).' That is the kārṇaśravasa-ness of kārṇaśravasa. One is not deaf, one hears with one's ears — he who knows thus. They also call it gauloma. Golomā Āṅgirasa, desiring cattle, performed austerities. He perceived this sāman. He praised with it. He approached this ilā (nourishment). Cattle are ilā. Then he obtained cattle. This is the sāman pertaining to cattle. One obtains cattle, one becomes rich in cattle — he who knows thus. Because Golomā Āṅgirasa perceived it, it is called gauloma. 'The soma juices purify themselves, the drops, for us finding the best path, friendly, of beautiful sound, faultless' — the ru sound is the form of the revatī verses.
3.164: Now the madhuścyut-nidhana, belonging to bṛhat. Therefore it is performed on the bārhata day. And also: honey (madhu) is the end of essences (rasa), heaven is the end of worlds, this is the final day — 'with the final sāman may we reach the final heavenly world.' Now the krauñca. Kruṅ the Āṅgirasa found this day, as it were, desirable (īṣya). This sixth day is as if desirable. Therefore it is performed on the sixth day. They also call it udvat and śāmmada. With the udvat (upward-going), the gods went upward to the heavenly world. Because they went upward to the heavenly world, that is the udvat-ness of udvat. This is a sāman leading to heaven. One goes upward to the heavenly world by means of this — he who knows thus. Now the śāmmada. When the gods were victorious, these worlds, trembling, departed from them. They desired: 'May these worlds return to us; may they not depart from us.' They perceived this sāman. They praised with it. Then these worlds returned to them. Then they did not depart from them. They said: 'These worlds have delighted (amādiṣuḥ) us peacefully (śam).' That is the śāmmada-ness of śāmmada. These worlds delight him peacefully; these worlds return to him; these worlds do not depart from him — he who knows thus. It has upward ilā, being the form of bṛhat, on the bārhata day. Thereby it is complete in form. 'With this purification, purify yourself; with these riches, O drop, rush into the lake; whose speed like the wind of the golden sun, the generous one established for Takva, the man.'
3.165: In those, the śnauṣṭa. By this, Agni Vaiśvānara blazed forth. They sit for the sattra called dīdayāma ('let us blaze'). With this they cause him to blaze. Now Agni Vaiśvānara was formerly like these embers. He desired: 'May my flames be born, my splendor.' He perceived this sāman. He praised with it. Then his flames, his rays, were born. These are his flames — the rays. That was his splendor, his brahma-radiance, that was born. One becomes splendid, endowed with brahma-radiance and with flames — he who knows thus. It has, as it were, a settling quality — the form of the end. For this is the final day.
3.166: Now the yajñāyajñīya has its brāhmaṇa stated elsewhere. Now these are two-footed (dvipadā) verses, for the attainment of the un-exhausted state of the metres. At this point the days and metres are exhausted; only the two-footed (verses) are un-exhausted. Because they are used here, thereby this day is made un-exhausted. Keśin Dārbhya used to sing this: 'Always on the mountain there is something good; one lives even on a leaf. When one does not find a dwelling, then one runs to hope.' 'Whatever kind of brāhmaṇas there may be, of that kind you are making this — applying two-footed (verses) when the metres are exhausted.' Cattle are the pṛṣṭhya six-day rite; the person is the two-footed one among metres. When these are used here, they place the person upon the cattle, as it were. Therefore the person stands above the cattle, as their overlord. And also, this pṛṣṭhya six-day rite is the heavenly world; the sacrificer is connected to it by means of the two-footed verses.
3.167: When the metres were raising up yonder sun (āditya), they could not raise him up. They said to the two-footed metres: 'Standing upon you, let us raise him up.' 'Then be food for me,' (the two-footed said). 'We will be food for you,' they said. For the brāhmaṇa is the two-footed metre; these (metres) are his food, both here and in the other world. Standing upon the two-footed, these metres raise up yonder sun and these ritual acts. If they should ask, 'Why are two-footed (verses) performed on the sixth day?' — one should answer: 'For the sake of having a firm footing.' Therefore these should not be omitted here.
3.168: The Gaupāyanas cursed Asamāti Rāthaprauṣṭha. They were sitting for a sattra in Khāṇḍava. Now at the home of Asamāti Rāthaprauṣṭha, two Kirāta women (kirātākulī) dwelt, possessing asura-magic. They used to cook rice without fire and meat without fire. When they sowed from the front, (the crop) appeared behind — so skilled in magic were they. Now the Ikṣvākus, having always eaten the asura-food, had perished. The oblations of the Gaupāyanas burned Asamāti Rāthaprauṣṭha. He said: 'These two Kirāta women — the oblations of the Gaupāyanas are burning me.' They said: 'We are your physicians, we are the expiation. We shall act so that these (oblations) do not burn you.' They went and, while Subandhu the Gaupāyana was sleeping, heedless, they took out his breath (asu) and placed it within the enclosure (of the fire). The fires at that time had enclosures. While Subandhu had not yet awakened, they realized: 'The two asura-magic women have taken his breath.' They said: 'Come, let us follow after Subandhu's breath.' They set out from Khāṇḍava: 'May we not go astray from the path; may we not stray from the sacrifice, O Indra Somin; may not adversaries stand in our way.'
3.169: Having searched afar, they recognized his fire — 'Varūthya is the name of his fire.' Now before this, the fire had announced its name: 'Varūthya is my name. Whoever satisfies you — whatever he tells you, do that.' They approached him (with the verse): 'O Agni, you are nearest to us, and a protector; be gracious, O Varūthya.' He said to them: 'With what desire have you come?' 'We wish to win back Subandhu's breath,' they said. 'It is within the enclosure,' he said. 'Take it.' They called it out: 'This is the mother, this is the father, this is the life-force that has come; this is your pathway — O Subandhu, come, come out.' The breath entered Subandhu again. He became as before. They reported this to the two Kirāta women: 'The ṛṣis have called out the breath.' The two, unable to maintain themselves, fled away. Their asura-magic departed from them as they fled.
3.170: Now before this, the two (Kirāta women) had been beautiful in appearance through their magic. One of them, as the fire was blazing, said: 'Thus indeed does one act who seeks to destroy truth with untruth.' Then they cut the cattle. The other one, approaching, being cut at the throat, said: 'Thus indeed does one act who seeks to destroy truth with untruth.' The two died right there. These are the verses that slay rivals and slay demons (rakṣas). One slays the hostile rival; evil and demons are smitten — for one who has praised with these verses. Now they say: 'If the Vālakhilya verses are separated, how does one praise when they are separated?'
3.171: In those, the gūrda. The gods and asuras contended for food. The gods wrested the food of the asuras. But among the asuras, a great food called gūrda remained. The gods desired: 'May we wrest this gūrda food of the asuras.' They perceived this sāman. They praised with it. With it they wrested the gūrda food of the asuras. They swallowed it (agūrdan). Because they swallowed it, that is the gūrda-ness of gūrda. This is the sāman for the attainment of food. One obtains food; one wrests the food of the hostile rival — he who knows thus. Prajāpati created food. All the gods were possessive of it — 'mine, mine.' Agni desired: 'May I win this food.' He perceived this sāman. With it he encompassed it (the food). He won it. Therefore all food is in Agni. Therefore to whatever deity they offer, they offer into Agni. He swallowed it (agūrdat). Because he swallowed it, that too is the gūrda-ness of gūrda. This is the sāman for the attainment of food. One obtains food, one becomes the foremost food-eater among one's own people — he who knows thus. 'May we now establish these worlds, Indra and all the gods together' — these are vaiśvadeva. The vṛṣakapi is vaiśvadeva. This day is vaiśvadeva. 'May Indra together with the Ādityas establish our sacrifice, our body, and our progeny' — this is for the sake of success.
3.172: In those, Gotama's bhadra. Gotama, desiring splendor (śrī), performed austerities. He perceived this sāman. He praised with it. Then he attained splendor. For splendor (śrī) is bhadra (auspicious). Progeny is bhadra. The progeny that is progeny — that is the greater good. One who knows thus sees progeny from progeny. Because Gotama perceived it, it is called Gotama's bhadra. 'Forth to Indra, the greatest Vṛtra-slayer' — these are vārtraghna (Vṛtra-slaying), for the sake of final victory. The gods, having conquered with the six-day rite, said: 'What shall be at the end for us, so that the asuras may not reconquer what we have conquered?' They drew out the essences of the pṛṣṭhas. They chanted over them. That rose upward like a bamboo pole. It became the udvaṃśīya (ascending the bamboo). That is the udvaṃśīya-ness of the udvaṃśīya. It exceeded the sacrifice. Therefore they drew out this 'son of the bamboo.' From the essence, this essence — they made the udvaṃśīya of the chandomāna (metre-rite) at the end of the pṛṣṭhya six-day rite. These two thunderbolts stand raised at the end, for the protection of cattle and to prevent the carrying away of cattle. One's wealth is not taken away — he who knows thus. This is the form, essence, and splendor of all the pṛṣṭhas gathered together. In this form, essence, and splendor of all the pṛṣṭhas — in the unconquered metre — they stand at the end of the sacrifice. They approach the pratihāra before the nidhana. For the sacrificer is upheld by the prastāva and pratihāra. At this point they are, as it were, beyond. Therefore, by approaching the pratihāra before the nidhana, they do not overstep. It has a svāra. It sounds forth to the seventh day. That svāra serves this purpose. By the svāra they commence on the following day.
3.173: Prajāpati created this sacrifice, the dvādaśāha (twelve-day rite). He obtained everything with the pṛṣṭhya six-day rite alone. He considered: 'How shall I fashion these four (remaining) days?' He fashioned the seventh day from the gāyatrī alone, with the caturviṃśa (stoma); the eighth with the forty-four (catuścatvāriṃśa), from the triṣṭubh; the ninth with the forty-eight (aṣṭācatvāriṃśa), from the jagatī; and the tenth day he fashioned from the anuṣṭubh alone. From all the days and metres he fashioned it — that is the chandomāna-ness of the chandomāna (metre-days). Now they say: the stomas are completed, the metres are completed, the deities are completed, the seasons and grahas are completed — when this pṛṣṭhya six-day rite is completed. Because the bahiṣpavamāna of the seventh day has the caturviṃśa (stoma) — for the year consists of twenty-four half-months, and the year is the sacrifice — they take hold of the sacrifice at the beginning of the sacrifice. And also, the gāyatrī has twenty-four syllables. The gāyatrī verses re-extend the completed sacrificial rites.
3.226: Now the svāśira arka — arka is food — for the obtaining of food. The svāśiras are indeed apsarases by name; they are these plants. They desired: 'May we be many, may we procreate, may we attain this world.' They saw this sāman. They praised with it. Thereupon they became many, they procreated, they attained this world. This entire world is covered with plants, even the mountains. This is the sāman of procreation. One who knows thus becomes abundant in offspring and cattle, procreates, and attains this world. Because the svāśiras apsarases saw it, therefore it is called the svāśira arka. 'Abhi dyumnaṃ bṛhad yaśaḥ' — it has 'abhi,' which is the form of the rathantara. This day is rāthantara. 'Jinvan gaviṣṭaye dhiyaḥ' — they become rich in cattle and rich in animals, for the obtaining of cattle.
3.227: Among those, the aiṣira belongs to the lineage of Vāyu. Vāyu is indeed iṣira (vigorous). Whatever is lost for the sattra-performers in the sattra, Vāyu restores that to them. Vāyu is indeed prāṇa. Prāṇa heals that for them. They make a remedy, an expiation, with this. 'Prāṇā śiśur mahīnām' — with this they approach the form of cattle, for the young one (śiśu) belongs to cattle. Among those, the traita has its brāhmaṇa already stated. 'Abhī no vājasātamam' — it has 'abhi,' which is the form of the rathantara. This day is rāthantara. 'Rayim arṣa śataspṛham / indo mahasrabharṇasaṃ tuvidyumnaṃ vibhāsaham' — whatever contains a hundred, whatever contains a thousand, that is the form of the chandomās (metre-animals). Cattle are indeed chandomās. 'Pari sya svāno akṣarad indur avye madacyutaḥ' — they become intoxicating (madvatī). They thus place the essence and intoxication in the third pressing in order. 'Vayaṃ te asya rādhaso vasor vaso puruspṛhaḥ / ni nediṣṭhatamā iṣa syāma sumne te adhrigo' — they become rich in cattle and rich in animals, for the obtaining of cattle. For cattle are indeed chandomās.
3.228: Now the śuddhāśuddhīya. Indra, having slain the asuras, considered himself as if unpurified, unholy. He desired: 'Let them praise me, the pure one, with a pure sāman, so that I be entirely pure.' He said to the ṛṣis: 'Praise me.' The ṛṣis saw this sāman. They praised him with it: 'Eto nv indraṃ stavāma śuddhaṃ śuddhena sāmnā / śuddhair ukthair vāvṛdhvāṃsaṃ śuddhair āśīrvān mamattu' — thus. Thereupon Indra became pure, purified, and holy. This is the pavitra (purifying) sāman. One who knows thus becomes pure, purified, and holy. Now the audala. Udala Daivarāta desired: 'May I be foremost, chief, possessed of brahman-splendour.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereupon he became foremost, chief, possessed of brahman-splendour. 'May we be foremost, chief, possessed of brahman-splendour' — with this they sit for the sattra. They indeed become foremost, chief, possessed of brahman-splendour. It is made as svāra in the anuṣṭubh metre, for completeness of form. Being gāyatra in origin, it is made in the anuṣṭubh. For cattle put forth their powers in pairs. Cattle are the chandomās. Therefore it is made in the chandomās.
3.229: Now the pāñcavāja. With this, Pañcavāja Kautsa drank both beverages — both the divine and the human. He drank them both. Pañcavāja Kautsa desired: 'May I drink both beverages — both the divine and the human.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereupon he drank both beverages — both the divine and the human. Therefore, having placed the surā vessel, he would use it for the soma purpose. This is the sāman for the obtaining of food. One who knows thus drinks both beverages — both the divine and the human. Because Pañcavāja Kautsa saw it, therefore it is called the pāñcavāja. Now the krauñca. Kruṅ Āṅgirasa found a day that was as if envied (īṣya). These chandomās are as if envied. Therefore it is made in the chandomās. It has svara in the middle. Svara is indeed prāṇa. These chandomās are as if in need of healing. The days are joined in pairs of two.
3.230: Now the rayiṣṭha has its nidhana in the middle, for establishment. Those who approach the ārbhava pavamāna float about as if without a hold in the ocean. When the nidhana is in the middle, it is indeed for establishment. Prajāpati created cattle. They, having been created, departed from him. He desired: 'May the cattle not depart from me; may they turn back to me.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereupon the cattle turned back to him. Thereupon they became non-departing from him. He said: 'Wealth (rayi) has indeed stood firm in me.' That is the rayiṣṭha-ness of the rayiṣṭha. This is the sāman pertaining to cattle. Wealth stands firm in him, cattle turn back to him, cattle do not depart from him — one who knows thus. It contains the il̥ā in a ratio of one and a half — cattle are indeed il̥ā; cattle are chandomās — for the obtaining of cattle in order. Now these become two-footed (dvipadā), their brāhmaṇa already stated. These are: 'Pavasva soma mahān samudraḥ' — for great is this day, the forty-fourth. 'Pitā devānāṃ viśvābhi dhāma' — it has 'abhā,' which is the form of the rathantara.
3.231: Among those is the dharma. By dharma the gods, having taken up dharma, having made the asuras lawless (vidharman) by their lawlessness, overthrew them. By dharma, having taken up dharma, having made the hostile rival lawless, one overthrows him — one who knows thus. For prosperity (śrī) is indeed dharma, sovereignty is indeed dharma; by dharma the king is dharma, dharma belongs to one's own people, one's own people go to him, dharma sustains him. They say it should be done on the ninth day. 'That yonder is the kṣatra which is bārhata; this here is the viś which is rāthantara.' But they also say: 'Kings are of different dharma (vidharman); they are not of the same dharma as other men. This here is the kṣatra, the forty-fourth which is traiṣṭubha. Therefore the vidharma should be on the ninth day itself, here.' 'Hinvanti sūram usraya svasāro jāmayas patim / mahām induṃ mahīyuvaḥ' — for great is this day, the forty-fourth. Among those, the viśoviśīya has its brāhmaṇa already stated. It contains il̥ā — cattle are indeed il̥ā.
3.232: Now the yajñāyajñīya has its brāhmaṇa already stated. Now the auśana. Uśanā Kāvya sought the immortal gandharva-world among the gods. He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereupon he attained the immortal gandharva-world among the gods — this hairy one (romaṇvant). These are the Kāvyas. One who has praised with this attains the immortal gandharva-world among the gods in the hairy realm. Agni indeed dwelt among the gods. The gods did not please him. Being unpleased, he went to Uśanā Kāvya. He said to him: 'O ṛṣi, please me, for I am unpleased.' He desired to please him. He saw this sāman. He pleased him with it: 'Preṣṭhaṃ vo atithiṃ stuṣe mitram iva priyam / agne rathaṃ na vedyam' — thus he made him the most beloved guest. Indeed, at this time he who has carried the sacrifice is as if unpleased. They please him here with this. He, being pleased, carries whatever remains of the sacrifice. Because Uśanā Kāvya saw it, therefore it is called the auśana. 'Endra no gadhi priya satrājid agohya / girir na viśvataḥ pṛthuḥ patir divaḥ // abhi hi satya somapā' — it has 'abhi,' which is the form of the rathantara. This day is rāthantara. 'Ubhe babhūtha rodasī' — these two are indeed the two worlds (heaven and earth). With this one addresses them.
3.233: Among those is the sāṃvarta, a padanidhana rāthantara. Therefore it is made on a rāthantara day. Saṃvarta Āṅgirasa, desiring heaven, performed austerity. He saw this sāman. He praised with it. By saying 'patir divaḥ, patir divaḥ' (lord of heaven), he ascended to the heavenly world. Just as one might fly — such is it when one says 'patir divaḥ.' This is the sāman that leads to heaven. One who knows thus attains the heavenly world. Because Saṃvarta Āṅgirasa saw it, therefore it is called the sāṃvarta. The gods and asuras were in contest. The asuras had spread out greatly. Indra desired: 'Let me crush the asuras together and slay them.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it: 'Tvaṃ hi śaśvatīnām indra dhartā purām asi / hantā dasyor manor vṛdhaḥ patir divaḥ' — thus he crushed them together and slew them. That is indeed the sāṃvarta-ness of the sāṃvarta.
3.234: Now the maidhātitha. The Vibhindukīyas, whose householder was Medhātithi, sat for a sattra. Dṛḍhacyut Āgasti was their udgātṛ, Gaurīviti the prastotṛ, Acyutacyut the pratihartṛ, Vasukṣaya the hotṛ, Sanaka and Navaka the two Kāvya adhvaryus. Medhātithi desired cattle; Sanaka and Navaka desired wives; the others desired whatever they each desired. In former times they would sit for a sattra with different goals. They would arise having indeed attained their various desires. For them, Indra, having taken the form of Medhātithi's ram, would partake of the soma. They would drive him away saying: 'Medhātithi's ram is partaking of the soma!' And he, having assumed his own form, would partake of their soma. Therefore, from that time onwards, people call a ram 'Medhātithi's ram.' This Medhātithi, desiring cattle, saw this sāman. He praised with it: 'Purāṃ bhindur yuvā kaviḥ' — he thus split open the Vala cave.
3.235: From that, cattle were released. Those that were released first — they are these present-day cattle. And golden-horned, double-uddered cows followed along. And two apsarases wearing golden ornaments followed along. The householder reflected upon them. He said: 'These are mine, for I kindled them in my gārhapatya fire. All prosperity belongs to the householder.' 'No,' said Sanaka and Navaka. 'You desired cattle. These cattle are yours. We desired wives. These two are our wives.' And while they were disputing, one of them (Sanaka or Navaka) approached one of the apsarases. She struck back. She is the one who has what looks like a lump (kabalika). Then the other apsaras smiled. She is the hariṇikā (doe). Therefore, the ram pursues cattle — 'bring the cattle-folk.' Now those golden-horned, double-uddered cows ran back to where they had come from, saying: 'The householder has committed untruth. We do not belong to one who commits untruth.' These are even today known in Vibhinduka as fierce-bulled Śārī cows. Two Kāṇvāyanas competed in sacred knowledge — Medhātithi and Triśoka. They said: 'Come, let us walk past a kindled fire.' They walked past a kindled fire. Triśoka walked over it easily, but the other's eyelashes were singed. He said to him: 'I have defeated you.' 'No,' he said. 'You are a son of an asurī; the deity has merely touched you and does not consider it significant.' They said: 'Come, let us walk through the water.' They walked through a waterlogged chariot-rut. Triśoka walked over it easily, but the other's wheel-rim stuck there. That is why 'wet-rimmed' for the chariot's spoke-hole. He said to him: 'I have defeated you.' 'No,' he said. 'You are a son of an asurī; the deity has merely touched you and does not consider it significant.'
3.236: Triśoka immediately released hairless, ear-marked cattle. Those, scorched there, collapsed and died. That one perished. Therefore they say 'cattle perish.' Therefore one whose life-span is incomplete should not eat meat. Then some, being scorched, ran westward. They entered the Sindhu river. That became salt. Therefore they say 'cattle are salt.' Therefore one who trades with salt — those become cattle (i.e., the trade is equivalent to cattle). Then Medhātithi spread the prastara (grass-seat) for a year, desiring cattle. From this very Vala cave, sealed with a stone, he released cattle — 'tvaṃ valasya gomataḥ.' This is the sāman of cattle-release. One who knows thus obtains cattle and becomes rich in cattle.
3.237: They also call it the sāman of the Āṅgirasas. The Āṅgirasas were indeed sons of ṛṣis by name. They desired: 'May we attain the heavenly world.' They saw this sāman. They praised with it. Thereupon they attained the heavenly world. This is the sāman that leads to heaven. One who knows thus attains the heavenly world. Because the Āṅgirasas saw it, therefore it is called the sāman of the Āṅgirasas. They also call it the māruta. The Maruts desired: 'May we be the strongest, the most powerful, the most numerous, the most vigorous among the gods; may we win the heavenly world.' They saw this sāman. They praised with it. Thereupon they became the strongest, the most powerful, the most numerous, the most vigorous among the gods; they won the heavenly world. One who knows thus becomes the strongest, the most powerful, the most numerous, the most vigorous among his own people, and wins the heavenly world.
3.238: They also call it the vaiśvāmitra. The Bharatas were on the far side of the Sindhu, driven out by the Ikṣvākus. Viśvāmitra and Jamadagni were dwelling among them. Indra, giving a boon, lent his two horses (harī), which they requested. But he did not give them. When they were not given, they cried out: 'Indrakrośa!' (Cry to Indra). He, standing among the common folk, said: 'Viśvāmitra and Jamadagni — seize these cattle of the Ikṣvākus!' Those two, being on the far bank, heard this. They said to the Bharatas: 'Indra calls us — seize these cattle of the Ikṣvākus! Come, let us seize the cattle!' 'Make a ford for us across this.' 'You yoke up then.' They yoked up. They descended to the water. The two said: 'Cast off your garments of rags.' They cast them off. But a certain rājanya-kinsman, who had rags, tied them below the axle. The two desired: 'May there be a ford for us.' Viśvāmitra saw this sāman. He praised with it. 'Purāṃ bhindur yuvā kavir amitaujā ajāyata / indro viśvasya karmaṇo dhartā vajrī puruṣṭutaḥ // tvaṃ valasya gomato 'pāvar adrivo bilam / tvāṃ devā abibhyuṣas tujyamānāsa āviṣuḥ // tavāhaṃ śūra rātibhiḥ praty āyaṃ sindhum āvadan' — thus speaking, the two returned. 'Upātiṣṭhanta girvaṇo viduṣ ṭe tasya kāravaḥ' — the waters stood still for them. There was a ford for them. They crossed by the ford. But the one who had the rags — the waters cut him off. 'This one has perished' — they are the Naṃśavas. 'The yoking was not driven' — they are the Āyogavas.
3.239: Young girls playing then abandoned them. That very place became the Yauṣita mountain. And these forests and these rivers were formerly on the far side of the Sindhu. Those two, going around from behind, dammed them eastward. Those that were bull-herds became these salt-lakes. Those that stood still became these small trees. And these are the Pīrustaccha things. And the rivers that they made crossable — these are the rivers that the Vivasatihottamas made crossable. While they were being made crossable, the Sindhu was displeased. He said: 'Have we not heard that your father Gāthin was a brāhmaṇa? Whence came to you, O Viśvāmitra, a thousandfold strength?' He replied: 'And the son of Mamatā, Dīrghatamas, speaking while still in the womb — the wise one was born arranging the limbs of the sacrifice. How then, O Dhanvan, do you haggle?' Thereupon they found those cattle. This is the sāman pertaining to cattle. One who knows thus obtains cattle and becomes rich in cattle. Because Viśvāmitra saw it, therefore it is called the vaiśvāmitra. It becomes the il̥ā acchāvāka-sāman. Cattle are indeed il̥ā.
3.240: 'Akrān samudraḥ parame vidharman' — the horse is the highest among cattle, the ocean is the highest, the ninth day is the highest; therefore these are done on the ninth day. 'Janayan prajā bhuvanasya gopāḥ' — they become birth-bearing (janadvatī). Birth-bearing is the form of the jagatī metre. This day is jāgata. 'Vṛṣā pavitre adhi sāno avye bṛhat somo vāvṛdhe svāno adriḥ' — they become possessing bulls (vṛṣaṇvatī) and possessing greatness (bṛhadvatī), which is the form of the bṛhat. This day is bārhata. 'Mahat tat somo mahiṣaś cakāra' — for great is this day, the forty-eighth. 'Apāṃ yad garbho 'vṛṇīta devān / adadhād indre pavamāna ojo 'janayat sūrye jyotir induḥ' — they become birth-bearing. From the morning pressing they generate this ninth day. 'Matsi vāyum iṣṭaye rādhase ca matsi mitrāvaruṇā pūyamānaḥ / matsi śardho mārutaṃ matsi devān matsi dyāvāpṛthivī deva soma' — it becomes all-divine (sarvadevatya).
3.241: These are triṣṭubh verses with jagatī colouring, complete in the form of the day. Where they complete something in the form of the day, there it succeeds. It succeeds for him who knows thus. 'Eṣa devo amatyaḥ' — the sūkta becomes the anurūpa — cattle are indeed the sūkta; cattle are the chandomās — for the obtaining of cattle. Moreover, abundance is cattle; abundance is the sūkta. They make cattle into anurūpas of cattle. Here they do not make visible anurūpas. Therefore cattle generate offspring of various forms — a white cow gives birth to a black calf, a black cow to a white one, a red cow to a spotted one, a spotted cow to a red one. Among these, the beginnings are the same but the endings differ: 'eṣa devo amartyaḥ, eṣa devo vipā kṛtaḥ, eṣa devo vipanyubhuḥ.' This is the first foot, this is the second, that is the third. They say: 'Those who perform the dvādaśāha depart from this world, for they reach an end, as it were, since these have the same beginnings but different endings.' They thereby establish themselves in this very world. On the sixth day, those with different beginnings but the same ending occur.
3.242: It becomes a ten-versed hymn — the virāj has ten syllables; food is the virāj — for the obtaining of food through the virāj. It becomes eight-versed. With this it addresses the forty-eighth stoma. Moreover, the gāyatrī has eight syllables; cattle have eight hooves (split hooves). Cattle are the chandomās — for the obtaining of cattle. Indra slew Vṛtra. When he had slain Vṛtra, all beings from these worlds called after him: 'This is the hero, this is the Vṛtra-slayer!' They thus placed heroic power in him in succession. One who reaches this day is as if he has performed a heroic deed, as if he has slain Vṛtra. When they say 'eṣaḥ' (this one), they place heroic power in him in succession. They say: 'The pṛṣṭhya ṣaḍaha is seasonally ordered (ṛtumant); the chandomās are not seasonally ordered. Those chandomās are as if established without seasonal order.' When these become five-versed and six-versed, thereby the chandomās are made seasonally ordered. The last one of them becomes six-versed. When something is visible, they say 'eṣaḥ' (this); when it is invisible, they say 'saḥ' (that). Those who reach this day go as if invisibly. Therefore the last one becomes six-versed. That by which they go forth, by that they ascend. The stoma is the forty-eighth. The jagatī has forty-eight syllables. Cattle are the jagatī. Cattle are the chandomās.
3.243: 'Aganma mahā namasā yaviṣṭham' — the ājya verses become 'going' (gadvatī) on the day of rising (utthānīya), for those who reach this day are as if going. 'Yo dīdāya samiddha sve duroṇe' — with this they illuminate him. 'Citrabhānuṃ rodasī antar ūrvī' — these two are indeed heaven and earth; this intermediate space is the Maruts. 'Svāhutaṃ viśvataḥ pratyañcam' — for he (the three-day period) extends backwards. 'Sa mahnā viśvā duritāni sāhvān' — they become universal (viśvavatī). For this day is vaiśvadeva. 'Agniṣṭave dama ā jātavedāḥ / sa no rakṣiṣad duritād avadyād asmān gṛṇata uta no maghonaḥ' — with this one prays for a blessing. 'Tvaṃ varuṇa uta mitro agna' — it becomes āgnāvāruṇī, for the remedy of what has been wrongly performed. For whatever in the sacrifice has been wrongly praised, wrongly recited, or gone awry — this is for its remedy. 'Tvāṃ vardhanti matibhir vasiṣṭhāḥ' — for this day is an advanced (vṛddha) one.
3.244: 'Pra mitrayor varuṇayoḥ' — it becomes maitrāvaruṇa. 'Stomo na etu śūṣyaḥ / namasvān tuvijātayoḥ' — it is birth-bearing (janadvatī). With this they generate the ninth day. 'Yā dhārayanta devāḥ' — for firmness (dhṛti). For at this point they are reaching an end, as it were: 'Let us not fall beyond the chandomās.' 'Sudakṣā dakṣapitarā / asuryāya pramahasā // tā naḥ stipā tanūpā varuṇā jaritr̥̄ṇām / mitra sādhayataṃ dhiyaḥ' — for success indeed. 'Mahaṃ indro ya ojase' — it becomes aindra. For great is this day, the forty-eighth. 'Parjanyo vṛṣṭimān iva / stomair vatsasya vāvṛdhe' — for this is an advanced day. 'Kaṇvā indraṃ yad akrata stomair yajñasya sādhanam' — for success indeed. 'Prajām ṛtasya piprata' — it is birth-bearing. From the ninth day they generate the tenth day. For the tenth day is born from the ninth. That which on the second day is aindrāgna — that aindrāgna is bārhata. For this day is bārhata. The ājya verses become harmonized (samayīn). For the pṛṣṭhya ṣaḍaha and the chandomās are as if from different villages. Just as those from different villages, joining alliances, would sit in safety — such is it when the ājya verses become harmonized. Some make them non-harmonized. Therefore harmonized ones alone should be made. The stoma is the forty-eighth. The jagatī has forty-eight syllables. Cattle are the jagatī. Cattle are the chandomās.
3.245: 'Pavamānasya jighnataḥ' — this is the form of the bṛhat. 'Hareś candrāḥ' — this is the jagatī form. 'Pavamāno rathītamaś śubhremiś śubhraśastamaḥ / hariścandro marudgaṇaḥ' — they become possessing the Maruts (marutvatī). The form of the midday pressing is accompanied by Maruts. They do not depart from the form of the midday pressing. 'Pavamāna vy aśnuhi' — they approach the vaiśvadeva form. For this day is vaiśvadeva. They become slaying (ghadvatī). The ninth day is the end. Having reached this end, they slay all evil with these. Among those, the gāyatra has its brāhmaṇa already stated. Now the gātuvinnidhana. 'May we find a path (gātu)' — with this they sit for the sattra. They find a path indeed. It becomes upariṣṭobhava (having the stobha above), with external nidhana, and is bārhata. Therefore it is performed on a bārhata day. They also call it the ādārasṛt of Bharadvāja. The kṣatra of Pratardana — the ten kings surrounded the Dāśarājña in human battle. Bharadvāja was his purohita. He ran to him: 'O ṛṣi, we run to you. Recognize us here.' Bharadvāja desired: 'May we win the battle.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. He said, after praising: 'Indra will indeed come to our invocation.'
3.246: Now there was a beautiful woman named Upamā Sāvedasī, the wife of King Pratardana. They had slain her brother. That was a mourning. Indra noticed her as she was running about lamenting. He put on a dry hide and descended. On one side of his carrying-pole was an offering cake (apūpa), on the other side was clotted milk (āmikṣā) mixed with ghee. He undertook to dance near her. He would smear her with ghee as he went. When she would push him away, he would dance right near her again. Evening came; they dispersed. Her husband asked her: 'Did you see anything today during the day?' She said: 'Nothing else, but an old man danced near me today. On one side of his carrying-pole was an offering cake, on the other clung clotted milk mixed with ghee. When I pushed him away, the swaying one danced right near me again.' He said: 'Indra has indeed come to our invocation!'
3.247: That was Indra. Do not harm him. Make him your ally and say: 'Let us win the battle.' The next day they went out. He, with just such a carrying-pole, undertook to dance even farther away. When she tried to approach him, the other would go even farther. She decided: 'Let me address him.' She addressed him: 'What is it, little one, with the carrying-pole?' (said Upamā Sāvedasī). 'Come, you are well-fitted, come down!' (said Indra). 'May your carrying-pole, little one, be broken — the great one of the pleasure-race!' (said Upamā Sāvedasī). 'Even if broken, child, the father will reassemble it!' (said Indra). 'May poison be on your carrying-pole, creeping as in a precipice!' (said Upamā Sāvedasī). 'Even the poison, dear child — with that the father slays the deer!' (said Indra). 'Run away, little one, going downstream on the path!' (said Upamā Sāvedasī). 'Even running far, child, the father comes upstream!' (said Indra). 'My dear mahira is a delight to see — let him touch me with his hand, father!' (said Upamā Sāvedasī). 'Himi! You make a pevasma! Throw the hand! What happened? How did it happen? How is the dear mahira? Let him touch with hand, father!' (said Indra). 'Thus it happened, thence it happened, just as your dear mahira — let him touch with hand, father!' (said Indra).
3.248: He, having run up to her, said: 'Let us win the battle!' He, wrapping on a hide, said: 'Just as these women fly off in all directions, so may the enemies fly off from human kṣatra!' — and shook off the hairs of the deer-hide. The horse-chariots then rose up. With those he won that battle. Bharadvāja said: 'We did not run to a wife (dāra)!' Because Bharadvāja said 'We did not run to a wife,' that is the ādārasṛt-ness of the ādārasṛt. This is the sāman of victory, the slayer of the rival. One who knows thus is victorious, slays the hostile rival, drives the hostile rival far away, and does not himself have to flee far. Moreover, it is associated with Indra. Indra came to their invocation through this; Indra comes to the invocation of one who knows thus. It has an eight-syllable nidhana — the gāyatrī has eight syllables; cattle have eight hooves; cattle are the chandomās — for the obtaining of cattle.
3.249: Now the saindhukṣita. Sindhukṣit saw the end of speech. They seek speech through the chandomās. Speech is, as it were, attained in the chandomās. They find it with this. 'With the final sāman may we attain the final heavenly world' — thus. It contains il̥ā. Cattle are il̥ā. Cattle are chandomās — for the obtaining of cattle. Now the hariśrīnidhana. In the beginning, śrī (splendour) belonged to Prajāpati. Indra desired: 'May the śrī that is in Prajāpati be in me.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. With it, saying 'hariśrīḥ,' he took away the śrī of Prajāpati. That is the hariśrīnidhana-ness of the hariśrīnidhana. This is the sāman of śrī and power. One who knows thus attains śrī. The gods and asuras were in contest. Śrī belonged to both sides. Indra desired: 'May I take away the śrī of the asuras.' He saw this sāman. With it, saying 'hariśrīḥ,' he took away the śrī of the asuras. That is indeed the hariśrīnidhana-ness of the hariśrīnidhana. This is indeed the sāman of śrī. One who knows thus attains śrī and takes away the śrī of the hostile rival.
3.250: Now the niveṣṭa of the Aṅgirasas. The Aṅgirasas desired: 'May we obtain cattle.' They saw this sāman. They praised with it. Thereupon cattle came and settled around them (abhinyaveṣṭa) from all directions. They said: 'Our il̥ās have flowed together.' Cattle are il̥ā. Therefore the flowing together of il̥ā. Now the bābhrava, with gvāmir-nidhana, external nidhana, and bārhata. Therefore it is performed on a bārhata day. Babhru Kumbhya desired: 'May I be born with cattle.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. He approached the nidhana saying 'gvābhiḥ' (with cows). Thereupon he was born with cattle. This is the sāman pertaining to cattle. One who knows thus obtains cattle and becomes rich in cattle. Because Babhru Kumbhya saw it, therefore it is called the bābhrava. Now the ṛṣabha pavamāna. Cattle are indeed simās (boundaries), cattle are revatīs (riches), cattle are the bull (ṛṣabha). They release the bull among the cattle for pairing, for procreation. One who knows thus procreates through pairing.
3.251: Now the gauṣūkta, with external nidhana, bārhata. Therefore it is performed on a bārhata day. There were Gauṣūkti and Aśvasūkti. Having received much, they felt as though they had swallowed poison. They desired: 'May we destroy this poison we have swallowed.' They saw these two sāmans. They praised with them. With one, saying 'agnir āhutaḥ' (fire oblated), he offered into this world. For nothing in this world surpasses fire. With the other, saying 'śukra āhutaḥ' (the bright one oblated), he offered into the other world. For nothing in the other world surpasses the sun. Thereupon they destroyed the poison they had swallowed. After that, they had ease as a two-toothed infant might have ease. If one thinks he has swallowed poison — having accepted what should not be accepted, having eaten food that should not be eaten — he should praise with these two. He destroys the poison he has swallowed. Whatever evil he has done, that he destroys. These two have a five-syllable nidhana — the paṅkti has five feet; cattle are pāṅkta; cattle are chandomās — for the obtaining of cattle.
3.252: 'Parīto ṣiñcatā sutam' — they become encircling (parivatī), which is the form of the end. For this is the final day. 'Somo ya uttamaṃ haviḥ' — for this offering is made as the final one, which is the chandomās. 'Dadhanvāṃ yo naryo apsv antar ā suṣāva somam adribhiḥ // nūnaṃ punāno 'vibhiḥ pari sravādabdhas surabhintaraḥ / sute cit tvāpsu madāmo andhasā' — they become possessing andhas (food-bearing), for andhas is food — for the obtaining of food. 'Śrīṇanto gobhir uttaram' — they become rich in cattle and rich in animals, for the obtaining of cattle. For cattle are chandomās. 'Pari svānaś cakṣase devamādanaḥ kratur indur vicakṣaṇaḥ' — it becomes four-versed, for cattle are four-footed. Cattle are chandomās — for the obtaining of cattle. Among those is the pṛṣṭha (ridge). By the pṛṣṭha the gods became the pṛṣṭha (summit). 'May we become the pṛṣṭha' — with this they sit for the sattra. They indeed become the pṛṣṭha. The gods desired: 'May we become the pṛṣṭha.' They saw this sāman. They praised with it. Thereupon they became the pṛṣṭha. The pṛṣṭha is this fire of this world, this Vāyu of the intermediate space, that Āditya of heaven. 'Pṛṣṭham' — they praise and establish here. Śrī is indeed the pṛṣṭha, eminence is the pṛṣṭha. One who knows thus attains śrī and becomes eminent. It has upward il̥ā, the form of the bṛhat, on a bārhata day.
3.253: Now the saṃkṛti. The gods, going upward to the heavenly world by a three-night rite, found it unsupported and wavering. They supported it with the gāyatra side, extended it with the santanina, completed it with the saṃkṛti, joined it with the triṣandhi, smoothed it with the śrāyantīya, and made it unharmed with the ariṣṭa. When the gods went to the heavenly world by the three-night rite, they went by this. It wavered apart for them. Therefore most sāmans are performed in this. They heal this very three-night rite with these. They make a remedy, an expiation, with this. With the saṃkṛti the gods completed offspring, cattle, śrī, and food. That is the saṃkṛti-ness of the saṃkṛti. One who knows thus perfects offspring, cattle, śrī, and food. It has two vṛkaṇa sounds, for the attainment of both kinds of speech — both the domestic and the wild. It has a four-fold nidhana — for cattle are four-footed.
3.254: Now the kaunmalabarhiṣa. With this, Kunmalabarhis won both kinds of cattle — bovine and equine. One wins them with this. Kunmalabarhis desired: 'May I obtain both kinds of cattle — bovine and equine.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. 'Tvaṃ hy ehi cerave vidā bhagaṃ vasuttaye / ud vāvṛṣasva maghavan gaviṣṭaya ud indro aśvamiṣṭaye' — thus he obtained both bovine and equine cattle. Having sacrificed with those cattle, he went to the heavenly world. This is the sāman pertaining to cattle that leads to heaven. One who knows thus obtains cattle and goes to the heavenly world.
3.255: Now the devasthāna. With the third three-day period, the gods went upward to the heavenly world. It, unsupported, collapsed for them. They saw this sāman. With it, saying 'e sthād idaṃ, e sthād idam' (stand here, stand here), they established it. They made it firm with this. Because they established it with this, that is the devasthāna-ness of the devasthāna. When the devasthāna occurs here, they establish this very three-night rite, they make it firm. Having completed it, they went to the heavenly world with it. That they stood firm in the heavenly world — that too is the devasthāna-ness of the devasthāna. When they went to the heavenly world, everything became concealed, as its nidhana indicates. With this sāman one wins a great unconquerable victory. This is its verse: 'The gods, clothing themselves in the vairūpa, stood upon it, and it spread out for them. Having uttered the hiṃkāra, ascending again, they shook off all the old age of their bodies.' Having completed this very chandomya three-day rite, ascending the devasthāna sāman, they went to the heavenly world. This too is its verse: 'When the vairūpa was laid down and they stood divided, uttering the hiṃkāra they stood firm in the course. They created the devasthāna sāman; by it the gods went to immortality.'
3.256: Prajāpati created food. That food, fearing diminishment, fled to the directions. He desired: 'May I obtain food.' He saw this sāman. With it, saying 'e sthād idam, e sthād idam' (stand here, stand here), he obtained food from all the directions. One who desires food should say it four times. There are four directions. He obtains food from the directions. One who desires heaven should say it three times — for there are three worlds — for the completeness of these worlds. Now the arkapuṣpa — arka is food; its flower is that which is of the forest, for it grows in the forest — for the obtaining of wild food. The gods, desiring heaven, performed austerity. They saw these two sāmans. They praised with them. Thereupon they attained the heavenly world. These are the two sāmans that lead to heaven. One who knows thus attains the heavenly world. Just as, after rain, a low place becomes visible — even so the heavenly world became visible through these two. They are indeed heavenly.
3.257: Now the dairghaśravasa — dairghaśravasa is food — for the obtaining of food. Dīrghatamas Māmateya, going away to seek wealth, said to Dīrghaśravas, his younger brother: 'Be the overseer of these our dependents.' Dīrghaśravas desired: 'May I obtain food; may these dependents not go hungry.' He saw this sāman. He praised Agni with it: 'Yo viśvā dayate vasu hotā mandro janānām / madhor na pātrā prathamāny asmai pra stomā yantv agnaye' — thus. Agni, being praised, made all this sweet, honey-like food for him. His dependents were as if eating honey. This is indeed a great essence, a great food. With this sāman one obtains great food indeed.
3.258: Now yaśas (glory). By yaśas the gods became glorious. 'May we become glorious' — with this they sit for the sattra. They indeed become glorious. Śrī is indeed yaśas. One who knows thus attains śrī. It has a nidhana of thirty-seven syllables. The bṛhatī has thirty-six syllables. The bṛhatī is the heavenly world. The sun is the thirty-seventh. In that heavenly world they establish themselves at the end. At the end they pray this beautiful blessing: 'Āyur viśvāyur viśvaṃ viśvam āyur aśīmahī' (May we obtain life, universal life, everything, all life). With that they attain abundant prosperity. Now śulka (splendour). By śulka the gods became splendid. 'May we be splendid' — with this they sit for the sattra. They become splendid. Śrī is indeed śulka. One who knows thus attains śrī. They also call it āśva. The gods, desiring heaven, performed austerity. They saw this sāman. They praised with it. Thereupon they attained the heavenly world. This is the sāman that leads to heaven. One who knows thus attains the heavenly world. Now bhargas (radiance). By bhargas the gods became radiant. 'May we become radiant' — with this they sit for the sattra. They become radiant. Śrī is indeed bhargas. One who knows thus attains śrī. Now the abhiśava, for holding both reins. As one would drive with one rein — so it is when there is only one; as one would drive with both reins — so it is when there are both. By these two the dvādaśarātra proceeds unharmed and held together.
3.259: Now the arka of Dīrghatamas — arka is food — for the obtaining of food. Dīrghatamas Māmateya, desiring food, performed austerity. He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereupon he obtained food. This is the sāman for the obtaining of food. One who knows thus obtains food, becomes the best eater among his own people. Because Dīrghatamas Māmateya saw it, therefore it is called the arka of Dīrghatamas. 'Asāvi somo aruṣo vṛṣā hariḥ' — they become possessing bulls (vṛṣaṇvatī), which is the form of the bṛhat. This day is bārhata. 'Rājeva dasmo abhi gā acikradat / punāno vāram aty eṣy avyayaṃ śyeno na yoniṃ ghṛtavantam āsadat // parjanyaḥ pitā mahiṣasya parṇino nābhā pṛthivyā giriṣu kṣayaṃ dadhe / svasāra āpo abhi gā ud āsaran saṃ grāvabhir vasate vīte adhvare' — they become rich in cattle and rich in animals, for the obtaining of cattle. For cattle are chandomās. 'Kavir vedhasyā pary eṣi māhinam atyo na nikto abhi vājam arṣasi / apasedhaṃ duritā soma no mṛḍa ghṛtā vasānaḥ pari yāsi nirṇijam' — they become encircling (parivatī), which is the form of the end.
3.260: Among those is the sāmarāja. Śrī is sovereignty. One who knows thus attains śrī and goes to sovereignty. They also call it saṃvad. The gods went to the heavenly world downhill (pravatā), and flew up uphill (udvatā). They attained it by the level path (saṃvatā). When these sāmans occur, they go to the heavenly world downhill, fly up uphill, and attain it by the level path. They have nidhana in speech (vāṅnidhana). They seek speech through the chandomās. Speech is as if attained at this time.
3.261: Now the bṛhat. Eminence (varṣman) is the bṛhat. They go establishing themselves in eminence. It occurs on the jāgata day. The bṛhat is the form of the male; the jagatī is the female. When the bṛhat occurs here as the pṛṣṭha, this day is thereby made a pair (mithuna). Now the vāmadevya. Cattle are indeed the vāmadevya. Those who praise with it become rich in cattle. 'Śrāyanta iva sūryaṃ viśved indrasya bhakṣataḥ' — they become universal (viśvavatī). For this day is vaiśvadeva. 'Vasūni jāte janimāny ojase' — they become birth-bearing (janadvatī). Birth-bearing is the form of the jagatī. This day is jāgata. 'Prati bhāgaṃ na dīdhimaḥ // alarṣirātiṃ vasudām upa stuhi bhadro indrasya rātayaḥ / yo asya kāmaṃ vidhatā na roṣati mano dānāya codayan.'
3.262: Among those is the śrāyantīya. The gods, approaching the sattra, said: 'Let us remove what is cruel in our selves. Let us not approach with cruelty.' Whatever cruelty was in their selves, they fashioned it and deposited it in two clay dishes as sweepings. Then they approached the sattra. From that this god Akhala was born. Because he was born from the two clay dishes (śarāva), that is his name. This is indeed that Agni who was born. He does not harm one who knows thus. He said to the gods: 'For what purpose did you generate me?' 'For surveillance,' they said — 'you shall slay whoever transgresses.' Prajāpati desired his own daughter in an improper way. She stood before him having become a red doe (rohit). He, having become a spotted deer (pṛṣata), mounted her. He (Akhala/Rudra) thought: 'The gods generated me for surveillance. This one transgresses. Let me pierce him.' He pierced him.
3.263: This is the three-pointed arrow. Therefore the spotted deer is the sweetest-tasting. Therefore it burns, as it were. The seed of the pierced one fell away. It came to rest on the snowy mountain (Himavant). It became human. The gods and ṛṣis, having come together, said: 'May this not be corrupted (mā idaṃ duṣad).' Because they said 'mā idaṃ duṣad,' that is the māduṣa-ness of māduṣa. That is indeed its name, māduṣa; it is called mānuṣa (human). They kindled it with fire. The Maruts blew upon it. They smoothed it with the śrāyantīya. That is the śrāyantīya-ness of the śrāyantīya. From that, cattle were released. Those released first were red (rohita). Those released from what was being heated were tawny (aruṇa). Those released from what was heated were brown (babhru). Those released from what was being burnt were white and black. Therefore this very fire burns what is both white and black.
3.264: From the ashes mixed with embers, three barren cows were released — one belonging to Mitra-Varuṇa, one to Bṛhaspati, one to the Viśvedevas. From the ashes alone the donkey was released. Therefore it resembles ash. Therefore it survives even when it is being dried out. This is the sāman of hero-generation and pertaining to cattle. For heroes were born then — namely the Aṅgirasas — and cattle were released. A hero is born for him, he obtains cattle, becomes rich in cattle — one who knows thus. If one has gone astray in the sacrifice, they should make the śrāyantīya the brahmasāman among his pṛṣṭha chants. For the pṛṣṭha chants and the stotra chants shake off one who has gone astray in the sacrifice. They thereby establish him in the pṛṣṭha chants and the stotra chants. 'Yata indra bhayāmahe tato no abhayaṃ kṛdhi / maghavan śagdhi tava tan na ūtaye vi dviṣo vi mṛdho jahi' — with this they slay the evil one who is hostile. Among those, the samanta has its brāhmaṇa already stated. It becomes the il̥ā acchāvāka-sāman. Cattle are indeed il̥ā.
3.265: 'Tvaṃ somāsi dhārayuḥ' — for firmness (dhṛti). For at this point they reach the end, as it were, of the chandomās: 'Let us not fall beyond.' 'Tvaṃ suto madintamaḥ' — they become intoxicating (madvatī); essence (rasa) is indeed intoxication (mada). The third pressing is as if drunk (dhīta). When these become intoxicating, they place essence in it. They make it swell with this. 'Tvaṃ suṣvāṇo adribhiḥ' — it has 'tvam,' which is the form of the bṛhat. This day is bārhata. 'Abhy arṣa kanikradat / dyumantaṃ śuṣmam uttamam' — for this three-day period that is the chandomās is made as the last. They have the same beginnings but different endings. This is the first foot, this the second, that the third. They say: 'Those who perform the dvādaśāha depart from this world, for they reach an end, as it were.' Because these have the same beginnings but different endings, they establish themselves in this very world. On the sixth day, those with different beginnings but the same ending occur. With those they also establish themselves in the other world. Among those, the gāyatra has its brāhmaṇa already stated. Now the aidāvasunidhana, with external nidhana, bārhata. Therefore it is performed on a bārhata day. There was a ṛṣi named Asur who had no cattle. He desired: 'May I obtain cattle.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. He approached the nidhana saying 'idaṃ vasu' (this wealth). That is the aidāvasunidhana-ness of the aidāvasunidhana. Cattle are indeed wealth (vasu). Thereupon he obtained cattle. This is the sāman pertaining to cattle. One who knows thus obtains cattle and becomes rich in cattle. It has a four-syllable nidhana — for cattle are four-footed.
3.266: Now the kāśīta. Kaśīti Saubhara was afflicted by disease (yakṣma) during the sattra. He desired: 'May I destroy the disease.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it: 'Vāta ā vātu bheṣajaṃ śaṃbhu mayobhu no hṛde / pra na āyūṃṣi tāriṣat // yad ado vāta te gṛhe 'mṛtasya nidhir hitaḥ / tato no dehi jīvase // uta vāta pitāsi na uta bhrātota nas sakhā / sa no jīvātave kṛdhi' — thus Vāta (Wind) made medicine for him. This is the remedy; they make an expiation with it. It is also wish-fulfilling (kāmasani): he desired this wish, and that wish was fulfilled for him. Whatever wish one desires when praising with this sāman, that wish is fulfilled. In this they approach the il̥ā in the reverse direction: 'Let us not fall beyond the chandomās' — cattle are il̥ā; cattle are chandomās — for the obtaining of cattle. Because Kaśīti Saubhara saw it, therefore it is called the kāśīta. Now the aśvasūkta has its brāhmaṇa already stated. It has a five-syllable nidhana — the paṅkti has five feet; cattle are pāṅkta; cattle are chandomās — for the obtaining of cattle. Now the śāmmada has its brāhmaṇa already stated. Now the hāviṣkṛta has its brāhmaṇa already stated; it is the explanation (nirukti) of the havis. It has a four-syllable nidhana — cattle are four-footed; cattle are chandomās — for the obtaining of cattle. 'Tvaṃ hy aṅga daivye' — it has 'tvam,' which is the form of the bṛhat. This day is bārhata. 'Pavamāna janimāni dyumattama' — they become birth-bearing (janadvatī). Birth-bearing is the form of the jagatī. This day is jāgata. Among those, the sauparṇa has its brāhmaṇa already stated. 'Pavasva devavītaye' — it becomes all-divine (sarvadevatya). For this day is vaiśvadeva and jāgata. 'Tava drapsā udaprutaḥ indraṃ madāya vāvṛdhuḥ' — for this is an advanced day.
3.267: Among those is the vaiśvamanasa. A man named Viśvamanas, a fire-kindler (samiddhāra), who had gone out to the forest, was found by a bear (ṛkṣa). He desired: 'May I destroy the bear.' He saw this sāman. He sang it. Indra saw him: 'A bear has found Viśvamanas.' He ran toward him. As he was running, he hid behind his breath. He asked him: 'Viśvamanas, who is this beyond you?' 'A sūrmi (beast), O Maghavan,' he said. 'Say sūrmī maghavan,' he instructed. He said 'sūrmī maghavan.' 'Now strike him with the stick.' He said: 'Pull out a reed and gently place it on him.' He pulled out a reed and gently placed it on him. Indra then poured the thunderbolt into it. It slew that one. This is a sāman that slays the rival and destroys rākṣasas. One who knows thus slays the hostile rival and destroys rākṣasas and evil.
3.268: 'Pati tyaṃ haryataṃ harim' — they become encircling (parivatī), which is the form of the end. For this is the final day. 'Babhruṃ punanti vāreṇa / yo devān viśvaṃ it pari' — they become universal (viśvavatī). For this day is vaiśvadeva. 'Madena saha gacchatī' — they become intoxicating (madvatī). They place essence and intoxication in the third pressing in order. 'Dvir yaṃ pañca svayaśasaṃ svasāro adrisaṃhatam / priyam indrasya kāmyaṃ prasnāpayanta ūrmayaḥ' — with this one prays for a blessing. 'Indrāya soma pātave vṛtraghne pari ṣicyase' — they become Vṛtra-slaying, for the final victory. Among those, the gaurivīta has its brāhmaṇa already stated. Now the nihava. Indra, loathing the third pressing, departed. For it was as if drunk (dhīta). The Viśvedevas called him back with this sāman: 'Come here, come here (āyihī, āyihī)!' Thereupon Indra returned to the third pressing. Thereupon he became non-departing from it. This is the sāman associated with Indra. One's sacrifice becomes associated with Indra and the gods. Indra turns back to one's sacrifice. Indra does not depart from the sacrifice of one who knows thus. They also call it the nihava of Vasiṣṭha. Vasiṣṭha, defeated and bereft of sons, desired: 'May I call back offspring and cattle.' He saw this sāman. He praised with it. With it, saying 'āyihī, āyihī' (come here, come here), he called back offspring and cattle. That is indeed the nihava-ness. One who knows thus calls back offspring and cattle. It has stobha interwoven with anutunna. From the anutunna, offspring and cattle are born. Cattle are the chandomās. Thereby it is complete in form.
3.269: Now the yadvāhiṣṭhīya. Agni did not wish to carry the oblation from the ninth day to the tenth day for the gods. The gods desired: 'May Agni carry the oblation across for us.' They saw this sāman. They praised him with it: 'Yad vāhiṣṭhaṃ tad agnaye bṛhad arca vibhāvaso / mahiṣīva tvad rayis tvad vājā ud īrate // tava dyumanto arcayo grāvevocyate bṛhat / uto te tanyatur yathā svāno arta tmanā divaḥ // evo agniṃ vasūyavas sahasānaṃ vavandima / sa no viśvā ati dviṣaḥ parṣan nāveva sukratuḥ' — just as one would ferry across by a boat, even so Agni ferried the oblation across from the ninth day to the tenth day for them.
