He is indeed able to ferry across. When the yadvāhiṣṭhīya occurs here, it is for the continuity of the sacrifice. The sacrifice is extended for him; Agni carries his oblation across — one who knows thus. It has upward il̥ā, the form of the bṛhat, on a bārhata day.
3.270: Now the āsita. The ṛṣis said: 'Come, let us seek to conquer that heavenly world above, wherein these Atharvans dwell.' Preṇi Saubhāhita, Madhucchandas Vaiśvāmitra, Asita Daivala, and whatever others there were who wished to be consecrated near him — for them, though they had been sacrificing for a long time, they had not heard anything for many years. But then they heard: 'He makes the āśrāvaṇa call,' or 'He makes the vaṣaṭkāra.' Then one of the Atharvans named Udvanta ascended, cup in hand. He said to them: 'With what desire do you perform?' 'We wish to conquer this heavenly world above, in which the Atharvans dwell.' He said: 'Do you go to the village from behind?' 'We do.' 'For what purpose?' 'For a path.' 'Yes,' he said, 'but that is not heavenly for you.' Then he looked into the cup. He said: 'Do you eat meat?' 'We do.' 'For what purpose?' 'For sight and the vital breaths.' 'Yes,' he said, 'but that is not heavenly for you.' Then he looked into the cup again. He said: 'Do you have union with women?' 'We do.' 'For what purpose?' 'For offspring, for continuity, for non-interruption.' 'Yes,' he said, 'but that is not heavenly for you.' Then he looked into the cup again. He said: 'Do you speak untruth?' 'We do.' 'For what purpose?' 'For the sake of women, for jest, or for friendship.'
3.271: 'Rise up, state your desires — but there is no prospect for you of conquering that world.' Now Preṇi Saumāhita was ugly. He said: 'I choose virility equal to seven billy-goats of poor potency. And whatever woman I address, may she desire me.' Then Madhucchandas Vaiśvāmitra said: 'I choose the mouth of a brāhmaṇa (i.e., sacred eloquence).' Then Asita Daivala said: 'I shall just look into this cup.' He (Udvanta) said: 'This one alone among them observes the vow.' He, divinely looking, saw this sāman. He praised with it: 'Rāye agne mahe tvā dānāya sam idhīmahi / īl̥iṣvā hi mahe vṛṣaṃ dyāvā hotrāya pṛthivī' — heaven and earth are all these worlds. Thereupon he wandered through all these worlds. In the forenoon he was in the assembly of the gods, at midday among humans in the court of Drupada Vārddhraviṣṇa, in the afternoon among the ancestors. This is the sāman that leads to heaven. One who knows thus wanders through all these worlds and attains the heavenly world.
3.272: Now the sādhra, for success indeed. It has a nidhana in the middle, for establishment. Those who approach the ārbhava pavamāna float about as if without a hold in the ocean. When the nidhana is in the middle, it is indeed for establishment. The gods desired: 'May all that we have done succeed.' They saw this sāman. They praised with it. Thereupon all that they had done succeeded. 'May all that we have done succeed' — with this they sit for the sattra. All that is done indeed succeeds for them. Because what they had done succeeded by this sāman, therefore it is called the sādhra. Sadhri Vairūpa, desiring cattle, performed austerity. He saw this sāman. He praised with it. He approached this il̥ā. Cattle are il̥ā. Thereupon he obtained cattle. This is the sāman pertaining to cattle. One who knows thus obtains cattle and becomes rich in cattle.
3.273: Now the ākūpāra. Akūpāra Kaśyapa went down to the ocean with his companions (kalis). He sought a foothold in it. He saw this sāman. He praised with it. Thereupon he found a foothold in the ocean — this very earth. Therefore his companions sit on his back. This is the sāman of firm footing. One who knows thus stands firm. The ocean is the chandomās. Kaśyapa is indeed able to ferry across the ocean. When the ākūpāra occurs here, it is for crossing the ocean. Because Akūpāra Kaśyapa saw it, therefore it is called the ākūpāra. Now these become two-footed (dvipadā); their brāhmaṇa is already stated. These are: 'Pavasva soma mahe dakṣāya.' For great is this day, the forty-eighth. Among those, the vidharma has its brāhmaṇa already stated. It has a four-syllable nidhana — cattle are four-footed; cattle are chandomās — for the obtaining of cattle. 'Upo ṣu jātam apturam' — they become birth-bearing (janadvatī). Birth-bearing is the form of the jagatī. This day is jāgata. 'Gobhir bhaṅgaṃ pariṣkṛtam / induṃ devā ayāsiṣuḥ // arṣā nas soma śaṃ gave dhukṣasva pipyuṣīm iṣam / vardhā samudram ukthyam' — they become rich in cattle and rich in animals, for the obtaining of cattle. For cattle are chandomās.
3.274: Among those is the śrudhīya. Prajāpati created the sacrifice. He offered it to the gods. It, fearing diminishment, ran away. The gods desired: 'May it hear us.' They saw this sāman. They called it back with it, saying 'środhiya' (listen!). It heard. They said to it: 'Turn back to us.' 'I fear diminishment,' it said. 'No,' they said. 'Then make a pledge (śraddhā) to me.' They made the pledge 'środhiya' to it. They called it saying 'come here.' Thereupon the sacrifice did not depart from them. Thereupon it turned back to them. At this time, on the ninth day, the sacrifice is as if ending. When the śrudhīya occurs here, they call the sacrifice back together. Therefore one should use 'środhiya' in the first verse and 'środhema' in the latter two.
3.275: Now the yajñāyajñīya has its brāhmaṇa already stated. Now the daivānīka. The gods and asuras were in contest. The gods desired: 'With Agni as our leader (anīka), may we conquer the asuras.' They saw this sāman. They praised with it. 'Bhadro no agnir āhutaḥ' — they made Agni their leader (anīka). 'Bhadrā rātiḥ subhaga bhadro adhvaraḥ / bhadrā uta praśastayaḥ // bhadraṃ manaḥ kṛṇuṣva vṛtratūrye yenā samatsu sāsahiḥ' — in battles they overcame them. 'Ava sthirā tanuhi bhūri śardhatām' — whatever strength and power the asuras had, they struck it down with this. 'Vanemā te abhiṣṭaye' — thus they overpowered them. Thereupon the gods, with Agni as their leader, conquered the asuras. That is the daivānīka-ness of the daivānīka. This is the sāman of victory that slays the rival.
3.326: In the beginning, there was verily one syllable — Vāc (Speech) alone. She desired: 'May I become many, may I be born forth, may I attain abundance, may I extend the sacrifice.' She offered twelve times, saying 'svāhā, svāhā, svāhā.' She produced thirty and thirty [syllables]. She herself became [a metre] of thirty syllables. Two syllables crept up behind her, as it were. That became this anuṣṭubh. Therefore, a woman's breasts swell forth from behind, as it were. This virāj is indeed the one with breasts. When she offered twelve times, she thereby created the twelve months. These became the twelve days of the dvādaśāha; they became the twenty-four half-months; they became the seven hundred and twenty day-and-nights. She created the year as the sacrifice. This sacrifice is verily the year. She did not know: 'I have created the sacrifice.' She thought only: 'What indeed have I created?' Joy came upon her. Then she knew: 'I have indeed created the sacrifice.'
3.327: With eight syllables she fashioned the first day, with eleven the second, with twelve the third. A single three-syllabled syllable-group remained over. She distributed that over the latter three days. Thus she fashioned this pṛṣṭhya ṣaḍaha (six-day sequence of pṛṣṭha chants). Having fashioned this pṛṣṭhya ṣaḍaha, she considered: 'By what means shall I fashion this third three-day period?' She saw only her own self: 'That self of mine which was there is this one here. Come, let me fashion it upon the self itself.' With eight syllables she fashioned the seventh day, with eleven the eighth, with twelve the ninth. Then the syllable-group that remained over — that became the tenth day of the dvādaśāha. This Vāc, exhausted, having drawn out the sacrifice, like a lioness who has just given birth, or a tigress, or a crocodile, lay stretched out in her lair, weary and feeble. If one were to seize her directly now, one would come to grief. And if one were to go beyond her, one would go beyond all this.
3.328: The hotṛ does not recite from the anuṣṭubh; he recites from the virāj alone. The gods said: 'This one [Vāc] has created the sacrifice; let us sacrifice with these.' They said to the bṛhatī: 'You carry for us this third day. Our abhijit is indeed unaccomplished.' The bṛhatī is indeed of six [syllables] when it serves as the pṛṣṭha. Having carried this third day, the bṛhatī did not wish to carry further. With the rathantara they conquered this world, with the bṛhatī the mid-region, and with the bṛhat the yonder world. With this three-day period they conquered. Just as one goes with a conquered [army], driving Sindhu horses, carrying an elephant, riding a chariot seized by the pole, going with two hulubhya-horses, going with split-yoke horses — even so with a donkey-chariot.
3.329: The bṛhadrathantare took back these forms: the vairūpa, the vairāja, the mahānāmnī, and the revatī. And the bṛhatī took back the virāja, the śakvarī, and the revatī. These [forms] passed beyond them. The gods perceived them as one might see a fish moving with a wave. On the seventh day they rose to the surface, as fishes might rise up. All these are combined in the chandomas. The bṛhat is in the bṛhatī, the rathantara is in the bṛhatī. Just as one might cross a deep ford by holding on, even so the gods crossed this third three-day period. They reached the svarga loka — that is the tenth day. The tenth day is verily the svarga loka. That [day] is of twenty-four [stomas]. Of those, the twenty constitute the virāj, and the four are the seat of Bradhna. There the gods milked all desires. One offers with [the formula]: 'Releasing the support to the mother, the support suckles the mother; may he bestow upon us wealth, nourishment, food, and vigor — svāhā.' Whatever desire one desires, one should meditate upon it. When this [oblation] is offered, that desire is fulfilled for one who knows thus.
3.330: Going forward, going back, swift, extended, golden, lustrous, being yoked — this is the form of the first day, of the trivṛt stoma, of the gāyatrī metre, of the rathantara sāman. Bull-like, Vṛtra-slaying, wealth-bearing, all-pervading, established — this is the form of the second day, of the pañcadaśa stoma, of the triṣṭubh metre, of the bṛhat sāman. Upward-going, threefold, directional, going forth, ancient — this is the form of the third day, of the saptadaśa stoma, of the jagatī metre, of the vairūpa sāman. Kingly, generating people, sovereign, solar — this is the form of the fourth day, of the ekaviṃśa stoma, of the anuṣṭubh metre, of the vairāja sāman. Brilliant, with offspring, of paṅkti and śakvarī [metres], with extended syllables, rich in cattle, bull-like, thunderbolt-bearing, mighty — this is the form of the fifth day, of the triṇava stoma, of the paṅkti metre, of the śākvara sāman.
3.331: Because this [agni-vibhakti], though being the same, is divided across the ṣaḍaha and yet becomes different [in each day], therefore the year is of varied forms, and what is born from it is of varied forms. 'Agna' is the vibhakti of the first day, 'agnim' of the second, 'agninā' of the third, 'agnir' of the fourth, 'agner' of the fifth, and 'agna' again of the sixth. These two ends are joined together. Following this, the year is endless. Its two ends are hemanta (winter) and vasanta (spring). Following this, the two ends of a village meet; following this, the two ends of a gold necklace meet; following this, a serpent lies with its coils encircled. This is food. That divine wheel, imperishable and ever-renewed, revolves back and forth. He who knows this thus wins this very endlessness, this imperishability, this ever-renewal. With 'Agna ā yāhi vītaye' they summon him. With 'Agniṃ dūtaṃ vṛṇīmahe' they choose him. With 'Agninā agnis sam idhyate' they kindle him. With 'Agnir vṛtrāṇi jaṅghanad' they conquer by this. With 'Tava śriyo carṣasyeva vidyutaḥ' they place splendour in him. He who knows the vibhakti of the sixth day lives a full life. 'Jātavedas' is verily the vibhakti of the sixth day, and 'agna' also.
3.332: The gods sought splendour in the pṛṣṭhya ṣaḍaha. They did not find it with 'agna' on the first day. They did not find it with 'agnim' on the second day. They did not find it with 'agninā' on the third day. They found it with 'agnir' on the fourth day. What had come into being they spoke forth on the latter [days]. Because this agni-vibhakti, though being the same, becomes different — these [fires] are entered into it. Moreover, by means of these agnis, all the seasons become possessed of fire. The iḷā of the first day has a flowing forth; the second has an upward [iḷā]; the third has an enclosed [iḷā]; the fourth has iḷā upon iḷā; the fifth has a half-extra [iḷā]; the sixth has one intensified with the iha-sound. And Vāsyaska asked the Naimiśīyas: 'What do you accomplish with the first day?' 'We go forth,' [they said]. 'What with the second?' 'We make a resting place.' 'What with the third?' 'We swim around.' 'What with the fourth?' 'We place reality upon reality.' 'What with the fifth?' 'We go forth, separating the cattle.' 'What with the sixth?' 'We arrive,' they said. 'And we heal those cattle.'  That which [they said] 'We go forth' — that is indeed being driven forward, for one goes forward when one sets out.
3.333: As for their saying 'We make a resting place' — one rests indeed, as if unyoking; one makes a halt. For the iḷā of the second day reaches its end in a resting. As for 'We swim around' — one swims around, as it were, for the iḷā of the third day reaches its end by flowing around. As for 'We place reality upon reality' — that is iḷās upon iḷās, cattle upon cattle. That is reality placed upon reality. As for 'We go forth, separating the cattle' — the half-extra iḷā is indeed cut off, being made into food. Cattle are indeed the iḷā. Therefore cattle are enjoyed through cutting and transformation. As for 'We arrive' — with 'iha' (here) they established themselves in this very [world]. And thereby those cattle were healed. Because this iḷā-vibhakti, though being the same, becomes different, therefore cattle are of varied forms. What is born from them is of varied forms. What begins with ṛc is the form of the first day. The enclosed [form] is of the second; the doubly-enclosed is of the third; the connected is of the fourth; the intensified is of the fifth; the one intensified with iha-sound is of the sixth. Because this svara-vibhakti, though being the same, becomes different, therefore this wind (vāyu) blows according to the season — cold in winter, hot in summer. 'Indra' is the vibhakti of the first day, 'indram' of the second, 'indreṇa' of the third, 'indra' [nominative] of the fourth, 'indrād' of the fifth, and 'indra' again of the sixth.
3.334: Thus indeed was this [universe] in the beginning. It desired: 'May I come into being, may I be born forth, may I attain abundance.' It conceived with mind. Going a second time, it called upon another. Therefore one goes a second time and calls upon another. With this mind it performed tapas. Mind is verily tapas. While performing tapas, it created speech. With these two it praised: 'sto me, sto ma.' When it said 'sto ma,' that is the stoma-nature of the stoma. 'By praising with this, this one of ours is the best' — [thus knows] he who knows this. These two stomas came into being — the outward and the inward. They are prāṇa and apāna; they are day and night; they are the waxing and waning fortnights; they are these two worlds; they are Indra and Agni; they are Mitra and Varuṇa; they are the Aśvins; they are the divine couple; whatever pair exists, that they became. These two used to come together within Prajāpati. Then this mind sprang upon speech. That golden embryo was conceived — immortal. This fire (agni) is verily death. When gold is cast into it, it becomes more beautiful. This embryo was born.
3.335: When they stripped off its first membrane, that became black iron. When they stripped off its second membrane, that became silver. When they stripped off its third membrane, that became gold. Therefore that [gold] is the most lustrous, for it was its innermost layer. And the afterbirth that was in the channel became copper. Therefore that is the most foul-smelling. Seventeen syllables were born. They divided them in two — nine on one side, eight on the other. That became the trivṛt stoma and the gāyatrī metre. He considered: 'This stoma, this metre — these two are sufficient for him to generate. Come, let me tell these two to generate.' Having praised with the trivṛt, he said to the gāyatrī: 'Expand! Grow to your full extent!' She, being of eight syllables, made the syllables into groups of three. They became twenty-four.
3.336: That became the pañcadaśa (fifteen-fold) stoma. He took two more. That became the saptadaśa (seventeen-fold) stoma. He took four more. That became the ekaviṃśa (twenty-one-fold) stoma. With half [of its expansion] the gāyatrī reached the viṣuvant (solstice). He took six more. That became the triṇava (twenty-seven-fold) stoma. He took six more. That became the trayastriṃśa (thirty-three-fold) stoma. Beyond that he did not find [anything further].
3.337: He combined the trivṛt and the pañcadaśa. That became the twenty-four-fold seventh day, with gāyatrī as its metre. Therefore both bṛhadrathantare are performed there, for both these stomas belong to the bṛhat and rathantara. The rathantara should properly be there in the mother's [role], but the bṛhat is made the pṛṣṭha. He combined the saptadaśa and the triṇava. That became the forty-four-fold eighth day, with triṣṭubh as its metre. Therefore the rathantara is made the pṛṣṭha there. He combined the ekaviṃśa and the trayastriṃśa. From that, six syllables were left over. And the forty-eight that remained — that became the forty-eight-fold ninth day, with jagatī as its metre. Therefore the bṛhat is made the pṛṣṭha there.
3.338: He uttered hiṅkāra over those six syllables. From that he measured out six more. They became twelve. He uttered hiṅkāra over them again. From that he measured out six more. They became eighteen. He uttered hiṅkāra over them again. From that he measured out six more. They became twenty-four. That became the twenty-four-fold tenth day, with anuṣṭubh as its metre. Therefore a sāman with triple hiṅkāra [is used]. They say: 'The stomas go beyond the trivṛt, but the metres do not go beyond the gāyatrī.' One should say: 'The stomas do not go beyond the trivṛt, nor do the metres go beyond the gāyatrī.' For the stomas have three beginnings, three middles, and three endings. And all metres do not go beyond the gāyatrī. This dvādaśāha is the fullness of both the trivṛt stoma and the gāyatrī metre. The gāyatrī is verily brahman. The trivṛt is kṣatra. That which is the vajra (thunderbolt) — by that is kṣatra. This dvādaśāha is both brahman and kṣatra. But they also say: 'The trivṛt is brahman, the gāyatrī is brahman; this dvādaśāha is brahman. This entire dvādaśāha is to be known as brahman alone.' Of this there are these two verses: 'Four go forth yoked in the beginning; they become six in the highest space. Becoming two by two, they fly on wings; having gone, they are established in this very one. Two stomas clothe the gāyatrī; two clothe the triṣṭubh; two clothe the jagatī.'
3.339: Prajāpati verily was this [universe] in the beginning. He desired: 'May I become many, may I be born forth, may I attain abundance.' He performed tapas. He saw this dvādaśāha sacrifice with extended metres. He brought it forth. He sacrificed with it. He extended it. When he extended it, he extended his own limbs. Those bodies that were his — they became these mortal creatures. But his self became mind-made. Mind was his body; the vital breaths entered into him. He was as if disjointed. He said to the gods: 'Seek a body for me.' 'How shall we seek it for you?' 'Cause me to sacrifice with the dvādaśāha of combined metres.' 'So be it.' They caused him to sacrifice with the dvādaśāha of combined metres.
3.340: Prajāpati became mind-made, prāṇa-made, sight-made, hearing-made, speech-made, ṛc-made, yajus-made, sāman-made, brahman-made, golden, immortal. That is this very lightning. And that is the threefold Veda. The upward [flashes of] lightning are the sāmans; the horizontal ones are the ṛcs; the frequent flickering is the yajus. He who knows this thus becomes this very deity. This deity, when praised, becomes the sacrifice. If they should ask: 'What deity becomes the sacrifice when praised?' — one should say: 'Lightning.' This deity indeed becomes the sacrifice when praised. Therefore, if one should sacrifice with the dvādaśāha of extended metres, one should indeed sacrifice with the dvādaśāha of combined metres.
3.341: There was verily one self-existent deity — Prajāpati alone. He desired: 'May I become many, may I be born forth.' In this world, with the trivṛt stoma, he created the gods, the Vasus. They rushed upon him. 'Sweet, sweet!' — they ate him. They suckled him, as a new-born calf suckles its mother. He thought: 'If I remain here, they will consume me. Come, let me ascend upward.' He also thought: 'Come, let me create a substitute, so that thinking it is me, seeing it, they will remain here content.'
3.342: Having established that [substitute] in this world, he ascended upward with the pañcadaśa [stoma]. He reached the place where this wind (Vāyu) is. There he also created the Rudras. These approached him; they rushed upon him. They were intent on devouring him. He thought: 'Come, let me ascend upward.' Having established Vāyu in this world, he ascended upward with the saptadaśa [stoma]. He reached the place where this moon is. There he also created the Ādityas. These approached him; those rushed upon him. They were intent on devouring him.
3.343: Having established the moon in this world, he ascended upward with the ekaviṃśa [stoma]. He reached the place where this sun (Āditya) is. There he also created the Viśve Devāḥ. He created that as the limit. These approached him; those rushed upon him. They were intent on devouring him. He thought: 'Come, let me ascend upward.' Having established the sun in this world, he ascended upward with the triṇava [stoma]. He reached the place where Varuṇa is. There he did not create [anything]. They followed him still. They were intent on devouring him.
3.344: Having established Varuṇa in this world, he ascended upward with the trayastriṃśa [stoma]. He reached the place where Mṛtyu (Death) is. There he became Death. He made his limbs into a face. Having come upon Death who had become all-faced, they just sat watching, unable to overpower him. They did not eat him there. He thought: 'I am verily pāpman (evil), since I am Death. Come, let me ascend upward.' Having established Death in this world, he ascended upward with the caturviṃśa [stoma]. He reached the world called Rocana (the Shining). There he became hunger (aśanāyā). Those [creatures], fearing him who had become hunger, did not follow him. They just stood watching: 'That one shines, that one shines.' That is his luminosity. He thought: 'I am verily evil, since I am hunger. Come, let me ascend upward.' He established day and night. These two became thirst and hunger — day is thirst, night is hunger. Just as two door-panels would be set up, even so these day and night were set up, so that none may pass beyond.
3.345: Having established day and night in this world, he ascended upward with the catuścatvāriṃśa (forty-four-fold) [stoma]. He reached the world called Kāma (Desire). There, to prevent this evil from following, he created rivers of food between himself and it, with coverings of delight. And he made them wish-fulfilling. He thought: 'Come, let me ascend upward.' He ascended with the aṣṭācatvāriṃśa (forty-eight-fold) [stoma]. He reached the world called Suvar (Light). There he rested for a long time: 'Much that is my own exists here.' That is its suvar-nature. He thought: 'Come, let me gather from these worlds into one world.' He gathered from these worlds into one world. That became the tenth day of the dvādaśāha. He said: 'This was not for nothing.' Because he said 'this was not for nothing (na akam),' that is the nāka-nature of nāka (heaven).
3.346: Just as one might climb a tree or a mountain and look around, even so from that world he surveys the other worlds. He looked upon these creatures suffering from hunger. They ran towards him in two forms — dark blue and golden. The dark [form] is the black of the rain-cloud about to rain. The golden is that which flashes within the waters. He rained upon them. From that, cooked rice (odana) was born. Having eaten the cooked rice, it became udana. That is the udana-nature of udana. Its name is verily udana; they call it odana indirectly. A second, greater food was born. Having come together, he churned it. That became the mantha (stirred drink). That is the mantha-nature of mantha. These are the two forms of food — what one eats and what one drinks. What one eats is the form of odana; what one drinks is that of mantha. Both of these are found in one's house for one who knows thus. These two — rice and barley — are verily the two breasts of Prajāpati.
3.347: Upodaka ('near the waters') is the name of the world in which this fire (Agni) dwells. Ṛtadhāman ('abode of cosmic order') is that in which Vāyu dwells. Śiva ('the auspicious') is that in which the moon dwells. Aparājita ('the unconquered') is that in which the sun (Āditya) dwells. Adhidyu ('above the sky') is that in which Varuṇa dwells. Pradyu ('the forth-shining') is that in which Mṛtyu dwells. Rocana ('the luminous') is that in which hunger dwells. Beyond this, day and night do not follow; they are established only once from here downward — like the appearance of lightning, or like stacked or kindled logs. But from here upward, these are the worlds of Prajāpati. Kāma is the eighth, Suvar the ninth, and Nāka is the tenth day. This [world] is the prāyaṇīya (opening); that [world] is the udayanīya (closing). These worlds are indeed the days of the dvādaśāha.
3.348: Upodaka is the name of the world established in food; humans are its guardians; Agni is its overlord; the form of flames is its form; it is established in the waters. Ṛtadhāman is the name of the world established among birds; the Gandharvas and Apsarases are its guardians; Vāyu is its overlord; white is its form; it is established in prāṇa. Aparājita is the name of the world established among the stars; the moon is its guardian; Āditya is its overlord; the form of the rising and setting sun is its form; it is established in the mind. Adhidyu is the name of the world established in sacrifice; the Ādityas are its guardians; Varuṇa is its overlord; dark blue is its form — the dark of the rain-cloud about to rain, or of a kārtasvara gem; it is established in untruth. Pradyu is the name of the world established in immortality; the Rudras are its guardians; Mṛtyu is its overlord; red is its form — like the colour of the gaura-cow's hide, or of a great crimson garment; it is established in ṛta, which one does not reach. Rocana is the name of the world established in tapas; the Vasus are its guardians — for they went first among the gods, therefore they are supreme; Yajña is its overlord; the form of golden gold is its form; it is established in satya (truth). These are the worlds, these are the guardians, these are the overlords.
3.349: Prajāpati created the gods. Together with śrī and speech he created them — together with food, together with the waters. The gods, not knowing food, śrī, and speech, were as if at a feast without [sustenance] — for food is śrī. He grieved. He thought: 'How shall I take away their splendours?' Becoming mind, he entered into them. He stripped away all their splendour, as one might pick off jujube berries. With mind he seized their speech. This speech is seized by mind even to this day, for whatever one desires with the mind, that one speaks with speech. He thought: 'In what form shall I take away their speech?' He made a human form. In human form he took away their speech. Therefore a human speaks all speeches. Having gathered up these waters, he ascended upward. He reached the world called Ṛṣabha ('the bull'), which is the world of food. Food is verily the bull; when one eats, one becomes bull-like. He thought: 'What form shall I take so that no other may assail me?' He became a speckled goat. Therefore we cannot find a speckled goat. Those waters that he carried off — they are these upper waters. And that speech he carried off — that is the thunder above. And that which flashes within the waters [is also that].
3.350: [The creatures were] babbling in blind darkness. Having cut off one syllable from this speech, he released it as 'vāk' (speech). Having fallen, it stopped in the middle of these worlds. That became this [solar] disc. He followed it. He became prāṇa. Dawn broke for them. Though hungry, he let fall one drop from these waters, [wondering:] 'How much will be enough for them?' It became tenfold, hundredfold, thousandfold — as vast as this earth, so vast it became. Having come together, he churned it. That became the mantha (stirred drink). That is the mantha-nature of mantha. Having eaten the cooked rice, it became udana. That is the udana-nature of udana. These are the two forms of food — what one drinks and what one eats. What one drinks is the form of mantha; what one eats is that of odana. Both are found in one's house — both what one drinks and what one eats — for one who knows thus. They say: 'Three drops come down — a water-drop, a ghee-drop, and a honey-drop.' Of these, the water-drop enters this [earth]; the ghee-drop enters the plants; the honey-drop enters the trees. Thereupon Āmalaka Akūpayeya, Ahīnā Āśvatthī, and Keśī Dārbhya deliberated on this: 'Which drop do you consider to be coming? Which drop do you? Which drop do you?' Keśī said: 'I consider it to be the ghee-drop.' Ahīnā said: 'I consider it to be the honey-drop.' Āmalaka said: 'I consider it to be the water-drop.' They agreed upon that one. For everything here is born from water. That speech which he released — that is Agni. And that prāṇa which he followed — that is Indra.
3.351: He is indeed the moon. And he is verily Prajāpati. This radiance stands fashioned, shining, from beyond [down] to here — Agni and Indra. This is [its correspondence] in the self: that speech — this is that speech; this prāṇa sits above her. And that generator of creatures, in the bull-world of food, within the waters — this is that person. These same deities [exist] here and there. They go here, they go there. Those that are here are those yonder; those yonder are these. He who meditates on them in the self becomes one who does not lack the divine. The selves of these gods are deposited in Prajāpati. And the selves of these prāṇas are deposited in this person. Surrounding this person, they guard him. Of this there is this verse: 'The bull is the world, a great being; nothing existed before it nor after. The gods who are the prāṇas surround and guard this person — the god. He who knows this knows the great world, the great person.' These divine prāṇas indeed guard this divine person by surrounding him — this one who is within the eye. He is the vāmana (the lovely one). He who meditates on him as vāmana — this deity brings him to one lovely thing after another. It is for this reason that men of the warrior class look at a dwarf with some [curious] expectation. They know him to be near to the name of this deity. He does not decay. He is the same for both youth and old age. Gathering up the prāṇas and shaking off the body, he ascends upward together with the prāṇas. With this [the deity] carries away the bodies that are cast off; Mṛtyu carries away [dead] bodies. For this deity, [its two feet are] like two lumps of clay placed upon two reeds fixed in the heart. When one cuts those off, then one dies. Even the unlearned say regarding this: 'It has been cut off indeed.'
3.352: The gods overcame the asuras and rākṣasas through exertion, tapas, and brahmacarya. The asuras, having learned 'They are prevailing,' assumed the forms of women — adorned, wearing fine garments, of the most beautiful appearance. They approached the gods. The gods welcomed them, for women are not displeasing to anyone. Into them [the women], whatever tejas (brilliance), indriya (vital power), and vīrya (heroic strength) the gods had won through exertion — that they discharged. The asuras, having realized 'This much was their tejas, indriya, and vīrya,' seized it and ran away. The gods were displeased. They felt as if they had been diminished. They said: 'How shall we take back this tejas, indriya, and vīrya?'
3.353: With 'et' — one syllable — they took back the rathantara. Therefore it is performed on the first day. With 'pramet' — two syllables — they took back the bṛhat. Therefore it is performed on the second day. With 'pratimet' — three syllables — they took back the vairūpa. Therefore it is performed on the third day. With 'srīvak' — four syllables — they took back the vairāja. Therefore it is performed on the fourth day. With 'sirikamasi' — five syllables — they took back the śākvara. Therefore it is performed on the fifth day. With 'chardirindriyāvat' — six syllables — they took back the raivata. Therefore it is performed on the sixth day. With 'ā gayaṃ vīryaṃ yonim ā dadhvaṃ vidvaj janasya chāye' they took back the vāmadevya. Therefore it is performed in the middle of the sacrifice. Whatever tejas had been spilled beyond the source, that they took back with 'ā dadhvaṃ vasu' — the yajñāyajñīya.
3.354: Others say: The gods were indeed free of evil. They did not sleep. But from the abundance of exertion, they closed their eyes. While they slept heedlessly, the asuras took their tejas, indriya, and vīrya and submerged them in the waters. Viṣṇu perceived this. When the gods awoke, they were displeased. Viṣṇu said to them: 'Let there be no displeasure for you. I have indeed seen it.' He led them to the waters. They saw it within the waters, as a hump might be seen rising from a back. Having taken it out with these very vyāhṛtis, they gave it to Viṣṇu. Viṣṇu bore this tejas, indriya, and vīrya, and he contracted. Therefore Viṣṇu is short. Therefore they sacrifice a short [animal] consecrated to Viṣṇu. Where he strode while bearing [it], that became the step of Viṣṇu. Therefore the Viṣṇupadīya Kaikeya is preeminent in the sacrifice, for they found this sacrifice in him.
3.355: Prajāpati created these worlds and these directions. Having created them, he created both gods and asuras. Conflict found them. They deposited brahman in Prajāpati. Prajāpati did not lift it up. He made it enter the waters. The gods conquered the asuras. Having conquered, they returned and said: 'Prajāpati, give us that brahman which [we deposited] in you.' He said: 'I did not lift it up. I made it enter the waters.' He led them to the waters. They saw it within the waters, as a hump might be seen rising from a back. They said: 'Let us bring it forth from here.'
3.356: From the eastern direction they brought forth the rathantara as radiance. The sound of Agni followed it. Therefore, at the recitation of the rathantara, they kindle the fires. With 'om' — this very syllable — from this southern direction they brought forth the bṛhat as radiance. The sound of Parjanya (rain-cloud) followed it. Therefore, at the recitation of the bṛhat, they sound the drums. Parjanya becomes a rain-giver. With 'om' — this very syllable — from this western direction they brought forth the vairūpa as radiance. The sound of Vāyu followed it. Therefore, at the recitation of the vairūpa, they shake with fans. The wind blows downward and approaches. With 'om' — this very syllable — from this northern direction they brought forth the vairāja as radiance. The sound of birds followed it. Therefore, just as birds repeat their call, so with the vairāja one praises as if repeating. With 'om' — this very syllable — from this upward direction they brought forth the śākvara as radiance. The sound of waters followed it. Therefore, at the recitation of the śākvara, they place water nearby and praise. With 'om' — this very syllable — from this [last] direction they brought forth the raivata as radiance. The sound of cattle followed it. Therefore, at the recitation of the raivata, they make the sound of cattle and let the calves join their mothers. They said: 'We know whence we have brought these forth.' They found the vāmadevya in the waters themselves. They took it. All speeches followed it — each pṛṣṭha separately. These pṛṣṭhas, once created, shone from the directions. They have been shining ever since. 'He shines for his own; he shines!' — thus people say of one who knows this. This deity meditated upon [them]. That which rises in the east — for it was then in human form. With the trivṛt stoma and the gāyatrī metre, it took the radiance of the rathantara. It placed it here. With the pañcadaśa stoma and the triṣṭubh metre, it took the radiance of the bṛhat. It placed it here. With the saptadaśa stoma and the jagatī metre, it took the radiance of the vairūpa. It placed it here. With the ekaviṃśa stoma and the anuṣṭubh metre, it took the radiance of the vairāja. It placed it here. With the triṇava stoma and the paṅkti metre, it took the radiance of the śākvara. It placed it here. With the trayastriṃśa stoma and the atichandas metre, it took the radiance of the raivata. It placed it here. Therefore all beings are ultimately established in the waters. As it took [the radiance], beings spoke: 'ādā, ādā' (he takes, he takes). That is the āditya-nature of Āditya.
3.357: All beings came to him [saying]: 'He is arya (noble), he is arya!' That is the sūrya-nature of Sūrya. His name is verily Sorya; they call him Sūrya indirectly. 'You are our arya, you are our sustainer' — thus his own [people] and his followers worship him who knows thus. When he goes westward, taking [the radiance] and sounding forth — that verily is the sūrya-nature of Sūrya. That indeed is the svara-nature of svara — that he sounds forth. The gods said: 'He has taken it indeed!' That became the day (ahar). 'It has been given to him' — that became the night (rātri). That is the birth of day and night. He who knows this birth of day and night attains day and night; they do not pass beyond him. Everything else indeed has an end. But day and night have no end. Revolving, they attain everything. Just as one standing on the seat of a chariot might look at both sides, even so these day and night, standing in the world of Āditya, look both ways. He said to his father Prajāpati: 'Father, that tejas, indriya, and vīrya of yours — give it to me.' 'So be it.' He gave it to him.
3.358: He took dominion from heaven, sovereignty from the stars, selfhood from the mid-region, form from Vāyu, command over humans, sight from cattle, vigor from the waters, essence from the plants, movement from the trees, the reproductive organ from the birds, the flame from Agni, the heart from the earth, fragrance from gold, thunder from speech, the gathering-place from the ancestors, and radiance from the moon. Because he took (ādatta) from these beings, that is the āditya-nature of Āditya. He who knows this takes the tejas of his hateful rival. This deity is indeed one who has undergone dīkṣā (consecration) — with matted hair and wearing a black antelope skin. Its downward rays are the beard; the upward ones are the head-hair. Day and night are the two black antelope skins. Because it made its dwelling in heaven, therefore it is 'consecrated' (dīkṣita). For he who sacrifices indeed makes his dwelling in heaven.
3.359: Uṣas (Dawn) is its wife; the moon is its soma-draught. Therefore one who is consecrated has a wife. This deity takes away the tejas, indriya, vīrya, and fame of one who is being consecrated. One about to undergo dīkṣā should worship the sun [with this mantra]: 'You are the deity who has undergone dīkṣā. You take the tejas, indriya, vīrya, and fame of one being consecrated. Do not take my tejas, indriya, vīrya, and fame. I undergo my dīkṣā following your dīkṣā.' Then this deity does not take away the tejas, indriya, vīrya, and fame of one being consecrated. This is the pillar of the uktha. With its downward rays, these worlds that are below it are supported, as well as everything that has breath and body. With its upward rays, those worlds that are beyond it are supported, as well as everything that is unembodied. Because this deity stands propping up both gods and asuras between [the worlds], therefore this is the 'pillar' (stambha). He who is propped up should [remain] firm. And if someone should criticize him who meditates on this pillar, one should say to him: 'I cut you off from the deity that is the pillar; you shall fall into this misery.' He falls into divine misery. Its downward rays — these are involuntary, mortal, food-bearing. Its upward rays — these are indeed immortal worlds. The downward rays are called horayas — like a hollow reed, or a horse running forward. The upward ones are called uparīyāṃsas. Just as an elephant picks up [something] with its trunk and places it on its back, even so this deity, taking up with its rays the one who knows this, places him in the farther [worlds].
3.360: 'There was not the non-existent, nor was there the existent at that time. There was not the atmosphere, nor the sky beyond. What enveloped? Where? In whose shelter? Was there water, a deep abyss?' In that [state], when there was neither non-existence nor existence, when there was nothing at all, ṛta (cosmic order) floated up, full of light; satya (truth) floated up, full of light; tapas (creative heat) floated up, full of light. That which is ṛta — that is Vāc (Speech). That which is satya — that is Prāṇa. That which is tapas — that is Manas (Mind). Their light was food. These became one. Having become one, it nourished itself with this food-as-light. It came into being like a mass of honey poured into ladles. It considered: 'Come, let me make an opening (prāṇa) below.' It made an opening below, just as a woman has an opening below. From that, waters were created, with the sound 'balavalabali.' This was the great flood of water. What they say — 'In the beginning there was a great flood of waters' — these are those waters. Waves formed, with the sound 'phālphāśli.'
3.361: Its lower shell was green; its upper shell was silver. Having lain for a hundred divine years, it was about to burst open — or a thousand dyumnas. For dyumnas were the [units of time] then. As long as this year is, that many are the equivalents of the year. They used to reckon the year by dyumnas. Before that, day and night were merged together, undifferentiated. They were differentiated by the agnihotra. It burst open with this sound: 'The ladle-rests, the channels, the two sides — the shells are splitting apart! Toward what world is [this embryo] being born?' With the sound 'phal' it burst — this egg. Its lower shell became this earth. Its upper shell became that sky. What was in between became this mid-space. The speech that he spoke while being born — that became the threefold Veda. Whatever speech this deity spoke while being born, that became brahman itself. He considered: 'I have created a great foundation, these worlds. Whatever I shall create hereafter will indeed know [this] foundation. Come, let me create.' With mind he meditated upon himself.
3.362: He said to them [the two drops]: 'Create!' 'In which direction?' 'In this one.' With 'om' — this very sound — they created. That itself was their food, that their light; it shone upon them. Thereby the two became a kingdom. One who knows this verily attains a kingdom. With mind he again meditated upon himself. In him two drops formed — the two months of summer. He said to them: 'Create!' 'In which direction?' 'In this one.' With 'om' — this very sound — they created. Having fallen in that direction, they established themselves. The bṛhat radiance followed them. That itself was their food, that their light; it shone upon them. Thereby the two became a kingdom. One who knows this verily attains a kingdom. [He repeated this process:] With mind he again meditated upon himself. In him two drops formed — the two months of the rains. He said to them: 'Create!' 'In which direction?' 'In this one.' With 'om' they created. Having fallen in that direction, they established themselves. The vairūpa radiance followed them. That was their food, that their light; it shone upon them. Thereby the two became a kingdom. One who knows this verily attains a kingdom. In him two drops formed — the two months of autumn. He said: 'Create!' With 'om' they created. They established themselves. The vairāja radiance followed them. That was their food and light. Thereby the two became a kingdom. In him two drops formed — the two months of early winter. He said: 'Create!' With 'om' they created. They established themselves. The śākvara radiance followed them. That was their food and light. Thereby the two became a kingdom. In him two drops formed — the two months of late winter. He said: 'Create!' 'In which direction?' 'In this one.' With 'om' they created. They established themselves. The raivata radiance followed them. That was their food, that their light; it shone upon them. Thereby the two became a kingdom.
3.363: Then he trembled in his two jaws. Therefore cattle tremble in their jaws during the śiśira (late winter) season. He considered: 'Having created this threefold [universe], come, let me create from it.' With the trivṛt stoma he meditated upon the gāyatrī. From that he created the Vasus. They went toward this direction. With the pañcadaśa stoma he meditated upon the triṣṭubh. From that he created the Rudras. They went toward this direction. With the saptadaśa stoma he meditated upon the jagatī. From that he created the Ādityas. They went toward this direction. With the ekaviṃśa stoma he meditated upon the anuṣṭubh. From that he created the Viśve Devāḥ. They went toward this direction. With the triṇava stoma he meditated upon the paṅkti. From that he created the Maruts. They went toward this direction. With the trayastriṃśa stoma he meditated upon the atichandas. From that he created the Sādhyas and the Āptyas.
3.364: These deities, once created — stomas, metres, seasons, cups (graha) — their separate splendours stood shining; they did not submit to one another's supremacy. They said: 'We do not submit to one another's supremacy. Let us seek the one who would rule over us.' Approaching Prajāpati, their progenitor, they said: 'We do not submit to one another's supremacy. Create for us one who would rule over us.' Prajāpati said: 'All my tejas, indriya, and vīrya was spent when I created you. Perform tapas for a year. Having performed tapas for a year, come to me — whatever one finds, whatever another, whatever yet another.' They performed tapas for a year. Having performed tapas for a year, they came to him. Whatever one found, whatever another, whatever yet another — that they poured into his manyau (wrath-vessel), into his womb. From that, Indra came into being. Regarding this, this verse is recited: 'They say he came from a horse; they say he was born from strength — but I consider him born from wrath. From wrath he came, in the chambers he stood; whence he was born, Indra alone knows.' 'From wrath he came' — because they poured into his wrath-vessel, into his womb. 'In the chambers he stood' — the chambers are verily the hearts. For one becomes wrathful in the heart.
3.365: Gaurvīta asked him: 'You have indeed seen Indra being born.' He spoke this [verse]: 'They say he came from a horse; they say he was born from strength — but I consider him born from wrath. From wrath he came, in the chambers he stood; whence he was born, Indra alone knows.' 'I consider him born from strength,' he said. 'But Indra alone knows whence he was born.' Having thrust out his head, he looked around. He saw all this of theirs as contested. He said: 'Since all this of theirs is contested, in what world shall I be born? Give that to me.'
3.366: 'Give me the two months of spring.' 'I will give you these two.' Saying 'Food, food!' and having fallen, he took from this eastern direction the radiance of the rathantara. He placed it here. 'One alone takes the strength of two — he who knows thus.' He entered [the womb] again. He [Prajāpati] said: 'Be born again.' He [Indra] said: 'Give me these two months of summer.' 'I will give you these two.' Saying 'Food, food!' and having fallen, he took from this southern direction the radiance of the bṛhat. He placed it here. 'One alone takes the strength of four — he who knows thus.' He entered again. 'Be born again.' 'Give me these two months of the rains.' 'I will give you these two.' Saying 'Food, food!' he took from the western direction the radiance of the vairūpa. He placed it here. 'One alone takes the strength of six — he who knows thus.' He entered again. 'Be born again.' 'Give me these two months of autumn.' 'I will give you these two.' Saying 'Food, food!' he took from the northern direction the radiance of the vairāja. He placed it here. 'One alone takes the strength of eight — he who knows thus.' He entered again. 'Be born again.' 'Give me these two months of early winter.' 'I will give you these two.' Saying 'Food, food!' he took from the upward direction the radiance of the śākvara. He placed it here. 'One alone takes the strength of ten — he who knows thus.' He entered again. 'Be born again.' 'Give me these two months of late winter.' 'I will give you these two.' Saying 'Food, food!' he took from that direction the radiance of the raivata. He placed it here. 'One alone takes the strength of twelve — he who knows thus.'
3.367: He saw [Prajāpati] lying on his back. With his head he seized the two [months] in front; with his arms, these on both sides; with his feet, the two behind; with his chest, the two above; with his back, the two below. Having seized them thus, he wrapped [them] around from below. This [sun] shines, mounted upon this splendour in the seasons and the year. He entered [the womb] again with all this. He [Prajāpati] said: 'I could not raise you alone in the beginning. You will not be able to rise with all this. Be born again.' In that [womb] he perceived waters oozing. He said: 'Give me these waters, and then I shall be born.' He gave him six thousand [streams] of water. Of this there is this verse: 'No other crosses beyond them, nor is one able to swim across them. They carry for him honey, milk, ghee, and curds from all sides — six thousand mother-waters.' These are verily those mother-waters — these directions themselves. Each direction flows a thousand for him; these nourish him. He was born. They rejoiced over him at his birth: 'Tejas was born, indriya was born, vīrya was born, brahman was born, kṣatra was born, the controller of the seven kinds of cattle was born, the upholder of the seven directions was born; the born one was born, the begetter was born, the mother of the begetter was born.' He even caused himself to rejoice, saying 'the mother of the begetter was born.' One who knows this becomes one who causes rejoicing. For him, at his birth, they set down this royal throne.
3.368: The Vasus [installed him] for rājya (kingship), the Rudras for vairājya (sovereign rule), the Ādityas for svārājya (self-sovereignty), the Viśve Devāḥ for sāmrājya (imperial sovereignty), the Maruts for sārvavaśya (universal dominion), the Sādhyas and Āptyas for pārameṣṭhya (supreme overlordship). Though he was thus [established], his father Prajāpati coveted him. He said to the Vasus: 'Give him to me.' They said: 'We cannot harm our father Prajāpati.' He said to the Rudras — they did not consent. He said to the Ādityas — they did not consent either. Then he said to the Maruts: 'What would be ours [in return]?' 'That niṣkevalya midday pressing of mine — that shall be shared between me and you.' 'So be it.' Having overpowered him, they handed him over. As he [Prajāpati] kept saying 'candram me, candram me' ('splendid to me, splendid to me'), he took that tejas with which this [sun] now shines. Because he said 'candram me,' therefore [he is called] Candramās (the moon). Whatever he took again and again — even so much remained [of the sun]. Therefore even now it still has radiance. As this [sun] shines by day, so that [moon] shines by night. He [the sun] knew: 'This one is inexhaustible.' He challenged him. He [Prajāpati-moon] said: 'Do not, my son, shine against me.' 'No,' he said, 'I shall indeed shine against you.' Therefore one sees both of them during both day and night. They shine against each other. These two — the moon and the sun — are verily father and son. The moon is the father; the sun is the son. The moon is the elder; the sun is the younger. These two have attained supremacy over all the deities. Just as these two have attained supremacy over all deities, even so one attains supremacy over one's own [people] who knows this. This is regarding the deities. Now regarding the self: this [right eye] is the moon; this [left eye] is the sun. This should be meditated upon regarding the self. This is also the Bhṛgu-vision. To one whom he hates, one should say: 'I look at you with the Bhṛgu-vision.'
3.369: When the metres performed the sacrifice, the bṛhatī alone sang the udgītha. The two latter pragāthas served as the prastotṛ and pratihartṛ. The amitacchandas was the gṛhapati; the virāj was the brahman; the gāyatrī was the maitrāvaruṇa; the jagatī was the hotṛ; the paṅkti was the adhvaryu — its fifth foot being the pratiprasthātṛ; the triṣṭubh was the brāhmaṇācchaṃsin; the uṣṇih and kakubh metres were the neṣṭṛ and the poṭṛ; the anuṣṭubh was the acchāvāka; the dvipada was the unnetṛ; the akṣarapaṅkti was the grāvastut; the ekapada was the subrahmaṇya; the aticchanda was the āgnīdhra. This sacrifice, the dvādaśāha, encompasses all metres as pertaining to gāyatrī. For the dvādaśāha is measured out with all these metres. All these metres are employed in the dvādaśāha. Therefore, if one desires an invitation among these metres, one should desire an invitation among the priests. When one sacrifices, one sacrifices having become these very metres. When one causes another to sacrifice, one causes [him] to sacrifice having become these very metres. There is no misfortune whatsoever for one who knows this.
3.370: They ask: 'In which stoma, in which metre, in which deity is the dvādaśāha ultimately established?' One should say: 'In the trivṛt stoma, in the gāyatrī metre, in Agni as deity — there the dvādaśāha is ultimately established.' For they begin with the trivṛt stoma, the gāyatrī metre, and Agni as the deity; and with these same they conclude. They ask: 'What is the one stoma, what is the one metre, what is the one deity that carries the dvādaśāha?' One should say: 'The trivṛt is the one stoma, the gāyatrī is the one metre, Vāc is the one deity that carries the dvādaśāha.' For all stomas are born from the trivṛt; all metres from the gāyatrī. Vāc is the one deity that carries the dvādaśāha. They ask: 'What is the stoma, what is the metre, what is the deity of the dvādaśāha?' One should say: 'The ekaviṃśa is the stoma, the virāj is the metre, Āditya is the deity of the dvādaśāha.' For Āditya is the ekaviṃśa; Āditya is the virāj; Āditya is the deity of the dvādaśāha. They ask: 'The sāman that is the dvādaśāha — that sāman is all this. What is the stobha of the sāman, what is its foot, what is its syllable?' One should say: 'Āditya is the stobha of the sāman; the mid-space is the foot; Vāc is the syllable.' They ask: 'What is the stobha of the stobha, what is the foot of the foot, what is the syllable of the syllable?'
3.371: They ask: 'How do the stomas become one stoma? How do the metres become one metre? How do the days become one day? How do the forms become one form? How do the pṛṣṭhas become one pṛṣṭha? How do the deities become one deity? How do the ṛcs become one ṛc? How do the yajus-formulas become one yajus? How do the sāmans become one sāman? How do the Vedas become one Veda? How does all this, having become one, become established — and in what?' One should say: 'By [the word] stoma, the stomas become one. By [the word] chandas, the metres become one. By [the word] ahar, the days become one. By [the word] rūpa, the forms become one. By [the word] pṛṣṭha, the pṛṣṭhas become one. By [the word] devatā, the deities become one. By [the word] ṛc, the ṛcs become one. By [the word] yajus, the yajus-formulas become one. By [the word] sāman, the sāmans become one. By [the word] veda, the Vedas become one. Through mind, all this becomes one and is established in prāṇa.'
3.372: One should say: 'In the puruṣa.' For prāṇa is established in the puruṣa. And that [prāṇa] which is in the puruṣa — for one who knows thus, prāṇa is established [in him]. Therefore one who knows thus should be regarded as weighty, for he would not make all this light [insignificant]. Moreover, he should know: 'I am verily all this; in me all this is established.' His world does not go to ruin. They say: 'He should know the dvādaśāha, or he may consider that he knows it, who knows it as established in all the directions.' Ten are the directions. Ten are the days of the dvādaśāha. These two worlds are its prāyaṇīya and udayanīya — this world is the prāyaṇīya, that world is the udayanīya. This eastern direction is its first day, this [southern] is the second, this [western] is the third, this [northern] is the fourth, this [upward] is the fifth, this [downward] is the sixth, this intermediate direction is the seventh day, this [another intermediate] is the eighth, this is the ninth, this is the tenth. This dvādaśāha is established in all the directions. One attains the same world as all the gods. The gods cannot do without him — that he should not come to them. He is indeed one of the gods, one who knows thus. Prajāpati is verily the dvādaśāha. The directions are his worlds. This dvādaśāha is established in all the worlds. He who knows this dvādaśāha as established in all the worlds becomes established in all the worlds. This dvādaśāha is verily that which shines [the sun]. It is Indra. It is Prajāpati. It is all this.
3.373: Kṣatra (ruling power) and rāṣṭra (dominion), ṛta (cosmic order) and satya (truth) — these are below. Tapas (creative heat), tejas (brilliance), svadhā (self-sustaining power), and amṛta (immortality) — these are above. That which here is kṣatra — Indra, or Varuṇa, or Soma the king — that is that. That which is rāṣṭra — the Viśve Devāḥ, the Maruts — that is that. Ṛta is the night. Satya is the day. Tapas is lightning. Tejas is the thunderbolt that falls from the lightning — that is that. Svadhā is rain. Food — that is amṛta. Of this there is this verse: 'Kṣatra, rāṣṭra, ṛta, satya — these are deposited as the lower [supports] of brahman. Tapas, tejas, svadhā, amṛta — these sit above. In the middle, brahman shines forth.' This brahman indeed shines in the middle. Those who know thus — they sit above.
3.374: When the gods performed the sacrifice, Bṛhaspati alone sang the udgītha. Gau and Āyāsya served as the prastotṛ and pratihartṛ. Prajāpati himself was the gṛhapati. Indra was the brahman-priest. He recited from the brāhmaṇa. Mitra and Varuṇa were the maitrāvaruṇas. Agni was the hotṛ. He performed the office of āgnīdhra. The Aśvins were the adhvaryus. The Maruts were the neṣṭṛ and poṭṛ. The Viśve Devāḥ were the acchāvāka. Arbuda was the grāvastut. Viṣṇu indeed lauded the sacrifice. Soma was the unnetṛ. Brahman itself called the subrahmaṇyā invocation — or [it was] Gāyatreya. This sacrifice of all deities is the dvādaśāha. The dvādaśāha is measured out with all these deities. All these deities are employed in the dvādaśāha. Therefore, if one desires an invitation among these deities, one should desire an invitation among the priests. When one sacrifices, one sacrifices having become these very deities. When one causes another to sacrifice, one causes [him] to sacrifice having become these very deities. There is no misfortune whatsoever for one who knows this.
3.375: They ask: 'While other sattra-sessions are designated by their nights, why is the dvādaśāha designated by its days?' One should say: The Viśve Devāḥ approached their father Prajāpati and said: 'How great are you?' He said: 'I am the dvādaśāha.' Because he said 'I am the dvādaśāha,' that is the dvādaśāha-nature of the dvādaśāha. He was verily the year, Prajāpati. He said to these twelve months: 'I am the dvādaśāha.' They ask: 'What is dvā? What is daśa? What is aha?' One should say: 'Āditya is dvā (the door); Āditya is daśa (the ten); Āditya is aha (the day).' Because Āditya is dvā — he is the door of the svarga loka. Because Āditya is daśa — he shines in the ten directions. Because Āditya is aha — he is the day itself. They say: 'Should one cause another to sacrifice with the dvādaśāha, or not?'
